\end{array}$
Select the correct answer from the following

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Ectophloic siphonostele is found in
questions.solution: In the ectophloic siphonostele the xylem surrounds pith and this xylem is surrounded by phloem, pericycle and endoderm is respectively. e.g., Osmunda and Equisetum.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The protein rich alga is :
questions.solution: Chlorella, commonly known as space alga is a protein rich alga, containing about $50 \%$ protein, $20 \%$ lipids, $20 \%$ carbohydrates and a little percentage of vitamin- $\mathrm{B}_2, \mathrm{~B}_{12}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{K}$ etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Parthenogenesis is a term of
questions.solution: Parthenogenesis is a form of sexual reproduction in which the ovum develops into a new individual without fertilisation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: A monocarpic plant is one, which
questions.solution: On the basis of the frequency of flowering or fruiting in the life time, plants may be either monocarpic or polycarpic. Monocarpic plants are those, in which flowering and fruiting occurs only once in their life, e.g., all annual and biennial plants and some perennial plants like bamboo and Agave. In contrast, polycarpic plants bear flowers and fruits repeatedly after attaining maturity,  e.g., mango, Acacia, Eucalyptus, etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Reproduction without actual fertilisation is called as
questions.solution:
Reproduction without actual fertilisation is called as parthenogenesis. It is a type of reproduction in which formation of an embryo from an unfertilised egg takes place.
Parthenocarpy is the process of production of fruit without fertilisation (pollination).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Vegetative propagation in Bryophyllum takes place through
questions.solution: Vegetative reproduction in Bryophyllum takes place through leaf buds.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In which genera endospores are formed for reproduction?
questions.solution: Some species of bacteria reproduce sexually with the help of endospores. Endospores are thick-walled spores, formed singly in a bacterial cell. These are commonly seen in the species of Bacillus and Clostridium.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In animals normally which organism has maximum number of chromosomes?
questions.solution: Butterflies have most numbers of chromosome in animal kingdom about 380 .

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In which one pair both the plants can be vegetatively propagated by leaf pieces?
questions.solution: Marginal notches in Kalanchoe and Bryophyllum possess adventitious buds in their leaves for vegetative propagation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In Spirogyra sometimes a ladder like structure is present due to :
questions.solution: Sexual reproduction in Spirogyra takes place by conjugation. Scalariform conjugation occurs between the cells belonging to different filaments. Hence, these species are heterothallic. Lateral conjugation is primitive than scalariform conjugation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Development of an embryo without fertilization is called as
questions.solution: Parthenogenesis (virgin origin) is the development of an embryo from an unfertilized egg or if a spermatozoan does penetrate the egg, there is no union of male and female pronuclei. It is found in many plants (dandelions and hawk weeds) and (aphids and honey bees).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Choose the incorrect about gemmules.
options: They are asexual structure which are produce by binary fission
They can give rise to new organisms
questions.solution: Statement (c) is incorrect because Gemmules are asexual structures, produced by organisms (e.g. sponge) during unfavourable condition by the process of internal budding. They resemble endospore of bacteria. Rest statements are correct about gemmules.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: During unfavourable conditions, the sponges for
questions.solution: Under unfavourable conditions like high temperature, dry condition, sponges body gets divided into numerous small spore like bodies called gemmules to tide over the adverse environmental conditions. Cysts are formed during unfavourable conditions in Amoeba. Spicules are endoskeletons of sponge body.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Lens of eyes is derived from
questions.solution: Eye lens is derived from ectoderm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: What is the RQ of glucose?
questions.solution:
(a) The RQ (Respiratory Quotient) of glucose is 1 RQ is the ratio of the volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ produced to the volume of oxygen consumed in respiration over a period of time.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6+6 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{RQ}=\frac{6 \mathrm{CO}_2}{6 \mathrm{O}_2}=1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Decarboxylation is involved in
questions.solution: Decarboxylation is the removal of carbon from a compound to make carbon dioxide. During Krebs' cycle, the acetyl groups pass round the cycle, and the two carbon atoms are lost in the form of carbon dioxide in to decarboxylation reactions.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Phase common in aerobic and anaerobic respiration is
questions.solution: Glycolysis is an essential and first path of respiration. It is common in both aerobic and anaerobic respiration and occurs in the cytosol of all living cells of prokaryotes as well as eukaryotes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q:
Match the following columns
$\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline \text{Column I} & \text{Column II} \\
\hline \text{A. Molecular oxygen} & \text{1. } \alpha-\text{ketoglutaric acid} \\
\hline \text{B. Electron acceptor} & \text{2. Hydrogen acceptor} \\
\hline \text{C. Pyruvate dehydrogenase} & \text{3. Cytochrome-C} \\
\hline \text{D. Decarboxylation} & \text{4. Acetyl Co-A} \\
\hline
\end{array}$
questions.solution:
The correct matching
$\begin{array}{l|l}  
\hline \text{Molecular oxygen} & \text{Hydrogen acceptor} \\ 
\hline \text{Electron acceptor} & \text{Cytochrome-C} \\ 
\hline \text{Pyruvate dehydrogenase} & \text{Acetyl Co-A} \\ 
\hline \text{Decarboxylation} & \alpha-\text{ketoglutaric acid} 
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: During which stage in the complete oxidation of glucose are the greatest number of ATP molecules formed from ADP?
options: Conversion of pyruvic acid to acetyl Co-A
questions.solution:
In glycolysis 4 ATP and $2 \mathrm{NADH}_2$ molecules are formed. These $2 \mathrm{NADH}_2$ molecules go to electron transport chain.
In oxidative decarboxylation no ATP molecule is formed but two molecules of $\mathrm{NADH}_2$ are formed from two molecules of pyruvate. These two $\mathrm{NADH}_2$ go to electron transport chain. In Krebs cycle 2 ATP, $6 \mathrm{NADH}_2$ and $2 \mathrm{FADH}_2$ molecules are formed from two molecule of acetyl Co-A. These $\mathrm{NADH}_2$ and $\mathrm{FADH}_2$ go to electron transport chain. In electron transport chain all $\mathrm{NADH}_2$ and $\mathrm{FADH}_2$ pass to electron carriers and yield 3 ATP and 2 ATP molecules per $\mathrm{NADH}_2$ and $\mathrm{FADH}_2$ respectively.
Thus,
4 ATP are formed in glycolysis
2 ATP in Krebs cycle and
34 ATP from electron transport chain
40 ATP
2 ATP molecules are used during glycolysis.
So, net gain of ATP molecules during one complete oxidation of a glucose molecule is 38 ATP.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The incomplete breakdown of sugars in anaerobic respiration results in the formation of :
questions.solution: The anaerobic respiration occurs in many bacteria, some fungi, germinating seeds and some fruits. It involves the incomplete breakdown of sugars forming $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ as end products and 27 kcal energy is released.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The net requirement of assimilatory power for the formation of 6 hexose molecules in maize plant is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{C}_4$ plants (maize) require 30 ATP and 12 NADPH for synthesis of one hexose molecule. Therefore synthesis of six hexose molecules require 180 ATP and 72 NADPH.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The respiratory quotient during cellular respiration would depend on the
questions.solution: R Q is the ratio of the volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ eleased to the volume of oxygen taken in respiration. It depends on the nature of the substrate, which is oxidised, for carbohydrates $\mathrm{R} Q$ is one, for fats and protein less then one, and is more than one for organic acids.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: RQ of malic acid is
questions.solution: RQ of malic acid is 1.33. Respiratory Quotient (RQ) is the ratio of the volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ produced to the volume of $\mathrm{O}_2$ consumed in respiration over a period of time. RQ is slightly more than unity when organic acids are broken down as respiratory substrates under aerobic conditions, e.g. malic acid.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: How many molecules of pyruvic acid are formed in glycolysis?
questions.solution:
In glycolysis, 2 molecules of pyruvic acid are formed. It is the first step of degradation of glucose. The main purpose of glycolysis is the generation of energy in the form of ATP.
However, comparatively more ATPs are formed downstream of glycolysis through the complete oxidation of pyruvate.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: During anaerobic conditions, the rate of glycolysis increases, is called :
questions.solution: Pasteur's effect refers to the increase of rate of glycolysis in absence of $\mathrm{O}_2$ due to the non-availability of those enzymes which are easily available in the presence of $\mathrm{O}_2$.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In which part of mitochondria does ATP synthesis occur?
questions.solution: During the oxidation process (occurs in inner mitochondrial membrane during electron transport system), enormous amount of free energy is released, some of which is utilixed by inner membrane sub units or $\mathrm{F}_1$ particles containing three coupling factors and ATP enzyme, in the synthesis of ATP molecules.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Fat, prior to its oxidation associate with
questions.solution: The respiratory decomposition of fatty acids is known as beta oxidation, which occurs in liver and adipose tissue. First of all, there is activation of fatty acid, then dehydrogenation of activated fatty acid takes place. This is followed by hydration. The $\beta$-hydroxyl acyl derivative is converted to $\beta$-keto derivative, which then reacts with Co-A. Finally there is oxidation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The net gain ATP from complete oxidation of 1 molecule of glucose in eukaryote is
questions.solution: There is a total gain of 38 ATP molecules during aerobic respiration of one molecules of glucose. Out of these two molecules of ATP are required for transporting the NADH produced in glycolysis (in cytoplasm) into the mitochondria for further oxidation, hence the net gain of ATP is 36 molecules.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Glycolysis occurs in
questions.solution: Glycolysis or Embden Myerhof Parna spathway takes place in cytoplasm. It is a common phase between aerobic and anaerobic respiration. The enzymes occur in cytosol.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The terminal electron acceptor during electron transport system is
questions.solution:
Oxygen is the terminal acceptor of electron in electron transport system, which in the presence of proton forms a water molecule
$\begin{gathered}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2+2 e \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}^{2-} \\ 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Common phase in aerobic and anaerobic respiration is
questions.solution: Glycolysis is a common phase for aerobic and anaerobic respiration where 1 molecule of glucose (6 C)breakdown into 2 molecules of pyruvic acid (3 C).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: How many ATP are formed by oxidation of one glucose molecule in liver cells ?
questions.solution:
In liver cells and prokaryotic cells, 38 ATP are produced by the complete oxidation of one molecule of glucose.
$\begin{aligned} \text { Glycolysis } 2 \text { ATP directly } & =2 \text { ATP } \\ 2 \text { mol. of NADH } & =6 \text { ATP }\end{aligned}$
Pyruvic acid to
Acetyl Co-A 2 mol. of NADH = 6 ATP
Citric acid 6 mol. of NADH = 18 ATP
    cycle
$\begin{array}{ll}2 \mathrm{~mol} . \text { of FADH } & =4 \mathrm{ATP} \\ 2 \mathrm{~mol} . \text { of GTP } & =2 \mathrm{ATP} \\ \text { Total } & =38 \mathrm{ATP}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: R.Q. is always less than one in
questions.solution: Respiratory Quotient is the ratio of the volume of carbon-dioxide evolved to that of oxygen consumed by an organism tissue, or cell in a given time. Castor is a rich source of fats. In fats, the value of R.Q. is less than one.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the following statements is correct?
options: Hard outer layer of pollen is called intine
questions.solution: Tapetum is innermost nutritive structure of anther wall which nourishes the developing pollen grain.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: With respect to angiosperms, identify the incorrect pair from the following
questions.solution: Out of the given pairs in the option only antipodal is wrongly matched because antipodals are halploid cells ( $n$ ) found within the mature embryo sac towards the chalazal end. Rest all three options are correctly matched.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the following is not a flower?
questions.solution: Sunflower (Helianthus annuus) is a member of Asteraceae family. Its flower is not a real flower but a whole inflorescence having a group of many small flowers. This inflorescence is known as capitulum or head inflorescence.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q:
An irregular mode of reproduction resulting in the development of an embryo without fertilisation is called
I. parthenogenesis
II. apogamy
III. sporophytic budding
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.solution:
In parthenogenesis formation of fruit occurs without fertilisation.
In apogamy haploid $(n)$ vegetative cell forms embryo without gametogenesis and fertilisation.
In sporophytic budding diploid (2n) vegetative cell forms embryo from bud by vegetative reproduction.
Thus, all of these processes are irregular modes of reproduction.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Cotyledons and testa are edible parts of
questions.solution: Cotyledons and testa are edible parts of ground nut and pomegranate respectively. The edible part of walnut is cotyledon, tamarind-mesocarp, french beans-seeds, coconut-endosperm testa, cotyledons and embryo, cashewnut-cotyledons and fleshy pedicels and of litchi is fleshy aril.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Hardest substance in plant kingdom is
questions.solution: Sporopollenin is the hardest substance in plant kingdom. It is the major constituent of outer layer (exine) of pollen grains. It is the most resistant organic material that can withstand high temperature as well as strong acids and alkali. No enzyme is known which can degrade it.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In coconut, the liquid endosperm is formed because
options: karyokinesis is not followed by cytokinesis
formation of liquid endosperm is not dependent upon karyokinesis and cytokinesis
questions.solution: In coconut the karyokinesis is not followed by cytokinesis after a free nuclear division. So, the endosperm takes a liquid form.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Rarely among angiosperms, the pollen grains influenced the endosperm. This is called as
questions.solution: The direct or indirect effect of pollen is seed or fruit has been termed by Foke (1881) as xenia. This phenomenon is seen is Zea mays alone and in limited to the endosperm part only.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Aleurone grains are rich in
questions.solution: Aleurone grains are rich in protein. This is the peripheral part of endosperm and is very important physiologically because it secrete or accumulates the hydrolysing enzymes which help in digestion of reserve food material during seed germination.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The following is the diagram of TS of anther. Identify the parts labelled as A, B and C.
questions.solution: The typical anther is tetrasporangiate. It has column of sterile tissue called connective tissue. Mature anther is comprised of an epidermis followed by endothecium, 2 or 3 middle layer and single layered tapetum (inner most).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Endosperm of gymnosperms is
questions.solution: Endosperm of gymnosperm is a pre-fertilisation tissue. It is basically the female gametophyte hence it is haploid unlike triploid in angiosperm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q:
Study the following statements and select the correct option.
I. Tapetum nourishes the developing pollen grains.
II. Hilum represents the junction between ovule and funicle.
III. In aquatic plants such as water hyacinth and water lily, pollination is by water.
IV. The primary endosperm nucleus is triploid.
options: II II, and IV are correct, but III is incorrect
I and II are correct, but III and IV are incorrect
I and IV are correct, but II and III are incorrect
I, III and IV are correct, but I is incorrect
questions.solution: In a majority of water plants like water hyacinth and flowers, water lily emerge above the water level and are pollinated by insects.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Masses of pollen grains, i.e., pollinia is found in
questions.solution:
Pollinia is a coherent mass of pollen grains. They are the product of only one anther lobe, but are transferred during pollination as a single unit.
This is found in orchids (Orchidaceae).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: A mature pollen grain of Pinus has
questions.solution: In  Pinus, the microspore nucleus divides by a periclinal wall and forms a very small prothallial cell and large central cell. The central cell cuts off a second prothallial cell and antheridial cell. The nucleus of the antheridial cell divides to form generative cell and tube cell. Thus, the pollen grain of Pinus is shed at four cell stage when it consists of two vegetative prothallial cells, a generative cell and a tube cell.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: When pollen tube enters through micropyle, the process is called
questions.solution: The pollen tube may enter the ovule by following one of three routes given below: (1) Porogamy Pollen tube enters the ovule through micropyle, e.g., most angiosperms. (2) Mesogamy Pollen tube enters the ovule through integuments (e.g., Cucurbita) or through funiculus (e.g., Pistacia). (3) Chalazogamy Pollen tube enters the ovule through chalazal tissues, e.g., Casuarina, Beta vulgaris, Juglens regia, etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Outer wall of pollen grain is made up of
questions.solution: A pollen grain contains two layered cell wall, the outer exine and inner intine. Sporopollenin is found in the exine of pollen grains, which is the most resistant biological material known, of microbial and chemical decomposition. Due to this, pollen grains are well preserved during fossilization. Intine of pollen grain is made up of pectocellulose.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which of the following floral parts forms pericarp after fertilization?
questions.solution: After fertilization, the mature, ripened ovary develops into fruit. The ovary wall forms the covering of the fruit called fruit wall or pericarp. Some other post fertilization changes also occur like nucellus develops into perisperm, ovules develop into seeds, outer integument forms testa, inner integument forms tegmen, etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Unisexuality of flowers prevents
questions.solution:
The transfer of pollen from anther to stigma is called pollination. Autogamy also called self-pollination involves the transfer of pollen grains of one flower to the stigma of same flower. Self-pollination occurs only in bisexual or hermaphrodite flowers.
Geitonogamy involves the transfer of pollen grains from a male flower to stigma of female flower of same plant. Thus, geitonogamy operates only in monoecious plants, i.e., the plants having male and female flowers on different places.
Allogamy or xenogamy also known as cross-pollination involves the transfer of pollen grains of male flower to the stigma of genetically different female flower. It takes place via various agencies like wind, water, insects, etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Double fertilization occurs in
questions.solution: Double fertilization is the characteristic feature of angiosperms, e.g., Capsella. It includes fusion of one male gamete with egg and second male gamete with secondary nucleus (this forms endosperm).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Stem cuttings are commonly used for the propagation of
questions.solution: Stem cuttings are used in rose, Citrus, Cocoa. Rooting of stem cuttings is hastened by dipping in NAA or IBA. Grafting is done in case of mango. Rhizomes take part in vegetative propagation due to presence of buds, eg, banana, ginger.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Ubisch bodies are present in
questions.solution: Ubisch bodies : Tapetum is the innermost layer of another wall which surrounds the sporogenous tissues. These cells are nutritive in function. In cells of tapetum, ubisch bodies are present, which help in the formation of microspore wall or exine of pollen grain. Pollen grain (microspore) is generally a round haploid structure from which gametophyte is developed. Pollen tube helps in carrying male gametes to female gamete for fertilisation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Find odd one out.
questions.solution: Stamen is the odd one out among the other options. Stamen represent the male reproductive part of a flower, where as, stigma, style and ovary, are the parts of a carpel or pistil which represents the female reproductive part of flower.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: An obligate root parasite is
questions.solution: Obligate parasites are organism which obtain their food from host throughout their life, eg, Rafflesia, Orobanche (root parasite). Facultative parasites are organism which are normally parasitic but can obtain their food from dead organic matter under certain conditions. Obligate root parasite Rafflesia, Orobanche. Partial stem parasite Viscum, Loranthus. Partial root parasite Santalum. Obligate stem parasite Cuscuta.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The one advantage of cleistogamy is
options: it leads to greater genetic diversity
seed dispersal is more efficient and wide spread
seed set is not dependent on pollinators
each visit of a pollinator results in transfer of hundreds of pollen grains
questions.solution: Cleistogamous flowers do not open so, they self-pollinate and thus, do not depend on pollinators.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Match the following columns

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Pollenkitt is present in
questions.solution: Pollenkitt is present in entomophily. It is a sticky covering on the surface of pollen grains. Its major function is to make the pollen to stick to the bugs. Thus, it helps in the pollination.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In Cycas, pollination takes place in
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l|l}
\hline \text{Plant} & \text{Pollination takes place in} \\
\hline \text{Cycas} & 3-\text{celled stage} \\
\text{Pinus} & 4 \text{ to } 5-\text{celled stage} \\
\text{Angiosperm} & 2 \text{ to } 3-\text{celled stage} \\
\hline
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: If root of a flowering plant has 24 chromosomes, then its gamete has how many chromosomes?
questions.solution:
The root cell of flowering plant is diploid $(2 n=24)$ while the gamete is haploid therefore, the number of chromosomes becomes 12 in the gamete.
The cells of stem, leaf and somatic cells of flower are diploid, therefore, they contain the chromosome number $2 n=24$.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The cavity of diencephalon is known as
questions.solution: Diencephalon is situated between the cerebral hemispheres above and midbrain below. Its cavity known as the third ventricle or diocoel is connected to each lateral ventricle of cerebral hemisphere by foramen of Monro, separately.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Growth of pollen tube towards embryo sac is
questions.solution: Chemotaxis is locomotory movement in response to presence of chemicals, eg, growth of pollen tube towards egg.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Male gamete in angiosperm is produced by
questions.solution: In angiosperms, male gametophyte consists of a tube cell and generative cell which divides to form two male gametes.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Number of chromosomes in an angiospermic plant is 14 , then the number of chromosomes in synergid cells will be
questions.solution: 7 , the number of chromosomes in root cells is $2 n$ while it is $n$ in synergids because it develops by reductional division.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In a type of apomixis known as adventive embryony, embryos develop directly from the
options: synergids or antipodals in an embryo sac
accessory embryo sacs in the ovule
questions.solution: Apomixis does not involve gamete formation and fertilization as found in amphimixis. Adventive embryo formation is a type of apomixis in which embryos are formed from diploid integumental or nucellar cells.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Through which cell of the embryo sac, does the pollen tube enter the embryo sac?
questions.solution: Egg apparatus is present towards the micropylar end of an ovule. Egg apparatus has two lateral synergid cells and one centrally located egg cell. During entry of pollen tube within the ovule synergid cells become disintegrate and provide path for entry of pollen tube within the chamber of embryo sac.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In the given diagram, parts labelled as A, B, C, D, E and F are respectively indentified as
options: synergids, polar nuclei, central cell, antipodals, filiform apparatus and egg
polar nuclei, egg, antipodals, central cell, filiform apparatus and synergids
egg, synergids, central cell, filiform apparatus, antipodals and polar nuclei
central cell, polar nuclei filiform apparatus, antipodals, synergids and egg
questions.solution: Polygonum type of ovule, Emhryo sac 7 celled, 8 nucleate, 3 antipodal, 2 synergids, one egg and one central cell.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the following represents an ovule, where the embryo sac becomes horse-shoe shaped and the funiculus and micropyle are close to each other?
questions.solution: In an amphitropous ovule, the embryo sac becomes horse-shoe shaped and the funiculus and micropyle are close to each other. This type of ovule is found in Alismaceae, Butamaceae families.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Perisperm is :
questions.solution: Perisperm is the remnant of nucellus. It is found in Piperaceae and Scitaminaceae. It is formed when the nucellus is not completely used up and is present in the form of a thin layer in the seed enclosing the embryo and the endosperm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Why is vivipary an undesirable character for annual crop plants?
options: It reduces the vigour of plant
The seeds cannot be stored under normal conditions for the next season
It adversely affects the fertility of the plant
questions.solution: Vivipary is the condition when seeds germinate on the plant. It is an undesirable character for annual crop plants because germinated seeds cannot be stored under normal conditions for the next season.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: An orthotropus ovule is one in which micropyle and chalaza are :
questions.solution: Orthotropous ovule is also called atropous. In this type of ovule the micropyle, chalaza and funicle lie in one straight line, e.g., Polygonaceae, Piperaceae etc.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Double fertilisation was discovered by
questions.solution: The two gametes released by the pollen tube move in opposite direction most probably the streaming currents of cytoplasm. One gamete fuses with egg called fertilisation and second fuses with two polar nuclei called triple fusion i.e. fertilisation and triple fusion jointly called double fertilisation. It was first observed by Nawaschin (1848) in Lilium and Fritillaria. This phenomenon is unique to angiosperm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: If the pollen tube enters the ovule through funiculus, the phenomenon is known as
questions.solution: In porogamy the pollen tube enters the ovule through micropyle, in chalazogamy pollen tube enters through chalaza and in mesogamy pollen tube enters through funiculus.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Double fertilization means
options: fusion of nucleus of male gamete with the egg nucleus
fusion of sperm nucleus with secondary nucleus
fusion of one sperm nucleus with nucleus and fusion of other sperm nucleus with secondary nucleus
questions.solution: Double fertilization is the process the one sperm fuses with the egg while the other sperm fuses with the two polar nuclei. mien pollen tube enters ovule, it strikes one of the synergids and burst open to release the two male gametes which fuses with two different structures in the same female gametophyte. The fusion two polar nuclei forms endosperm. So, the correct answer is option D.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In gymnosperms, the pollen chamber represents
options: a cell in the pollen grain in which the sperms are formed
a cavity in the ovule in which pollen grains are stored after pollination
an opening in the megagametophyte through which the pollen tube approaches the egg
the microsporangium in which pollen grains develop
questions.solution: In gymnosperms, the pollen chamber is a cavity in the ovule in which pollen grains are stored after pollination.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Nucellus forms which of the following part of fruit?
questions.solution:
The post fertilization changes in the seeds are as follows-
Ovule—Seed
Ovary—Fruit
Nucellus-Perisperm (a nutritive tissue)
Integument—Seed coat.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: After a deep inspiration and maximum expiration, the capacity of lungs is known as
questions.solution:
Vital capacity is the amount of air which one can inhale and exhale with maximum effort. It is the sum of tidal volume, inspiratory reserve volume and expiratory reserve volume.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{VC} & =\mathrm{TV}+\mathrm{IRV}+\mathrm{ERV} \\ & =500+3100+1200 \\ & =4800 \mathrm{~mL}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Ligament is mainly made up of
questions.solution: Ligament is tough cord or fibrous band of dense regular connective tissue that contains numerous parallel arrangements of collagen fibres. It connects bones or cartilages and serves to strengthen joints.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: For the formation of tetrasporic embryosac, how many megaspore mother cells are required?
questions.solution:
- One megaspore divides meiotically to form four megaspores.
- Sometimes meiotic division of the megaspore mother cell is not accompanied by cytokinesis and hence all the four haploid nuclei lie in a single cell called coeno-megaspore.
- All the four nuclei of coeno-megaspore participate in the formation of the embryo sac.
- This type of embryo sac is called as tetrasporic like in Peperomia.
- The four haploid nuclei of coeno-megaspore undergo two successive mitotic divisions forming 16 nuclei.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which of the following is the most resistant material in pollen grain?
questions.solution: Sporopollenin is most resistant chemical substance in pollen grains wall.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the given pollination technique/adaptation is different than others?
questions.solution:
Geitonogamy is a technique of self-pollination, in which pollen of one flower is deposited on the stigma of another flower of same plant.
While others are mechanisms adopted by bisexual flowers to avoid self-pollination and promote cross-pollination.
- Dichogamy The condition where maturing time of anther and stigma is different. Anther matures first, i.e. protandry, stigma matures first, i.e. protogyny.
- Herkogamy Includes mechanical barrier in flower to ensure cross-pollination.
- Heterostyle The height of stigma and anther is different to avoid self-pollination.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the following pair is not correctly matched?
questions.solution: Guava (Psidium guajava) fruit is an pseudocapric berry, in which pericarp and placenta are edible.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Thigmotaxis is not shown by
questions.solution:
Ascaris do not show thigmotaxis.
Amoeba shows thigmotaxis. On coming in contact with a solid object Amoeba generally avoid it by moving in a different direction by forming pseudopodia.
Hydra is known to respond to a variety of stimuli, both external and internal. On contact with a solid object other than a prey, the whole body usually contracts rather rapidly.
On contact with a hard solid object Paramecium induces avoiding response.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The basic unit of classification is
questions.solution: The basic unit of classification is species. It is the lowest category of classification. It is a group of closely related individuals with similar morphological, anatomical, biochemical and cytological characters. It is a group of naturally interbreeding population with ability to produce fertile offsprings. Individuals of a species share a common gene pool. Species is reproductively isolated, thus genetically closed system. Species has the real existence in nature. The term 'species' was given by John Ray, an English naturalist.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: A horse and a donkey can breed to produce mule which is an infertile animal. The infertility is because horse and donkey belong to different
questions.solution: A horse (Equus ferus caballus) and a donkey (Equus africanus asinus) can breed to produce mule, which is an infertile animal. The infertility is because horse and donkey belong to different species. Mule are medium weight animals that are more hardworking and long-lived than horse and donkey.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: One of the most important functions of botanical gardens is that
options: one can observe tropical plants there
they allow ex situ conservation of germplasm
they provide the natural habitat for wild life
they provide a beautiful area for recreation
questions.solution: A Botanical garden is a collection of various types of living plants. Ex situ conservation means conservation of plants or animals in the artificial habitats, which are quite similar to the normal habitats of these organisms. In this way botanical gardens provide ex situ conservation of germplasm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q:
Match the following entities of column I with their respective orders of column II and choose the correct combination form the option.
$\begin{array}{ll|ll}
\hline & \text{Column I} & & \text{Column II} \\
\hline \text{A.} & \text{Wheat} & \text{1.} & \text{Primate} \\
\text{B.} & \text{Mango} & \text{2.} & \text{Diptera} \\
\text{C.} & \text{Housefly} & \text{3.} & \text{Sapindales} \\
\text{D.} & \text{Man} & \text{4.} & \text{Poales} \\
\hline
\end{array}$
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l|l|l|l|l}
\hline \text{Common Name} & \text{Scientific Name} & \text{Family} & \text{Order} & \text{Class} \\
\hline \text{Wheat} & \text{Triticum aestivum} & \text{Poaceae} & \text{Poales} & \text{Monocotyledons} \\

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Sertoli cells are found in testis. These cells are
questions.solution: Sertoli cells are found in testis. These cells nourish the developing sperms.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Ruminants belong to order
questions.solution: Ruminants have the habit of chewing the cud. Stomach of ruminants have four parts, ie, rumen, reticulum, omassum and abomassum. They belong to order-Artiodactyla of class-Mammalia, eg, cow, sheep, goat. Elephants belong to order-Proboscidea of class-Mammalia. Marsupials are the members of subclassMetatheria, in which a pouch marsupium is present for keeping and nourishing the young ones, eg, kangaroo.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Taxonomic key is one of the taxonomic tools in the identification and classification of plants and animals. It is used in the preparation of
questions.solution:
Taxonomic keys are tools that help in identification of organisms based on the description of specific characters.
e.g. flora (A resource providing information on the taxonomy, nomenclature and descriptive data of newly discovered plants).
Monographs (A comprehensive treatement of a taxon).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Systema Naturae was written by
questions.solution: 'Systema Naturae' was written by Carolus Linnaeus. Linnaeus is called father of taxonomy.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Organisms who are able to freely interbreed producing fertile offsprings and having similar blue print for making these organisms are referred to as
questions.solution: The term species was given by John Ray and is the lowest category of classification. Species is a group of closely related individuals having similar morphological, anatomical, biochemical and cytological characteristics. It consists of naturally interbreeding population with the ability to produce fertile offsprings. This group is reproductively isolated and genetically closed.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Most accepted theory for ascent of sap is
questions.solution: Transpiration pull is the tension which develops due to transpiration. It has been demonstrated and evidenced, that rate of water absorption and hence, ascent of sap closely follows the rate of transpiration and hence, ascent of sap closely follows the rate of transpiration.Cohesion andtranspiration pull theory, given by Dixon and Jolly (1894) is the most accepted theory for ascent of sap.Root Pressure theory (Priestley; 1916) is a manifestation of active water absorption. It was objected, as it has not been applicable to all plants (eg, gymnospermous tallest trees). Moreover, this pressure is low to raise the sap to the top of trees and also many reasons which this theory was unable to proove.Pulsation theory orvital force theory (J C Bose; 1923) was also discarded because living cells do not seen to be involved in the ascent of sap as water continuous to rise upward in the plant in which roots have been cut or living cells of stem are killed by poison and heat (Strassburger; 1891).Capillarity theory (Bohm; 1863), laid stress on capillarity movement of water due to adhesion and cohesion forces balanced by downward pull of gravity. It was also discarded, as the value of capillary is too small and applicable only to small sized plants and tall plants with narrow vessels.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Root hair absorbs water from soil through
questions.solution: Root hair absorbs water from soil through osmosis. Osmosis is a special type of diffusion of a liquid, when solvent moves through a semipermeable membrane from a place of higher diffusion pressure to a place of lower diffusion pressure.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Two cells A and B are contigous. Cell A has osmotic pressure 10 atm , turgor pressure-7 atm and diffusion pressure deficit 3 atm . Cell B has osmotic pressure 8 atm , turgor pressure 3 atm and diffusion pressure deficit 5 atm . The result will be
options: movement of water from cell $B$ to $A$
movement of water from cell A to B
questions.solution: The water movs from lower DPD to higher DPD.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The rupture and fractionation do not usually occur in the water column in vessel/tracheids during the ascent of sap because of
questions.solution:
The vertical conduction of water from root to aerial parts of plant is called ascent of sap. The water molecules remain joined to each other due to a force of attraction called cohesion force. This attraction is due to the presence of hydrogen bonds between them. The magnitude of this force is very high therefore, the continuous water column in the xylem cannot be broken easily due to the force of gravity or other obstructions offered by internal tissues in the upward movement of water. This adhesive property of water, i.e., attraction between the water molecules and the walls of xylem ensures the continuity of water column in the xylem.
Due to transpiration water evaporates to outer atmosphere through stomata. As a result leaf cells develop low water potential and water from leaf veins moves into leaf cells. The xylem vessel in turn draw water from xylem of main stem. A pressure (pull) is thus, exerted by all the leaves on the stems, which is called transpiration pull. This is strong enough to pull up the column of water to a great height.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The ruptere and fractionation do not usually occur in water column in vessel/trachieds during the ascent of sap because of
questions.solution: The vertical conduction of water from root to arial part of plant is called ascent of sap. The water molecules remain joined to each other due to a force of attraction calledcohesion force. Attraction between water molecules and the walls of xylem is due to adhesion forces. These forces help to ensure the continuity of water column in xylem.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In plants, water supply is due to
questions.solution: The force between walls and water called adhesion force. Diffusion of water from its pure or dilute solution into a stronger solution when the two are separated by a semi-permeable membrane is termed asosmosis. In plants water supply is due toosmosis. The absorption of water by the solid particles of an adsorbant without forming a solution is calledimbibition. The loss of water in the form of liquid droplets from the leaves and other parts of an uninjured or intact plant is called guttation.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Osmotic pressure of a solution is
options: less than or greater than pure solvent
questions.solution: Osmotic pressure of solution is greater than pure solvent. When a solution is separated from pure solvent by a semi-permeable membrane, the solvent begins to flow towards the solution due to development of osmotic pressure.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Cell placed in hypertonic solution is shown by
questions.solution:
The figure given in option (a) depicts that the movement of water is outside the cell and the cell is plasmolysed, i.e. the cell membrane has got shrinked, these observations signify that the cell is placed in hypertonic solution. The balanced inflow and outflow of water in the cell shown in option (b) indicates that it is placed in isotonic solution.
In option (c), there is net inflow of water inside the cell and the cell is turgid, thus it can be said that the cell is placed in hypotonic solution.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Potometer works on the principle of
options: amount of water absorbed equals the amount transpired
potential difference between the tip of the tube and that of the plant
questions.solution: Evaporation of water in the form of vapours from aerial parts of a living plant is known as transpiration. Measurement of transpiration can be done with the help of potometer. It works on the principle of amount of water absorbed equals the amount of water transpired.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Transpiration would be lowest when :
options: enough water is in the soil
questions.solution: When atmospheric RH is high, the temperature of atmosphere will be low. This will reduce the rate of evaporation and also reduces the rate of transpiration.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Who said that "transpiration is a necessary evil" ?
questions.solution: Curtis (1926) called transpiration as necessary evil because it provides some beneficial aspects but on the other hand, many functioning and energy gets wasted from absorption of water upto the stage of transpiration.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which of the following exhibits a direct porportionality to transpiration?
questions.solution: Temperature and wind have the direct proportional to the transpiration. When the winds faster and dry and temperature is high, the rate of water evaporation will also high, increasing the rate of transpiration.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Potometer is used to measure
questions.solution: Potometer is used to measure transpiration rate. Potometers are of four types simple, Ganong's, Bose's and Farmer's potometer.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Water in plants is transported by :
questions.solution: The water absorbed by root hairs is transported to other parts of the plant through xylem. Phloem transfers food prepared in leaves to the other parts. Cambium is responsible for secondary growth while epidermis is the outermost protective layer of cells.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The most important force which pulls water up in tall trees is :
questions.solution: The most important force which pulls water up in the tall trees is the cohesive (inter-molecular) force between $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ molecules. It also helps in maintaining the continuity of water column in tall trees.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The magnitude of root pressure ranges from:
questions.solution: The term root pressure was coined by Stephen Hales (1727). The evidences that support the existence of this phenomenon are bleeding and guttation. The magnitude of root pressure is $1-2 \mathrm{~atm}$ and very rarely 5 atm.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: The real forces responsible for the movement of water from one cell to another cell is mainly
questions.solution: The net force with which water is drawn into a cell is equal to difference of OP and TP, known as diffusion pressure deficit or suction pressure. DPD of pure water is maximum ( $\approx 1236 \mathrm{~atm}$ ) solvent moves from cell of low DPD to high DPD. DPD, or $\mathrm{SP}=\mathrm{OP}-\mathrm{TP}$.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In which form does the food transported in plants?
questions.solution: Translocation is the movement of organic nutrients (ie, food) from the region of source or supply to the region of sink or utilisation. Translocation of food occurs through phloem. This organic food is translocated in the form of sucrose, a disaccharide, which is relatively inactive and highly soluble sugar.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Ovulation takes place in a month between
questions.solution:
The menstrual cycle can be divided into four phases: menstruation, proliferative, ovulatory and secretory phase.
Day 1 - 5 (menstruation phase) experience lower estrogens and progesterone level, which in turn results in disintegration of endometrium and menses.
Day 5 - 13 (proliferative phase) is marked by endometrium thickening under the influence of estrogens as secreted by developing follicle.
Day 11-14 comes under proliferative phase. Proliferative phase is followed by ovulation, i.e., rupture of mature Graafian follicle and release of an ovum in the body cavity, which is regulated by higher LH levels.
Ovulation occurs on \(14^{\text {th }}\) day of 28 days cycle. Ovulation is followed by the secretory phase in (day \(15-28\)), which is marked by uterine endometrium thickening under the influence of progesterone as secreted by corpus luteum, to prepare it for implantation. Thus, the correct answer is option B.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Munch hypothesis is based on
options: translocation of food due to Turgor Pressure (TP) gradient and imbibition force
translocation of food due to Turgor Pressure (TP) gradient
translocation of food due to imbition force
questions.solution: Munch hypothesis is based on the movement of phloem sap along a turgor pressure gradient. The mass flow of organic solutes takes place from the site of higher concentration, (source) to the site of lower concentration (sink).

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: Which one of the following doesn't help in molecule transport?
questions.solution: Surface tension doesn't help in molecule transport. The process of diffusion and osmosis transport molecules is without using energy but in case of active transport energy (ATP) is used.

questions.subject: Botany
questions.q: In which form does the food transported in plants?
questions.solution: In plants food produced by photosynthesis is transported in the form of sucrose through phloem. It is estimated that $90 \%$ of the total solute carried in the phloem is the carbohydrate sucrose, a disaccharide which is relatively inactive and highly soluble sugar with little direct role in metabolism.

Thus, the main product of the reaction is triphenyl phosphate $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5\right)_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the correct sequence of step involved in the mechanism of Cannizzaro reaction?
options: Nucleophilic attack, transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{-}$and transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$
Transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{-}$, transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and nucleophilic attack
Transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$, nucleophilic attack and transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{-}$
Electrophilic attack by $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$and transfer of $\mathrm{H}^{-}$
questions.solution: Cannizzaro reaction involves the aldehydes which do not have an $\alpha$-hydrogen atom, undergo self oxidation and reduction reaction on heating with conc. alkali. $\mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text { conc. } \mathrm{KOH}} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HCOO}^{-}$ Mechanism Step I Attack of nucleophile $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$to the carbonyl carbon. Step II Transfer of hydride ion from anion I to second molecule of aldehyde and finally rapid transfer of proton takes place.

questions.subject: Chemistry

options: $S_N 1$ mechanism carried out in this reaction is rapidly in presence of HI
questions.solution: The cleavage of ether $(R O R)$ by a halogen hydracid is eventually a $S_N 2$ type displacement where halide ion $\left(X^{-}\right)$acts as a nucleophile. Since, $\mathrm{I}^{-}$is a very strong nucleophile compared to $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$, reaction easily occurs when HI is used and not by HCl .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following compounds is most acidic?
questions.solution: Phenols are much more acidic than alcohols, due to the stabilisation of phenoxide ion by resonance. Phenoxide ion is stabilized due to following resonating structures : While in alcohols Ortho nitrophenol is most acidic because in it $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ electron attracting group is attached on ortho position which helps in stabilising of negative charge on the oxygen of phenoxide ion. Hence, due to this reason acidic character of phenol is increased, while on attachment of $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group (electron donating group) acidic strength of phenol is decreased in cresol due to destabilisation of phenoxide ion.

$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{HCHO} \xrightarrow[\text { Heat }]{\text { Dil. } \mathrm{NaOH}} A \xrightarrow[\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}]{\mathrm{HCN}} B$
The structure of compound $B$ is
questions.solution:
It undergoes aldol condensation to give \(\mathrm{CH}_2(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2(\mathrm{CHO})\) which dehydrates to give \(\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHCHO}\)
When this is treated with \(\mathrm{HCN} / \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}\), it gives the carboxylic acid.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{HCHO} \xrightarrow{\text { Dil. } \mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{CH}_2(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \\
& \mathrm{CH}_2(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CHCHO} \\
& \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CHCHO} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HCN}} \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CHCH}(\mathrm{OH})(\mathrm{CN}) \\
& \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CHCH}(\mathrm{OH})(\mathrm{CN}) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{COOH})(\mathrm{OH})
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is most basic in nature?
questions.solution:
Basicity of amines depends upon the availability of lone pair of nitrogen for donation. Electron releasing group like $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ increases the basicity of amines by increasing electron density over nitrogen, which facilitate the donation of lone pair of electrons.
Electron withdrawing groups like -Ph group withdraws electrons and thus, make amines less basic by decreasing electron density over nitrogen. Hence, the order of basic nature of the given amines is
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{NH}\gt\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{~N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2\gt\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{NH}_2\gt\mathrm{NH}_3$
Thus, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{NH}$ is most basic.

questions.subject: Chemistry

$\because$ Electron releasing groups increases the basic nature while electron withdrawing group decreases the basis strength of amines.
Therefore, correct order is III $\gt$ II $\gt$ IV $\gt$ I.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The relative reactivities of acyl compounds towards nucleophilic substitution are in the order of
options: acyl chloride $\gt$ acid anhydride $\gt$ ester $\gt$ amide
ester $\gt$ acyl chloride $\gt$ amide $\gt$ acid anhydride
acid anhydride $\gt$ amide $\gt$ ester $\gt$ acyl chloride
acyl chloride $\gt$ ester $\gt$ acid anhydride $\gt$ amide
questions.solution: The ease of nucleophilic substitution is depend upon the nature of leaving group. When the leaving tendency of a group in a compound is high, then the compound is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution. The nucleophilic acyl substitution is completed in two steps as shown below The reactivity of the compound may be explained on the basicity of the leaving group. A weaker base is a better leaving group. The basicity order is as $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\gt\mathrm{RCOO}^{-}\gt\mathrm{RO}^{-}\gt\mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$ Hence, the order of the leaving tendency is $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\gt\mathrm{RCOO}^{-}\gt\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{O}^{-}\gt\mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$ and therefore, the order of reactivity of acyl compound is as Acyl chloride $\gt$ Acid anhydride $\gt$ Ester $\gt$ Amide

questions.q: The correct order of increasing acidic strength is
options: phenol $ \lt $ ethanol $ \lt $ chloroacetic acid $ \lt $ acetic acid
ethanol $ \lt $ phenol $ \lt $ chloroacetic acid $ \lt $ acetic acid
ethanol $ \lt $ phenol $ \lt $ acetic acid $ \lt $ chloroacetic acid
chloroacetic acid $ \lt $ acetic acid $ \lt $ phenol $ \lt $ ethanol
questions.solution:
Due to - I effect of Cl , chloroacetic acid is a stronger acid than acetic acid. Further dur to stablisation of phenoxide ion by resonance and no such stabilisation in case of ethoxide ion, phenol is a stronger acid than ethanol.
Hence, overall acid strength increases in the order; ethanol $ \lt $ phenol $ \lt $ acetic acid $ \lt $ chloroacetic acid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following undergoes nucleophilic substitution exclusively by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism?
questions.solution:
Aliphatic $S_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction is carried out in two steps.
In form of slow step
Step (i) carbonium (carbocation) ion is formed and its formation is based upon the stability Stability order of carbocation
$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2\gt\mathrm{CH}_3-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CH}_3\gt\mathrm{CH}_3-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2$
and in step (ii) nucleophile is attracted towards the carbonium ion in form of fast step to give final product.
Hence, in benzyl chloride, ethyl chloride and isopropyl chloride order of $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction is benzyl chloride $\gt$ isopropyl chloride $\gt$ ethyl chloride
In chlorobenzene, mechanism of $S_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ reaction is differ to aliphatic alkyl halide. The aryl halides are much less reactive as compared to alkyl halides, towards nucleophilic reagents in either $S_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ or $S_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction.
The carbon-halogen bond in the aryl halide is quite strong and only forcing conditions can break up this bond.
Hence,
Step (i) $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Cl} \xrightarrow{\text { Slow }} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Rate $\propto\left[\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}\right]$
Step (ii) $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{Nu}^{-} \xrightarrow{\text { Fast }} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Nu}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has octet around central atom?
questions.solution:
Key Idea Electrons are transferred or shared such that each atom gains an electronic configuration of a noble gas $\left(\mathrm{ns}^2 \mathrm{np}^6\right.$ ) i.e. having 8 outer shell (valency) electrons. This arrangement is called octetrule. An outermost shell which had eight electrons is called octet.
Carbon tetrachloride has octet around central atom. The number of bonded electrons in given compounds are as follows
$\mathrm{PF}_5\left(10 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right), \mathrm{SF}_6\left(12 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right), \mathrm{CCL}_4\left(8 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right),-\mathrm{BF}_3\left(6 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct statement with regard to $\mathrm{H}_2^{+}$and $\mathrm{H}_2^{-}$is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2^{+}: \sigma 1 s^1, \dot{\sigma} 1 s^0$
Bond order $=\frac{1}{2}$
$\mathrm{H}_2^{-}: \sigma 1 s^2, \sigma 1 s^1$
Bond order $=\frac{2-1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$
The bond order of $\mathrm{H}_2^{+}$and $\mathrm{H}_2^{-}$are same but $\mathrm{H}_2^{+}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_2^{-}$. It is due to the presence of one electron in the antibonding molecular orbital in $\mathrm{H}_2^{-}$.

questions.solution: Key Idea Single bond $=1 \sigma$, Double bond $=1 \sigma+1 \pi$, Triple bond $=1 \sigma+2 \pi$ The structure of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_2$ is $\therefore$ Number of $\sigma$ bonds $=3$ Number of $\pi$ bonds $=2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the dot structure shown, the most likely elements $\mathrm{X}=\ldots$ and $\mathrm{Y}=\ldots$ are
questions.solution: X is tetravalent without lone pair. Y has three lone pair and one bond pair. Thus, X is carbon and Y is halogen or, $\mathrm{X}=$ carbon, $\mathrm{Y}=$ fluorine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following pair of compounds will have three $s p^2$-hybrid orbitals?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{SO}_2$ : trigonal planar : three $s p^2$-hybride orbitals
$\mathrm{CH}_4$ : tetrahedral : four $s p^3$-hybrid orbitals
$\mathrm{SO}_3$ : trigonal planar : three $s p^2$-hybrid orbitals
$\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_4$ : trigonal planar : three $s p^2$-hybrid orbitals
$\mathrm{BF}_3$ : trigonal planar : three $s p^2$-hybrid orbitals
$\mathrm{SF}_4$ : irregular tetrahedral : five $s p^3 d$-hybrid orbitals
$I_3^{-} L$ linear five $s p^3 d$-hybrid orbitals

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has the highest bond order ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{N}_2\left(7+7=14 e^{-}\right)= & K K^*, \sigma 2 s^2, \stackrel{\star}{\sigma} 2 s^2, \pi 2 p_x^2 \\ \approx & \pi 2 p_y^2, \sigma 2 p_z^2 \\ \mathrm{BO}= & \frac{1}{2}(8-2)=3 \\ \mathrm{O}_2\left(8+8=16 e^{-}\right)= & K K^*, \sigma 2 s^2, \sigma_\sigma^* 2 s^2, \sigma 2 p_z^2, \\ & \pi 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi 2 p_y^2, \stackrel{*}{\pi} 2 p_x^1 \approx \pi 2 p_y^1 \\ \mathrm{BO}= & \frac{1}{2}(8-4)=2 \\ \mathrm{He}_2\left(2+2=4 e^{-}\right)= & \sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^2 \\ \mathrm{BO}= & \frac{1}{2}(2-2)=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{H}_2\left(1+1=2 e^{-}\right) & =\sigma 1 s^2 \\
\text { BO } & =\frac{1}{2}(2-0)=1
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{N}_2$ has the highest bond order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The charge/size ratio of a cation determines its polarising power. Which one of the following sequences represents the increasing order of the polarising power of the cationic species, $\mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}, \mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ ?
questions.solution:
High charge and small size of the cations increases polarisation.
As the size of the given cations decreases as
$\mathrm{K}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}\gt\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}\gt\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$
Thus, polarising power increases as
$\mathrm{K}^{+} \lt \mathrm{Ca}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Be}^{2+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following carbonates decomposes readily at low temperatures ?
questions.solution:
Lithium carbonate decomposes readily at low temperature.
$\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H}$ bond angle in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $104.5^{\circ}$ and not $109^{\circ} 28^{\prime}$ because of
questions.solution: The hybridisation of oxygen in water molecule is $s p^3$. So, water molecule must be tetrahedral and have bond angle $109^{\circ} 28^{\prime}$. But due to the presence of two lone pair of electrons on oxygen atom (repulsion between lone pair-lone pair) the bond angle decreases about $104.5^{\circ}$ and the geometry becomes distorted tertrahedral or V-shaped.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The most stable compound is
questions.solution:
The electronegativity of halogen is as :
\(\mathrm{F} \gt \mathrm{Cl} \gt \mathrm{Br} \gt \mathrm{I}\)
Among alkali metals the electropositive character increases as :
\(\mathrm{Li}  \lt \mathrm{Na} \lt \mathrm{K} \lt \mathrm{Rb} \lt \mathrm{Cs}\)
Hence, LiF is the most stable compound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The hybridisation state of carbon in fullerene is
questions.solution: The hybridisation state of carbon in fullerene is \(s p^2\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The bond length of HCl bond is $2.29 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$.
The percentage ionic character of HCl , if measured dipole moment is $6.226 \times 10^{-30} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{m}$, is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mu_{\text {cal }}=e \times d=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.29 \times 10^{-10} \\ & =3.664 \times 10^{-29} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{m} \\ & \begin{aligned} \% \text { ionic character } & =\frac{\mu_{\text {exp }}}{\mu_{\text {cal }}} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{6.226 \times 10^{-30}}{3.664 \times 10^{-29}} \times 100 \\ & =17 \%\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hybridization of 1 and 2 carbon atom in $\stackrel{1}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2=\stackrel{2}{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{CH}_2$ are
questions.solution:
In this compound carbon atoms have following hybridization :
$\underset{s p^2}{\stackrel{1}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2}=\underset{s p}{\stackrel{2}{\mathrm{C}}}=\mathrm{CH}_2$
Carbon one contains one $\pi$ bond and carbon two contains two $\pi$ bonds and hence show $s p^2$ and $s p$ hybridization respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is paramagnetic?
questions.solution:
Oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ contains 2 unpaired electron, hence it is paramagnetic
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{O}_2=\sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^* 1 s^2 \sigma 2 s^2 \sigma^* 2 s^2 \sigma 2 p_x^2 \\& \pi 2 p_y^2 \pi 2 p_z^2 \pi^* 2 p_y^1 \pi^* 2 p_z^1\end{aligned}$

questions.q: Which of the following has $s p^2$-hybridisation?
