questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Lunar caustic is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ is also called lunar caustic as it leaves white scar on skin due to reduction to silver by skin.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following does not have coordinate bond?
questions.solution:
A. $\mathrm{SO}_2$ (Sulfur Dioxide)
1. Sulfur has 6 valence electrons and each oxygen has 6 valence electrons.
2. In $\mathrm{SO}_2$, sulfur forms two double bonds with two oxygen atoms.
3. One of the oxygen atoms can donate a lone pair to sulfur, forming a coordinate bond.
4. Therefore, $\mathrm{SO}_2$ has a coordinate bond.
B. $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ (Nitric Acid)
1. Nitrogen has 5 valence electrons, each oxygen has 6, and hydrogen has 1.
2. In $\mathrm{HNO}_3$, nitrogen forms one double bond with one oxygen and single bonds with the other oxygen and hydrogen.
3. The nitrogen can use a lone pair to form a coordinate bond with one of the oxygens.
4. Therefore, $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ has a coordinate bond.
C. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3$ (Sulfurous Acid)
1. Sulfur has 6 valence electrons, each oxygen has 6, and hydrogen has 1.
2. In $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3$, sulfur forms two single bonds with two hydroxyl groups \((\mathrm{OH})\) and one double bond with an oxygen atom.
3. The sulfur can donate a lone pair to form a coordinate bond with the remaining oxygen.
4. Therefore, $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3$ has a coordinate bond.
D. $\mathrm{HNO}_2$ (Nitrous Acid)
1. Nitrogen has 5 valence electrons, each oxygen has 6, and hydrogen has 1.
2. In HNO 2, nitrogen forms one double bond with one oxygen and a single bond with the hydroxyl group (OH).
3. The nitrogen does not have a lone pair available to donate to form a coordinate bond.
4. Therefore, $\mathrm{HNO}_2$ does not have a coordinate bond.
Conclusion
The compound that does not have a coordinate bond is $\mathrm{HNO}_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following forms molecular solid?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{SO}_2$, on cooling comes into solid form, which is due to weak van der Waal's forces. Such type of solids are called molecular solids. All other are covalent solids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the following equilibrium (omitting charges)
I. $M+\mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow M \mathrm{Cl}, K_{\mathrm{eq}}=\beta_1$
II. $M C l+\mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow M C l_2, K_{\text {eq }}=\beta_2$
III. $M \mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MCl}_3, K_{\text {eq }}=\beta_3$
IV. $M+3 \mathrm{Cl} \longrightarrow M \mathrm{Cl}_3, K_{\mathrm{eq}}=K$
then relationship between $K, \beta_1, \beta_2$ and $\beta_3$ is
questions.solution:
On adding I, II and III, we get IV
$\therefore \quad K=\beta_1 \beta_2 \beta_3$
or, $\log K=\log \beta_1+\log \beta_2+\log \beta_3$
Or $-\log K=-\log \beta_1-\log \beta_2-\log \beta_3$
$P_{\mathrm{K}}=P_{\beta_1}+P_{\beta_2}+P_{\beta_3}$ (where,$\left.p_x=-\log x\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The value of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$ for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}$ is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. At a given time, the composition of reaction mixture is $[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. In which direction, the reaction will proceed?
questions.solution:
For the reaction, the reaction quotient $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}$ is given by
$\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=[\mathrm{B}][\mathrm{C}] /[\mathrm{A}]^2$
Given that,
$[\mathrm{A}]=[\mathrm{B}]=[\mathrm{C}]=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$
Then, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)}{\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2}=1$
As, $\mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{C}}\gt\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{C}}$, so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At equilibrium, the rate of dissolution of a solid solute in a volatile liquid solvent is
options: less than the rate of crystallisation
greater than the rate of crystallisation
equal to the rate of crytallisation
questions.solution: At equilibrium, rate of dissolution $=$ rate of crystallisation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$, the equilibrium constant is $K_1$. The equilibrium constant is $K_2$ for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$. What is $K$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{NO}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_2(g)+\mathrm{O}_2(g)$ ?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}) ; \mathrm{K}_1\ldots(i)$
$2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) ; \mathrm{K}_2\ldots(ii)$
$\mathrm{N}_2(g)+2 \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g) ; K=K_1 \times K_2$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { For } \mathrm{NO}_2(g) & \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~N}_2(g)+\mathrm{O}_2(g) ; \\ K & =\left[\frac{1}{K_1 K_2}\right]^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the homogeneous reaction,
$\mathrm{NH}_3+\mathrm{OCl}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{H}_4+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
the equilibrium constant $K_c$ has the units
questions.solution:
For the given reaction, $\Delta n_g=n_p-n_r$ (where, $n_p=$ number of moles of gaseous products and $n_r=$ number of moles of gaseous reactants).
$\Delta n_g=(6+4)-(4+5)=+1$
Units of $K_c=$ (concentration) ${ }^{\Delta n_g}$
$=(\text { concentration })^{+1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { If } \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2^{+} ; K_1=1.7 \times 10^7 \\ & \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{AgCl} ; K_2=5.4 \times 10^9\end{aligned}$
Then, for
$\mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
equilibrium constant will be
questions.solution:
For the given reaction,
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2^{+} ; K_1=1.7 \times 10^7 \\ \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{AgCl} ; K_2=5.4 \times 10^9\end{aligned}$
Hence for
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AgCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\ & K_2^{\prime}=\frac{1}{K_2}=\frac{1}{5.4 \times 10^9}\end{aligned}$
Now for the reaction,
$\mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
$\begin{aligned} K=K_1 \times K_2^{\prime} & =1.7 \times 10^7 \times \frac{1}{5.4 \times 10^9} \\ & =0.31 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Both Mg and Fe metal can reduce copper from a solution having $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ion, according to equilibria.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Mg}_{(s)}+\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(s)} ; K_1=6 \times 10^{90} \\ & \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu}_{(s)} ; K_2=3 \times 10^{26}\end{aligned}$
Choose the correct option regarding above equilibrium
options: Both will equally remove $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ from solution
Both metals cannot remove $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ from solution
questions.solution: Since, $K_1 \gt K_2$, the product in the first reaction is much more favoured than in the second one. Mg thus removes more $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ from solution than Fe does.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In III group precipitation, $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ is added before adding $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ to
questions.solution:
In III group precipitation, $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ is added before adding $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ to decrease the conc. of$\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, as $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ dissociates as follows.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \rightleftharpoons \underset{\substack{\text { common } \\
\text { ion }}}{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}}
\end{aligned}$
Due to presence of common ion, conc. of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ decreases and for III group precipitation lesser conc. of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$is required.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$9.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is heated in a 1 L vessel till equilibrium state is established
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$
In equilibrium state $50 \% \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ was dissociated, equilibrium constant will be (mol. wt. of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4=92$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
Molar concentration of
$\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]=\frac{9.2}{92}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
In equilibrium state when it 50\% dissociates,
$\begin{aligned}
{\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right] } & =0.05 \mathrm{M} \\
{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right] } & =0.1 \mathrm{M} \\
K_c & =\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]} \\
& =\frac{0.1 \times 0.1}{0.05}=0.2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solubility product of \(\mathrm{PbCl}_2\) at 298 K is \(1 \times 10^{-6}\). At this temperature solubility of \(\mathrm{PbCl}_2\) in \(\mathrm{mol} / \mathrm{L}\) is
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{PbCl}_2 \underset{s}{\rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}}+\underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
K_{S P} & =(S)(2 S)^2 \\
1 \times 10^{-6} & =4 S^3 \\
S & =\left(\frac{1}{4} \times 10^{-6}\right)^{1 / 3} \\
& =\left(0.25 \times 10^{-6}\right)^{1 / 3}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equilibrium constants $K_1$ and $K_2$ for the following equilibria $\mathrm{NO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \stackrel{K_1}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$ and $2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g) \stackrel{K_2}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NO}(g)+\mathrm{O}_2(g)$ are related as
questions.solution:
(i) $\mathrm{NO}(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \stackrel{K_1}{\rightleftharpoons} \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$
So $K_1=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{[\mathrm{NO}]\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}}...(i)$
(ii) $2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g) \stackrel{K_2}{\rightleftharpoons} 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_2(g)$
So $K_2=\frac{[\mathrm{NO}]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}...(ii)$
From Eq. (i)
$K_1^2=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{NO]}^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]\right.}$
or $\frac{1}{K_1^2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}...(iii)$
From Eqs. (ii) and (iii)
$K_2=\frac{1}{K_1^2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $A+B \rightleftharpoons C+D$. If initially the concentration of $A$ and $B$ are both equal but at equilibrium, concentration of $D$ will be twice of that of $A$, then what will be the equilibrium constant of reaction?
questions.solution: $K_c=\frac{[C][D]}{[A][B]}=\frac{2 x \times 2 x}{x \times x}=4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{NO}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_2$, the value of $K_p$ is equal to $K$. What will the value of $K_p$ for reaction,
$\mathrm{NO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_2$
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{NO}_2 \quad 2 \mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_2$
$K_p=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NO}_2}^2 \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NO}_2}}=K \quad \ldots(i)$
For reaction, $\mathrm{NO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{NO}_2$
$K_p^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NO}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{NO}} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}^{1 / 2}} \quad \ldots(ii)$
From equation (i) and (ii)
$\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}}^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The dissociation equilibrium of a gas $A B_2$ can be represented as
$2 A B_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 A B(g)+B_2(g)$
The degree of dissociation is ' $x$ ' and is small compared to 1 . The expression relating the degree of dissociation ( $x$ ) with equilibrium constant $K_p$ and total pressure $p$ is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{array}{cccc} 
& 2 A B_2(g) & \rightleftharpoons & 2 A B(g) & + & B_2(g) \\
\text {Initial moles } & 1 & & 0 & & 0 \\
\text {At equilibrium } & 2(1-x) & & 2 x & & x
\end{array}\)
where, $\quad x=$ degree of dissociation
$\begin{aligned}\text { Total moles at equilibrium } & =2-2 x+2 x+x \\& =(2+x)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { So, } \quad \rho_{A B_2}= \frac{2(1-x) p}{(2+x)} \\ & \rho_{A B}=\frac{2 x p}{(2+x)}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} p_{B_2} & =\frac{x p}{(2+x)} \\ K_p & =\frac{\left(p_{A B}\right)^2\left(p_{B_2}\right)}{\left(p_{A B_2}\right)^2}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\left(\frac{2 x p}{2+x}\right)^2\left(\frac{x}{2+x} p\right)}{\left(\frac{2(1-x)}{(2+x)} p\right)^2}=\frac{x^3 p}{(2+x)(1-x)^2}$
$\begin{aligned} {[\because x \ll 1 \text { and } 2 \text {, so }} & (1-x)=1,(2+x)=2] \\ & =\frac{x^3 p}{2}\end{aligned}$
$x=\left(\frac{2 K_p}{p}\right)^{1 / 3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a gaseous reaction is given by the expression $k[A][B]$. If the volume of the reaction vessel is suddenly reduced to $\frac{1}{4}$ th of the initial volume, the reaction rate relating to original rate will be
questions.solution: Rate $=k[A][B]$; when volume is reduced to $\frac{1}{4}$ th, concentration will become 4 times: Therefore, new rate $=k[4 A][4 B]=16 k[A][B]$ $=16$ times

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A chemical reaction was carried out at 300 K and 280 K . The rate constant were found to be $\mathrm{k}_1$ and $\mathrm{k}_2$ respectively, then
questions.solution:
For every $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise, rate constant gets doubled. Hence, for every $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise, rate constant will be 4 times.
Therefore, according to the given statement,
Temperature $\quad 280 \mathrm{~K} \quad 300 \mathrm{~K}$
Rate constant $\quad \mathrm{k}_2 \quad \mathrm{k}_1\left(4 \mathrm{k}_2\right)$
Thus, $\quad \mathrm{k}_1=4 \mathrm{k}_2 \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}_2=0.25 \mathrm{k}_1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The value of $K_c$ for the reaction,
$2 A \rightleftharpoons B+C$ is $2 \times 10^{-3}$. At a given time, if the composition of reaction mixture is $[A]=[B]=[C]=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$. Which is true?
options: The reaction will proceed in forward direction
The reaction will proceed in backward direction
The reaction will proceed in any direction
questions.solution:
Reaction quotient, $Q_C=[B][C] /[A]^2$ as
as
$[A]=[B]=[C]=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$
$Q_C=\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right) /\left(3 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2=1$
As $Q_C \gt K_c$, so the reaction will proceed in the reverse direction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the equation for the equilibrium constant $\left(K_c\right)$ for the following reaction?
$\frac{1}{2} A(g)+\frac{1}{3} B(g) \stackrel{T(K)}{\rightleftharpoons} \frac{2}{3} C(g)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} A(g)+\frac{1}{3} B(g) \stackrel{T(K)}{\rightleftharpoons} \frac{2}{3} C(g) \\ & \text { Equilibrium constant, } K_c=\frac{\text { Rate product }}{\text { Rate of reactant }} \\ & \qquad K_c=\frac{[C]^{2 / 3}}{[A]^{1 / 2}[B]^{1 / 3}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Observe the following reaction
$2 A+B \longrightarrow C$
The rate of formation of $C$ is
$2.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-13} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. What is the value of $-\frac{d[A]}{d t}\left(\right.$ in $\left.\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \min ^{-1}\right) ?$
questions.solution:
$2 A+B \longrightarrow C$
Rate of reaction,
$=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d[A]}{d t}=-\frac{d[B]}{d t}=\frac{d[C]}{d t}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore & -\frac{d[A]}{d t}=2 \frac{d[C]}{d t} \\ & =2 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-3}=4.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Look at the graph Choose the correct equation from the following which best suited to the above graph
questions.solution: The equation is $\left[A_t\right]=\left[A_0\right]+K t$ On comparing it with general straight line equation, $Y=m x+c$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Slope }(m) & =+K \\ \text { intercept }(c) & =\left[A_0\right]\end{aligned}$ Therefore,the graph is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the following graph. The slope of line $A B$ gives the information of the
questions.solution:
$\because \operatorname{In} K=\ln A-\frac{E_a}{R T}$
$\operatorname{In} K=\operatorname{In} A-\frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$
Thus, $\log _{10} K=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303 R T}$ 
$\log K=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the chemical reaction, $2 \mathrm{O}_3 \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{O}_2$
The reaction proceed as follows
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{O}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{O}_2+\mathrm{O} \text { (fast) } \\ \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_2 \text { (slow) }\end{gathered}$
The rate law expression will be
questions.solution:
As slowest step is the rate determining step.
Hence, from slow reaction, $r=K[\mathrm{O}]\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]...(i)$
From fast reaction, $K_C=\frac{\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right][\mathrm{O}]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]}$
$\begin{aligned} {[\mathrm{O}] } & =\frac{K_C \cdot\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}, \text { i.e. } \\ r & =\frac{K_C\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]} \cdot\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow K_C \cdot\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]^{-1}$
$\therefore$ Rate $(r)=K^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]^2 \cdot\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]^{-1}$ $\left(K^{\prime}=K_C \cdot K\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If half-life of a substance is 36 minutes. Find amount left after 2 hrs . Initial amount is 10 gm ?
questions.solution:
Given,
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=36 \mathrm{~min}, \mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{hrs}=120 \mathrm{~min},[\mathrm{Ao}]=\log$
For first order reaction, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad k & =\frac{0.693}{36}=0.01925 \\
t & =\frac{2303}{k} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{A}_0\right]}{[\mathrm{A}]}
\end{aligned}$
where, $\left[\mathrm{A}_0\right]=$ initial amount
$\begin{aligned}
& {[\mathrm{A}]=\text { final amount } } \\
& 120=\frac{2303}{0.01925} \log \frac{10}{[\mathrm{~A}]} \\
& \log 10-\log [\mathrm{A}]=1 \\
& 1-\log [\mathrm{A}]=1 \\
& \log [\mathrm{A}]=0 \\
& {[\mathrm{~A}] }=1 \mathrm{gm}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the reversible reaction, $2 \mathrm{NO}_2 \underset{\mathrm{k}_2}{\stackrel{\mathrm{k}_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{NO}_2 \underset{\mathrm{k}_2}{\stackrel{\mathrm{k}_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$
Rate of reaction $=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}$
$=\mathrm{k}_1\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2-\mathrm{k}_2\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]$
$\therefore$ Rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{NO}_2$
i.e. $\frac{-\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{k}_1\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2-2 \mathrm{k}_2\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two similar reactions have the same rate constant at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, but at $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, one of the reaction has a higher rate constant than the other. The appropriate reason for this is
options: due to higher population of molecules
questions.solution: At room temperature i.e., $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, two similar reactions have the same rate constant. But when this temperature increases to $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the rate constant becomes 2 to 3 times greater than other. This is happened because at higher temperature, activation energies of reacting molecules are different so that collisions be effective. Therefore, at different activation energies rate constant also become higher than initial one.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a zero order reaction
questions.solution:
For a zero order reaction
rate, $-\frac{d[R]}{d t}=k$
On integrating, we get
$[R]=-k t+[C]$
When $t=0,[R]=\left[R_0\right]$
$\therefore \quad[C]=\left[R_0\right]$
$\therefore \quad[R]=-k t+\left[R_0\right]$
When, $t=t_{1 / 2},[R]=\frac{\left[R_0\right]}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\left[R_0\right]}{2}-\left[R_0\right]=-k t_{1 / 2}$
$\therefore \quad t_{1 / 2}=\frac{\left[R_0\right]}{2 k}$
$t_{1 / 2} \propto\left[R_0\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The rate equation for a reaction,
$A \longrightarrow B$
is $r=k[A]^0$. If the initial concentration of the reactant is $a \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{dm}{ }^{-3}$, the half-life period of the reaction is
questions.solution:
$A \longrightarrow B$
$\because \quad r=k[A]^0$ or $r=k$
This is a zero order reaction.
$\therefore \quad t_{1 / 2}=\frac{a}{2 k}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 2 g of a radioactive sample having half-life of 15 days was synthesised on 1st Jan 2009. The amount of the sample left behind on 1st March, 2009 (including both the days) is
questions.solution:
$N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$
Given, $\quad N_0=2 \mathrm{~g}$
$t_{1 / 2}=15$ days
$t=60$ days
$\Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{60}{15}=4$
$\therefore \quad N=2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4$
or $\quad N=0.125 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the first order reaction, $75 \%$ of the reactant gets disappeared in 1.386 hours. The rate constant of the reaction is
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction,
$k=\frac{2.303}{1.386 \times 60 \times 60} \log \frac{a}{a-0.75 a}$
$=\frac{2.303}{1.386 \times 60 \times 60} \log 4$
$=2.8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is \(X\) in the following nuclear reaction?
\({ }_7 \mathrm{~N}^{14}+{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^1 \longrightarrow{ }_8 \mathrm{O}^{15}+X\)
questions.solution: \({ }_7 \mathrm{~N}^{14}+{ }_1 \mathrm{H}^1 \longrightarrow{ }_8 \mathrm{O}^{15}+ \gamma\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order reaction $A \longrightarrow B$, the reaction rate at reactant concentration of 0.01 M is found to be $2.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The half-life period of the reaction is
questions.solution:
For first order reaction
$A \longrightarrow B$
Given that rate $=k \times[A]$
Rate $=2.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$[A]=$ Conc. of $A=0.01 \mathrm{M}$
So, $2.0 \times 10^{-5}=k \times 0.01$
$\begin{aligned} k & =\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.01} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & =2.0 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
For first order reaction
$\begin{aligned} T_{1 / 2} & =\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =346.5 \approx 347 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the order of the reaction?
questions.solution:
Order of a reaction is the sum of the power to which the concentration terms are raised in the rate law equation to express the observed rate of the reaction.
Hence, order $=\frac{1}{2}+2=\frac{5}{2}=2.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of reaction between two reactants $A$ and $B$ decreases by a factor of 4 , if the concentration of reactant $B$ is doubled. The order of this reaction with respect to reactant $B$ is
questions.solution:
$A+B \longrightarrow$ Product
Rate $\propto[A][B]^{-2}...(i)$
The rate decreases by factor 4 if the concentration of reactant ' $B$ ' is doubled.
So, Rate $\propto[A][2 B]^{-2}$
$\propto \frac{[A][B]^{-2}}{4}...(ii)$
Hence, order of reaction wrt reactant $B$ is -2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate constant of a reaction depends on
options: the initial conc. of the reactant
questions.solution: The rate constant of a reaction depends upon the temperature of the reaction, i.e., rate constant ( k$)$ is constant for a particular reaction at fixed temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction, the half-life is independent of initial concentration. What is the order of that reaction?
questions.solution:
For an $n^{\text {th }}$ order reaction,
$t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{C^{n-1}} \quad(C=\text { initial conc. })$
Hence, for Ist order reaction $(n=1)$, we have
$t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{C^{1-1}}$
$\therefore \quad t_{1 / 2} \propto C^0$
Thus half life of Ist order reaction is independent of the initial concentration of reactant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Formula for half- life of a zero order reaction is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A first order reaction is $50 \%$ completed in $1.26 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~s}$. How much time would it takes for 100\% completion?
questions.solution:
$k$-for first order. $\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{1.26 \times 10^{14}}$ for $100 \%$ completion.
$\because t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x}$
$t=\frac{2.303 \times 1.26 \times 10^{14}}{0.693} \log \frac{100}{0}$
$\therefore t=\infty$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A hypothetical reaction, $X_2+Y_2 \longrightarrow 2 X Y$ follows the following mechanism
$X_2 \rightleftharpoons X+X \ldots . . . . . .$. fast
$X+Y_2 \longrightarrow X Y+Y \ldots \ldots$ slow
$X+Y \longrightarrow X Y$.......... fast
The order of the overall reaction is
questions.solution:
Rate $=k[X]\left[Y_2\right]$
But, $k=\frac{[X]^2}{\left[X_2\right]}$ or, $[X]=\sqrt{k \cdot\left[X_2\right]}$
Rate $=k \cdot \sqrt{k} \sqrt{\left[X_2\right]}\left[Y_2\right]=k^{\prime}\left[X_2\right]^{1 / 2}\left[Y_2\right]$
Thus, order, $=1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The variation of concentration of the product $P$ with time in the reaction, $A \rightarrow P$ is shown in following graph. The graph between $\frac{-d[A]}{d t}$ and time will be of the type
questions.solution: Concentration of the product increases linearly with time $t$, thus order $=0$ and $-\frac{d[A]}{d t}=$ constant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At radioactive equilibrium, the ratio between two atoms of radioactive elements $A$ and $B$ is $3.1 \times 10^9: 1$. If the half-life period of $A$ is $2 \times 10^{10}$ yrs, then the half-life of $B$ is
questions.solution:
At radioactive equilibrium,
$A \longrightarrow B$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{N_A}{N_B} & =\frac{K_B}{K_A}=\frac{t_{1 / 2 A}}{t_{1 / 2 B}} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{3.1 \times 10^9}{1} & =\frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{t_{1 / 2 B}} \text { and, } t_{1 / 2 B}=6.45 \mathrm{yrs} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $50 \%$ of a radioactive substance dissociates in 15 min , then the time taken by substance to dissociate $99 \%$ will be
questions.solution:
Radioactive disintegration is a Ist order reaction, hence
$\begin{aligned}
k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x} \\
\therefore \quad k & =\frac{2.303}{15} \log \frac{100}{100-50} \\
\text { also } \quad k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{100-99} \\
\therefore \frac{2.303}{15} \log \frac{100}{50} & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{100}{1} \\
\frac{1}{15} \log 2 & =\frac{1}{t} \log 100 \\
\frac{0.3010}{15} & =\frac{2}{t} \\
t & =\frac{2 \times 15}{0.3010}=99 \mathrm{~min}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half life of a radioactive element is 40 days. Calculate the average life
questions.solution:
We know
Average life $(T)=1.44 \times$ half life $\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)$
Hence $T=1.44 \times 40$
or $\quad T=57.6$ days
So, average life of that radioactive element is 57.6 days.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life of a first order reaction having rate constant $k=1.7 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ is :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & k=1.7 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693}{1.7 \times 10^{-5}}=11.3 \mathrm{~h}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide is a popular
questions.solution: Cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide which is a germicide, is a popular cationic detergent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Aspirin is :
questions.solution: Aspirin is used as antipyretic as well as analgesic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Saccharin an artificial sweetener is manufactured form
questions.solution: Saccharin is a low calorie sweetener manufactured from toluene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following enhances lathering property of soap?
questions.solution: Sodium rosinate enhance the lathering porperty of soap.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Lauryl alcohol on treatment with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ followed by neutralisation forms a product which is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is employed as antihistamine?
questions.solution: Diphenyl hydramine (benadryl) is used as an antihistamine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one among the following is added to soap to impart antiseptic property?
questions.solution: Bithional is an antiseptic. It is mixed with soap to impart antiseptic property.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which is used as medicine?
questions.solution: Urotropine is a medicine, which is used for the treatment of Athritis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which type of drugs come under anti-microbial drugs?
questions.solution: Bacteria, virus, fungi and other pathogens are microorganisms. These may cause diseases in human beings and animals. An antimicrobial drug prevents the development or inhibit the pathogenic action of microorganisms such as bacteria (antibacterial drug), virus (antiviral drugs), fungi (antifungal drugs) or other parasite (antiparasite drugs selectively). Antibiotics, antiseptics and disinfectants are antimicrobial drugs.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is present as an active ingredient in bleaching powder for bleaching action?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OCl})_2$, calcium hypochlorite is the active ingredient in bleaching powder which releases chlorine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Sodium sulphite is used in preserving squashes and other mildly acidic foods due to
options: potassium metabisulphite is not influcenced by acid
sulphur dioxide and sulphurous acid formed kill bacteria and germs
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3+\mathrm{SO}_2 \\ & \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{SO}_2$ and sulphurous acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3\right)$ kill the bacteria and prevent the decay of food.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The dispersed phase and dispersion medium in soap lather are respectively
questions.solution: Soap lather is an example of colloidal system foam, in which the dispersed phase is gas and dispersion medium is liquid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is known as invert soap?
questions.solution: Trimethyl stearyl ammonium bromide has a chiral nitrogen atom, thus is known as invert soap.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Valence electrons in the element X are 3 and that in element Y is 6. Most probable compound formed from X and Y is
questions.solution:
Valence electrons in $\mathrm{X}=3$
Valence electrons in $\mathrm{Y}=6$
Thus, X is electropositive and Y is electronegative, X can lose three electrons and Y can gain two electrons to attain stable configuration.
Hence, X exists as $\mathrm{X}^{3+}$ and Y as $\mathrm{Y}^{2-}$ and compound is $X_2 Y_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following relation is correct ?
options: $\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }}$ IE of C $\gt\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }}$ IE of B
questions.solution: The minimum amount of energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from an isolated gaseous atom so as to convert it into a gaseous cation, is called its ionisation energy. Because the size of $C$ is smaller than that of the $B$ due to effective nuclear charge, hence $\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }} \mathrm{IE}$ of $\mathrm{C}\gt\mathrm{I}^{\text {st }}$ IE of B

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The most basic element is
questions.solution:
The basic nature of elements increases from top to bottom in a group. Therefore, iodine is the most basic element.
$\mathrm{F}_2 \lt \mathrm{Cl}_2 \lt \mathrm{Br}_2 \lt \mathrm{I}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The set representing the correct order for first ionisation potential is
questions.solution:
The IP decreases in a group on moving downward because atomic radius increases hence the correct order is :
\(\mathrm{Be} \gt \mathrm{Mg} \gt \mathrm{Ca}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The magnitude of screening effect depends upon the number of
questions.solution: The magnitude of screening effect depends upon the number of inner electrons, i.e. higher the number of inner electrons, greater shall be the value of screening effect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following oxide is most acidic?
questions.solution:
More be the positive oxidation state of a non-metallic oxide, more be its acidic nature.
$\therefore \mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ is most acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: H -bond is strongest in
questions.solution: All compound which have hydrogen atom attached to $\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{O}$ or N atom (i.e. highly electronegative atoms) exhibit H bonding. In the question HF exhibit strongest H bonding because F is most electronegative element.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following order of ionisation energy is correct?
questions.solution: Ionisation energy generally increase in a period from left to right. But due to comparatively stabler configuration of Be $\left(1 s^2, 2 s^2\right)$, it has more ionisation energy than Boron $\left(1 s^2, 2 s^2, 2 p^1\right)$. Hence, the correct order is : $\mathrm{B} \lt \mathrm{Be} \lt \mathrm{C} \lt \mathrm{N}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The enthalpies of all elements in their standard states are
questions.solution: The enthalphy of elements are zero in their standard state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following pair behaves as strong and weak base hydride repectively $\mathrm{NH}_3, \mathrm{PH}_3, \mathrm{AsH}_3, \mathrm{SbH}_3, \mathrm{BiH}_3$
questions.solution:
The basic strength of given hydrides decreases in the order as follows
$\mathrm{NH}_3\gt\mathrm{PH}_3\gt\mathrm{AsH}_3\gt\mathrm{SbH}_3\gt\mathrm{BiH}_3$
This is due to the decrease in the availability of the lone pair of electrons which depends on the size of central atom that increase from N to Bi. Therefore, $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{BiH}_3$ acts as a strong and weak base hydride respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: In long form of Periodic Table, the properties of the elements are a periodic function of their
questions.solution: In modern Periodic Table, the physical and chemical properties of the elements are a periodic function of their atomic number, ie, if the elements are arranged in order of their increasing atomic number, the elements with similar properties are repeated after certain regular intervals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Highest energy will be absorbed to eject out the electron in the configuration.
questions.solution: The electron will move from a lower stationary state to a higher stationary state when required amount of energy is absorbed by the electron or energy is emitted when electron moves from higher stationary state to lower stationary state. The energy does not take place in a continuous manner.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following is a wrong statement? In a given period of the Periodic Table, the $s$-block element has, in general, a higher value of
questions.solution: Electronegativity, ionisation energy and electron affinity of $s$-block elements in their respective period is low but the atomic radius of the $s$-block elements is larger in their respective period due to less effective nuclear charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: What is the correct relationship between the pHs of isomolar solutions of sodium oxide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_1\right)$, sodium sulphide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_2\right)$, sodium selenide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_3\right)$ and sodium telluride $\left(\mathrm{pH}_4\right)$ ?
questions.solution:
The correct order of pH of isomolar solution in sodium oxide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_1\right)$, sodium sulphide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_2\right)$, sodium selenide $\left(\mathrm{pH}_3\right)$ and sodium telluride $\left(\mathrm{pH}_4\right)$ is $\mathrm{pH}_1\gt\mathrm{pH}_2\gt\mathrm{pH}_3\gt\mathrm{pH}_4$ because in aqueous solution, they are hydrolysed as follows.
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Base }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}}+{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}{ }$
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Strong base }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}+}  \underset{\text { Weak acid }}{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}}$
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Se}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Strong base }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}+}  \begin{gathered}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se} \\ \text { Weak acid }\end{gathered}$
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Te}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Strong base }}{2 \mathrm{NaOH}+}  \begin{gathered}\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te} \\ \text { Weak acid }\end{gathered}$
Order of acidic strength
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Order of neutralisation of NaOH
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}\gt\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Hence, their aqueous solutions have the following order of basic character due to neutralisation of NaOH with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} . \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}$
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}\gt\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}\gt\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Se}\gt\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Te}$
$(\therefore \mathrm{pH}$ of basic solution is higher than acidic or least basic solution.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following have highest electron affinity ?
questions.solution: Electron affinity of halogens is maximum in a period and it decreases in a group from top to bottom but the EA of chlorine is greater than fluorine due to smaller atomic size of fluorine.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In $\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$, there are three unpaired electrons present. The $\mu_{\text {calculated }}$ is 3.87 BM which is quite different from the $\mu_{\text {experimental }}$ of 4.40 BM .
This is because of
options: increase in number of unpaired electrons
some contribution of the orbital motion of the electron to the magnetic moment
change in orbital spin of the electron
questions.solution: In $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$, there are three unpaired electrons present as, $\mu_{\mathrm{cal}}=\sqrt{n(n+1)} \mathrm{BM}=\sqrt{3(3+1)} \mathrm{BM}=3.87 \mathrm{BM}$ As, the $t_{2 g}$ orbital is unsymmetrically filled hence due to orbital motion contribution, magnetic moment goes with the value 4.40 BM

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The magnetic moment of $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ ion (atomic number of $\mathrm{Ni}=28$ ) in BM unit is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Ni}_{28}=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 4 \mathrm{~s}^2, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 4 \mathrm{~s}^2$ Number of unpaired electrons $=2$ Magnetic moment, $\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)}=\sqrt{2(2+2)}=\sqrt{8}=2.84 \mathrm{BM}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one follow 18 electron octet rule?
questions.solution:
The 18 electron rule is used for predicting the stability and rationalizing the formula, especially organomettalic complexes.
The number of valence electrons of the given species are as follows:
$\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]-\mathrm{Mn}\left\{[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^5 4 \mathrm{~S}^2\right\}=7 \text { valence }$
electrons
$\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]-\operatorname{Cr}\left\{[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^4 4 \mathrm{~S}^2\right\}=6 \text { valence }$
electrons
$\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]-\mathrm{Fe}\left\{[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^6 4 \mathrm{~S}^2\right\}=8 \text { valence }$
electrons
$\therefore\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]$ follow 18 electron octet rule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not expected to show paramagnetism?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ due to absence of unpaired electrons does not show paramagnetic.
$\mathrm{Ni} \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 d^8 4 s^2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In acidic medium, dichromatic ion oxidises ferrous ion to 'ferric ion. If the gram molecular weight of potassium dichromate is 294 g, its gram equivalent weight (in grams) is
questions.solution: In acidic medium, $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ acts as strong oxidising agent and itself gets reduced to $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$. Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ $=\frac{\text { Molecular weight }}{6}=\frac{294}{6}=49$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following species is paramagnetic in nature?
questions.solution:
The number of unpaired electrons and electronic configuration of the given species is shown below
- $\mathrm{V}^{4+}[\mathrm{Z}=23]=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^1$
No. of unpaired electron $=1$
- $\mathrm{Sc}^{3+}[\mathrm{Z}=2 \mathrm{l}]=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^0$
No. of unpaired electron $=0$
- $\mathrm{V}^{5+}[\mathrm{Z}=23]=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^0$
No. of unpaired electron $=0$
- $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}[\mathrm{Z}=30]=[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10}$
Number of unpaired electron $=0$
$\therefore$ The correct option among the following is (4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\left[\mathrm{CuCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ exists while $\left[\mathrm{Cul}_4\right]^{2-}$ does not exist, because
questions.solution:
AsI- is stronger reducing agent, i.e., reductant than $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ and reduces $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ into Cul and itself is oxidised into $\mathrm{I}_2$. $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$is not that much strong reducing agent and does not reduce $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$.
Therefore, $\left[\mathrm{CuCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ exists but $\left[\mathrm{CuCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ does not.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 0.001 mole of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)\right]$ was passed through a cation exchanger and the acid coming out of it required 20 mL of 0.1 M NaOH for neutralisation. Thus, the complex is
questions.solution:
Millimoles of acid required for neutralisation
$\begin{aligned} & =20 \times 0.1=2 \text { millimoles } \\ & =0.002 \text { moles }\end{aligned}$
If 0.001 mole is neutralising 0.002 moles of base, thus, 0.001 mole of base remains unreacted. Hence, acid coming out should be basic.
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)\right] \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right]^{2+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}\right.}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+},\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$, $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{FeCl}_6\right]^{3-}$, more stability is shown by
questions.solution: More basic and chelate forming ligand stabilises the complex to more extent $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{2-}$ is a bidentate chelating ligand thus stabilises the complex $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right)_3\right]^{3-}$ to a high extent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is added to ice cold solution of acidified potassium dichromate in ether and the contents are shaken and allowed to stand
options: a blue colour is obtained in ether because of formation of $\mathrm{CrO}_5$
a blue colour is obtained in ether because of formation of $\mathrm{CrO}_3$
a blue colour is obtained in ether because of formation of $\mathrm{Cr}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3$
questions.solution:
When $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is added to acidified potassium dichromate in ether solution, potassium dichromate is oxidised to blue peroxide of chromium $\left(\mathrm{CrO}_5\right)$ which is soluble in ether and produces blue coloured solution.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \\ & 2 \mathrm{CrO}_5+\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding the reaction?
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{\oplus}+\mathrm{OH} \\ & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+\mathrm{Ag} \\ & \end{aligned}$
options: Three moles of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$are required in the above reaction
questions.solution:
Oxidation:
$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+3 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ ...(i)
Reduction:
$\left\{\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{\ominus} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}+2 \mathrm{NH}_3\right\} \times 2$ ...(ii)
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{\oplus}+3 \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{Ag}+4 \mathrm{NH}_3 \\ & \end{aligned}$
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}=44$
Equivalent weight $=\frac{M W}{n \text {-factor }}=\frac{44}{2}=22$
Therefore, acetaldehyde is not an oxidising agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acidified $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ solution turns green when $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3$ is added to it. This is due to the formation of
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+3 \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \\ & 3 \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{Cr}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A chelating agent has two or more than two donor atoms to bind a single metal ion. Which of the following is not a chelating agent?
questions.solution: Thiosulphato (SCN) is ambidentate/unidentats ligand.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many ions obtain after dissociation of this complex $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3$ ?
questions.solution:
Primary valency of coordination compounds are ionisable. Thus, dissociation of the complex, $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3$ takes place as follows $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3 \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Therefore, total number of ions obtain will be 4 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following transition metal ions is diamagnetic?
questions.solution:
Transition metal ion having absence of unpaired electron is diamagnetic.
(1) $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^7$ (three unpaired electrons)
(2) $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8$ (two unpaired electrons)
(3) $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^9$ (one unpaired electron)
(4) $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{10}$ (no unpaired electron)
Thus, $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ is diamagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ are the examples of which type of isomerism?
questions.solution: The complexes $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ are the examples of coordination isomerisms. This isomerism occurs only in those complexes in which both cation and anion are complex. It occurs due to exchange of ligands between cation and anion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Magnetic moment of $\left[\mathrm{Ti}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{4+}$, $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ can be represented as $X, Y$ and $Z$.
They are in order of
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{X}=[\mathrm{Ti} & \left.\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{4+}, \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{C} \text { of } \mathrm{Ti}^{4+}=3 d^{\circ} \\ n & =0 \\ \mu_B & =0 \\ Y & =\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}, \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{C} \text { of } \mathrm{Mn}^{+2}=3 d^5 \\ n & =3 \\ \mu_B & =3.87 \mathrm{BM} \\ \mathrm{Z} & =\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}, \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{C} \text { of } \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}=3 d^3 \\ n & =3 \\ \mu_B & =3.87 \mathrm{BM}\end{aligned}$
Hence, the correct order of magnetic moment of $X, Y, Z$ is
$X \lt Z \lt Y$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following coordination compounds would exhibit optical isomerism?
questions.solution: Tris-(ethylene diamine) cobalt (III) bromide, i.e. $\left[\mathrm{Co}(e n)_3\right] \mathrm{Br}_3$ exhibits optical isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the complex, $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$, the oxidation number of Cr is
questions.solution:
Let the oxidation number of Cr is x in the complex $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4 \mathrm{Cl}_2\right]^{+}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{X}+4 \times(0)+2 \times(-1) & =+1 \\ x-2 & =+1 \\ x & =+3\end{aligned}$
Thus, the oxidation number of Cr is +3 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following complexes is outer orbital complex?
questions.solution: Generally weaker field ligands form outer orbital complex. Between $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}$, the later form outer orbital complex because of its $d^8$ configuration. (The configuration of $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}=d^6$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The complex ion which has no $d$-electrons in the central metal atom is
questions.solution:
In $\left[\mathrm{MnO}_4\right]^{-}$, the oxidation state of Mn is +7 .
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{Mn}(25) & =[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^2 \\
\mathrm{Mn}^{7+} & =[\mathrm{Ar}]
\end{aligned}$
Thus, in $\left[\mathrm{MnO}_4\right]^{-}$no $d$-electron is present.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Oxidation number Fe in $\left.\mathrm{K}_3[\mathrm{Fe(CN)}_6\right]$ is
questions.solution:
Let, oxidation number of Fe be ' $x$ '
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad 3(+1)+x+6(-1) & =0 \\ 3+x-6 & =0 \\ x & =+3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Considering $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ as weak field ligand, the number of unpaired electrons in $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ will be (Atomic number of $\mathrm{Mn}=25$ )
questions.solution: Mn in $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ is present as $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\left(3 d^5\right)$. $s p^3 d^2$ hybridisation occurs in $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$. Due to the presence of weak field ligand pairing of electron does not take place, hence the number of unpaired electrons is five.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which does not give a precipitate with \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\) solution?
questions.solution: \(\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{Cl}_3\right]\) does not give a precipitate with \(\mathrm{AgNO}_3\) solution because in it all the chlorine atoms are attached by secondary valencies.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The geometry of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ and $\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ are :
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ and $\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{PPh}_3\right)_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ are tetrahedral in geometrical shape, because co-ordination number of Ni is 4 in both cases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is wrong statement?
options: $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ has zero oxidation number for Ni
questions.solution:
In $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$, carbonyl group is a neutral species. So oxidation number of Ni is zero.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4 & \\ x+4 \times 0 & =0 \\ x & =0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following species, the one which become paramagnetic on heating at 850 K is
questions.solution:
All magnetically ordered solids, i.e. ferromagnetic; antiferromagnetic; ferrimagnetic changes to paramagnetic state at high temperature because of the randomisation of spins.
Ferrimagnetic species, such as $\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4$ (magnetite) and ferrites, i.e. $\mathrm{MgFe}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ and $\mathrm{ZnFe}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ loses ferrimagnetism on heating and transforms to paramagnetism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is ' $d$ ' -block element?
questions.solution:
The element in which last electron enters in $d$-orbital is called $d$-block element. The electronic configuration of the given elements are as follows
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Gd}(64) & =[\mathrm{Xe}] 6 s^2, 4 f^7, 5 d^7 \\ \mathrm{Hs}(108) & =[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 s^2, 5 f^{14}, 6 d^6\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \operatorname{Es}(99)=[\mathrm{Rn}] 7 s^2, 5 f^{11} \\ & \operatorname{Cs}(55)=[\mathrm{Xe}] 6 s^1\end{aligned}$
Thus, Hs is a d-block element.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $M$ is element of actinoids series, the degree of complex formation decreases in the order
questions.solution:
The higher the charge on the metal ion, smaller is the ionic size and more is the complex forming ability. Thus, the degree of complex formation decreases in the order
$\mathrm{M}^{4+} \gt \mathrm{MO}_2^{2+} \gt \mathrm{M}^{3+} \gt \mathrm{MO}_2^{+}$
The higher tendency of complex formation of $\mathrm{MO}_2^{2+}$ as compared to $\mathrm{M}^{3+}$ is due to high concentration of charge on metal atom M.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On addition of small amount of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ to conc. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$, a green oily compound is obtained which is highly explosive in nature. Identify the compound from the following.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{KMnO}_4+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \text { (conc.) } & \longrightarrow \\ \mathrm{Mn}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 & +2 \mathrm{KHSO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct increasing order of ionic radii of the following $\mathrm{Ce}^{3+}, \mathrm{La}^{3+}, \mathrm{Pm}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Yb}^{3+}$ is
questions.solution: Due to lanthanide contraction, size of +3 ions decreases from $\mathrm{La}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Lu}^{3+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The value of n in
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+n e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Actinides exhibit larger number of oxidation state than that of corresponding lanthanides. The reason behind this aspect is
options: lesser energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$-orbitals than between $4 f$ and $5 d$-orbitals
larger atomic size of actinides than the lanthanides
more energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$ orbitals than between $4 f$ and $5 d$-orbitals
greater reactive nature of the actinides than the lanthanides
questions.solution: Because of lesser energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$ orbitals than that between $4 f$ and $5 d$-orbitals actinides exhibit larger number of oxidation state than that of corresponding lanthanides.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $E^{\circ}$ values for Mn and Zn are more negative than expected because
options: they have either half-filled and fully-filled configurations
it is Quite easy to remove electrons from their orbitals
questions.solution:
The values of standard electrode potentials for Mn, and Zn are more negative than expected. The reason behind this is that they have exactly half-filled and fully-filled configuration ons.
$\begin{aligned} & { }_{25} \mathrm{Mn}: \left[\mathrm{Ar}\right] 3 d^5 4 s^2 \\ & { }_{30} \mathrm{Zn}:[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{10} 4 s^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has the maximum number of unpaired electrons?
questions.solution: The outer electronic configuration of the given ions is as

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The maximum oxidation state exhibited by actinide ions is
questions.solution: Actinoids exhibit variable oxidation states, which vary from +3 to +7 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The aqueous solution containing which one of the following ions will be colourless ?
(Atomic no. $\mathrm{Sc}=21, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Ti}=22, \mathrm{Mn}=25$ )
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & { }_{21} \mathrm{Sc}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^1, 4 s^2 \\ & \text { So, } S c^{3+}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6\end{aligned}$ (It is colourless due to absence of unpaired electrons in $d$-stubshell) $\begin{aligned} & { }_{26} \mathrm{Fe}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^6, 4 s^2 \\ & \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^6\end{aligned}$ (It is coloured due to presence of four unpaired electrons in $d$-subshell.) $\begin{gathered}{ }_{22} \mathrm{Ti}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^2, 4 s^2 \\\mathrm{Ti}^{3+}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2, 3 s^6 3 d^1\end{gathered}$ (It is coloured due to presence of one unpaired electron in $d$-subshell). $\begin{aligned} & 25 \mathrm{Mn}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^5, 4 s^2 \\ & \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^5\end{aligned}$ (It is coloured due to presence of five unpaired electrons in $d$-subshell)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(\mathrm{BaO}_2\) and ozone reacts to produce
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{BaO}_2\) and ozone reacts to produce BaO and oxygen.
\(\mathrm{BaO}_2+\mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaO}+2 \mathrm{O}_2\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The main reason for larger number of oxidation states exhibited by the actinides than the corresponding lanthanides, is
options: lesser energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$ orbitals than between $4 f$ and $5 d$ orbitals
larger atomic size of actinides than the lanthanide
more energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$ orbitals than between $4 f$ and $5 d$ orbitals
greater reactive nature of the actinides than the lanthanides
questions.solution: The main reason for larger number of oxidation states exhibited by actinides than corresponding lanthanides is lesser energy difference between $5 f$ and $6 d$ orbitals than between $4 f$ and 5d-orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following have maximum number of unpaired electrons?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} \longrightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^0$
Number of unpaired electrons $=5$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ in acidic medium reacts to have change in oxidation number of Mn from
questions.solution:
In this reaction change in oxidation number is from +7 to +2 .
$\underset{+7}{\mathrm{KMnO}_4} \longrightarrow \underset{+2}{\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following turns potassium dichromate paper green?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{SO}_2$ gas is a reducing agent, hence turns dichromate paper green due to formation of chromium sulphate. Sulphite radical $\left(\mathrm{SO}_3^{2-}\right)$ is identified by this:
$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+3 \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \underset{\text { green }}{\mathrm{Cr}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_2} +\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The incorrect configuration is
questions.solution: The correct configurations of $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{Cu}$ and Cr are $[\mathrm{Ar}] 4 s^1,[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{10} 4 s^1,[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^1$ respectively. As half filled and completely filled d orbitals are more stable, hence in case of Cr and Cu one electron shifts from $4 s$ orbital to $3 d$ orbital to give them stable electronic configuration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compound is not coloured?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cd} . \mathrm{Cl}_4$ has no unpaired electron due to presence of $d^{10}$ electrons. Hence, $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ is a colourless compound.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The magnetic nature of elements depends on the presence of unpaired electrons. Identify the configuration of transition element, which shows highest magmetic moment.
questions.solution:
$3 d^5$ has maximum unpaired electrons $\left(5 e^{-}\right)$,
so it has magnetic moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Name the type of the structure of silicate in which one oxygen atom of $\left[\mathrm{SiO}_4\right]^{4-}$ is shared?
questions.solution: In pyrosilicate, only one oxygen atom is shared.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$, the number of unpaired electrons, are
questions.solution:
$\operatorname{In}\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ the oxidation number of Ni is $+2$
Electronic configuration of Ni
$\begin{aligned} &\text { (at no. }=28) \\ & \mathrm{Ni}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8 4 s^2 \\ & \mathrm{Ni}^{+2}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8\end{aligned}$
or $\quad[\mathrm{Ar}]$
$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline\uparrow \downarrow & \uparrow \downarrow & \uparrow \downarrow & \uparrow & \uparrow \\
\hline
\end{array}$
It is evident that in $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ the number of unpaired electrons are two.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $\mathrm{I}^{-}$is oxidised by $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$in alkaline medium, $\mathrm{I}^{-}$converts into
questions.solution:
When $\mathrm{I}^{-}$is oxidised by $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$in alkaline medium, $\mathrm{I}^{-}$converts into $\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}$. The reaction occurs as follows.
$\begin{gathered}2 \mathrm{KMnO}_4+2 \mathrm{KOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+[\mathrm{O}] \\ \xrightarrow[2]{\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MnO}_2+4 \mathrm{KOH}+2[\mathrm{O}]} \\ 2 \mathrm{KMO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { Alkaline }} 2 \mathrm{MnO}_2+2 \mathrm{KOH}+3[O]\end{gathered}$
$\mathrm{KI}+3[\mathrm{O}] \longrightarrow \mathrm{KIO}_3$
i.e. $\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}$-ion

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
. Given, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}}^0=-0.72 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}=-0.42 \mathrm{~V}$.
The potential for the cell $\mathrm{Cr} \mid \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \| \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M}) \mathrm{Fe}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0 & =\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }} \\
& =-0.42-(-0.72)=0.3
\end{aligned}$
At anode $\left[\mathrm{Cr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-}\right] \times 2$
At cathode $\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}\right] \times 3$
$2 \mathrm{Cr}+3 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{Fe}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^0-\frac{0.0591}{6} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right]^3} \\ & =0.3-\frac{0.0591}{6} \log \frac{(0.1)^2}{(0.01)^3} \\ & =0.26 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.solution: For the electrochemical cell, $\mathrm{Ag}^{-}|\mathrm{AgCl}| \mathrm{KCl} \|\left|\mathrm{AgNO}_3\right| \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ The cell reaction is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The product of electrolysis of aqueous NaCl solution on anode and cathode respectively is
questions.solution:
In case of electrolysis of aqueous NaCl , oxidation and reduction reaction occurs at anode and cathode respectively as shown below:
At cathode
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})$
At anode
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{e}^{-}$
Net cell reaction is as follows
$\mathrm{NaCl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})$
$+\mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
Thus, the product of electrolysis of NaCl solution on cathode and anode is $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the correct statement from the following.
options: The greater positive value of $E_{\mathrm{OP}}^{\circ}$ indicates greater reactivity of metal
$\mathrm{F}^{-}$is strong oxidant whileCu2+ ${ }^{2+}$ is weak reductant
The metals placed above Mg in the electrochemical series do not decompose water at ordinary temperature
Oxides of Hg do not decompose on heating
questions.solution:
According to the applications of electrochemical series on moving from top to bottom in electrochemical series, the values of $E_{\mathrm{oP}}^{\circ}$ of metals decrease, and thus reactivity of metals of $E_{\mathrm{op}}^{\circ}$ indicates greater reactivity of metal. On the other hand, $\mathrm{F}^{-}$is poor reductant while $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ is strong oxidant.
The metals placed above Mg in ECS decompose water at ordinary temperature to liberate $\mathrm{H}_2$. Oxides of Hg decomposes on heating.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If molar conductivity of $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions are 119 and $71 \mathrm{Sm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, then the molar conductivity of $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ at infinite dilution is
questions.solution:
Given,
$\begin{array}{r}\Lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}=119 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \Lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Cl}^{-}=71 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{array}$
As we know that,
$\begin{array}{ll} & \Lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CaCl}_2=\Lambda_{\mathrm{Ca}^2+}^{\circ}+2 \times \Lambda^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}} \\ \therefore \quad & \Lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CaCl}_2=119+2 \times 71 \\ & \Lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{CaCl}_2=261 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The standard reduction potential for $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+} / \mathrm{Ni}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}$ are -0.76 , -0.23 and -0.44 V , respectively.
The reaction $X+Y^{2+} \longrightarrow X^{2+}+Y$ will be spontaneous when
questions.solution: The reaction is spontaneous when $\Delta G \lt 0$ and $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}\gt0 .$ $\begin{aligned} & \because \Delta G=-n F E^{\circ} \text { and } E^{\circ}\gt0 \\ & \text { Also } E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\text {oxi }}^{\circ}+E_{\text {red }}^{\circ} \text { (or) } E_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}\end{aligned}$ Here, Thus, $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ for (a) -0.21 V , i.e. $E_{\text {cell }}^0 \lt 0$ (b) -0.53 V , i.e. $E_{\text {cell }}^0 \lt 0$ (c) -0.32 V , i.e. $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} \lt 0$ (d) 0.53 V , i.e. $E^{\circ}\gt0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When a lead storage battery is discharged;
questions.solution: $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ is consumed during discharging of lead storage battery as, Pb changes to $\mathrm{PbSO}_4$, and $\mathrm{PbO}_2$ changes to $\mathrm{PbSO}_4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A student made the following observations in the laboratory.
I. Clean copper metal did not react with 1 molar $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2$ solution
II. Clean lead metal dissolved in a 1 molar $\mathrm{AgNO}_3$ solution and crystals of Ag metal appeared
III. Clean silver metal did not react with 1 molar $\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2$ solution
The order of decrease in reducing character of three metals is
questions.solution:
Metal placed above in electrochemical series replaces the other from its salt solution which is lower in the series.
The low $E^{\circ}$ value containing metals have the high reducing character.
Thus, order of reducing character of three metals is
$\mathrm{Pb}\gt\mathrm{Cu}\gt\mathrm{Ag}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The conductivity of $0.001028 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ acetic acid is $4.95 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. Find out its dissociation constant if $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}$ for acetic acid is $390.5 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{K}{C} & =\frac{4.95 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}}{0.001028 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}} \times \frac{1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3}{\mathrm{~L}} \\ & =48.15 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \alpha & =\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}}=\frac{48.15}{390.5}=0.1233 \\ K & =\frac{C \alpha^2}{(1-\alpha)}=\frac{0.001028 \times(0.1233)^2}{(1-0.1233)} \\ & =1.78 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement is correct?
options: $E_{\text {cell }}$ and $\Delta_t G$ of cell reaction both are extensive properties.
$E_{\text {cell }}$ and $\Delta_r G$ of cell reaction both are intensive properties.
$E_{\text {cell }}$ in the intensive property while $\Delta_r G$ of cell reaction is an extensive property.
$E_{\text {cell }}$ is an extensive property while $\Delta_r G$ of cell reaction is an intensive property.
questions.solution: $E_{\text {cell }}$ is intensive while $\Delta_r G$ is extensive. (In the relation, $\Delta G=-n F E, E$ depends only on the nature of the cell reaction so it is intensive whereas value of $\Delta G$ depends upon the value of $n$. So it is extensive.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Standard electrode potential of three metals $X$, $Y$ and $Z$ are $-1.2 \mathrm{~V},+0.5 \mathrm{~V}$ and -3.0 V$ respectively. The reducing power of these metals will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & E_y^{\circ}=-1.2 \mathrm{~V} \\ & E_x^{\circ}=+0.5 \mathrm{~V} \\ & E_z^{\circ}=-3.0 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Higher the reduction potential, lesser the reducing power.
$\therefore$ Order of reducing power is $Z \gt X \gt Y$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Electrolysis of fused NaCl will give
questions.solution:
When molten or fused NaCl is electrolysed, it yields metallic sodium and gaseous chlorine. Industrially, this process is called Down's process. Reactions occurring in Down's cell are as follows
$\begin{array}{rlr}\mathrm{NaCl}(l) & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} & \\ \mathrm{Na}^{+}+e^{-} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na} & \text { (at cathode) } \\ \mathrm{Cl}^{-} & \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2+e^{-} \quad \text { (at anode) }\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If the $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ for a given reaction has a negative value then which of the following gives the correct relationships for the values of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ and $K_{\text {eq }}$ ?
questions.solution:
$\Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}$
When $E^{\circ}$ is negative, then $\Delta G^{\circ}\gt0$
$\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T$ in $K_{\mathrm{eq}}$
When, $\Delta G^{\circ}\gt0, K_{\text {eq }}=10^{-x}$ which is less than one, i.e., $K_{\text {eq }} \lt 1$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How long it will take to deposit 1.0 g of chromium when a current of 1.25 A flows through a solution of chromium (III) sulphate ? (Molar mass of $\mathrm{Cr}=52$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}(s)$
3 mol or $3 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$ of electricity are needed to deposit 1 mol or 52 g of Cr .
52 g of Cr require current
$=3 \times 96500 \mathrm{C}$
1 g of Cr will require current
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{3 \times 96500}{52} \mathrm{C} \\
& =5567.3 \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$
Now, number of coulombs
$\begin{aligned}
& =\text { current (ampere) } \times t \text { (seconds) } \\
& \therefore \text { Time (s) required }=\frac{\text { no. of coulombs }}{\text { current (ampere) }} \\
& =\frac{5567.3 \mathrm{C}}{1.25 \mathrm{~A}} \\
& =4453.8 \mathrm{~s} \\
& \text { or } \\
& =1.24 \mathrm{~h} \\
&
\end{aligned}$
