questions.solution: This is an example of Kolbe's electrolysis. $2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOK} \xrightarrow{\text { electrolysis }} 2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}$ At cathode : $\begin{array}{r} 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K} \\ 2 \mathrm{~K}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \uparrow \end{array}$ At anode :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the electrode potential (in V ) of the following electrode at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
$\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(0.1 \mathrm{M}) \mid \mathrm{Ni}(s)$
(Standard reaction potential of $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Ni}$ is $-0.25 \mathrm{~V}, \frac{2.303 R T}{F}=0.06$ )
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} & =E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \log \left[\frac{[\mathrm{Ni}]}{\left[\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\right]}\right] \\ & =-0.25-\frac{0.06}{2} \log \left(\frac{1}{0.1}\right) \quad(\because n=2) \\ & =-0.25-0.03 \times 1 \\ E_{\text {cell }} & =-0.28 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 4.5 g of aluminium (Atomic mass 27 amu) is deposited at cathode from $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ solution by a certain quantity of electric charge. The volume of hydrogen produced at STP from $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions in solution by the same quantity of electric charge will be
questions.solution:
From second law of Faraday
$\frac{m_{\mathrm{Al}}}{m_{\mathrm{H}}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{Al}}}{E_{\mathrm{H}}}$
$\therefore \frac{4.5}{m_{\mathrm{H}}}=\frac{27 / 3}{1}$
or $m_{\mathrm{H}}=0.5 \mathrm{~g}$
$\because 2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_2$ volume at $\mathrm{STP}=22.4 \mathrm{~L}$
$\therefore 0.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{H}_2$ volume at STP
$=\frac{22.4 \times 0.5}{2} \mathrm{~L}=5.6 \mathrm{~L}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What amount of $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ gas liberated at anode, if 1 ampere current is passed for 30 minute from NaCl solution?
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \underset{1 \text { mole }}{\mathrm{Cl}_2}+\underset{2 \times 96500 \text { coulomb }}{2 e^{-} \text {(At anode) }}$
$\begin{aligned} Q & =i t=1 \times 30 \times 60 \\ & =1800 \text { coulomb }\end{aligned}$
The amount of chlorine liberated by passing 1800 coulomb of electric charge
$=\frac{1 \times 1800 \times 71}{2 \times 96500}=0.66 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How much chlorine will be liberated on passing one ampere current for 30 min through NaCl solution?
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{w}{E}=\frac{i t}{96500} \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{w}{35.5}=\frac{1 \times 30 \times 60}{96500} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad=0.662 \mathrm{~g} \\ & \end{aligned}$
so, 0.662 g chlorine is liberated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two metals 'A' and 'B' have $\mathrm{E}_{\text {red }}^{\circ}$ value equal to 0.36 V and 1.23 V . What will be the correct cell equation?
questions.solution:
From $E_{\text {red }}^{\circ}$. values, it is clear that $A$ is more electropositive than $B$ (as it has lesser $E^{\circ}{ }_{\text {red }}$ ), hence it can replace $B$ from its salt solution and act as anode in galvanic cell. Hence, correct cell equation will be:
$A+B^{2+} \longrightarrow A^{2+}+B$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}, \mathrm{Zn}\right)=-0.763 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}\right)=-0.44 \mathrm{~V}$, then the emf of the cell $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(\mathrm{a}=0.00 \mathrm{l}) \| \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(\mathrm{a}=0.005)\right| \mathrm{Fe}$ is
questions.solution:
The cell reaction is
$\mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+\mathrm{Fe}$
From Nernst equation
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{\mathrm{n}} \log \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}} \\ & =(0.763-0.44)-\frac{0.0591}{1} \log \frac{0.001}{0.005} \\ & =0.364 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Point out the incorrect reaction from the following.
questions.solution:
Pyrolusite ore, $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ on fusion with KOH in presence of air forms potassium manganate, $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4$
$2 \mathrm{MnO}_2+4 \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{MnO}_4+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When an iron object is plated with tin, tin does not act as sacrificial anode in protecting against corrosion, because.
options: tin is more reactive than iron
tin is less reactive than iron
reactivity of tin and iron is same
tin is oxidising agent while iron is not so
questions.solution: Because tin is less reactive than iron, so when an iron object is plated with tin, it does not act as sacrificial anode in protecting against corrosion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the cell reaction
$\mathrm{Pb}+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}+\mathrm{Sn}$
Given that, $\mathrm{Pb} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, E^{\circ}=0.13 \mathrm{~V}$
$\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Sn} ; E^{\circ}=-0.14 \mathrm{~V}$
What would be the ratio of cation concentration for which $E=O$ ?
questions.solution:
For the given cell reaction,
$E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-0.14 \mathrm{~V}+0.13 \mathrm{~V}=-0.01 \mathrm{~V}$
According to Nerst equation,
$\begin{aligned} E_{\text {cell }} & =E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]} \\ \Rightarrow \quad 0 & =-0.01=\frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}=\frac{0.01}{-0.0296}=-0.3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}=\operatorname{antilog}(-0.3) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\left[\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}\right]}=0.5 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The value of reaction quotient $(Q)$, for the following cell
$\mathrm{Zn}(s)\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}(0.01 \mathrm{M})\right|\left|\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(1.25 \mathrm{M})\right|$ $\operatorname{Ag}(s)$ is
questions.solution: The cell reaction is $\therefore Q=\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2}=\frac{0.01}{(1.25)^2}=6.4 \times 10^{-3}$ $\therefore$ Value of $Q=6.4 \times 10^{-3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement is not correct about an inert electrode in a cell?
options: It does not participate in the cell reaction.
It provides surface either for oxidation or for reduction reaction.
It provides surface for conduction of electrons.
It provides surface for redox reaction
questions.solution: Inert electrodes provide surface for oxidation or reduction reaction but not for redox reaction. Hence, option (d) is incorrect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of moles of oxygen obtained by the electrolytic decomposition of 108 g water is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{array}{ll}
2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\text { Electrolysis }} 2 \mathrm{H}_2+ &\mathrm{O}_2 \\
2 \text{mol} & 1 \text{mol} \\
2 \times 18 =36 g
\end{array}\)
\(\because 36 \mathrm{~g}\) of \(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\) produce 1 mole of oxygen
\(\therefore 108 \mathrm{~g}\) of water will produce oxygen
\(=\frac{108}{36}=3 \mathrm{~mol}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 500 mL of a sample of water required 19.6 mg of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ for the oxidation of dissolved organic matter in it in the presence of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$. The COD of water sample is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & 500 \mathrm{~mL} \mathrm{H} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 19.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \\ & 106 \mathrm{~mL} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 39.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \\ & 49 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \longrightarrow 8 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & 39.2 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \longrightarrow 6.4 \mathrm{~g} \text { of } \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & \therefore \quad \text { COD }=6.4 \mathrm{ppm} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following region is coldest?
questions.solution: Meso-sphere is the coldest region having about $-100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Excess fluoride (over 10 ppm ) in drinking water can cause
options: harmful effect of bones and teeth
questions.solution: Excess fluoride over 10 ppm causes harmful effects to bones and teeth.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Eutrophication of a lake means, it
options: has excess amount of organic matter
questions.solution: Eutrophication means high concentration of phosphates and nitrates from fertiliser and detergents in aquatic ecosystems.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following soap/detergent is least, reduce space biodegradable?
questions.solution: Alkyl benzenesulphonates derived from branch chain alkanes have much lower biodegradability.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is mainly responsible for depletion of ozone layer?
questions.solution:
Chlorofluorocarbons are responsible for the depletion of ozone layer as they provide Cl free radical which infact reacts with ozone and converts it into $\mathrm{O}_2$ as
$\mathrm{Cl}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{ClO}^{\bullet}+\mathrm{O}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Freon used as refrigerant is
questions.solution: Freon is \(\mathrm{CCl}_2 \mathrm{~F}_2\). It is used as refrigerant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is secondary pollutant?
questions.solution: Pollutants which are formed by reaction amongst the primary pollutants (persist in the environment in the form they are passed into it) are called as secondary pollutants e.g., peroxyacyl nitrates (PAN) are formed through reaction between nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons in the presence of sunlight.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Fischer-Ringe's method of separation of nobles gas mixture from air, $\qquad$ is used
questions.solution:
In Fischer Ringe's method, air free from moisture and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is passed over a heated mixture $\left(800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ of $90 \% \mathrm{CaC}_2+10 \% \mathrm{CaCl}_2$ in an iron tube, when following reactions take place
$\mathrm{CaC}_2+\mathrm{N}_2 \xrightarrow{800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{CaCN}_2+\mathrm{C}$
$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO} \\ & \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{r}2 \mathrm{CaC}_2+3 \mathrm{CO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CaCO}_3+5 \mathrm{C} \\ \mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{CO}_2\end{array}$
$\mathrm{CO}_2$ gas is now absorbed by KOH solution. Thus a mixture of inert gases is obtained.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Green chemistry means such reactions which
options: reduce the use and production of hazardous chemicals
are related to the depletion of ozone layer
questions.solution: Green chemistry involves such reactions which reduce the use and production of hazardous or toxic chemical to reduce pollution from environment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement(s) is not correct?
options: Suspended particulate matter is an important pollutant released by diesel vehicles
Soot particles (size $ \lt 5 \mu$ ) cause fibrosis of the lung living
Photochemical smog is formed by oxides of sulphur, smoke and dust particles
questions.solution: When climate is warm, dry and sunny, the oxides of nitrogen and unsaturated hydrocarbons are converted in the components such as PAN, formaldehyde which form photochemical song, by the action of sunlight.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During nitration of benzene, the attacking electrophile is
questions.solution: During nitration of benzene the attacking electrophile is $\mathrm{NO}_2^{+}$. It is formed by the reaction between $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ :

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.solution: The IUPAC name of compound is 1,4-dibromobenzene while interval system it is called para bromobenzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: IUPAC name of
questions.solution: IUPAC name of given compound is 2-methylcyclohex-3-en-1-ol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following amino acid is optically inactive?
questions.solution: Except glycine, all other $\alpha$-amino acids are optically active as their $\alpha$-carbon is asymmetric. $\text { Thus, glycine is optically inactive. }$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which can not behave as a nucleophile for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction?
questions.solution: In $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction, nucleophile transfers electron pair to carbon of substrate, leading to the formation of transition state. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is a neutral nucleophile while $\mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{NH}_2^{-}$and $\mathrm{I}^{-}$are negatively charged nucleophile. Thus, $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ cannot behave as a nucleophile for $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2$ reaction.

questions.q: Which of the following compound can exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution: Presence of $\alpha-\mathrm{H}$ atom along with a multibonded functional group is an essential condition for exhibiting tautomerism. Satisfies this condition, therefore exhibit tautomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Kjeldahl's method, the nitrogen present in an organic compound is quantitatively converted into
questions.solution: (d) In Kjeldahl's method, the nitrogen is quantitatively converted into ammonia by heating with NaOH .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the correct IUPAC name?
questions.solution: 2, 3-dimethyl pentanoyl chloride

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following sodium compound/compound(s) are formed when an organic compound containing both nitrogen and sulphur is fused with sodium?
questions.solution:
When organic compound containing both nitrogen and sulphur is fused with sodium, sodium-thiocyanate is formed as follows
$\mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{N} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaSCN}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Consider the following structures. Choose the correct statement regarding the above structures.
options: Dipole moment varies as II $\gt$ III $\gt$ I
II is more stable than I
I is the most reactive among three
questions.solution: Hence, Dipole moment decreases as II $\gt$ III $\gt$ I Stability $=$ II $\gt$ III $\gt$ I Reverse is correct for reactivity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Duma's method involves the determination of nitrogen content in the organic compound in the form of
questions.solution:
Duma's method involves the determination of nitrogen content in the organic compound in the form of $\mathrm{N}_2$
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu} \longrightarrow \mathrm{N}_2+\mathrm{CuO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In Duma's method of estimation of nitrogen, 0.35 g of an organic compound gave 55 mL of nitrogen collected at 300 K temperature and 715 mm pressure. The percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound would be
(Aqueous tension at $300 \mathrm{~K}=15 \mathrm{~mm}$ )
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1}=\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2} \\ & \text { or } \frac{\left(\rho-\rho_1\right)}{T_1} V_1=\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2}\end{aligned}$
where,
$p_1=\rho-\rho_1$
$\rho=715 \mathrm{~mm}$ (pressure at which $\mathrm{N}_2$ collected).
$\begin{aligned} & \rho_1=\text { aqueous tension of water }=15 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & t_1=t+273=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & V_1=55 \mathrm{~mL}=\text { Volume of moist nitrogen in } \\ & \text { nitrometer } \\ & \rho_2=\text { pressure of } \mathrm{N}_2 \text { at } \mathrm{STP}=760 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & t_2=\text { temperature of } \mathrm{N}_2 \text { at } \mathrm{STP}=273 \mathrm{~K} \\ & V_2=\text { ? }\end{aligned}$
$\therefore V_2=\frac{(715-15)}{300} \times 55 \times \frac{273}{760}=46.098 \mathrm{~mL}$
$\%$ of nitrogen in the given compound
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{28}{22400} \times \frac{V_2}{W} \times 100=\frac{28}{22400} \times \frac{46.098}{0.35} \times 100 \\ & =16.45 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which amongst the following is the most stable carbocation?
questions.solution:
The most stable carbocation is $t$-alkyl carbocation because the order of stability of alkyl carbocation is $t$-alkyl $\gt s$-alkyl $\gt$ p-alkyl $\gt$ $\mathrm{CH}_3^{+}$ carbocation. This stability order is described with the help of hyperconjugation and inductive effect. On the basis of hyper conjugation, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2 \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}$ shows six resonating structures due to presence of six $\alpha \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds,

due to presence of nine $\alpha \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds $\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_3$ does not show the property of resonance while $\mathrm{CH}_3-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2$ shows three resonating structures due to presence of three $\alpha$ $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds. Hence, larger number of resonating structures are possible in (2), so it is most stable. The above order of stability is also explained with the help of (+) I-effect of $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group. More the number of $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group. more will be tendency to displace the electrons towards positive charged carbon of carbocation. Thus (+) charge is decreased or compensated i.e, positive charge is decreased and stability of carbocation is increased.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: In cyamide extraction process of silver from argentite ore, the oxidising and reducing agents are respectively.
questions.solution:
The extraction of silver involves in cyanide process are
(i) $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{CN}^{-}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}+\mathrm{SO}_2$
(ii) $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}+\mathrm{Ag}$
Thus, in (i) $\mathrm{O}_2$-act as oxidising agent (oxidise sulphide to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ) and in (ii) Zn act as reducing agent (reduce $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$to Ag ).

questions.solution: Catenation tendency decreases with the decrease in $M-M$ bond energy. Thus, bond energies of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Si}-\mathrm{Si}$ and Ge - Ge must be 348, 180 and $167 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.

questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: For the formation of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ and $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, values of $\Delta_f G^{\circ}$ are $540 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $-827 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. What will be the possibility for reaction of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ by Al ?
options: Reduction of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ by Al will take place
Oxidation of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ by Al will take place
Neither oxidation nor reduction will take place
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{Cr}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 ; \Delta_f \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-540 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \ldots \\ & 2 \mathrm{Al}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 ; \Delta_f \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-827 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \ldots\end{aligned}$
Substrating equation (i) from (ii)
$\begin{gathered}2 \mathrm{Al}+\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+2 \mathrm{Cr} \\ \Delta_r G=[-827-(-540)] \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}=-287 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{gathered}$
because for the reduction by $A l$, value of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is negative, therefore reaction is feasible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: The extraction of which of the following metals involves bessemerisation?
questions.solution:
In a Bessemer converter, copper pyrites are oxidised to FeO and $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{FeO}$ is slagged off. $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$ reacts with $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ left unoxidised to give Cu .
$2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2 \uparrow$
$2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \uparrow$
Thus, extraction of Cu involves bessemerisation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: In the extraction of copper from its sulphide ore, the metal is formed by the reduction of $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$ with
questions.solution:
In the extraction of copper from its sulphide ore, the metal is formed by the reduction of $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$ with $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}$. This reaction completes with the process of auto-reduction. Chemical reaction occuring in this reaction is as follows
$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2 \uparrow \\ & \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Cu}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{SO}_2\end{aligned}$
The molten copper is allowed to cool. During cooling, dissolved $\mathrm{SO}_2$ comes out in the form of blisters, soit is called blister copper.

questions.q: Lead is extracted from galena $(\mathrm{PbS})$, either by following ways. Auto-reduction process is
questions.solution:
In auto-reduction, no external reducing agent is required. In such reduction reaction, the product formed acts as a reducing agent and reduces the oxide to metal. Thus, only reaction II is called auto-reduction.
$\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{PbS}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { Roasted in air }} 2 \mathrm{PbO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{PbO}+\mathrm{PbS} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{~Pb}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \\ & \begin{array}{l}\text { (Act as a } \\ \text { reducing agent) }\end{array}\end{aligned}$

questions.q: In electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, the nitro group is meta directing because it
options: decreases electron density at ortho and para positions
decreases electron density at meta position
increases electron density at meta position
increases electron density at ortho and para positions
questions.solution: When nitro group is present in the benzene nucleus, it withdraws electrons from $o$ and $p$-positions. Thus, the electron density at the $o$ and $p$-positions decreases. m-positions become positions of comparatively higher electron density and therefore, electrophilic attack occurs at m-positions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow{\text { Alc. } \mathrm{KOH}}$ Final product is
questions.solution:
When haloalkane containing $\beta$-hydrogen atom is heated with alc. KOH solution, then alkene is formed
$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br} \xrightarrow{\text { alc. } \mathrm{KOH}} \mathrm{CH}_3 - \underset{\text { Propene }}{\mathrm{CH}}=\mathrm{CH}_2$
In this reaction, hydrogen is elimated from $\beta$-carbon and the halogen is lost from $\alpha$-carbon atom. As a result, an alkene is formed as a product.
Due to the involvement of elimination of $\beta$ -hydrogen atom, the process is often called $\beta$-elimination.

The suitable reagent for Wurtz reaction is $\mathrm{Na} /$ ether. An ethereal solution of an alkyl halide is treated with sodium to produce higher alkanes. This reaction involves the formation of new $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond. e.g.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ for the reaction, $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{s}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}(s)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})$, are $30.56 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $66.00 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The temperature at which the free energy change for the reaction will be zero, is
questions.solution:
From the Gibb's - Helmholtz equation,
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
At equilibrium, $\Delta G=0$
Hence, $\quad \Delta H-T \Delta S=0$ or, $\Delta H=T \Delta S$
Or,$T=\frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S}$
Given that, $\Delta H=30.56 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\begin{aligned} & =30560 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \therefore \quad \Delta S & =66.0 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \therefore \quad T & =\frac{30560}{66.0}=463 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of these is not true for benzene?
options: It forms only one type of monosubstituted product
There are three carbon-carbon single bonds and three carbon-carbon double bonds
Heat of hydrogenation of benzene is less than its theoretical value
The bond angle between carbon-carbon bonds is $120^{\circ}$
questions.solution:
The Benzene has \(120^{\circ}\) bond angle.
The more heat released, the more negative the value. Benzene releases less heat (has a lower heat of hydrogenation) because of its aromatic properties which gives it remarkable stability.
Benzene does not have 3 single and 3 double bonds. All bonds are partially double bonds due to resonance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Most common isotopes of hydrogen (non-radioactive) is
questions.solution:
Protium $\left({ }_1^1 H\right)$ deuterium or heavy hydrogen $\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}\right.$ or D$)$ and tritium $\left({ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}\right.$ or T $)$ are the isotopes of hydrogen.
Protium or ordinary hydrogen has one proton and no neutron in the nucleus and one electron revolves around the nucleus. It is the most abundant and common form of hydrogen (approximately $99.98 \%$ ).
${ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_{\mathrm{l}}^2 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 100 mL of a solution contains 2 g of acetic acid and 3 g of sodium acetate providing $K_a=1.8 \times 10^{-5}$, then choose the correct option.
options: This solution is basic in nature
This solution is acidic in nature
This solution is amphoteric in nature
This solution is neutral in nature
questions.solution:
We have the formula for pH
$\mathrm{pH}=-\log K_a+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]}$
$[$ Salt $]=\frac{3 \times 1000}{82 \times 100} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
and
$\begin{aligned} {[\text { Acid }] } & =\frac{2 \times 1000}{60 \times 100} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{pH} & =-\log 1.8 \times 10^{-5}+\log \frac{\frac{3 \times 1000}{82 \times 100}}{\frac{2 \times 1000}{60 \times 100}} \\ \mathrm{pH} & =4.7851\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{p} K_a$ of acetic acid and $\mathrm{p} K_b$ of ammonium hydroxide are 4.76 and 4.75 respectively. Calculate the pH of ammonium acetate solution.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{pH}=7+ & \frac{1}{2}\left[\mathrm{p}^{K_a}-\mathrm{p}^{K_b}\right] \\ & =7+\frac{1}{2}[4.76-4.75] \\ & =7+\frac{1}{2} \times 0.01=7+0.005 \\ & =7.005\end{aligned}$

questions.solution: NaCl is a neutral salt, thus the pH value of its aqueous solution is 7 .

questions.q: The expression for the solubility product of $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \underset{2 s}{2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}}+\underset{s}{\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}} \\ & K_{\mathrm{sp}}=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}\right]=(2 s)^2 \cdot s \\ & \therefore \quad K_{\mathrm{sp}}=4 s^3 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $10^{-6} \mathrm{M~} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is diluted 100 times. The pH of the
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$in the diluted base $=\frac{10^{-6}}{10^2}=10^{-8}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Total }\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] & =10^{-8}+\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \text {of water } \\ & =\left(10^{-8}+10^{-7}\right) \mathrm{M} \\ & =10^{-8}[1+10] \mathrm{M} \\ & =11 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{M} \\ \mathrm{pOH} & =-\log \left(11 \times 10^{-8}\right) \\ & =-\log 11+8 \log 10 \\ & =6.9586 \\ \mathrm{pH} & =14-6.9586 \\ & =7.0414\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When 750 mL of 0.5 M HCl is mixed with 250 mL of 2 M NaOH solution, the value of pH will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}n_{\mathrm{HCl}} & =M_{\mathrm{HCl}} \times V_{\mathrm{HCl}} \text { and } \\
n_{\mathrm{NaOH}} & =M_{\mathrm{NaOH}} \times V_{\mathrm{NaOH}}
\end{aligned}$
where, $n=$ number of moles
$\begin{aligned}& M=\text { molarity, } V=\text { volume } \\
& \therefore n_{\mathrm{HCl}}=0.5 \times 750=375 \text { milimoles. } \\
& n_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=2.0 \times 250=500 \text { milimoles. } \\
& \because n_{\mathrm{NaOH}} \gt n_{\mathrm{HCl}}
\end{aligned}$
The solution is basic and has $\mathrm{pH} \gt 7$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The solubility of saturated solution of $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$ is $s$ mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$. What is its solubility product?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$ ionises completely in the solution as
$\underset{s \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4 \longrightarrow} \underset{2 s \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}}{2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}}+\underset{s \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-}}$
Hence, the solubility product,
$\begin{aligned}
K_{\text {sp }} \text { of } \mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4 & =\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]^2\left[\mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-}\right] \\
& =(2 s)^2(s) \\
& =4 s^3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If 50 ml of 0.1 HBr is mixed with 50 ml 0.2 M NaOH , find pH of resulting mixture
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_{\text {Mix }} \mathrm{V}_{\text {Mix }}=\underset{\text { (Base) }}{\mathrm{N}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1}-\underset{\text { (Acid) }}{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2}\ldots(i)$
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{M}_1 & =0.2 \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{N}_1=0.2 \mathrm{~N} \\
\mathrm{M}_2 & =0.1 \mathrm{M}, \mathrm{N}_2=0.1 \mathrm{~N} \\
\mathrm{~V}_1 & =50 \mathrm{ml}, \\
\mathrm{V}_2 & =50 \mathrm{ml}, \mathrm{V}_{\text {mix }}=100 \mathrm{ml}
\end{aligned}$
On substituting the given values is equation. (i), We get
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{N}_{\text {mix }} \times 100 & =50 \times 0.2-50 \times 0.1 \\
\mathrm{~N}_{\text {mix }} & =\frac{5}{100}=5 \times 10^{-2} \\
\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] & =5 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$
Now,
$\begin{aligned}
{ }_{\mathrm{P}} \mathrm{OH} & =-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=-\log \left[5 \times 10^{-2}\right] \\
& =2-\log 5=1.3
\end{aligned}$
Also, $\mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14$
$\mathrm{pH}=14-1.3=12.7$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{OH}^{-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}$and $\mathrm{NH}_3$ are Lewis base because they can donate a lone pair of electrons while $\mathrm{H}^{+}$is a Lewis acid because it can accept a lone pair of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement is correct?
options: The pH of one litre solution containing \(0.49 \mathrm{~g}\) of \(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4\) is 2.0
Phenolphthalein is colorless in basic medium
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & w=0.49 \mathrm{~g} \\ & E=49 \\ & V=1000 \mathrm{~mL} \\ & \therefore \quad N=\frac{w \times 1000}{E \cdot V}=\frac{0.49 \times 1000}{49 \times 1000}=0.01 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \begin{array}{ll}\therefore & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=0.01}\end{array} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\ & =-\log \left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)=2 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the dissociation constant of a base, BOH , is $1.0 \times 10^{-12}$. The concentration of hydroxyl ions in $0.01 M$ aqueous solution of the base would be
questions.solution:
Base $B O H$ is dissociated as follows
$\mathrm{BOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
So, the dissociation constant of $B O H$ base
$K_b=\frac{\left[B^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{[B \mathrm{OH}]}...(i)$
At equilibrium $\left[B^{+}\right]=\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$
$\therefore K_b=\frac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^2}{[B \mathrm{OH}]}$
Given that $K_b=1.0 \times 10^{-12}$ and $\mathrm{BOH} = 0.01 \mathrm{~M}$
$\begin{aligned} \text{Therefore, } {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]^2 } & =1 \times 10^{-14} \\ {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] } & =1.0 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A 0.1 aqueous solution of a weak acid is $2 \%$ ionised. If the ionic product of water is $1 \times 10^{-14}$, the $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]$is
questions.solution:
Since, it is a weak acid, the equation to calculate $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$is
$\begin{aligned}
& =C \times \alpha(\alpha=\% \text { of ionisation }) \\
& =0.1 \times 0.02=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M} \\
K_w & =\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\
{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] } & =\frac{K_w}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \\
& =5 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which has minimum solubility?
questions.solution:
Solubilities of salts are as follows :
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Bi}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3 & =\sqrt[5]{\frac{K_{s p}}{108}} \\ & =\sqrt[5]{\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-72}}{108}}=1.7 \times 10^{-15} \\ \mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{~S} & =\sqrt[3]{\frac{K_{s p}}{4}}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-49}}{4}} \\ & =3.4 \times 10^{-17} \\ \mathrm{PbS} & =\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-29}} \\ & =3.3 \times 10^{-15} \\ \mathrm{CoS} & =\sqrt{K_{s p}}=\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-27}}=4.5 \times 10^{-14}\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ is least soluble.
So minimum solubility is of $\mathrm{Ag}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ as its force of attraction is greatest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given pH of a solution $A$ is 3 and it is mixed with another solution $B$ having pH 2 . If both mixed, then resultant pH of the solution will be :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{pH} \text { of } A=3 \quad \mathrm{pH} \text { of } B=2 \\ & \therefore\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-3} \mathrm{M}, \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \\ & \text { Total }\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\ & =10^{-3}+10^{-2}=10^{-3}+10 \times 10^{-3} \\ & =11 \times 10^{-3} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=-\log _{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(11 \times 10^{-3}\right) \\ & =3-\log 11 \\ & =3-1.04=1.96 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{NaOH}(a q), \mathrm{HCl}(a q),$ and $\mathrm{NaCl}(a q)$ concentration of each is $10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$. Their pH will be respectively
questions.solution:
Given $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion conc. is $10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ 
we know $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-14}$
$\begin{array}{cc}\text { or } & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{10^{-14}}{10^{-3}}} \\ \text { or } & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=10^{-11}} \\ \because & \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left[10^{-11}\right]\end{array}$
Similarly pH of HCl is 3 and pH of NaCl is 7.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $K a$ of an acid is $3.2 \times 10^{-5}$. The degree of dissociation of the acid at concentration of 0.2 M is
questions.solution:
From Ostwald's dilution law
$\alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K a}{c}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{\frac{3.2 \times 10^{-5}}{0.2}} \\ & =1.26 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pH of $10^{-8} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ is
questions.solution:
For $10^{-8} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$,
$\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-8}+10^{-7} \quad$ (from water)
$=11 \times 10^{-8}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=-\log (11 \times 10^{-8})=8-1.04=6.96$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-6.96=7.04$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is acidic towards litmus while of NaCl is not. The correct reason behind this is
options: $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ furnishes both $\mathrm{H}^{+}$well as $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ion in the solution
$\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is the salt of strong base and strong acid
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is the salt of weak base and strong acid which renders $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions on hydrolysis. Thus, aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$ is acidic in nature.
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_3+3 \mathrm{H}^{+}$
On the other hand, NaCl is the salt of strong acid and strong base which does not undergo hydrolysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A buffer solution is prepared in which the concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is 0.30 M and the concentration of $\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$is 0.20 M . If the equilibrium constant, $K_b$ for $\mathrm{NH}_3$ equals $1.8 \times 10^{-5}$, what is the pH of this solution? $(\log 2.7=0.43)$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_b & +\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]} \\ & =-\log K_b+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Base }]}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =-\log 1.8 \times 10^{-5}+\log \frac{0.20}{0.30} \\ & =5-0.25+(-0.176) \\ & =4.75-0.176=4.57\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-4.57=9.43$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The solubility product of $\mathrm{Hg}_2 \mathrm{I}_2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Hg}_2 \mathrm{I}_2(s) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Hg}_2^{2+}+2 \mathrm{I}^{-}$
$\therefore \quad K_{s p}=\left[\mathrm{Hg}_2^{2+}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}^{-}\right]^2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which buffer solution comprising of the following has its pH value greater than 7 ?
questions.solution: The pH value of basic buffer solutions is always greater than 7. Among the given, only $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ constitutes the basic buffer (a mixture of weak base and its conjugated salt), thus, has pH greater than 7 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pH of a 0.001 M solution of HCl is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] } & =0.001 \\
\mathrm{pH} & =-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\
& =-\log \left[10^{-3}\right] \\
& =3
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pH of a $10^{-10} \mathrm{M}-\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution is
questions.solution:
When the solution is very dilute, the concentration of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$produced from water cannot be neglected. Hence,
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=10^{-10}+10^{-7}} \\ & \text { (obtained from water) } \\ & =10^{-7}(0.001+1)=1.001 \times 10^{-7} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{pOH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right] \\ & =-\log \left(1.001 \times 10^{-7}\right) \\ & =7-0.01=6.99 \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH} \\ & =14-6.99=7.01 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Number of oxygen atoms shared per $\mathrm{SiO}_4^{4-}$ tetrahedron in
I. two dimensional sheet structured silicates
II. cyclic silicates and
III. single strand chain silicates respectively are
questions.solution: In two dimensional sheet silicates, three oxygens of each $\mathrm{SiO}_4^{4-}$ units are shared. Hence, it contain $\left(\mathrm{Si}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right)_n^{2-}$ type anions. Cyclic silicates are obtained by sharing of two oxygenatoms of each $\mathrm{SiO}_4^{4-}$ tetrahedron. Chain silicates are also formed by the sharing of two oxygen atoms of each $\mathrm{SiO}_4^{4-}$ units.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following statements about $\mathrm{C}_2$ molecule is wrong?
options: In vapour phase, $\mathrm{C}_2$ molecule is diamagnetic.
Double bond in $\mathrm{C}_2$ molecule consists of both $\pi$ - bonds because of the presence of $4 e_s^{-}$in two $\pi$-molecular orbitals.
double bond in $\mathrm{C}_2$ molecule consists of one $\sigma$-bond and on $\pi$-bond.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_2\left(12 e^{-}\right)=\sigma 1 s^2 \stackrel{*}{\sigma} 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s^2, \sigma_\sigma^* 2 s^2 \\ &\left(\pi 2 p x_2=\pi 2 p y_2\right)\end{aligned}$
In $\mathrm{C}_2$ molecule, double bond consists of both $\pi$ bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not allotrope of carbon?
questions.solution: The properties of catenation and $\mathrm{p} \pi-\mathrm{p} \pi$ bond formation allow carbon to exist in multiple allotropes. Diamond, graphite and fullerene are crystaelline allotrope of carbon. Coal, coke, charcoal, lamp are amorphous allotrope of carbon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the electron deficient molecule?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ is electron deficient molecule because boron atom has three half filled orbitals in excited state. The structure of $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ is represented as follows: In it two electrons of a $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{H}$ bond are involved in formation of three centre bond, these bonds are represented as dotted lines.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the reaction
$X+\mathrm{I}_2+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_4+2 \mathrm{HI}$
The correct option regarding $X$ is/are
options: It is an strong reducing agent
It is used as a reagent in test of $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ radical
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{SnCl}_2+\mathrm{I}_2+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{SnCl}_4+2 \mathrm{HI}$
$\mathrm{SnCl}_2$ is an strong reducing agent because it is readily oxidised into $\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}$ ion.
Since, Sn-atom in $\mathrm{SnCl}_2$ is $\mathrm{sp}^2$-hybridised.
So, it is an angular molecule.
On the other hand, it is used as a reagent in test of $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ radical.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The hardest substance is
questions.solution: The hardest substance in the nature is diamond due to complex tetrahedral structure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is carborundum?
questions.solution: Reason being SiC is also used as carborundum.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Carborandum is
questions.solution: When silicon reacts with carbon, it forms carborandum. Its formula is SiC.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Inorganic benzene is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{B}_3 \mathrm{~N}_3 \mathrm{H}_6$ (borazine) is known as inorganic benzene. Borazine is isoelectronic (ie, same number of electrons) and isosteric (ie, same number of atoms) with benzene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Red lead is
questions.solution: Red lead (minimum or sindhur), i.e., $\mathrm{Pb}_3 \mathrm{O}_4$ is a red powder, insoluble in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Graphite is soft while diamond is hard because
options: diamond has $s p^2$ hybridisation but graphite has $s p^3$ hybridisation
graphite is in planar form while diamond is in tetrahedral form
graphite is covalent and diamond is ionic
questions.solution: In diamond each carbon atom is $s p^3$ hybridised and is joined to each other by strong covalent bond to form a tetrahedral structure. While in graphite carbon atoms form planar, hexagonal rings to give it a sheet like structure. Each sheet is joined to each other by weak van der Waal force. Hence diamond is harder than graphite.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement is correct for isotope of carbon?
options: graphite is more stable thermodynamically than diamond
questions.solution:
The explanation of given statements are as follows
(i) Graphite is conductor of electricity because it has free valence electron which does not participate in bonding. Thus, statement (a) is correct.
(ii) Diamond has a three dimensional structure in which $\mathrm{sp}^3$-hybridised C -atoms are arranged tetrahedrally. Thus, statement (b) is correct.
(iii) Graphite is thermodynamically more stable than diamond and its free energy of formation is less than diamond. However, entropy of graphite is more than diamond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following compounds, sulphur show maximum oxidation number?
options: All have same oxidation number for sulphur
questions.solution:
All have same oxidation number for sulphate, i.e +6 .
(i)Oxidation number of S is
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4=+2+x-8=0 \\
& \therefore x=+6
\end{aligned}$
(ii) Oxidation number of S in $\mathrm{SO}_3=x-6=0$
$\therefore x=+6$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (iii) Oxidation number of } \mathrm{S} \text { in } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_7 \\ & =2+2 x-14=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore 2 x & =+12 \\ x & =+6\end{aligned}$
[where, $x=$ Oxidation number sulphur $(S)$ ]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is correct statement?
options: Aluminium dissolves in dilute mineral acids but is made passive by concentrated nitric acid.
Aluminium vessel can be used as storage of NaOH .
Aluminium chloride does not dissociate in water.
questions.solution:
- Aluminium is made passive by $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ due to formation of oxide layer. Hence, $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ does not react further but other acids react.
$\begin{gathered}2 \mathrm{Al}+6 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{AlCl}_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \\ 2 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2\end{gathered}$
- NaOH reacts with Al as
$2 \mathrm{NaOH}+2 \mathrm{Al} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaAlO}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Thus, NaOH cannot be stored in a vessel made of Al.
- Aluminium chloride dissociate in water.
- $\mathrm{AlF}_3$ is insoluble in anhydrous HF.
Thus, the only correct statement among the given options is (1).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Xenon hexafluoride reacts with silica to form a xenon compound X . The oxidation state of xenon in X is
questions.solution:
Xenon hexa fluoride reacts with silica to form $\mathrm{XeOF}_4$ as
$2 \mathrm{XeF}_6+\mathrm{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{XeOF}_4+\mathrm{SiF}_4$
The oxidation state of xenon in $\mathrm{XeOF}_4$ is +6 as calculated below:
Let the oxidation state of Xe is a
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{a}+1 \times(-2)+4 \times(-1) & =0 \\ \mathrm{a}-2-4 & =0 \\ \mathrm{a} & =+6\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When excess of NaOH solution is added to aqueous solution of iodine, the colour of solution becomes
questions.solution: The colour of solution is due to iodine present in it. When NaOH solution is added, it reacts with $\mathrm{I}_2$ and solution becomes colourless.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Sulphur reacts with chlorine in $1: 2$ ratio and forms $X$. Hydrolysis of $X$ gives a sulphur compound $Y$. The hydridisation of central atom in the anion $Y$ is
questions.solution: When sulphur reacts with chlorine in $1: 2$ ratio the $\mathrm{SCl}_4$ is obtained which on hydrolysis gives $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$. Hence, the compound ' $X$ ' is $\mathrm{SCl}_4$ and ' $Y$ ' is $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ $\mathrm{SCl}_4+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { Unstable }}{\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{OH})_4}+4 \mathrm{HCl}$ $\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{OH})_4 \longrightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { Sulphurous acid }}}{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ The hybridisation of sulphur in $\mathrm{SO}_3^{2-}$ is $s p^3$.

questions.solution: The structure and oxidation state of oxoacids of phosphorus are as follows Thus, oxidation state of orthophosphorous acid is +3 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following rare gas is the abundant in air?
questions.solution:
The $\%$ by volume of rare gases are
\begin{array}{ccc}
\hline & \% by volume & \% by weight \\
\hline He & 0.0005 & 0.000037 \\
\hline Ne & 0.0015 & 0.001 \\
\hline Ar & 0.932 & 1.285 \\
\hline Kr & 0.0001 & 0.00028 \\
\hline Xe & 0.0001 & 0.00004 \\
\hline
\end{array}
So, Ar is most abundant in air.

The correct decreasing order of reactivity of halogen molecule towards $\mathrm{H}_2$ is $\mathrm{F}_2\gt\mathrm{Cl}_2\gt\mathrm{Br}_2\gt\mathrm{I}_2$.

A mixture of $\mathrm{Ca}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$ and $\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is known as superphosphate of lime

A mixture of calcium cyanamide $\mathrm{CaCN}_2$ and coke (C) is called nitrolim. It is used as a fertilizer and can be prepared by passing nitrogen on $\mathrm{CaC}_2$.
$\mathrm{CaC}_2+\mathrm{N}_2 \xrightarrow{1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \underset{\text { nitrolim }}{\mathrm{CaCN}_2}+\mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following fluorides does not exist?
questions.solution: P , As and Sb form pentahalides of the general formula $E X_5$ (where, $E=\mathrm{P}$, As and Sb ) due to the presence of vacant $d$-orbitals in their respective valence shell. N does not form pentahalides due to the absence of $d$-orbitals in its valence shell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which will liberate bromine from a solution of potassium bromide?
questions.solution:
Since, $E^{\circ}$ of $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ is higher than that of $\mathrm{Br}_2$, therefore $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ has a higher tendency to accept electrons than $\mathrm{Br}_2$. Conversely, $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ion has a higher tendency to lose electrons than $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion. Therefore, the following reaction will occur
$\mathrm{KBr} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}$
$2 \mathrm{Br}^{-}+\mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}+\mathrm{Br}_2$
Thus, $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ions are oxidised to $\mathrm{Br}_2$ by $\mathrm{Cl}_2$, which itself is reduced to $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions.

Nitric oxide in solid state exhibits diamagnetic property

It is due to the increasing bond length down the group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not correct regarding the elecolytic preparation of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ ?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ can be prepared by electrolysis of $50 \%$ $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$. In this method, hydrogen is liberated at cathode.
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 \mathrm{HSO}_4^{-}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { At anode : } 2 \mathrm{HSO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8+2 e^{-} \\ & \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & \text { At cathode }: 2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \uparrow\end{aligned}$

\(\mathrm{Cu}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CuSO}_4+\mathrm{SO}_2+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\)

questions.q: Among the following compounds, which will produce $\mathrm{POCl}_3$ with $\mathrm{PCl}_5$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{PCl}_5$ on reaction with the given compounds produce $\mathrm{POCl}_3$.
$\begin{aligned}& 2 \mathrm{PCl}_5+\mathrm{SO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{POCl}_3+\mathrm{SOCl}_2 \\
& \mathrm{PCl}_5+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{POCl}_3+2 \mathrm{HCl} \\
& 2 \mathrm{PCl}_5+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2+2 \mathrm{POCl}_3+2 \mathrm{HCl} \\

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the incorrect statement about noble gas?
options: Boiling point increases with increasing atomic mass
Helium has least tendency to form compound
Noble gases have some value of electron affinity
Xenon has maximum number of compounds
questions.solution:
(1) Intermolecular van der Waals' force exists between atoms of noble gases. The magnitude of these forces increases with increasing atomic mass which directly related to boiling point.
(2) As ionisation energy of helium is maximum, thus, electrons are lightly held to the nucleus and therefore has least tendency to form compound.
(3) As valence shells of noble gases are complete, thus they do not show tendency to gain electrons. Therefore, they have zero electron affinity.
(4) As Xenon has lowest ionisation energy, they have maximum number of compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.solution: The inter-particle forces are strongest in \(\mathrm{NH}_3\) (Hydrogen bonds). In \(\mathrm{PH}_3\) and \(\mathrm{AsH}_3\) the inter-particle forces are dipole-dipole forces which are relatively stronger in \(\mathrm{AsH}_3\). So the increasing order of vaporization is \(\mathrm{PH}_3, \mathrm{AsH}_3\), and \(\mathrm{NH}_3\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following polymers in increasing order of their intermolecular forces.
Nylon-6, 6, buna-S, polythene, neoprene
questions.solution:
The increasing order of intermolecular forces in different polymers is elastomers  < plastics $ \lt $ fibres. So, in order to solve such problem, first classify the given polymer into the above classes and then arrange them in increasing order of forces. Neoprene (synthetic rubber) is an elastomer, buna-S is also an elastomer, polythene is plastic and nylon-6, 6 is a fibre. So, the increasing order of intermolecular forces of the given polymer is
Neoprene $ \lt $ buna-S $ \lt $ polythene $ \lt $ nylon- 6,6

questions.q: The monomer of teflon is
questions.solution: Tetrafluoroethene is the monomer of teflon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ gas when passed through a solution of cations containing HCl precipitates the cations of second group of qualitative analysis but not those belonging to the fourth group. It is because :
options: presence of HCl decreases the sulphide ion concentration
presence of HCl increases the sulphide ion concentration
solubility product of group II sulphides is more than that of group IV sulphides
sulphides of group IV cations are unstable in HCl
questions.solution: In qualitative analysis of cation of second group $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ gas is passed in presence of HCI , therefore due to common ion effect, lower concentration of sulphide ions is obtained which is sufficient for the precipitation of second group cations in form of their sulphides due to lower value of their solubility product ( $K_{s p}$ ). Here, fourth group cations are not precipitated because they require for exceeding their ionic product to their solubility products and higher sulphide ions concentration due to their higher $K_{s p}$ which is not obtained here due to common ion effect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On addition of conc. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ to a chloride salt, colourless fumes are evolved but in case of iodide salt, violet
questions.solution: HI being a stronger reducing agent than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$, reduces $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and is itself oxidised to $I_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ammonia is a Lewis base and it forms complexes with many cations. Which one of the following cations does not form a complex with ammonia?
questions.solution:
Ammonia is a Lewis base. It forms complexes with transition elements like $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ while it does not form complex with $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ because Pb is not a transition element.
$\mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right] \mathrm{Cl}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CuSO}_4+4 \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow {\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right] \mathrm{SO}_4 } +4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CdSO}_4+4 \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Cd}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right] \mathrm{SO}_4 +4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is disproportionation reaction?
questions.solution:
Reactions in which the same substance is oxidised as well as reduced are called disproportionation reaction. Writing the oxidation number of each element above its symbol in the given reactions.
$\begin{aligned} & \stackrel{-4}{\mathrm{C}} \stackrel{+1}{h}_4+4 \mathrm{Cl}_2^{\circ} \longrightarrow \stackrel{+4}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{Cl}_4+4 \stackrel{+1-1}{\mathrm{HCl}} \\ & 2 \mathrm{~F}_2^0+2 \stackrel{-2}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{H}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \stackrel{-1}{\mathrm{~F}}+\stackrel{+2}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{F}_2^{-1}+\stackrel{-1}{\mathrm{H}}_2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}^{-2} \\ & 2 \stackrel{+4-2}{\mathrm{~N} \mathrm{O}_2}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-2} \longrightarrow \stackrel{+3}{\mathrm{NO}_2^{-2}}+\stackrel{+5}{\mathrm{NO}_3^{-2}}+\stackrel{+1}{\mathrm{H}_2} \mathrm{O}^{-2} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Thus, in the reaction of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}, \mathrm{N}$ is both oxidised as well as reduced. Hence, the oxidation number increases from +4 in $\mathrm{NO}_2$ to +5 in $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$ and decreases from +4 in $\mathrm{NO}_2$ to +3 in $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many Faradays are required to reduce 1 mol of $\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}$ to $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Br}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
$\therefore$ Number of Faraday's required $=6 \mathrm{~F}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When hydrogen peroxide is added to acidified solution of dichromate $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$, a deep blue coloured complex of chromic peroxide $\mathrm{CrO}_5$ is formed. Oxidation state of Cr in $\mathrm{CrO}_5$ is
questions.solution: When $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is added to an acidified solution of dichromate $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$, a deep blue complex chromic peroxide, $\mathrm{CrO}_5$ is formed. $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CrO}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)_2+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ This deep blue coloured complex has the following structure Let the oxidation state of Cr be x . In $\mathrm{CrO}\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)_2$ $\begin{aligned} x+(-1) 4+(-2) & =0 \\ x-6 & =0 \\ x & =+6\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}$changes into $\mathrm{Br}_2$ in an acidic medium of a unbalanced equation. How many electron should be present on the balanced equation?
questions.solution:
The equation when $\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}$changes into $\mathrm{Br}_2$ in an acidic medium is as follows
$2 \mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Br}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Add $10 \mathrm{e}^{-}$in LHS to balance the charge on both sides
$2 \mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}+10 \mathrm{e}^{+}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Br}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \text {. }$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Alkali metals readily dissolve in liquid ammonia to give blue coloured solutions. The blue colour is believed to be due to
questions.solution: Alkali metals readily dissolve in liquid ammonia. It gives blue coloured solution due to ammoniated electrons. The reaction is shown below :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bordeaux mixture consist of
questions.solution: Bordeaux is a mixture of copper (II) sulphate $\left(\mathrm{CuSO}_4\right.$ ) and slaked lime $\left[\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right]$ used as a fungicide. It was invented in the Bordeaux region of france in the late 19th century. If it is applied in large quantities annually for many years, the copper in the mixture eventually becomes a pollutant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the correct order of stability for the given superoxides?
questions.solution: With progressive increase in the size of alkali metal ions, the stability of superoxides increases because the size of superoxide ion is large and large cation can be stabilised more by large anion. Therefore, the order of stability is $\mathrm{KO}_2 \lt \mathrm{RbO}_2 \lt \mathrm{CsO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion is larger than $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ions and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ ion is larger than $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion, which of the following will be stable salt in water?
questions.solution: Higher the lattice energy, lower the solubility and more stable the salt in water. Since, lattice energy varies directly with charge. Therefore, among the four combinations given, the lattice energy of MgS is higher than those of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}, \mathrm{MgCl}_2$ and NaCl . Hence, MgS is stable salt in water and least soluble salt.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following the least thermally stable is
questions.solution:
Thermal stability of carbonates increases on moving down a group. Thus, lithium carbonate, $\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ is least thermally stable.
$\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of decreasing first ionisation energy is
questions.solution:
On moving from left to right in a period, as the size decreases, ionisation energy increases. But first IE of elements of II A group is higher than that of the elements of III A group because of the stable $n s^2$ configuration (of II A group). Hence, the order of first ionisation energy is
\(\begin{array}{c} & \mathrm{C} & > & \mathrm{Be} & > & \mathrm{B} & > & \mathrm{Li} \\ \text{Group no.} & \text{ (IV) } & & \text { (II) } & & \text { (III) } & & \text { (I) } \end{array}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The chemical formulae of $X, Y$ and $Z$ are $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline & x & Y & z \\ \hline \text { } & \mathrm{CaO} & \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2 & \mathrm{NaOH} \\ \hline \text { } & \mathrm{NaOH} & \mathrm{CaO} & \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \\ \hline \text { } & \mathrm{NaOH} & \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2 & \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \\ \hline \text { } & \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2 & \mathrm{NaOH} & \mathrm{CaCO}_3 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Le-blanc process is employed in the manufacture of
questions.solution: Le-blanc process is used in the manufacture of potash, i.e., $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Glauber's salt is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l|l} 
\text{Formula of compound} & \text{Name of compound} \\
\hline \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & \text{Glauber's salt} \\
\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & \text{Hypo} \\
\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & \text{Blue vitriol} \\
\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~B}_4 \mathrm{O}_7 \cdot 10 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & \text{Borax}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Argol, a brown crust, formed during the fermentation of grape juice contains
questions.solution: Argol is potassium hydrogen tartarate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following metal carbonates decomposes on heating?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{MgCO}_3$ decomposes on heating to give MgO and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ while alkali metal carbonates (except $\mathrm{Li}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ ) do not.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The metal that produces red-violet colour in the non-luminous flame is
questions.solution: Alkali metals and their salts impart a characteristic colour to flame. The reason for flame colouration is that the energy of the flame causes an excitation of the outermost electrons which when return to their original position, give out the energy so absorbed in the visible region. Thus, Rb gives red-violet colour to the flame.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ammonia gas can be dried over
questions.solution: Ammonia is dried over quick lime $(\mathrm{CaO})$. It cannot be dried over conc. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4, \mathrm{P}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ and $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ because it reacts with these compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Common alum is
questions.solution: Common alum is $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \cdot \mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3 \cdot 24 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Plaster of paris is
questions.solution: Calcium sulphate hemihydrate \(\left(\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\) is called plaster of paris.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not correct?
options: Al reacts with NaOH and liberate $\mathrm{H}_2$
Al is used in the manufacture of electrical cables
NaOH is used during Hall's process of purification of bauxite
questions.solution: NaOH is used in the Baeyer's process. Hence, NaOH is used during Hall's process of purification of bauxite is not the correct statement.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct sequence of increasing covalent character is represented by
questions.solution:
On the basis of Fajan's rule, lower the size of cation higher will be its polarising power and higher will be covalent character.
$\therefore$ Polarising power $\propto \frac{1}{\text { size of cation }}$
Covalent character $\propto$ Polarising power 
So the correct order is
$\mathrm{NaCl} \lt \mathrm{LiCl} \lt \mathrm{BeCl}_2$
(The order of size of cation $\mathrm{Na}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Li}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct formula for plaster of Paris is
questions.solution:
Plaster of Paris (POP) is a hemihydrate of calcium sulphate. Its chemical formula is
$\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\left[\mathrm{or}\left(\mathrm{CaSO}_4\right)_2 \cdot \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is bubbled through a solution of barium peroxide in water then
questions.solution:
When $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is passed through aqueous solution of $\mathrm{BaO}_2, \mathrm{BaCO}_3$ is precipitated and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is formed.
$\mathrm{BaO}_2+\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaCO}_3 \downarrow+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ can also be prepared by reaction of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ with hydrated barium peroxide.
$\mathrm{BaO}_2 \cdot 8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{BaSO}_4+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Beryllium differs in properties from other elements of its own group but shows resemblance with aluminium because of
options: relatively bigger ionic radius and high polarising power of Be
relatively smaller ionic radius and high polarising power of Be
relatively bigger ionic radius is the only reason behind this
questions.solution: Due to relatively smaller ionic radius and high polarising power, Be differs in properties from other elements of its own group. Polarising powers of $\mathrm{Be}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ ions are almost the same, thus they show similarities in their properties.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The correct order of ease of adsorption of the hydrated alkali metal ions on an ion exchange resin is
questions.solution:
(a) Ease of adsorption of the hydrated alkali metal ions on an ion exchange resin decreases as the size of the alkali metal ion increases. Since, the order of the size of the alkali metal ions is $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Na}^{+}\gt\mathrm{K}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Rb}^{+}$.
Thus, the ease of adsorption follows the order
$\mathrm{Rb}^{+}\gt\mathrm{K}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Na}^{+}\gt\mathrm{Li}^{+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A nuclide of an alkaline earth metal undergoes radioactive decay by emission of three $\alpha$-particles in succession. The group of the periodic table to which the resulting daughter element would belong to
questions.solution: After decay of radioactive element, the last element (stable) Pb is obtained which belongs to group 14 of periodic table.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What will be the order of Ist ionisation energy ?
questions.solution: In a sub-group ionisation energy decreases from top to bottom because atomic size increases. So, the Ist IE of $\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{Na}$ and K are in the order $\mathrm{Li}\gt\mathrm{Na}\gt\mathrm{K}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the formula of bleaching powder ?
questions.solution: Bleaching powder is represented by the formula $\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OCl}) \mathrm{Cl}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following elements, is a component of a compound essential for photosynthesis?
questions.solution: Magnesium is found in chlorophyll, the green pigment, essential for photosynthesis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following dissolves in hot conc. NaOH solution?
questions.solution:
Zinc is quite reactive metal. It dissolves in excess of hot NaOH to give sodium zincate.
$\mathrm{Zn}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { sodium zincate }}{\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{ZnO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \uparrow}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among $\mathrm{LiCl}, \mathrm{BeCl}_2, \mathrm{BCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CCl}_4$, the covalent bond character follows the order
questions.solution:
Covalent character increases with decrease in the value of difference in electronegativites of binding atoms. Thus, the correct order is
$\mathrm{LiCl} \lt \mathrm{BeCl}_2 \lt \mathrm{BCl}_2 \lt \mathrm{CCl}_4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a crystalline solid, having formula $\mathrm{XY}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$, oxide ions are arranged in cubic close packed lattice while, cations X are present is tetrahedral voids and cations Y are present in octahedral voids. The percentage of tetrahedral voids occupied by $X$ is
questions.solution:
In a cubic close packed lattice of oxide ions, there would be two tetrahedral and one octahedral void per oxide ion.
Since, the formula shows the presence of 4 oxide ions, the number of tetrahedral voids is eight and that of octahedral voids is four. Out of the eight tetrahedral voids, one is occupied by X .
$\therefore$ Percentage of tetrahedral voids occupied
$=\frac{1}{8} \times 100=12.5 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solid $A B$ has NaCl structure. If the radius of cation $A^{+}$is 170 pm , then the maximum possible radius of the anion $B^{-}$is
questions.solution:
For NaCl type structure $\frac{r_{A^{+}}}{r_{B^{-}}}=0.414-0.737$
For maximum possible radius of $B^{-}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{r_{A^{+}}}{r_{B^{-}}}=0.414, \frac{170}{r_{B^{-}}}=0.414 \\ & r_{B^{-}}=\frac{170}{0.414}=410.6 \mathrm{pm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which type of defect present in NaCl ?
questions.solution:
Key Idea Schottky defect is a vacancy defect in ionic solids. It is shown by those ionic substances in which the cations and anions are of almost similar sizes.
Schottky defect is present in sodium chloride $(\mathrm{NaCl})$. This defect arises when equal number of cations and anions are missing from their lattice sites such that electrical neutrality is maintained. e.g. $\mathrm{CsCl}, \mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{KCl}, \mathrm{AgBr}$, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If oxide ions are arranged in hcp and the aluminium ions occupy two thirds of octahedral voids. What will be the formula of the compound?
questions.solution:
In hcp arrangement of oxide ion.
Number of octabedral (oh) voids for each oxide ion $=1$
Number of tetrahedral $(\mathrm{Td}$ ) voids for each oxide ion $=2$
$\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ occupies only $2 / 3$ of octahedral voids.
Thus, number of $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ for each oxide ion $=2 / 3$
$\Rightarrow$ Ratio of oxide ion : Aluminium ion
$\Rightarrow 3: 2$
Thus, formula is $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A cubic unit cell of a metal with molar mass of $63.55 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ has an edge length of 362 pm . Its density is $8.92 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The type of unit cell is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Density }=\frac{Z \times M}{a^3 \times N_A} \\Z & =\frac{d \times N_A \times a^3}{M} \\
& =\frac{8.92 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \times\left(362 \times 10^{-10}\right)^3}{63.55}=4\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ The metal crystallises in fcc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following arrangements correctly represents hexagonal and cubic close packed structure respectively?
options: $A B C A B C$ and $A B A B$
Both have $A B C A B C$ arrangement
Both have $A B A B$ arrangement
questions.solution:
In hexagonal close packed (hcp) structure, each sphere of third layer lies exactly above the sphere of first layer. Hence, hcp is abbreviated as $A B A B A B \ldots \ldots .$.
In cubic close packed (ccp) structre, the spheres of third layer do not come over those of first layer and sphere of fourth layer correspond with those in first layer. Hence, it can be shown as $A B C A B C \ldots . . .$. .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ferrous oxide has a cubic structure. The length of edge of the unit cell is $5 Å$ and the density of the oxide is $4.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. Then the number of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions present in each unit cell will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \because d=\frac{Z \times M}{N_A \times V} \\ & \therefore Z=\frac{d \times N_A \times V}{M}\end{aligned}$
As $a=5 Å=5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm} \therefore V=\left(5 \times 10^{-8}\right)^3 \mathrm{~cm}^3$
Molecular mass, $M=56+16=72 \mathrm{~g}$
$\begin{aligned} Z & =\frac{4 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \times\left(5 \times 10^{-8}\right)^3}{72} \\ & =4.16 \approx 4\end{aligned}$
Four molecules of ferrous oxide, FeO per unit cell, it means that $4 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ and $4 \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions are present in each unit cell.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Silicon carbide is example of
questions.solution: Silicon carbide (SiC) is an example of covalent solids. covalent or network solids are crystalline solids of non-metals result from the formation of covalent bonds between adjacent atoms throughout the crystal. These are insulators and do not conduct electricity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Graphite is a
questions.solution:
In graphite, the constituent particles are atoms and intermolecular force is covalent bonding.
Thus, it is an example of covalent solid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of atoms contained in a fcc unit cell of a monoatomic substance is
questions.solution:
In a fcc unit cell, the number of corner atoms
$=8 \times \frac{1}{8}=1$
( $\because$ Each corner is shared with 8 other unit cells) The number of face atoms $=6 \times \frac{1}{2}=3$ Thus, the total number of atoms per unit cell $=1+3=4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solid $A B$ has the NaCl structure. If radius of cation $A^{+}$is 120 pm , the maximum possible value of the radius of the anion $B^{-}$is
questions.solution:
For NaCl crystal,
Radius of cation/radius of anion $=0.414$
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{r_{A^{+}}}{r_{B^{-}}}=0.414 \\
& r_{B^{-}}=\frac{r_{A^{+}}}{0.414}=\frac{120}{0.414} \\
& =290 \mathrm{pm} \\
&
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $a$ be the edge length of the unit cell and $r$ be the radius of an atom then for fcc arrangement, the correct relation is
questions.solution: Given, the edge length of the unit cell $=a$ and, radius of the sphere $=r$ Consider $\triangle A B C$, $\begin{aligned} A C^2 & =A B^2+A C^2 \\ & =a^2+a^2 \\ & =2 a^2 \\ \therefore \quad A C & =\sqrt{2} \cdot a \end{aligned}$ But, from the figure, $A C=4 r$ $\therefore \quad 4 r=\sqrt{2} \cdot a$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Total volume of atoms present in a face centred cubic unit cell of a metal is ( \(r\) is atomic radius)
questions.solution:
In fcc unic cell \(z=4\)
\(\begin{aligned}
\text { Hence, occupied volume by sphere } & =4 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \\
& =\frac{16}{3} \pi r^3
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a face-centered cubic lattice, a unit cell is shared equally by how many unit cells?
questions.solution: In a face centered cubic lattice a unit cell is shared equally by six unit cells.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Pure silicon doped with phosphorus is a
questions.solution: Excess of one valence electron in P atom (5 valence electron) over Si (4 valence electron) will form \(n\)-type semiconductor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When electrons are trapped into the crystal in anion vacancy, the defect is known as :
questions.solution: F-centres are the sites where anions are missing and instead electrons are present. They are responsible for colour.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Frenkel and Schottky defects are
questions.solution: Frenkel and Schottky defects are called the Crystal Defects.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Frenkel defect is present in which of the following?
questions.solution:
Key Idea Frenkel defect is generally found in ionic crystals where anion is much larger in size than the cation. It is also known as dislocation defect.
Frenkel defect is present in AgBr. This defect arises when the smaller ion (usually cation) is dislocated from its normal site. It creates a vacancy defect at its original site and an interstitial defect at its new location.
$\text { e.g. } \mathrm{AgBr}, \mathrm{AgCl}, \mathrm{AgI}, \mathrm{ZnS} \text {. }$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An atom forms face centred cubic crystal with density $\mathrm{d}=8.92 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$ and edge length $\mathrm{a}=3.6 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$. The molecular mass of atom in amu is
questions.solution:
Given,
Density $(\mathrm{d})=8.92 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$
Edge length $(\mathrm{a})=3.6 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{Z}=4$ (fcc)
The molecular mass of atom in a lattice can be calculated by using the formula
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{Z} \times \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{a}^3} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{dN}_0 \mathrm{a}^3}{\mathrm{Z}} \\ & M=\frac{8.92 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \times(3.6)^3 \times\left(10^{-8}\right)^3}{4} \\ & M=62.6 \approx 63 \mathrm{amu} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Percentage of free space in a body centre cubic unit cell is
questions.solution:
Packing fraction
$=\frac{\text { Volume occupied by atoms in a unit cell }}{\text { Volume of the unit cell }}$
For body centred cube,
Packing fraction $=\frac{2 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}{\left(\frac{4 r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^3}=\frac{\sqrt{3} \pi}{8}=0.68$
$\therefore \quad$ Volume occupied $=68 \%$
and volume vacant $=32 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: KF has ccp structure. How many $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ions and octahedral voids are there in this unit cell respectively?
questions.solution: In fcc structure, there are $4 \mathrm{~F}^{-}$ions in the packing and hence 4 octahedral voids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Copper has face-centered cubic (fcc) lattice with interatomic spacing equal to $2.54 Å$. The value of lattice constant for this lattice is
questions.solution:
Interatomic spacing for a fcc lattice
$r=\left[\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2+(0)^2\right]^{1 / 2}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$
a being lattice constant.
$\therefore a=\sqrt{2} r=\sqrt{2} \times 2.54=3.59 Å$
