NOTE : Interatomic spacing is just the nearest neighbours distance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Certain crystals produce electric signals on application of perssure. This phenomenon is called :
questions.solution: Certain crystals produce electric signals on application of pressure. This phenomenon is called piezoelectricity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pair of boiling point and compound are given as, Which will show lowest vapour pressure at room temperature?
questions.solution: As boiling point is inversely proportional to vapour pressure. Hence, nitrobenzene having bp $212^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will show lowest vapour pressure at room temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For an ideal binary liquid solution with $p_x^0 \gt p_y^0$ in which relation between $X_x$ (mole fraction of $X$ in liquid phase) and $Y_X$ (mole fraction of $X$ in vapour phase) is correct, $X_y$ and $Y_y$ are mole fraction of $Y$ in liquid and vapour phase respectively
options: $X_x, Y_x, X_y$ and $Y_y$ cannot be correlated
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & p_x^1=p_x^0 \cdot X_x=p_T \cdot Y_x \\ & p_y^1=p_y^0 \cdot X_y=p_T \cdot Y_y\end{aligned}$
Therefore, $\frac{p_x^0}{p_y^0} \cdot \frac{X_x}{X_y}=\frac{Y_x}{Y_y}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\because & \frac{p_x^0}{p_y^0}\gt1 \\ \therefore & \frac{X_x}{X_y} \lt \frac{Y_x}{Y_y}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If osmotic pressure of $4 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{v})$ solution of sucrose is same as $2 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{v})$ solution of ' X ', then the molecular mass of $X(\mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol})$ is
questions.solution:
$4 \%$ solution of sucrose $(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{v})$ means 4 g of sucrose is dissolved in 100 mL of solution. Similarly, $2 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{v}$ ) solution of ' X ' means 2 g of X present in 100 mL of solution.
As we know that, osmotic pressure $(\pi)=$ CRT or $\pi=\frac{n}{V} R T$; where $n=$ number of moles of solute.
Since, $\pi_1$ (for sucrose solution) $=\pi_2$
(for X solution)
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_1} \mathrm{RT}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\mathrm{~V}_2} \mathrm{RT}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{4}{342}=\frac{2}{\text { Molecular weight }} \\ & {\left[\because \mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Molar mass of solute }}\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Molecular weight }=\frac{2}{4} \times 342\end{aligned}$
Molecular weight $=171 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following electrolytes has the same value of van't Hoff factor (i) as that of $\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ ?
questions.solution:
The value of van't Hoff factor of the given molecule is as follows :
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3 & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}, \mathrm{i}=5 \\ \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 & \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}, \mathrm{i}=3 \\ \mathrm{~K}_3\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] & \rightleftharpoons 3 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3+}, \mathrm{i}=4 \\ \mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_3 & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{NO}_3^{-}, \mathrm{i}=4\end{aligned}$
The van't Hoff factor for $\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$ is
$\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right] \rightleftharpoons 4 \mathrm{~K}^{+}+\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}, \mathrm{i}=5$
$\therefore \mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3$ has the same value of van't Hoff factor as that of $\mathrm{K}_4\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate molarity of one litre solution of $22.2 \mathrm{gm} \mathrm{CaCl}_2$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  Molarity (M) } \\ & =\frac{\text { mass of solute (in } \mathrm{g})}{\text { molecular weight of solute }}=\frac{222 \mathrm{~g}}{111 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 1 \mathrm{~L}} \\ & \times \text { Volume of solution (in } \mathrm{L} \text { ) } \\ & \qquad\left[\because \text { Molecular weight of } \mathrm{CaCl}_2=111 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right] \\ & =0.2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}=0.2 \mathrm{M} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following solutions,
$A=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ glucose, $B=0.05$ M  $\mathrm{NaCl}$,
$C=0.05$ M $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$, $D=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$
Which of the following pairs is isotonic?
questions.solution:
Isotonic solutions have same molar concentration of solute particles in solution. Molar concentration of particles in solution are
(a) = 0.1 M in glucose
(b) $=2 \times 0.05 \mathrm{M}$ in NaCl
(c) $=3 \times 0.05 \mathrm{M}$ in $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$
(d) $=4 \times 0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in $\mathrm{AlCl}_3$
Hence, 0.1 M glucose and 0.05 M NaCl solutions are isotonic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution with negative deviation among the following is
questions.solution: When the attraction forces between the two components of a solution are greater than that between the components themselves, the obtained solution exhibit negative deviation from Raoult's law. In case of solution of chloroform and acetone, the forces of attraction between chloroform and acetone is greater than that exists between the molecules of chloroform or between the molecules of acetone, thus this solution shows negative deviation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following would exert maximum osmotic pressure?
options: A solution obtained by mixing equal volumes of (2) and (3) and filtering
questions.solution:
Osmotic pressure is a colligative property, i.e., depends upon the number of particles of solute. Thus, the solution in which solute gives maximum number of ions, exerts maximum osmotic pressure. Number of particles,
(1) In decinormal aluminium sulphate
$\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3 \longrightarrow \underbrace{2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}}_{5 \text { particles }}$
(2) In decinormal barium chloride,
$\mathrm{BaCl}_2 \longrightarrow \underbrace{\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}_{3 \text { particles }}$
(3) In decinormal sodium chloride,
$\mathrm{NaCl} \longrightarrow \underbrace{\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}_{2 \text { particles }}$
(4) When equal volumes of (2) and (3) are mixed, the concentration becomes half of present concentration.
Thus, decinormal aluminium sulphate exerts maximum osmotic pressure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution made by dissolving 40 g NaOH in 1000 g of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is
questions.solution:
Number of moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}(n)$
$=\frac{\text { weight in } \mathrm{g}}{\text { molar mass }}$
$=\frac{40}{40}=1$
As molality of a solution is defined as the number of moles of the solute dissolved in $1000 \mathrm{~g}(1 \mathrm{~kg})$ of the solvent, thus the given solution of NaOH is ' 1 molal' solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The vapour pressure of 100 g water reduces from $3000 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ to $2985 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ when 5 g of substance ' $X$ ' is dissolved in it. Substance ' $X$ ' is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{n}{n+N} & =\frac{\frac{w}{m}}{\frac{w}{m}+\frac{W}{M}} \\ \because \quad \frac{w}{m} &  \lt \frac{W}{M} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{p^{\circ}-p_s}{p^{\circ}} & =\frac{w / m}{W / M}=\frac{w}{m} \times \frac{M}{W} \\ \frac{3000-2985}{3000} & =\frac{5}{m} \times \frac{18}{100} \\ m & =\frac{5 \times 18 \times 3000}{15 \times 100} \\ m & =180\end{aligned}$
180 is the molecular weight of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)$, thus the substance ' $X$ ' is glucose.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Disperse phase and dispersion medium in butter are respectively
questions.solution: Disperse phase and dispersion medium in butter are liquid and solid respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution of urea (mol. mass $56 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) boils at $100.18^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at the atmospheric pressure. If $k_f$ and $k_b$ for water are 1.86 and $0.512 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, the above solution will freeze at
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \because \Delta T_f & =k_f \times \text { Molality of solution } \\ \Delta T_b & =k_b \times \text { Molality of solution }\end{aligned}$
or $\frac{\Delta T_f}{\Delta T_b}=\frac{k_f}{k_b}$
Given that $\Delta T_b=T_2-T_1=100.18-100=0.18$
$\begin{aligned} & k_f \text { for water }=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & k_b \text { for water }=0.512 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{\Delta T_f}{0.18}=\frac{1.86}{0.512}$
$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_f & =\frac{1.86 \times 0.18}{0.512} \\ & =0.6539 \sim 0.654 \\ \Delta T_f & =T_1-T_2 \\ 0.654 & =0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-T_2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore T_2=-0.654^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
($T_2 \rightarrow$ Freezing point of aqueous urea solution)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution has a $1: 4$ mole ratio of pentane to hexane. The vapour pressure of the pure hydrocarbons at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are 440 mm of Hg for pentane and 120 mm of Hg for hexane. The mole fraction of pentane in the vapour phase would be
questions.solution:
Total vapour pressure of mixture $=$ (Mole fraction of pentane $\times \mathrm{VP}$ of pentane $)$ + (Mole fraction of hexane $\times \mathrm{VP}$ of hexane)
$=\mathrm{VP}$ of pentane in mixture +VP of hexane in mixture
$=\left(\frac{1}{5} \times 440+\frac{4}{5} \times 120\right)=184 \mathrm{~mm}$
$\because$ VP of Pentane in mixture. $=\mathrm{VP}$ of mixture $\times$ mole fraction of pentane in vapour phase
$88=184 \times$ mole fraction of pentane in vapour phase
$\therefore$ Mole fraction of pentane in vapour phase $=\frac{88}{184}=0.478$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a property of liquid state?
options: Intermolecular force of attraction in a liquid is quite large
All liquids accompanied by cooling on evaporation
Lower the boiling point of a liquid, greater is its vapour pressure at room temperature
A liquid boils at high temperature at the top of a mountain than at the sea level
questions.solution: We know that boiling point falls on decreasing the pressure. At mountain air pressure is low so liquid boils at lower temperature at the top of mountain than at the sea level.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If for a sucrose solution, elevation in boiling point is $0.1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then what will be the boiling point of NaCl solution for same molal concentration?
questions.solution:
Elevation in boiling point is a colligative property as it depends upon the number of particles.
$\Delta T_b \propto n$
For sucrose, $n=1, \Delta T_b=0.1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
For $\mathrm{NaCl}, n=2, \Delta T_b=0.2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Addition of a non-volatile solute causes lowering in vapour pressure of a solvent from 0.8 atm to 0.2 atm. What is the mole fraction of solvent?
questions.solution:
From Raoult's law, $\frac{P-P_s}{P}=$ mole fraction of solute $\left(x_A\right)$
$\therefore \quad x_A=\frac{0.8-0.2}{0.8}=\frac{0.6}{0.8}=0.75$
Hence, mole-fraction of solvent
$=1-0.75=0.25$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For preparing 3.00 L of 1 M NaOH by mixing portions of two stock solutions ( $A$ and $B$ ) of 2.50 M NaOH and 0.40 M NaOH respectively. Find out the amount of $B$ stock solution (in L ) added.
questions.solution:
Moles needed $=(3.00 \mathrm{~L})(1.00 \mathrm{M})=3 \mathrm{~mol}$.
Suppose that $x \mathrm{~L}$ of 2.50 M NaOH added, then $(3-x) \mathrm{L}$ of 0.40 M NaOH added. The number of moles of solute from the more concentrated solution is 2.50 , that become less concentrated solution is $(0.40)(3.00-x)$.
The total number of moles is 3.00 .
$(2.50 x)+(0.40)(3.00-x)=3.00$
or $2.10 x+1.2=3$
Or $2.10 x=1.8$
or $x=0.857 \mathrm{~L}$ of 2.50 M NaOH
$\therefore \quad(3-0.857)=2.14$ L of 0.40 M NaOH

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The molal freezing point depression constant for benzene $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6\right)$ is $4.90 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. Selenium exists as a polymer of the type $\mathrm{Se}_x$. When 3.26 g of selenium is dissolved in 226 g of benzene, the observed freezing point is $0.112^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ lower than that of pure benzene. The molecular formula of selenium is (atomic mass of $\mathrm{Se}=78.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$)
questions.solution:
To calculate the molar mass of selenium (Se)
$M_B=\frac{K_f \times W_B}{\Delta T_f \times W_A}$
Mas of selenium $\left(W_B\right)=3.26 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of benzene $\left(W_A\right)=226 \mathrm{~g}=0.226 \mathrm{~kg}$
Depression in freezing point $\left(\Delta T_f\right)=0.112^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Molar depression constant $\left(K_f\right)=4.9 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\therefore M_B=\frac{\left(4.9 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)(3.26 \mathrm{~g})}{(0.112 \mathrm{~K})(0.226 \mathrm{~kg})}=631.08 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Given, gram atomic mass of selenium
$=78.8 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
For $\mathrm{Se}_x\left(78.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)=631.08 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
or, $\quad x=\frac{631.08 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{78.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=8$
Thus, molecular formula of selenium $=\mathrm{Se}_8$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In order to prepare one litre 1 N solution of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$, how many grams of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ are required, if the solution to be used in acid medium for oxidation?
questions.solution:
In acidic medium,
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 e^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \text { (From } \mathrm{KMnO}_4 \text { ) }\end{aligned}$
Hence, equivalent weight of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ in acidic
medium $=\frac{\text { Moleculer weight }}{5}=\frac{158}{5}=31.6$
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~N}$ solution can be prepared by dissolving 31.6 g $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ in 1L solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which mixture is immiscible?
questions.solution:
Polar compounds are soluble in polar solvents and non-polar compounds are soluble in non-polar solvents. i.e. Like dissolves like.
(i) $\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_{12}$ (non-polar) + Alcohol (polar)
(ii) Alcohol (polar) + Amine (polar)
(iii) $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ (polar) + Alcohol (polar)
(iv) Alkane (non-polar) + Alkane (non-polar)
Thus, $\mathrm{C}_5 \mathrm{H}_{12}$ and alcohol mixture is immiscible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The van't Hoff factor for $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2$ solution is 2.74 . The degree of dissociation is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$ Total number of moles $=1-\alpha+\alpha+2 \alpha$ $=1+2 \alpha$ Then, van't Hoff factor, $\mathrm{i}=1+2 \alpha$ or, $\quad \alpha=\frac{i-1}{2}$ or, $\quad \alpha=\frac{2.74-1}{2}=0.87 \%$ $\%$ dissociation $=87$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The osmotic pressure of blood is 8.21 atm at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How much glucose would be used for an injection that is at the same osmotic pressure as blood?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \pi & =C R T \text { or } C=\frac{\pi}{R T} \\ & =8.21 \mathrm{~atm} / 0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 310 \mathrm{~K} \\ & =0.323 \mathrm{M}=0.323 \times 180 \mathrm{gL}^{-1} \\ & =58.14 \mathrm{gL}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $0.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ solution of glucose is found to be isotonic with a $2.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ solution of an organic compound. What will be the molecular weight of that organic compound ?
questions.solution:
Molarity of glucose solution
$=\frac{0.5 \mathrm{~g}}{180 \times 1 \mathrm{~L}}=0.00277 \mathrm{M}$
Isotonic solution have equal molar concentrations, hence molarity of organic compound solution $=0.00277 \mathrm{M}$
$\therefore$ Molecular weight of compound
$=\frac{2.5}{0.00277 \times 1}=900$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following aqueous solution should have highest boiling point?
questions.solution:
More be value of van't Hoff factor (i), more be the boiling point.
$\because i$ is maximum for $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4(=3)$
$\therefore$ It has maximum b.p.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A compound, containing only carbon, hydrogen and oxygen has a molecular weight of 44 . On complete oxidation, it is converted into a compound of molecular weight is 60 . The original compound is
questions.solution:
On complete oxidation, the obtained compound shows increment in molecular weight of only 16. It means only one oxygen atom is added here. This condition is fulfilled by only aldehyde which on oxidation gives acid.
$\mathrm{RCHO} \xrightarrow{[\mathrm{O}]} \mathrm{RCOOH}$
Hence, original compound must be an aldehyde. The aldehyde with molecular weight 44 is acetaldehyde.
$\underset{\begin{array}{l}\text { Molecular } \\ \text { weight }=44\end{array}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}} \xrightarrow{[\mathrm{O}]} \underset{\begin{array}{l}\text { Molecular } \\ \text { weight }=60\end{array}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is correctly arranged in order of increasing weight?
options: 0.0105 equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \lt 0.625 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Fe} \lt 0.006 \mathrm{~g}$ atom of $\mathrm{Ag} \lt 6.0 \times 10^{21}$ atoms of Zn
0.625 g of $\mathrm{Fe} \lt 0.0105$ equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \lt 6.0 \times 10^{21}$ atoms of $\mathrm{Zn} \lt $ 0.006 g atom of Ag

0.625 g of $\mathrm{Fe} \lt 6.0 \times 10^{21}$ atoms of $\mathrm{Zn} \lt 0.006 \mathrm{~g}$ atom of $\mathrm{Ag} \lt 0.0105$ equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

0.0105 equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \lt 0.006 \mathrm{~g}$ atom of $\mathrm{Ag} \lt 6.0 \times 10^{21}$ atoms of $\mathrm{Zn} \lt 0.625 \mathrm{~g}$ of Fe
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 0.625 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Fe} \\ & 0.0105 \text { eq. of } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=0.0105 \times 63 \\ & \quad=0.66 \mathrm{~g} \quad(\text { Eq. wt }=63) \\ & 6 \times 10^{21} \text { atoms of } \mathrm{Zn}=\frac{6 \times 10^{21}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \times 65=0.647 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$
0.006 g atom of $\mathrm{Ag}=0.006 \times 108=0.648 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ Increasing order of weight
$\mathrm{Fe} \lt \mathrm{Zn} \lt \mathrm{Ag} \lt \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element with mass number 81 contains $31.7 \%$ more neutrons as compared to protons. It symbol is
questions.solution:
Let, the number of protons $=\mathrm{x}$
$\therefore$ Number of neutrons $=\mathrm{x}+\frac{31.7 \mathrm{x}}{100}=\frac{131.7 \mathrm{x}}{100}$
mass number $=$ number of protons + number of neutrons $81=x+\frac{131.7 x}{100}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=35$
Thus, the symbol of the element is ${ }_{35} X^{81}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If molarity of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}$ ions is $3 \times 10^{-4}$ express this in ppm
questions.solution:
When solute is present in trace quantities, the concentration is expressed in parts per millions $(\mathrm{ppm})$ or $\mu \mathrm{gmL}^{-1}$.
100 ml of solution $=3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions
$10^6 \mathrm{ml}$ of solution $=\frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{100} \times 10^6=0.3 \mathrm{ppm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$2 \mathrm{KHCO}_3 \longrightarrow \ldots+\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ find amount of gases formed (in lit).
When amount of $\mathrm{KHCO}_3$ is 33 gm .
questions.solution:
The given reaction is as follows
$2 \mathrm{KHCO}_3 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ (l)
$\because 2$ moles of $\mathrm{KHCO}_3$ gives $=1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$
$\therefore 1$ moles of $\mathrm{KHCO}_3$ gives $=1 / 2 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$
$\therefore 33 \mathrm{gm}\left(\frac{33}{100} \mathrm{~mol}\right)$ of $\mathrm{KHCO}_3$ gives $=\frac{33}{200}$
mol of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$
Similarly,
$33 \mathrm{gm}\left(\frac{33}{100} \mathrm{~mol}\right)$ of $\mathrm{KHCO}_3$ gives $=\frac{33}{200} \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$
Total moles of gases $=\frac{33}{200}+\frac{33}{200}=\frac{66}{200} \mathrm{~mol}$
$\begin{aligned} 1 \mathrm{~mol} \text { of gas } & =224 \mathrm{l} \text { volume } \\ \therefore \quad \frac{66}{200} \mathrm{~mol} \text { of gas } & =\frac{66}{200} \times 22.4=7.46 \mathrm{l} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solid $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ will be neutralised by 40.0 mL of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ solution. What would be the weight of solid $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ in gram?
questions.solution: The required neutralisation reaction is, $2 \mathrm{NaHCO}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{CO}_2$ $m$-moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4=M \times V_{\mathrm{mL}}=40.0 \times 0.1=4 \mathrm{~m}$-mole or m-moles of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ neutralised $=4 \times 2=8 \mathrm{~m}$-mole But, m-mole $=\frac{w}{m} \times 1000$ $\begin{aligned} 8 & =\frac{w}{84} \times 1000 \\ \Rightarrow \quad w & =\frac{84 \times 8}{1000} \Rightarrow w=0.672 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 11.2 L of gas at STP Weight 14 g . The gas would be
questions.solution:
Molecular weight of gas $=\frac{14 \times 22.4}{11.2}=28$
i.e., $\mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{N}_2, \mathrm{~B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The mass of carbon anode consumed (giving only carbondioxide) in the production of 270 g of aluminium metal from bauxite by the Hall process is 
(Atomic mass $\mathrm{Al}=27$)
questions.solution:
In Hall and Heroult process
$\begin{aligned} 2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3 & \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \\ 4 \mathrm{C}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 & \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{CO} \uparrow \\ 2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+4 \mathrm{C} & \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Al}+2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{CO}\end{aligned}$
Only for removal of $\mathrm{CO}_2$, following equation is possible
$2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\underset{3 \times 12=36}{3 \mathrm{C}} \longrightarrow \underset{4 \times 27=108}{4 \mathrm{Al}}+3 \mathrm{CO}_2$
$\because$ For 108 g of $\mathrm{Al}, 36 \mathrm{~g}$ of C is required in above reaction.
$\therefore$ For 270 g of Al require amount of C
$=\frac{36}{108} \times 270=90 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The normality of $10 \%(\mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{V})$ of acetic acid is
questions.solution:
Normality $(N) \xrightarrow[X V(\mathrm{~mL})]{W \times 1000}=\frac{10 \times 1000}{60 \times 100}$
$=1.66$
$\therefore N=1.66 \mathrm{~N}$
$\mathrm{N} \approx 1.7 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Total number of atoms represented by the compound $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is :
questions.solution: Total number of atoms in $\mathrm{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is 21 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ obtained from complete decomposition of $20 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_3$ at STP is
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{r}\because 100 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_3, \text { on decomposition, gives } =22.4 \text { litre } \mathrm{CO}_2 \text { at STP }\end{array}$ $\therefore 20 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_3$, on decomposition, will give $=\frac{22.4 \times 20}{100}$ $=4.48 \text { litre } \mathrm{CO}_2 \text { at STP }$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 0.1 mol HCl is equal to
questions.solution:
Molecular mass of HCl
$=35.5$ (at. wt. of chlorine)+1 (at. wt. of hydrogen)
$=36.5$
As 1 mole is the amount of the substance which has mass equal to gram molecular mass.
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{HCl}$ is equal to 36.5 g .
and 0.1 mole HCl is equal to 3.65 g

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The volume of water to be added to 100 $\mathrm{cm}^3$ of $0.5 \mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ to get decinormal concentraction is
questions.solution:
$\because N_1 V_1=N_2 V_2$
Given, $N_1=0.5 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{~N}_2=0.1 \mathrm{~N}$
$V_1=100 \mathrm{~cm}^3, V_2=?$
$\therefore 0.5 \times 100=0.1 \times V_2$
$V_2=500 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ (Total final volume)
$\therefore$ Volume to be added $=500-100=400 \mathrm{~cm}^3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Combustion of glucose takes place according to the equation,
$\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6+6 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
$\Delta H=-72 \mathrm{k}-\mathrm{cal}$
The energy required for combustion of 1.6 g of glucose is
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}\text {Molar mass of } \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6=180 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \because 180 \mathrm{~g} \text { of glucose require energy }=72 \mathrm{kcal} \\ \therefore 1.6 \mathrm{~g} \text { of glucose require energy }=\frac{72 \times 1.6}{180}\end{gathered}$
$=0.64 \mathrm{k} \mathrm{cal}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following, is not a unit of pressure?
questions.solution:
Newton is not the unit of pressure. It is the unit of force. Atmosphere, torr and pascal $(P a)$ are the units of pressure.
$\begin{aligned} & 1 \text { atm. }=1.01 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa} \\ & 1 \text { torr }=133.3 \mathrm{~Pa}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The significant figures in $1.0024,1.240$ and 0.0020 are respectively
questions.solution: In 1.0024 , all figures are significant. In 1.240 ,the significant figures are four (zero tells about precision). Zero before a number are not significant, hence 0.0020 has only two significant numbers (2 and 0 after 2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 56 g of nitrogen and 96 g of oxygen are mixed isothermally and at a total pressure of 10 atm . The partial pressures of oxygen and nitrogen (in atm) are respectively
questions.solution:
According to Dalton's law,
$p_{\text {solute }}=x_{\text {solute }} \times p$
Given, mass of $\mathrm{N}_2=56 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ Moles of $\mathrm{N}_2\left(n_{\mathrm{N}_2}\right)=\frac{56}{28}=2 \mathrm{~mol}$
Mass of $\mathrm{O}_2=96 \mathrm{~g}$
$\therefore$ Moles of $\mathrm{O}_2\left(n_{\mathrm{O}_2}\right)=\frac{96}{32}=3 \mathrm{~mol}$
$x_{\mathrm{N}_2}=\frac{2}{2+3}=0.4$
$x_{\mathrm{O}_2}=\frac{3}{2+3}=0.6$
$\therefore \quad p_{\mathrm{N}_2}=x_{\mathrm{N}_2} \times p=0.4 \times 10=4 \mathrm{~atm}$
$p_{\mathrm{O}_2}=x_{\mathrm{O}_2} \times p=0.6 \times 10=6 \mathrm{~atm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 0.40 g of helium in a bulb at a temperature of $T \mathrm{~K}$ had a pressure of $p$ atm. When the bulb was immersed in water bath at temperature 50 K more than the first one, 0.08 g of gas had to be removed to restore the original pressure. $T$ is
questions.solution:
As pressure and volume remains constant,
$\begin{aligned} n_1 T_1 & =n_2 T_2 \\ n_1 & =\frac{0.4}{4}=0.1, T_1=T \mathrm{~K} \\ n_2 & =\frac{0.40-0.08}{4}=0.08, T_2=(T+50) \mathrm{K}\end{aligned}$
On putting the values,
$\begin{array}{ll} & 0.1 \times T=0.08 \times(T+50) \\ \text { or } & 0.1 \mathrm{~T}=0.08 T=4 \text { or } 0.1 \mathrm{~T}-0.08 \mathrm{~T}=4 \\ \text { or } & 0.02 T=4 \text { or } T=200 \mathrm{~K}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A 100 mL flask contained $\mathrm{H}_2$ at 200 torr, and a 200 mL flask contained He at 100 torr. The two flask were then connected so that each gas filled their combined volume. Assume, no change in temperature, total pressure is
questions.solution:
Given,
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{H}_2} & =200 \text { torr } \\ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}_2} & =100 \mathrm{~mL} \\ \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{He}} & =100 \text { torr }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl} & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{He}} & =200 \mathrm{~mL} \\ & & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2} & =\frac{\mathrm{pV}}{\mathrm{RT}}=\frac{20000}{\mathrm{RT}} \\ \therefore & & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{He}} & =\frac{\mathrm{pV}}{\mathrm{RT}}=\frac{20000}{\mathrm{RT}}\end{array}$
Total number of moles $=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{He}}$
$=\frac{20000}{R T}+\frac{20000}{R T}=\frac{40000}{R T}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Total volume }=300 \mathrm{~mL} \\ & \text { Total pressure }(\mathrm{p}) \quad=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{RT}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{40000 \times \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{RT} \times 300}=\frac{400}{3} \\ & =133.33 \mathrm{torr}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Moles of $\mathrm{N}_2=0.4$ and moles of $\mathrm{O}_2=0.1$ find $\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{N}_2}$ (Partial pressure) $\mathrm{N}_2=$ ?at atmospherim pressure
questions.solution:
Key Idea According to Raoult's law, "at a given temperature, for a solution of volatile liquids, the partial vapour pressure of each component of the solution is direcently proportional to its mole fraction present in solution.
According to Raoult's law,
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{A}} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}} \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}$
where, $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ partial pressure
$\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}=$ total pressure
$\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}=$ moles
Given, ${ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{N}_2=0.4,{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{O}_2=0.1, \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{T}}=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2} & =\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{0.4}{0.4+0.1} \times 1 \mathrm{~atm} \\
& =\frac{0.4}{0.5}=0.8 \mathrm{~atm}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Temperature of a gas is $t \mathrm{~K}$. What would be the temperature at which volume and pressure, both will reduced to half of the initial values?
questions.solution:
Given that,
$\begin{aligned} & p_1=p, T_1=t, V_1=v \\ & p_2=p / 2, V_2=v / 2, T_2=?\end{aligned}$
By using $\frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1}=\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2}$
$\frac{p \times V}{t}=\frac{p \times V}{2 \times 2 \times T_2} \Rightarrow T_2=t / 4$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The most probable velocity (in $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of hydrogen molecule at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Most probable velocity }=\sqrt{\frac{8 R T}{\pi m}} \\ & T=(27+273)=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \text { Molecular mass of } \mathrm{H}_2=2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{8 \times 8.314 \times 10^7 \times 300}{3.14 \times 2}} \\ & =17.8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s} \text {. } \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an ideal gas equation which is constant?
questions.solution:
According to ideal gas equation, $\mathrm{pV}=\mathrm{nRT}$
where, $\mathrm{p}=$ pressure, $\mathrm{V}=$ volume
$\mathrm{n}=$ no. of mole, $\mathrm{T}=$ temperature
$\mathrm{R}=$ universal gas constant
$\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
$=0.08205 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}=1.99 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
$=5.189 \times 10^{19} \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A gas mixture contains \(\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(\mathrm{N}_2\) in the ratio of \(1: 4\) by weight. The ratio of their number of molecules is
questions.solution:
\(\because\) The ratio of \(\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(\mathrm{N}_2\) by weight is \(1: 4\).
\(\therefore\) the ratio of \(\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(\mathrm{N}_2\) by moles \(=\frac{1}{32}: \frac{4}{28}\)
\(=7: 32\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The volume of a gas measured at \(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) and 1 atm pressure is 10 L. To reduce the volume to 2 L at 1 atm pressure, the temperature required is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A certain sample of gas has a volume of 0.2 L measured at 1 atm pressure and $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At the same pressure but $273^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its volume will be :
questions.solution:
According to Charles' law
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\frac{V_1}{T_1} & =\frac{V_2}{T_2} \\ & \frac{0.2}{273} & =\frac{V_2}{476} \\ \therefore \quad & V_2 & =0.4 \mathrm{~L}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ratio of molecular weights of $A$ and $B$ is $\frac{4}{25}$ then ratio of rates of diffusion will be
questions.solution:
According to Graham's rule
$\begin{array}{l}\frac{r_1}{r_2} =\sqrt{\frac{M_2}{M_1}} \\ \text {or } \frac{r_1}{r_2} =\sqrt{\frac{25}{4}} \\ \Rightarrow =\frac{5}{2}\end{array}$
Hence ratio of diffusion is $5: 2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{V}^2$ given in van der Waal's equation is for
questions.solution: For the explaination of real gas behaviour van der Waal gave an equation. The gas pressure is changed due to intermolecular attraction. By adding $\frac{a}{V^2}$ the pressure value is made correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $\mathrm{V}=1$ litre, 10 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and 10 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2$ gas are mixed at temperature $26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then calculate pressure of the gas.
questions.solution:
According to ideal gas equation, $\mathrm{pV}=\mathrm{nRT}$.
Given, $\mathrm{V}=1 \mathrm{~L}, \mathrm{n}_1=10$ mole, $\mathrm{n}_2=10$ mole.
$\mathrm{T}=26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=299 \mathrm{~K} \text {. }$
On substituting the given values in above equation, we get.
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{p} & =\frac{20 \mathrm{~mol} \times 0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 299 \mathrm{~K}}{1 \mathrm{~L}} \\
& =491 \mathrm{~atm} .
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compressibility of a gas is less than unity at STP, therefore
questions.solution:
Since, compressibility $(Z)=\frac{p V}{n R T}$ for $Z$ less than unity $\frac{p V}{n R T} \lt 1$ or, $p V \lt n R T$ [At STP, $p=1$ $\operatorname{atm}, T=273 \mathrm{~K}, R=0.0821 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]
i.e. $V \lt 1 \times 0.0821 \times 273$
Thus, $V \lt 22.4 \mathrm{~L}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The density of a gas is found to be $1.56 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$ at 745 mm pressure and $65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the moleculer mass of the gas?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The average kinetic energy of an ideal gas per molecule in SI units at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be
questions.solution:
Average kinetic energy per molecule $=\frac{3}{2} K T$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{3}{2} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 \\ & =6.17 \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{J} \text { per molecule }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Given that the reduced temperature, $\theta=\frac{T}{T_C}$
the reduced pressure, $\pi=\frac{F}{F_C}$ the reduced volume, $\phi=\frac{V}{V_C}$
Thus, it can be said that the reduced equation of state may be given as
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} p & =p_C \pi=\left(\frac{a}{27 b^2}\right) \pi \\ V & =V_C \phi=(3 b) \phi \\ T & =T_C \theta=\left(\frac{8 a}{27 R b}\right) \theta\end{aligned}$
Thus, substituting in the van der Waals' equation,
$\left(p+\frac{a}{V_2}\right)(V-b)=R T$
or $\quad\left(\pi+\frac{3}{\varphi^2}\right)(3 \varphi-1)=8 \theta$
This is reduced state equation
or, $\quad\left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{1}{\varphi^2}\right)(3 \varphi-1)=\frac{8}{3} \theta$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the temperature of an ideal gas is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the kinetic energy will be
questions.solution:
Increase in temperature of ideal gas from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ 
$\therefore$ Increase in $K E=\frac{3}{2} R T$
$\begin{aligned} T_1 & =27+273 \\ & =300 \mathrm{~K} \\ T_2 & =927+273 \Rightarrow=1200 \mathrm{~K} \\ \therefore \quad K E_1 & =\frac{3}{2} \times R \times 300 \mathrm{~K} \\ K E_2 & =\frac{3}{2} \times R \times 1200 \mathrm{~K} \\ \text { So, } \frac{K E_2}{K E_1} & =\frac{\frac{3}{2} \times R \times 1200 \mathrm{~K}}{\frac{3}{3} \times R \times 300 \mathrm{~K}}=4\end{aligned}$
Hence kinetic energy is increased four times.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Match the particle with its characteristic. $\begin{array}{ll|ll} \hline & \text{Column I} & & \text{Column II} \\ \hline \text{A.} & \alpha\text{-particle} & (p) & \text{Slow moving} \\ \text{B.} & \text{Isobar} & (q) & \text{High penetration power} \\ \text{C.} & \gamma\text{-ray} & (r) & \text{Same atomic mass} \\ \text{D.} & \beta\text{-particle} & (s) & \text{Consists of electron} \\ \hline \end{array}$
questions.solution: $\alpha$-particle is a slow-moving particle as it has high mass and charge.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If two atoms have equal number of electron, it is called
questions.solution: The species having same number of electrons are called isoelectronic. For example, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ have 10 electrons thus are isoelectronic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following set of quantum numbers, the impossible set is
questions.solution:
For each value of $n, l=n-1$ (max.) and for each value of $1, m=-1$ to +1 .
Thus, for $1=2, m=-2,-1,0,1,2$ and $m \neq-3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What will be the number of waves formed by a Bohr electron in one complete revolution in its second orbit?
questions.solution:
Number of waves $=\frac{\text { Circumeference of orbit }}{\text { de }- \text { Broglie wavelength of electron }}$
But,
$\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$
So,
$\lambda=\frac{2 \pi r \times \mu}{h}=\frac{2 \pi}{n} \times m v r$
but
$\begin{aligned} m v r & =\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=\frac{2 \pi}{h} \times \frac{n h}{2 \pi} \\ m v r & =n\end{aligned}$
For second orbit, $n=2$
$\therefore$ Number of waves $=2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Number of photons emitted by a $100 \mathrm{~W}\left(\mathrm{Js}^{-1}\right)$ yellow lamp in 1.0 s is ( $\lambda$ of yellow light is 560 nm )
questions.solution:
As we know that,
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{Nh} \nu=\mathrm{N} \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda} \\ & \mathrm{N}=\frac{\mathrm{E} \lambda}{\mathrm{hc}} \ldots(\mathrm{i})\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{E}=100 \mathrm{~W}\left(\mathrm{Js}^{-1}\right) \times 1 \mathrm{~s}=100 \mathrm{~J}$
Putting the value in eq. (i) we get
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{N} & =\frac{100 \mathrm{~J} \times 560 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js} \times 3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}} \\ & =2.82 \times 10^{20}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not iso electronic with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ ?
questions.solution:
Key Idea Iso electronic species are those which have same number of electrons. The total number of electrons in the given species are as follows
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} & =2+16=18 \text { electrons } \\
\mathrm{F}_2 & =9+9=18 \text { electrons } \\
\mathrm{O}^{2-} & =8+2=10 \text { electrons } \\
\mathrm{Ca}^{2+} & =20-2=18 \text { electrons } \\
\mathrm{Sc}^{3+} & =21-3=18 \text { electrons }
\end{aligned}$
Thus, $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ (oxide ion) is not iso electronic with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In an atom, an electron is moving with a speed of $600 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ with an accuracy of $0.005 \%$. Certainty with which the position of the electron can be located is
(Given, $h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, mass of electron $e_m=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ )
questions.solution:
Given that velocity of electron, $v=600 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Accuracy of velocity $=0.005 \%$
$\therefore \quad \Delta v=\frac{600 \times 0.005}{100}=0.03$
According to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle
$\Delta x . m \Delta v \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}$
Or, $\begin{aligned} \Delta x & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.03} \\ & =1.92 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Match the type of series given in Column I with the wavelength range given in Column II and choose the correct option.
questions.solution:
The correct match is
$(\mathrm{A}) \rightarrow$ Lyman ultra violet $\rightarrow$ (1) Ultraviolet
(B) $\rightarrow$ Paschen $\rightarrow(2) \rightarrow$ Near Infrared
(C) $\rightarrow$ Balmer $\rightarrow$ (4) $\rightarrow$ Visible
(D) $\rightarrow$ Pfund $\rightarrow$ (3) $\rightarrow$ Far infrared

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An electron is moving in Bohr's fourth orbit. Its de-Broglie wave length is $\lambda$. What is the circumference of the fourth orbit?
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's concept, an electron always move in the orbit with angular momentum (mvr) equal to $n h / 2 \pi$.
$\therefore \quad m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$
or $r=\frac{n}{2 \pi} \cdot\left(\frac{h}{m v}\right)$
Or $r=\frac{n \lambda}{2 \pi}$
(from de-Broglie equation, $\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$ )
For fourth orbit $(n=4)$,
$\begin{gathered}r=\frac{2 \lambda}{\pi} \\ \therefore \text { Circumference }=2 \pi r=2 \pi \times \frac{2 \lambda}{\pi}=4 \lambda\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Azimuthal quantum number $(\ell)$ defined
questions.solution:
Azimuthal quantum number ( l ) is also called subsidiary quantum number, denoted by 1 $[\ell=0,1,2,3, \ldots(\mathrm{n}-1)]$
It describes the shape of orbital occupied by the electron.
\begin{array}{ccl}
\hline Value of \boldsymbol{l} & Subshell notation & Shape of orbital \\
\hline 0 & s & Spherical \\
\hline 1 & \rho & Dumb-bell \\
\hline 2 & d except d z^2 & Double dumb-bell \\
\hline 3 & f & Complex \\
\hline
\end{array}

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell of an atom determined by the following?
questions.solution: Maximum number of electrons in a subshell of atom is determined by $4 I+2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following sets of quantum numbers represents the highest energy level in an atom?
questions.solution: According to \((n+l)\) rule, the energy of an orbital depends upon the sum of the values of the principal quantum number (\(n\)) and the azimuthal quantum number (\(l\)). Lower the value of \((n+l)\), lower is the energy. If two different orbitals have the same value of \((n+l)\), the orbital with lower value of \(n\) has lower energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ratio of the difference in energy between the first and the second Bohr orbit to that between the second and the third Bohr orbit is
questions.solution:
Energy of the \(\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}\) Bohr's orbit is given by
\(E_n=-13.6 \times \frac{z^2}{n^2} e V\)
For H -atom, \(\mathrm{Z}=1\)
The difference in energy between the first and the second Bohr orbit:
\(\begin{aligned}
& E_2-E_1=13.6 \times 1^2 \times\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right) \\
& =13.6 \times \frac{3}{4} e V
\end{aligned}\)
The difference in energy between the second and the third Bohr orbit:
\(\begin{aligned}
& E_3-E_2=13.6 \times 1^2 \times\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) \\
& =13.6 \times \frac{5}{36} e V
\end{aligned}\)
Taking the ratio, we get :
\(\frac{E_2-E_1}{E_3-E_2}=\frac{13.6 \times \frac{3}{4}}{13.6 \times \frac{5}{36}}=\frac{27}{5}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $n=6$, the correct sequence for filling of electrons will be
questions.solution:
For $n-6$ olectron aro filled as $6 s \rightarrow 4 f \rightarrow 5 d \rightarrow 6 p$
This is because electron first enters in that orbitals for which $(n+l)$ is lower. In case, if $(n+I)$ is same, it goes first in that orbital for which $n$ is lower.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: ${ }^{19} \mathrm{F}^{-1},~{ }^{16} \mathrm{O}^{-2},~{ }^{20} \mathrm{Ne}$ choose the correct statement
options: All given have equal no of $e^{-}$
questions.solution:
The total number of electrons in the given species are as follows.
$\begin{aligned}
& { }_9^{19} \mathrm{~F}^{-1}=9+1=10 \\
& { }_8^{16} \mathrm{O}^{-2}=8+2=10 ;{ }_{10}^{20} \mathrm{Ne}=10
\end{aligned}$
Thus, all the given statements are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The Vividh Bharti Station of All India Radio, Delhi, broadcasts on a frequency of $1,368 \mathrm{kHz}$ (Kilohertz). Calculate the wavelength $(\lambda)$ of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by transmitter. Which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong to?
questions.solution:
$v=1,368 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\lambda=\frac{C}{v}=\frac{3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}}{1368 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}=219.3 \mathrm{~m}$
The range of wavelength of radiowaves is 100 m to radiation is 10000 m .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A 600 W mercury lamp emits monochromatic radiation of wavelength 331.3 nm . How many photons are emitted from the lamp per second? $\left(h=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{s}\right.$; velocity of light $\left.=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
Given, power $=600 \mathrm{~W}$
$\lambda=331.3 \mathrm{~nm}=331.3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$
Power $=\frac{\text { energy }}{\text { time }}$
$=\frac{n h v}{t}=\frac{n h c}{\lambda t}$
or $\quad n=\frac{\text { power } \times \lambda t}{h c}$
$=\frac{600 \times 331.3 \times 10^{-9} \times 1}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}$
$n=1 \times 10^{21}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following configurations corresponds to element of highest ionisation energy?
questions.solution: The ionisation energy of $1 s^2, 2 s^2, 2 p^3$ configuration is highest due to the presence of half-filled (stable) orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the wavelength (in m ) of a particle of mass $6.62 \times 10^{-29} \mathrm{~g}$ moving with a velocity of $10^3 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ ?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The quantum numbers $+\frac{1}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{2}$ for the electron spin represent
options: rotation of the electron in clockwise and anticlockwise direction respectively
rotation of the electron in anticlockwise and clockwise direction respectively
magnetic moment of the electron pointing up and down respectively
two quantum mechanical spin states which have no classical analogues
questions.solution: The quantum numbers $+\frac{1}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{2}$ indicate the rotation of electrons around its axis in opposite directions. Hence, they represent the two quantum mechanical spin states which have no classical analogues.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelenght of a particle with mass 1 kg and velocity \(100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\) is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hund's rule state that
options: number of two $e^{-}$ can be in two separate orbitals
number of two $e^{-}$c an be present with similar spin in a orbital
no one $e^{-}$ can exist in d orbital
questions.solution: Hund's rule of maximum multiplicity states that, the pairing of electrons in the orbitals of a particular subshell ( p , d or f ) does not take place until all the orbital of a subshell are singly occupied. Thus, all the given statements are not related to Hund's rule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The electrons identified by quantum numbers $n$ and $l$, are as follows
I. $n=4, l=1$
II. $n=4, l=0$
III. $n=3, l=2$
IV. $n=3, l=1$
If we arrange them in order of increasing energy, i.e. from lowest to highest, the correct order is
questions.solution:
(i) $\because$ More be the sum of $n+1 \rightarrow$ more be the energy.
(ii) For same sum, the more value of $n$, have higher energy.
$\because n+1$ for $\rightarrow$
(i) $4+1=5$
(ii) $4+0=4$
(iii) $3+2=5$
(iv) $3+1=4$
$\therefore$ Correct order is $\rightarrow$ (iv) $ \lt $ (ii) $ \lt $ (iii) $ \lt $ (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the wave number of $4^{\text {th }}$ line in Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum?
$\left(R=1,09,677 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}\right. \text { ) }$
questions.solution:
$\bar{v}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
For Balmer series $n_1=2$ and for $4^{\text {th }}$ line in Balmer series $n_2=6$
$\begin{aligned}
\because \quad R & =109677 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\
\bar{v} & =109677\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{6^2}\right) \\
& =109677\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{36}\right) \\
\bar{v} & =24,372 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Neutron is discovered by
questions.solution: Neutron is discovered by Chadwick.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The effective neutron capture radius of a nucleus having a cross-section of 1.0 barr is [Given, 1 barr $=1.0 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ ]
questions.solution:
1.0 barr $=1.0 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^2=10^{-28} \mathrm{~m}^2$
The area of a circle is given by $A=\pi r^2$
Hence, $\begin{aligned} r & =\sqrt{A / \pi} \\ & =\sqrt{\left(1.0 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^2\right) / 3.14} \\ & =5.6 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Cr is member of $3 d$ transition series of atomic number 24. What will be its electronic configuration?
questions.solution: ${ }_{24} \mathrm{Cr} \longrightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5 4 s^1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Dual nature of particle was-given by
questions.solution: Dual nature of particle was given by de-Broglie.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If magnetic quantum number of a given atom represented by -3 , then what will be its principal quantum number?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { If } \quad m=-3 \\ & \therefore \quad l=3 \\ & n=4 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A golf-ball weigh 40.0 g . If it is moving with a velocity of $20.0 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, it's de-Broglie wavelength is
questions.solution:
From de-Broglie equation,
$\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \lambda & =\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{j} . \mathrm{s}}{40 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} \times 20.0 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}} \\ & =8.28 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =8.28 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^9 \mathrm{~nm} \\ & =8.28 \times 10^{-25} \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Number of orbitals in $L$ energy level
questions.solution: It is second energy level. Second energy level contains one $s$-orbital and three $p$ orbitals. Therefore total number of orbital in $L$ level is four.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ratio of radii of second and first Bohr orbits of H atom is
questions.solution:
According to Bohr rule, $r \propto n^2$ where $r$ is radii and $n$ is the number of orbit
So, $\quad \frac{r_1}{r_2}=\frac{(2)^2}{(1)^2}=4$
Hence, ratio of radii is 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Dry ice is
questions.solution: Solid \(\mathrm{CO}_2\) is called ice because it changes to vapour state directly without changing to liquid state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The shape of an orbital is determined by
questions.solution:
The azimuthal quantum number $(l)$ determines the shape of an orbital. For,
$l=0$ (sperical, $s$)
$l=1$ (dumb-bell, $p$) etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ions, has smallest size?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$are isoelctronic species. In isoelectronic ions, size decrease with increasing nuclear charge. Hence, $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ion is the smallest among these.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element ' $Y$ ' emits one $\alpha$-and two $\beta$-particles to give ' X '. $X$ and $Y$ are
questions.solution:
Let atomic mass and atomic number of $Y$ are ' $a$ ' and ' $b$ ' respectively. Hence,
\({ }_b Y^a \xrightarrow{-\alpha}{ }_{b-2} M^{a-4} \xrightarrow{-\beta}{ }_{b-1} N^{a-4}\xrightarrow{-\beta}{ }_b X^{a-4}\)
Hence, $X$ and $Y$ are isotopes (i.e., having equal atomic number but differing in mass number)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What are the values of $n_1$ and $n_2$ respectively for $\mathrm{H}_\beta$ line in the Lyman series of hydrogen atomic spectrum 44 ?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_\beta$ line is formed when electron jumps from $3 r d$ orbit to Ist orbit in Lyman series.
$\therefore \quad n_1=1, n_2=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Heisenberg uncertainty principle can be explained as
questions.solution:
According to Heisenberg uncertainty principle, the product of uncertainty in momentum ( \(\Delta P\) ) and in position ( \(\Delta x)\) remains constant.
\(\Delta x \times \Delta P \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of electrons, neutrons and protons in a species are equal to 10,8 and 8 respectively. The proper symbol of the species is
questions.solution: From the given data, it is clear that the atomic number, $Z$ or number of protons of the species is 8 . Number of neutrons is also 8, hence atomic mass is 16. Since, the number of electrons are two more than the number of protons, hence it is a binegative species. Thus, the species is ${ }_8^{16} \mathrm{O}^{2-}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which is the shortest wavelength line in the Lyman series of the hydrogen spectrum?
$\text { ( } R=1.097 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~nm}^{-1} \text { ) }$
questions.solution:
For Lyman series, $\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{(1)^2}-\frac{1}{n^2}\right)$ hence, for $\lambda$ to be smallest, $n$ should be greatest (i.e., $\infty$ )
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_{\min }}=1.097 \times 10^{-2}\left(1-\frac{1}{\infty^2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & =1.097 \times 10^{-2} \\ \text { or } \quad \lambda_{\min } & =\frac{1}{1.097 \times 10^{-2}}=91.16 \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The total number of orbitals in the fifth energy level is
questions.solution:
The total number of atomic orbitals (total $m$ ) in $n$th shell is equal to $n^2$.
So, in the fifth shell $=5^2=25$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The atomic number of an element ' $M$ ' is 26. How many electrons are present in the $M$-shell of the element in its $M^{3+}$ state ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l|c|c|c|c} 
& K & L & M & N \\
\hline M (26) & 1 s^2, & 2 s^2 2 p^6, & 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^6 & 4 s^2 \\
M { }^{3+} & 1 s^2, & 2 s^2 2 p^6, & 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^5, & 4 s^0
\end{array}$
Hence, 13 electrons are present in the $M$-shell of $M^{3+}$ state.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Red blood cells are placed in a solution and neither haemolysis nor crenation occurs. Therefore, the solution is
questions.solution: When red blood cells are placed in a solution and neither haemolysis nor crenation occurs. This means that solution is isotonic in nature, since in isotonic solution, there is no process of osmosis takes place.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $x$ is amount of adsorbate and $m$ is amount of adsorbent, which of the following relations is not related to adsorption process?
questions.solution: $\frac{x}{m}=p \times T$ is the incorrect relation. The correct relation is $\frac{x}{m} \propto \frac{p}{T}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a surfactant?
questions.solution:
Every surfactant contains two parts a hydrophobic part and a hydrophilic part. The compound $\mathrm{CH}_3\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{14} \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2$ contains only hydrophobic part but no hydrophilic part, hence, it is not a surfactant.
All other options are surfactant
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{CH}_3-\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{15}-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{N}}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{Br}^{-} \\ \mathrm{CH}_3-\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{16}-\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OSO}_2^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+} \\ \mathrm{OHC}-\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_{14}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{COO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following represents physical adsorption?
questions.solution: The physical adsorption isobar shows a decrease in $x / m$ throughout with rise in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following curves is in accordance with Freundlich adsorption isotherm?
questions.solution: According to Freundlich adsorption isotherm, a graph between $\log \frac{x}{m}$ against $\log p$ is a straight lline with slope equal to $1 / n$ and intercept equal to $\log k$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an experiment, addition of 4.0 mL of 0.005 M $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ to 16.0 mL of arsenious sulphide sol just causes complete coagulation in 2 h . The flocculating value of the effective ion is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$ sol is negatively charged owing to preferential adsorption of $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ ions. Cation would be effective in causing coagulation.
Flocculating value $=$ minimum millimole of the effective ion per litre of sol
$=\frac{4 \times 0.005}{(4+16) \times 10^{-3}}=1.0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not applicable to the phenomenon of adsorption?
questions.solution: Due to adsorption, attractive forces increases, thus $\Delta H \lt 0$, i.e $(-)$ ve.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Butter is a colloidal solution of
questions.solution: In butter, the dispersed phase is liquid and dispersion medium is solid. This type of colloid is called get. It is called liquid-solid emulsion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Cow milk, an example of natural emulsion, is stabilised by
questions.solution:
Cow milk an example of a neutral emulsion is stabilized by casein.
Cow milk is liquid fat particles dispersed in water. It is oil in water type emulsion. Casein is protective colloid with gold number \(0.01-0.02\).
Casein is a phosphoprotein which occurs in the milk of mammals as a suspension of calcium caseinate. It is the main protein of milk.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of phase in a colloidal system is
questions.solution: Colloidal solution is of heterogeneous nature. It consists of two phases ie, a dispersed phase and a dispersion medium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is correct?
options: Particle size of solute in true solution is $10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$
Hydrolysis of liquid ester in the presence of mineral acid is an example of heterogeneous catalysis reactions
questions.solution: Starch solution is a hydrosol as it forms colloidal solution with water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Gold number is associated with
questions.solution: The protective power of a colloid is expressed as gold number.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statement is not correct?
options: Adsorption energy for chemical adsorption is generally greater than that of physical adsorption
Physical adsorption is due to van der Waal forces
Physical adsorption decreases at high temperature and low pressure
questions.solution: Physical adsorption is due to van der Waal's forces which are weak forces, hence this type of adsorption is reversible and not irreversible. All other statements are correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following forms micelles in aqueous solution above certain concentration?
questions.solution: Surfactants detergents form micelles in aqueous solution above to their C.M.C (critical micelle concentration). Dodecyl trimethyl ammonium chloride is an example of surfactant (cationic surfactant), so it shows a following phenomena.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bleeding due to a cut can be stopped by applying ferric chloride solution in the laboratory. This is due to
options: coagulation of negatively charged blood particles by Fe ${ }^{3+}$ ions.
coagulation of positively charged blood particles by $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions.
reaction taking place between ferric ions and the haemoglobin forming a complex.
common element, iron, in both $\mathrm{FeCl}_3$ and haemoglobin.
questions.solution: Bleeding due to a cut can be stopped by applying $\mathrm{FeCl}_3$ or alum solution. This is due to coagulation of negatively charged colloidal blood particles (albuminoid substance) by positively charged $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions. These substances are used as styptic (which check the flow of blood).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is an example for heterogeneous catalysis reaction?
options: Hydrolysis of aqueous sucrose solution in the presence of a aqueous mineral acid
Hydrolysis of liquid in the presence of aqueous mineral acid
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2(I) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{s})} 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{I})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})$
In this reaction, reactant and catalyst are in different phase, hence it is an example of heterogeneous catalysis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On the basis of Langmuir adsorption isotherm the amount of gas adsorbed at very high pressure.
options: First increasing and then decreasing with pressure
questions.solution: According to Langmuir adsorption isotherm the amount of gas adsorbed at very high pressure reaches a constant limiting value.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the presence of a catalyst, the heat evolved or absorbed during the reacton
questions.solution: A catalyst does not alter the enthalpy change, $\Delta H$ of the reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solvent loving colloids are known as
questions.solution: Solvent loving colloids are called lyophilic colloids. These are natural, reversible colloids which easily form colloidal solution e.g., albumin, gelatin, gum-arabic etc

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Conversion of oxygen into ozone is non-spontaneous at
questions.solution:
Ozone is not stable at high temperature.
So, it decomposes into oxygen at high temperature.
$2 \mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{O}_2$
Thus, the reverse of this reaction is non-spontaneous at high temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A hypothetical reaction $A \longrightarrow 2 B$ proceeds through the following sequence of steps
I. $A \longrightarrow C ; \Delta_r H=q_1$
II. $C \longrightarrow D ; \Delta_r H=q_2$
III. $\frac{1}{2} D \longrightarrow B ; \Delta_r H=q_3$
The heat of hypothetical reaction is
questions.solution:
One mole of $A$ as reactant So, we write down equation
(i) Two moles of $B$ as product, so we multiply equation (iii) by (b). Finally, we add the three equation term-by-term
$\begin{array}{ll}A \longrightarrow C & q_1 \\ C \longrightarrow D & q_2 \\ D \longrightarrow 2 B & 2 q_3 \\ A \longrightarrow 2 B & q_1+q_2+2 q_3\end{array}$
Therefore, the heat of hypothetical reaction is $q_1+q_2+2 q_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In adiabatic conditions, 2 mole of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ gas at 300 K is expanded such that its volume becomes 27 times. Then, the work done is $\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=6 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right.$ and $\left.\gamma=1.33\right)$
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{T}_1=300 \mathrm{~K}, \mathrm{~V}_2=27 \mathrm{~V}_1, \mathrm{n}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
$C_V=6 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}, \gamma=1.33$.
In adiabatic conditions
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}$
or, $\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{1.33-1}$
$=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{0.33}=\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}=\frac{1}{3}$
or, $\mathrm{T}_2=300 \times \frac{1}{3}=100 \mathrm{~K}$
Thus, $\mathrm{T}_2 \lt \mathrm{T}_1$ hence, cooling takes place due to expansion under adiabatic condition.
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$
$\Delta \mathrm{E} \neq \mathrm{W} \quad(\mathrm{q}=0$ for adiabatic change $)$
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=-$ ve because gas expands.
Then, $\quad \mathrm{W}=-\Delta \mathrm{E}=-\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}\left(\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right)$
$=-6(100-300)=1200 \mathrm{cal}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the value of $\gamma$ for mono atomic gas (ideal gas)?
questions.solution:
Key Idea $\gamma=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}} / \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}$ where, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$ are heat capacities at constant pressure and volume, respectively.
For monoatomic gas, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=5 \mathrm{cal}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=3 \mathrm{cal}$
$\gamma=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{5}{3}=1.67$
For diatomic gas, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=7 \mathrm{cal}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=5 \mathrm{cal}$
$_{\mathrm{V}}=6 \mathrm{cal}$
$\gamma=\frac{C_P}{C_V}=\frac{8}{6}=1.33$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the heat of reaction at constant pressure is $-2.5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{cal}$ and entropy change is $7.4$ $\mathrm{cal} \mathrm{deg}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the reaction is predicted as
questions.solution:
For the reaction at constant pressure,
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta H=-2.5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{cal} \\
& \Delta S=7.4 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{deg}^{-1} \\
& \therefore \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S=(-) 2.5 \times 10^3-298 \times 7.4 \\
& =(-) 2.5 \times 10^3-2205.2 \\
& \Delta G=-4705 \mathrm{cal} \\
&\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Reaction is spontaneous.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
At $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, one mole of an ideal gas is compressed isothermally and reversibly from a pressure of 2 atm to 10 atm .
Choose the correct option from the following.
options: Change in internal energy is positive
questions.solution:
Work done in isothermal reversible process is
$w=-2.303 n R T \log _{10} \frac{p_1}{p_2}$
Given,
$\begin{aligned} n & =1, R=2 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ T & =(27+273) \mathrm{K}=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ p_1 & =2 \mathrm{~atm}, p_2=10 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad w & =-2.303 \times 1 \times 2 \times 300 \log _{10} \frac{2}{10} \\ w & =+965.54 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$
For isothermal change, $\Delta U=0$
Now, from first law of thermodynamics,
$\begin{aligned} & q=\Delta U-W=0-965.84 \mathrm{cal} \\ & q=-965.84 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Given that, for the reaction,
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}(\mathrm{HCl})=-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ & \text { B.E }\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right)=434 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\ & \text { B.E }\left(\mathrm{Cl}_2\right)=242 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}\end{aligned}$
The bond dissociation energy of HCl is
questions.solution:
Given,
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{HCl}, \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}=-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$\because$ In this reaction, one mole of HCl is formed
$\therefore \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}$ of the reaction will remain same, i.e. $-93 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
Let the bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond be x
For the given reaction,
$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}} & =\Sigma \mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\text {(reactants) }}-\Sigma \mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\text {(products) }} \\ -93 & =\frac{1}{2}(434)+\frac{1}{2}(242)-\mathrm{x} \\ -93 & =+338-\mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{X} & =431 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If equilibrium constant is $2.6 \times 10^8$ at 298 K then find the value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If a gas expands at constant temperature indicates that
options: kinetic energy of molecules remains as same
number of the molecules of gas increase
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}\because \quad \mathrm{KE}=\frac{3}{2} R T \\ \mathrm{KE} \propto T\end{gathered}$
Here, the temperature of gas remains constant hence kinetic energy of molecules remains the same.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard enthalpies of formation of $A\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right), B\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right), C(\mathrm{HI})$ and $D\left(\mathrm{SO}_2\right)$ are -46.19 , $-393.4,+24.94$ and $-296.9 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The increasing order of their stability is
questions.solution: Lower the value of standard enthalpy of formation, higher the stability. The increasing order of standard enthalpies of formation of given molecules is Thus, the increasing order of stability is $C \lt A \lt D \lt B$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the process to occur under adiabatic conditions, the correct condition is
questions.solution: Adiabatic process is a process in which there is no transfer of heat between the system and surroundings, $(q=0)$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the reaction,
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
at 373 K and 1 atm pressure
questions.solution:
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \cdot \Delta S$
For the reaction,
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$
$\Delta G=0$
(at equilibrium)
$\therefore \quad \Delta H=T \cdot \Delta S$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_3(g) ; K_p$ for this reaction is $2.47 \times 10^{-29}$. At $298 \mathrm{~K}, \Delta_r G^{\circ}$ for conversion of oxygen to ozone will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta_r G^{\circ}= & 2.303 \operatorname{log~Kp}\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \\ \Delta_r G^{\circ}= & -2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \times \log 2.47 \times 10^{-29} \\ = & 163000 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1}=163 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: According to the first law of thermodynamics which of the following quantities represents the change in a state function?
questions.solution:
$\Delta E=q_{\text {rev }}+W_{\text {rev }}$
$\Delta E$ is a state function.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The heat of formation of $\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})$ and $\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ are $\Delta H=-110$ and $\Delta H=-393 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively. What is the heat of reaction $(\Delta H)$ (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) for the following reaction?
$\mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The change in entropy for the fusion of 1 mole of ice is [mp of ice \(=273 \mathrm{~K}\), molar enthalpy of fusion for ice \(=6.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\) ]
questions.solution:
Entropy change of fusion
\(\begin{gathered}
\Delta S_f=\frac{\Delta H_f}{T} \\
\Delta H_f=60 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J}, \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, T=273 \mathrm{~K} \\
\Delta S_f=\frac{60 \times 10^3}{273} \\
=21.97 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{gathered}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A reaction occurs spontaneously if
options: $T \Delta S \lt \Delta H$ and both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are + ve
$T \Delta S\gt\Delta H$ and both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are + ve
$T \Delta S=\Delta H$ and both $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are + ve
$T \Delta S\gt\Delta H$ and $\Delta H$ is + ve and $\Delta S$ is - ve
questions.solution:
The spontaneity of reaction is based upon the negative value of $\Delta G, \Delta G$ is based upon $T, \Delta S$ and $\Delta H$ according to following equation (Gibbs-Helmholtz equation)
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
If the magnitude of $\Delta H-T \Delta S$ is negative, then the reaction is spontaneous.
when $T \Delta S\gt\Delta H$ and $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are + ve, then $\Delta G$ is negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following pairs of a chemical reaction is certain to result in a spontaneous reaction?
questions.solution:
If reaction is exothermic, therefore, $\Delta H$ is - ve and on increasing disorder, $\Delta S$ is + ve thus, at these condition, $\Delta G$ is negative according to following equation.
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
$\Delta G=-$ ve. Hence, for spontaneous reaction $\Delta G$ must be - ve.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The entropy values (in $\mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})=130.6, \quad \mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g})=223.0 \quad$ and $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})=186.7$ at 298 K and 1 atm pressure. Then entropy change for the reaction :
$\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{Cl}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$ is :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta S^{\circ} & =2 S^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{HCl}}-\left(S_{\mathrm{H}_2}^{\circ}+S^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{Cl}_2}\right) \\ & =2 \times 186.7-(130.6+223.0) \\ & =19.8 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\mathrm{C}_{\text {(dia) }}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H=-395.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{gr})}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\mathrm{C}_{(\mathrm{gr})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\text {(dia) }} ; \Delta H=$ ?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{C}($ dia $)+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2$
$(\Delta \mathrm{H}=-395.4 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole})$...(1)
$\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{gr})+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2$
$(\Delta \mathrm{H}=-393.5 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole})$...(2)
subtracting eq. (1) from (2), we have
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{gr}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\text {(dia) }} ; \\ & \begin{aligned}(\Delta \mathrm{H} & =+1.9 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mole}) \\ & \quad[\because \Delta \mathrm{H}=-393.5-(-395.4) \\ & \left.=+1.9 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The internal energy of a substance
options: remains unaffected with change in temperature
questions.solution: The internal energy of a substance always increases with increase in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an isothermal process
questions.solution:
In an isothermal process, the temperature is constant.
So, $\quad \Delta E=0$
Hence $\Delta H=P \Delta V$ (As, $\Delta H=\Delta E+P \Delta V$ )
$\Delta H=W$ $\qquad (\because W=P \Delta V)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the following reaction, Then, $\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{e} 0}$ for this equilibrium is
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ} & =-13.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{~T} & =298 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$
For equilibrium, the Gibb's free energy is calculated by using the formula.
$\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-2.303 \mathrm{RT} \log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}$
or, $\log \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}=-\frac{\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}}{2.303 \mathrm{RT}}$
$=-\frac{-13.6 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{2.303 \times 8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \times 298 \mathrm{~K}}=2.38$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If for a given substance, melting point is $T_B$ and freezing point is $T_A$ then correct variation of entropy by graph between entropy change and temperature is
questions.solution: For a pure substance $T_A$ and $T_B$ are same. Above to this temperature, entropy will increase and below this temperature entropy remains constant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What would be the heat released when an aqueous solution containing 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ is mixed with 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$?
(enthalpy of neutralisation is -57.1 kJ )
questions.solution: 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ equivalents to 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$. But only 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions are available. Thus, 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions will combine with 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions to form 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ and 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions will remain unreacted. Heat evolved when 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions combine with 1 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions $=57.1 \mathrm{~kJ}$. Therefore, heat evolved wher 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions combine with 0.3 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions $=0.3 \times 57.1=17.13 \mathrm{~kJ}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The energy released when 6 moles of octane is burnt in air will be [Given, $\Delta H_f^{\cdot}$ for $\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$, $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})$ and $\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}(\mathrm{l})$, respectively are -490 , -240 and $+160 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}]$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H=-490 \mathrm{~J}....(i) \\ \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \Delta H=-240 \mathrm{~J}....(ii) \\ 8 \mathrm{C}+9 \mathrm{H}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18} ; \Delta H=+160 \mathrm{~J}....(iii)\end{aligned}$
On applying, $8 x$ Eq. (i) $+9 x$ Eq. (ii)-Eq. (iii), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}+\frac{25}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8 \mathrm{CO}_2+9 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \Delta H_R^0=[8 \times(-490)]+[9 \times(-240)]+160
\end{aligned}$
$=-5920 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Hence, energy exchange when 6 moles of octane is burnt in air $=-5920 \times 6=-35520 \mathrm{~J}=-35.5$ kJ

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the $\Delta H$ of the reaction The average bond energies of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond and $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond are 416 kJ and $325 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The enthalpy of combustion at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ of $\mathrm{H}_2$, cyclohexane $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12}\right)$ and cyclohexene $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10}\right)$ are $-241,-3920$ and $-3800 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. The heat of hydrogenation of cyclohexene is :
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \Delta H=-241 \mathrm{~kJ} . .\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10}+\frac{17}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_2+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ \Delta H=-3800 \mathrm{~kJ} \ldots \text { (ii) }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12}+9 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow & 6 \mathrm{CO}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \Delta H=-3920 \mathrm{~kJ} \ldots \text { (iii) }\end{aligned}$
hydrogenation of cyclohexene-
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10}+\mathrm{H}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \\ & \mathrm{Eq} \text { (i) } \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Eq} & \text { (ii) }-\mathrm{Eq} \text { (iii) } \\ \Delta H & =-241-3800-(-3920) \\ & =-4041+3920 \\ & =-121 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The absolute enthalpy of neutralisation of the reaction $\mathrm{MgO}(s)+2 \mathrm{HCl}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgCl}_2(a q)+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l)$ will be
questions.solution: Heat of neutralisation of strong acid and strong base is $-57.33$ kJ . MgO is weak base while HCI is strong acid, so the heat of neutralisation of MgO and HCI is lower to -57.33 kJ because MgO requires some heat in ionisation, then net released amount of heat is decreased.

questions.subject: Physics
