questions.q: A transformer having efficiency of $75 \%$ is working on 220 V and 4.4 kW power supply. If the current in the secondary coil is 5 A . What will be the voltage across secondary coil and the current in primary coil?
questions.solution:
Given, $P_{\text {in }}=4.4 \mathrm{~kW}, V_p=220 \mathrm{~V}, i_s=5 \mathrm{~A}, V_s=$ ?
We know that, $\quad i_P=\frac{P_{\text {in }}}{V_P}=\frac{4.4 \times 10^3}{220}=20 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore$ Efficiency, $\eta=\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }}$
Percentage efficiency $\eta=\frac{P_{\text {out }}}{P_{\text {in }}} \times 100$
$\begin{aligned} 75 \% & =100 \times \frac{P_{\text {out }}}{4.4 \times 10^3} \\ \therefore \quad P_{\text {out }} & =\frac{75 \times 4.4 \times 10^3}{100} \\ & =3300 \mathrm{~W} \\ \therefore \quad P_{\text {out }} & =V_S \cdot i_S \\ \Rightarrow \quad V_S & =\frac{3300}{5}=660 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If we change the value of $R$, then
options: voltage does not change on $L C$ combination
questions.solution: If we change the value of $R$, then voltage on $L C$ combination changes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find resonance frequency in the given circuit
questions.solution:
In the given circuit diagram, both inductors of inductance L are parallel to each other, hence $\mathrm{L}_{\text {eg }}=\frac{\mathrm{L}_1 \mathrm{~L}_2}{\mathrm{~L}_1+\mathrm{L}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{L} . \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}+\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2}$ Similarly, $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eg}}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C}=2 \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore$ Resonance frequency
$\omega_1=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{cg}} \cdot \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{cg}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2} \cdot 2 \mathrm{C}}}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\mathrm{LC}}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the current drawn from the battery just after the switch is closed.
questions.solution: Just after closing the switch, inductor behaves as a open circuit, hence no current will flow in the inductor branch just after closing the switch. Hence, circuit diagram is redrawn as Current, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric bulb has a rated power of 50 W at 100 V . If it is used on an AC source $200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$, a choke has to be used in series with it. This choke should have an inductance of
questions.solution:
Resistance of bulb
$R=\frac{V^2}{P}=\frac{(100)^2}{50}$
$=200 \Omega$
Current through bulb $(I)=\frac{V}{R}$
$=\frac{100}{200}=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$
In a circuit containing inductive reactance $\left(X_L\right)$ and resistance $(R)$, impedance $(Z)$ of the circuit is
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}$ ...(i)
Here, $\quad Z=\frac{200}{0.5}=400 \Omega$
Now, $\quad X_L^2=Z^2-R^2$
$=(400)^2-(200)^2$
$(2 \pi f L)^2=12 \times 10^4$
$L=\frac{2 \sqrt{3} \times 100}{2 \pi \times 50}$
$=\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{\pi}=1.1 \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $220 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}, \mathrm{AC}$ source is connected to an inductance of 0.2 H and a resistance of $20 \Omega$ in series. What is the current in the circuit?
questions.solution:
From the formula
$\begin{aligned} I & =\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2+(\omega L)^2}} \\ I & =\frac{220}{\sqrt{(20)^2+(2 \pi \times 50 \times 0.2)^2}} \\ & =\frac{220}{66}=3.33 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An $L-C-R$ series circuit with a resistance of $100 \Omega$ is connected to 200 V (AC source) and angular frequency $300 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. When only the capacitor is removed, then the current lags behind the voltage by $60^{\circ}$. When only the inductor is removed the current leads the voltage by $60^{\circ}$. The average power dissipated in original $L-C-R$ circuit is
questions.solution:
Given, $R=100 \Omega, V=200 \mathrm{~V}, \theta=60^{\circ}, \omega=300 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Phase angle, $\tan \theta=\frac{X_L}{R}=\frac{X_C}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{X_L}{R}=\frac{X_C}{R}$
$\therefore \quad X_L=X_C=\sqrt{3} R$
i.e.
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+(\sqrt{3} R-\sqrt{3} R)^2}=R$
So average power,
$P=\frac{V^2}{R}=\frac{200 \times 200}{100}=400 \mathrm{~W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Real power consumption in a circuit is least when it contains.
questions.solution:
Power consumption $=I_{\text {rms }} V_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi$ where, $\cos \phi$ is power factor.
$\cos \phi=\frac{R}{Z}$
For $\cos \phi$ to be minimum, $R$ should be minimum and $Z$ should be higher. So, higher $Z$ (impedance), $L$ (inductance) should be high.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Voltage in the secondary coil of a transformer does not depend upon
options: ratio of number of turns in the two coils
questions.solution:
The voltage in primary and secondary coil is related as:
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{\text { no. of turns in secondary coil }\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{s}}\right)}{\text { no. of turns in primary coil }\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{p}}\right)}$
Clearly, voltage in secondary coil depends on voltage in primary coil and number of turns in primary and secondary coil. It is independent of frequency of the source.
Hence, option 1 is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a series $L-C-R$ circuit, resistance $R=10 \Omega$ and the impedance $Z=10 \Omega$. The phase difference between the current and the voltage is
questions.solution:
Impedance,
$\begin{aligned}
Z & =\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2} \\
\therefore \quad 10 & =\sqrt{(10)^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad 100 & =100+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2 \\
\Rightarrow X_L-X_C & =0....(i)
\end{aligned}$
Let $\phi$ is the phase difference between current and voltage
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore & & \tan \phi & =\frac{X_L-X_C}{R} \\
\therefore & & \tan \phi & =\frac{0}{R} \\
& \Rightarrow & \phi & =0
\end{aligned}$
[from Eq. (i)]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When power is drawn from the secondary coil of the transformer, the dynamic resistance
questions.solution: When the secondary coil circuit is open, the magnetic flux in the core is produced by the primary current only. When the secondary circuit is closed, the currents in the secondary coil also produce magnetic flux in the core but in opposite direction. This decreases the core flux and hence reduces the back emf produced in the primary coil. The source emf is now in excess of the back emf, more current is drawn in the primary coil. Hence, power factor is no longer zero. The power factor has increased or the phase difference is no longer $90^{\circ}$, ie, phase difference has decreased. Thus, dynamic resistance has increased.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer is used to light a 100 W and 110 V lamp from a 220 V mains. If the main current is 0.5 A , the efficiency of the transformer is approximately
questions.solution:
The efficiency of transformer
$=\frac{\text { Energy obtained from the secondary coil }}{\text { Energy given to the primary coil }}$
or
$\begin{array}{lrl}
\text { or } & \eta & =\frac{\text { Output power }}{\text { Input power }} \\
\text { or } & \eta & =\frac{V_s I_s}{V_p I_p} \\
\text { Given, } & V_s I_s & =100 \mathrm{~W}, V_p=220 \mathrm{~V}, I_p=0.5 \mathrm{~A} \\
\text { Hence, } & \eta & =\frac{100}{220 \times 0.5}=0.90=90 \%
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The primary and secondary coils of a transformer have 50 and 1500 turns respectively. If the magnetic flux $\phi$ linked with the primary coil is given by $\phi=\phi_0+4 t$, where $\phi$ is in weber, $t$ is time in second and $\phi_0$ is a constant, the output voltage across the secondary coil is
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the primary $c$ is given by
$\phi=\phi_0+4 t$
So, voltage across primary
$\begin{aligned}
V_p & =\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(\phi_0+4 t\right) \\
& =4 \mathrm{volt} \quad\left(\text { as } \phi_0=\right.\text { consta }
\end{aligned}$
Also, we have
$N_p=50 \text { and } N_s=1500$
From relation,
or
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_s}{V_p} & =\frac{N_s}{N_p} \\
V_s & =V_p \frac{N_s}{N_p} \\
& =4\left(\frac{1500}{50}\right) \\
& =120 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a circuit, $L, C$ and $R$ are connected in series with an alternating voltage source of frequency $f$. The current leads the voltage by $45^{\circ}$. The value of $C$ is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \tan \phi=\frac{X_C-X_L}{R} \\
& \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\frac{1}{\omega C}-\omega L}{R} \\
& R=\frac{1}{\omega C}-\omega L \\
& (R+2 \pi f L)=\frac{1}{2 \pi f C} \\
& \text {or } C=\frac{1}{2 \pi f(R+2 \pi f L)}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An L.C. circuit is in the state of resonance. If $C=0.1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $L=0.25$ Henry. Neglecting ohmic resistance of circuit. What is the frequency of oscillations?
questions.solution:
From the formula, the frequency of oscillation is
$\begin{aligned} f & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}} \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{0.25 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}} \\ & =1007 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transmitting station transmits radiowaves of wavelength 360 m . Calculate the inductance of coil required with a condenser of capacity $1.20 \mu \mathrm{F}$ in the resonant circuit to receive them
questions.solution:
The frequency of radiowaves (speed $c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) transmitted
$f=\frac{c}{\lambda}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{360}=8.3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Frequency of series resonant circuit
$\begin{aligned}f & =\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}} \\\text { or } \quad L & =\frac{1}{(2 \pi f)^2 C}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{\left(2 \times 3.14 \times 8.3 \times 10^5\right)^2 \times 1.20 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =3.07 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{H}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The peak value of alternating current is $5 \sqrt{2}$ ampere. The mean square value of current will be
questions.solution:
Here: $I_0=5 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~A}$
Root mean square value of current
$I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}=5 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lamp in which 10 amp current can flow at 15 V, is connected with an alternating source of potential 220 V. The frequency of source is 50 Hz. The inductance of choke coil required to light the bulb is
questions.solution:
The resistance of lamp
$R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{15}{10}=1.5 \Omega$
The impedance of circuit
$Z=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{220}{10}=22 \mathrm{ohm}$
Again impedance $Z=\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}$
or $\quad 22=\sqrt{(1.5)^2+(2 \times \pi \times 50)^2 L^2}$
or $\quad(22)^2=(1.5)^2+4 \pi^2 \times 50^2 \times L^2$
or $\quad L=0.07$ Henry

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a L-C circuit, angular frequency at resonance is $\omega$. What will be the new angular frequency when inductor's inductance is made two times and capacitor's capacitance is made four times?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Angular frequency at resonance,
$\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$  ...(i)
According to question, when inductor's inductance is made 2 times and capacitance is 4 times, then
$\omega^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
or $\quad \omega^{\prime}=\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$
$=\frac{\omega}{2 \sqrt{2}} \quad$ [From Eq. (i)]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor $(L=20 \mathrm{H})$, a resistor $(R=100 \Omega)$ and a battery $(E=10 \mathrm{~V})$ are connected in series. After a long time, the circuit is short-circuited and then the battery is disconnected. Find the current in the circuit at 1 ms after short circuiting.
questions.solution:
The initial current $i=i_0=\frac{E}{R}=\frac{10}{100}$
$=0.10 \mathrm{~A}$
the time constant, $\tau=\frac{L}{R}=\frac{20 \mathrm{mH}}{100 \Omega}$
$=0.20 \mathrm{~ms}$
The current at $t=1 \mathrm{~ms}$ is
$\begin{aligned} i & =i_0 e^{-t / \tau} \\ & =(0.10) e^{\left(\frac{-1 \mathrm{~ms}}{0.20 \mathrm{~ms}}\right)} \\ & =(0.10) e^5=6.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A choke is preferred to a resistance for limiting current in AC circuit because
options: there is no wastage of power
choke is a good absorber of heat
questions.solution:
In an AC circuit, the coil of high inductance and negligible resistance used to control current, is called the choke coil. The power factor of such a coil is given by
$\cos \phi=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}}$
$=\frac{R}{\omega L} \quad($ as $R \ll \lt \omega L)$
As $R \ll \omega L, \cos \phi$ is very small. Thus, the power absorbed by the coil is very small. The only loss of energy is due to hysteresis in the iron core, which is much less than the loss of energy in the resistance that can also reduce the current if placed instead of the choke coil.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a $L-C-R$ circuit, the capacitance is made one-fourth, then what should be change in inductance, so that the circuit remains in resonance?
questions.solution:
Resonance frequency $f=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}$
For the circuit to remain in resonance, $\sqrt{L C}=$ constant
$\begin{aligned} or \quad L_1 C_1 & =L_2 C_2 \\ or \quad L C & =L_2 \frac{C}{4} \\ \therefore L_2 & =4 L\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Inductance should be change to four times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $L-C-R$ circuit inductance is changed from $L$ to $L / 2$. To keep the same resonance frequency, $C$ should be changed to
questions.solution:
$v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}$
When $L$ is changed to $L / 2, C$ must be changed to 2 C to keep $v$ same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are the wavelengths of the first members of the Lyman and Paschen series respectively, then $\lambda_1: \lambda_2$ is
questions.solution:
For first line of Lyman series,
$n_1=1$ and $n_2=2$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right)=R\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{3 R}{4}$
For first line of Paschen series
$n_1=3$ and $n_2=4$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=R\left(\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}\right)$
$=\frac{7 R}{144}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{7 R}{144} \times \frac{4}{3 R}=\frac{7}{108}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The specific charge of a proton is $9.6 \times 10^7 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$. The specific charge of an alpha particle will be
questions.solution:
Specific charge on proton $=\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_p$
$=9.6 \times 10^7 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$
Specific charge on $\alpha$-particle,
$\left(\frac{q}{m}\right)_\alpha=\frac{2 e}{4 m}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{e}{m}\right)_p=\frac{1}{2} \times 9.6 \times 10^7$
$=4.8 \times 10^7 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: According to Bohr's model of hydrogen atom, relation between principal quantum number $n$ and radius of stable orbit is
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's model, relation between principal quantum number ( $n$ ) and radius of stable orbit $(r)$ are related as
$r \propto n^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An energy of 68.0 eV is required to excite a hydrogen-like atom in its second Bohr energy level to third energy level the charge of nucleus is Ze . The wavelength of a radiation required to eject the electron from first orbit to infinity is
questions.solution:
Given, $n_1=2, n_2=3$ and $\Delta E=68 \mathrm{eV}$
The difference in energies of two orbits
$\Delta E=13.6 Z^2\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]$
where, $n_1 \lt n_2$
$\begin{aligned} 68 & =13.6 Z^2\left[\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right] \\ & =13.6 Z^2\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right] \\ & =13.6 Z^2\left[\frac{9-4}{36}\right] \\ & =13.6 Z^2 \times \frac{5}{36}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad Z^2 & =\frac{68 \times 36}{13.6 \times 5}=36 \\ Z & =6\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Wavelength of photon, $\frac{1}{\lambda}=Z^2 R\left[\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right]$
$n_1=1$ and $n_2=\infty$
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=6^2 \times R\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]=36 R$
$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{1}{36 R}=\frac{1}{36 \times 1.097 \times 10^7}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{10^{-7}}{39.5} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =0.025 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =2.5 \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of hydrogen is about $-3.4$ eV . Its kinetic energy in this state is
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy is equal to the negative of total energy.
Kinetic energy of electron $K=\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$
Potential energy of electron $U=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Z e^2}{r}$
:. Total energy
$E=K+U=\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}-\frac{Z e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { or } E=-\frac{Z e^2}{8 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \\ & \text { or }  E=-K \\ & \text { or }  K=-E=-(-3.4)=3.4 \mathrm{eV} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential of an atom is given by $V=V_0 \log _e\left(r / r_0\right)$ where $r_0$ is a constant and $r$ is the radius of the orbit. Assuming Bohr's model to be applicable, which variation of $r_n$ with $n$ is possible ( $n$ being principal quantum number)?
questions.solution:
Given that, $V=V_0 \log _e\left(r / r_0\right)$
Field $E=-\frac{d V}{d r}=-V_0\left(\frac{r}{r_0}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & e E=\frac{m V^2}{r} \text { or } \frac{e V_0 I_0}{r}=\frac{m V^2}{r} \\ & V=\left(\frac{e V_0 I_0}{m}\right)^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$
$m V=\left(m_e V_0 r_0\right)^{1 / 2}=$ constant
$\therefore m v r=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}$, Bohr's quantum condition 
or $r \propto n$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy of an electron, which is accelerated in the potential difference of 100 V , is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{KE}=e V=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100=1.6 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: J.J. Thomson's cathode ray tube experiment demonstrated that
options: cathode rays are streams of negatively charged ions
all the mass of an atom is essentially in the nucleus
the $e / m$ of electrons is much greater than the $e / m$ of protons
the $e / m$ ratio of the cathode ray particles changes when a different gas is placed in the discharged tube
questions.solution: According to J.J. Thomson's cathode ray tube experiment, the $e / m$ of electrons is much greater than the $e / m$ of protons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radius of first Bohr orbit is $r$. What is the radius of 2 nd Bohr orbit?
questions.solution:
Radius of Bohr orbit
$\begin{array}{l} r \propto n^2 \\ \frac{r_1}{r_2} =\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2}\right)^2 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{r}{r_2} =\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{r}{r_2} =\frac{1}{4} \\ \Rightarrow r_2 =4 r .\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV . Hydrogen atom on the ground state rarely excited by monochromatic radiation of photon 12.1 eV . The special line emitted by a hydrogen atom according to Bohr's theory will be
questions.solution:
From the relation
$E_3-E_1=(-1.5)-(-13.6)=12.1$
As the energy is equal to the energy taken by $n=1, n=2, n=3$. Hence there will be three spectral line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electron of an H -atom is revolving around the nucleus in circular orbit having radius $\frac{h^2}{4 \pi m e^2}$ with $\left(\frac{2 \pi e^2}{h}\right)$. The current produced due to the motion of electron is
questions.solution:
As we know that time period, $T=\frac{2 \pi r}{V}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad T & =2 \pi \times \frac{h^2}{4 \pi m e^2} \times \frac{h}{2 \pi e^2} \\ & =\frac{h^3}{4 \pi^2 m e^4}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Current, } i & =\frac{\text { Charge }(q)}{T} \\ & =\frac{e}{T}=\frac{e}{\frac{h^3}{4 \pi^2 m e^4}} \\ & =\frac{4 \pi^2 m e^5}{h^3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Wavelength of light emitted from second orbit to first orbit in a hydrogen atom is
questions.solution:
Wavelength of light
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda}=1.097 \times 10^7 \times \frac{3}{4} \\
& \therefore \quad \lambda=1.215 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}=1215 Å
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given figure, the capacitors $C_1, C_3, C_4, C_5$ have a capacitance $4 \mu \mathrm{F}$ each. If the capacitor $C_2$ has a capacitance $10 \mu \mathrm{F}$, then effective capacitance between $A$ and $B$ will be
questions.solution:
When a battery is applied across $A$ and $B$, then the points $b$ and $c$ will be at the same potential
$\left(\because C_1=C_4=C_3=C_5=4 \mu \mathrm{F}\right)$
Therefore, no charge flows through $\mathrm{C}_2$. $\mathrm{As}, C_1$ and $C_5$ are in series.
$\therefore$ Their equivalent capacitance,
$C^{\prime}=\frac{C_1 \times C_5}{C_1+C_5}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=2 \mu \mathrm{F}$
Similarly, $C_4$ and $C_3$ are in series. Therefore, their equivalent capacitance
$C^{\prime \prime}=\frac{C_3 \times C_4}{C_3+C_4}=\frac{4 \times 4}{4+4}=2 \mu \mathrm{F}$
Now, $C^{\prime}$ and $C^{\prime \prime}$ are in parallel. Therefore, effective capacitance between $A$ and $B$
$=C^{\prime}+C^{\prime \prime}=2+2=4 \mu \mathrm{F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate condenser is filled with two dielectrics as shown in figure. Area of each pate is $A \mathrm{~m}^2$ and the separation is $d$ metre. The dielectric constants are $K_1$ and $K_2$ respectively. Its capacitance in farad will be
questions.solution:
From the formula
$\begin{aligned} C & =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{1}{k_1}+\frac{1}{k_2}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{t}\left(\frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1+k_2}\right) \\ & =\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left(\frac{k_1 k_2}{k_1+k_2}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two condensers, one of capacity $C$ and the other of capacity $\frac{C}{2}$, are connected to a $V$-volt battery, as shown. The work done in charging fully both the condensers is
questions.solution:
The two condensers in the circuit are in parallel order, hence $C^{\prime}=C+\frac{C}{2}=\frac{3 C}{2}$
The work done in charging the equivalent capacitor is stored in the form of potential energy.
Hence, $W=U=\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} V^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{3 C}{2}\right) V^2=\frac{3}{4} C V^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit find the heat produced between A and B (given $\mathrm{C}=1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ )
questions.solution: Key Idea Potential drop is zero along an ideal conductor because it has zero resistance. Potential at point $\mathrm{A}=10 \mathrm{~V}$ Potential at point $\mathrm{B}=0 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore$ Potential difference across $\mathrm{AB}=10 \mathrm{~V}$ $\begin{aligned} \text { Heat produced } & =\text { Energy stored }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times(10)^2=50 \mu \mathrm{J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacities $C_1$ and $C_2$ are charged upto the potential $V_1$ and $V_2$, then condition for not flowing the charge between on connected them in parallel is
questions.solution:
If charge on both the capacitors is same, then transfer of energy does not take place.
$Q=C V$
$\therefore \quad C_1 V_1=C_2 V_2$
It is the required condition.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the capacitance in shown figure
questions.solution:
This combination is same as the two capacitors are connected in series and distance between plates of each capacitor is $\mathrm{d} / 2$.
So, $\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{\mathrm{K} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}$ and $\mathrm{C}_2=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d} / 2}$
Hence, $C_{\text {net }}=\frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 K \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}\right)\left(\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}\right)}{\left(\frac{2 K \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}+\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{d}\right)}$
$=\frac{2 \mathrm{KA} \varepsilon_0}{(\mathrm{~K}+\mathrm{l}) \mathrm{d}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor having capacitance $1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ with air, is filled with two dielectrics as shown. How many times capacitance will increase?
questions.solution: Key Idea : After filling with dielectrics the two capacitors will be in parallel order. As shown, the two capacitors are connected in parallel. Initially the capacitance of capacitor $C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ where $A$ is area of each plate and $d$ is the separation between the plates. After filling with dielectrics, we have two capacitors of capacitance. $C_1=\frac{K_1 \varepsilon_0(A / 2)}{d}=\frac{8}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_0 A}{d}=4 C$ and $C_2=\frac{K_2 \varepsilon_0(A / 2)}{d}=\frac{4}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}=\frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A}{d}=2 C$ Hence, their equivalent capacitance $\begin{aligned} C_{\mathrm{eq}} & =C_1+C_2 \\ & =4 C+2 C=6 C\end{aligned}$ ie, new capacitance will be six times of the original.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Taking the earth to be a spherical conductor of diameter $12.8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~km}$. Its capacity will be
questions.solution:
Here : Diameter of earth
$\begin{aligned} & =12.8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~km} \\
\text {So, radius } R & =\frac{12.8}{2} \times 10^3 \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =6.4 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~km}=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} \\ \text { Capacity } C & =4 \pi \varepsilon_0 R =\frac{1}{9 \times 10^9} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \\ & =711 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}=711 \mu \mathrm{F}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The capacity of an air condenser is 2.0 $\mu \mathrm{F}$. If a medium is placed between its plates, the capacity becomes $12 \mu \mathrm{F}$. The dielectric constant of the medium will be
questions.solution:
Dielectric constant of medium
$K=\frac{\text { capacity of condenser with medium }}{\text { capacity of condenser without medium }}$
$=\frac{12}{2.0}=6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Capacitor $C_1$ of capacitance $1 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and capacitor $C_2$ of capacitance $2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are separately charged fully by a common battery. The two capacitors are then separately allowed to discharged through equal resistors at time $t=0$
options: the current in each of the two discharging circuits is zero at $t=0$
the currents in the two discharging circuits at $t=0$ are equal but non-zero
the currents in the two discharging circuits at $t=0$
Capacitor $C_1$ loses $40 \%$ of its initial charge sooner than $\mathrm{C}_2$ loses $40 \%$ of initial charge.
questions.solution:
Here, $V_0$ and $R$ in the two $C R$ circuits are same, hence the currents in the two discharging circuits at $t=0\left(V_0 / R\right)$ will be same.
We know that, time constant of a resistance and capacitance circuit is $C R$. Here, time constant of first circuit (capacity $C_1=1 \mu F$ ) is half of the time constant of second circuit (capacity $C_2=2 \mu F$ ). Hence, $C_1$ loses $50 \%$ its initial charge sooner.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three capacitors of capacitances $1 \mu \mathrm{F}, 2 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $4 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are connected first in a series combination, and then in a parallel combination. The ratio of their equivalent capacitances will be
questions.solution:
In series combination,
$\frac{1}{C_1}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{4+2+1}{4}=\frac{7}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \quad C_1=\frac{4}{7} \mu \mathrm{F}$
In parallel combination,
$\begin{aligned}
& C_1=1+2+4=7 \mu \mathrm{F} \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{C_1}{C_2}=\frac{4 / 7}{7}=\frac{4}{49}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor with air as the dielectric has capacitance $C$. A slab of dielectric constant $K$ and having the same thickness as the separation between the plates is introduced so as to fill one-fourth of the capacitor as shown in the figure. The new capacitance will be
questions.solution:
The condenser with air as the dielectric has capacitance
$C_1=\frac{\varepsilon_0}{d}\left(\frac{3 A}{4}\right)=\frac{3 \varepsilon_0 A}{4 d}$
Similarly, the condenser with $K$ as the dielectric constant has capacitance
$C_2=\frac{\varepsilon_0 K}{d}\left(\frac{A}{4}\right)=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{4 d}$
Since, $C_1$ and $C_2$ are in parallel
$C_{\text {net }}=C_1+C_2$
$=\frac{3 \varepsilon_0 A}{4 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{4 d}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[\frac{3}{4}+\frac{K}{4}\right]$
$=\frac{C}{4}(K+3)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a capacitor having capacitance 2 F and plate separation of 0.5 cm will have area
questions.solution:
$\because$ Capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor,
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C} & =\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}} \quad \mathrm{~A}=\frac{\mathrm{Cd}}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}} \\ & =1.130 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~m}^2=1130 \mathrm{~km}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor of capacitance $5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is connected as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the cell is $0.5 \Omega$. The amount of charge on the capacitor plates is
questions.solution:
In steady state, there will be no current in the capacitor branch. Net resistance of the circuit
$R=1+1+0.5=2.5 \Omega$
Current drawn from the cell
$i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{2.5}{2.5}=1 \mathrm{~A}$
Potential drop across two parallel branches
$\begin{aligned}
V & =E-\text { ir }=2.5-1 \times 0.5 \\
& =2.5-0.5=2.0 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
So, charge on the capacitor plates
$q=C V=5 \times 2=10 \mu \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor is charged and then made to discharge through a resistance. The time constant is $\tau$. In what time will the potential difference across the capacitor decrease by $10 \%$ ?
questions.solution:
As, we know that $q=q_0 e^{-t / \tau}$
According to the question, $V_C=\frac{q}{C}=\frac{q_0}{C} e^{-t / \tau}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \frac{q_0}{10} & =\frac{q_0}{C} e^{-t / \tau} \\ \Rightarrow & & e^{t / \tau} & =\frac{10}{9} \\ \Rightarrow & & t & =\tau \ln \frac{10}{9}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A toy truck of mass 2 m elastically collides with a toy car of mass $m$. speed of truck is $v$ and car is at rest. Find the velocity of car after collision.
questions.solution:
Mass of toy truck $m_t=2 \mathrm{~m}$
Mass of toy car $\mathrm{m}_c=\mathrm{m}$
Initial speed of truck $v_t=v$
and initial speed of car $u_c=0$
If $v_1$ and $v_2$ be the final velocity of truck and car after collision, then by law of conservation of momentum,
Total initial momentum $=$ total final momentum
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{t}} \cdot \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{t}}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{c}} & =\mathrm{m}_4 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{v}_2 \\
2 \mathrm{mv}+0 & =2 \mathrm{mv}_1+\mathrm{mv}_2 \\
2 \mathrm{v} & =2 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{v}_2
\end{aligned}$
For elastic collision, coefficient of restitution, $e=1$
i.e.
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{v_2-v_1}{u_1-u_c}=1 \\
& \frac{v_2-v_1}{v-0}=1 \Rightarrow v=v_2-v_1
\end{aligned}$
Solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have $\mathrm{v}_2=\frac{4 \mathrm{v}}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A stick of length $L$ and mass $M$ lies on a frictionless horizontal surface on which it is free to move in any way. A ball of mass $m$ moving with speed $V$ collides elastically with the stick as shown in fig below. If after the collision, the ball comes to rest, then what should be the mass of the ball?
questions.solution:
Applying the law of conservation of momentum
$m V=M V...(i)$
By conservation of angular momentum
$m V(L / 2)=\left(\frac{M L^2}{12}\right) \omega...(ii)$
As the collision is elastic, we have
$\frac{1}{2} m V^2=\frac{1}{2} M V^2+\frac{1}{2} I w^2...(iii)$
Substituting the values, we get
$m=M / 4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: One object of mass 20 kg is moving with speed $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in west direction another object of mass 10 kg is moving with $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in North direction. Both collide and stick together. Choose the correct alternative.
options: There K.E. is conserved as it is inelastic collision
There K.E. is conserved on it is elastic collision
There momentum is conserved as it is inelastic collision
There momentum is conserved as it is elastic collision
questions.solution: When object of mass 20 kg is moving with speed $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in west direction collide with object of mass 10 kg and stick together, hence it is perfectly inelastic collision. In inelastic collision, momentum is conserved only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two objects of mass $m$ each moving with speed $\mathrm{u~m} / \mathrm{s}$ collide at $90^{\circ}$, then final momentum is (assume collision is inelastic)
questions.solution:
Speed of objects $=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Since, both objects collide with $90^{\circ}$.
Hence, by the law of conservation of momentum,
Total moment before collision $=$ total momentum after collision
$\begin{aligned}
\mid \mathrm{muî}+\mathrm{mu \hat {j } |} & =\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \\
\sqrt{\mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{u}^2+\mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{u}^2} & =\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}} & =\sqrt{2} \mathrm{mu}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A sphere of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ hits inelastically with another stationary sphere of same mass. The ratio of their final velocities will be (in terms of $e$ )
questions.solution: As coefficient of restitution, $e=\frac{v_2-v_1}{u_1-u_2}=\frac{v_2-v_1}{u-0}$ $\Rightarrow \quad v_2-v_1=e u$...(i) By conservation of momentum, Momentum before collision = Momentum after collision $\begin{aligned} & m u=m v_1+m v_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v_1+v_2=u \\ & \end{aligned}$...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\begin{aligned} & v_1=\frac{u}{2}(1-e) \text { and } v_2=\frac{u}{2}(1+e) \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{1-e}{1+e} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A body weighing 8 g when placed in one pan and 18 g when placed on the other pan of a false balance. If the beam is horizontal when both the pans are empty, then the true weight of the body is
questions.solution: Consider the diagram in balance $8 x=w y$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{y}=\frac{w}{8}$ ...(i) Also, $w x=18 y$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{y}=\frac{18}{w}$ ...(ii) Dividing Eq. (i) and Eq. (ii) we get $\begin{aligned} \frac{x / y}{x / y} & =\frac{\frac{w}{8}}{18 / w} \\ \Rightarrow \quad w & =\sqrt{18 \times 8}=12 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball of mass $m$ hits the floor with a speed $v$ making an angle of incidence $\theta$ with the normal. The coefficient of restitution is $e$. The speed of reflected ball and the angle of reflection of the ball will be
questions.solution:
The parallel component of velocity of the ball remains unchanged. This gives
$v^{\prime} \sin \theta^{\prime}=v \sin \theta$ ...(i)
For the components normal to the floor, the velocity of separation $=v^{\prime} \cos \theta^{\prime}$
and the velocity of approach $=v \cos \theta$
Hence, $\quad v^{\prime} \cos \theta^{\prime}=e v \cos \theta$ ... (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii)
$\begin{aligned} v^{\prime} & =v \sqrt{\sin ^2 \theta+e^2 \cos ^2 \theta} \\ \text { and } \quad \tan \theta^{\prime} & =\frac{\tan \theta}{e}\end{aligned}$
For elastic collision, $e=1$, so that $\theta^{\prime}=\theta$ and $v^{\prime}=v$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A shell of mass 20 kg at rest explodes into two fragments whose masses are in the ratio $2: 3$. The smaller fragment moves with a velocity of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The kinetic energy of the larger fragment is
questions.solution:
Total mass of the shell $=20 \mathrm{~kg}$
Ratio of the masses of the fragments $=2: 3$
$\therefore$ Masses of the fragments are 8 kg and 12 kg
Now, according to the conservation of momentum
$m_1 v_1=m_2 v_2$
$\therefore \quad 8 \times 6=12 \times v$
$v$ (velocity of the larger fragment $)=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times(4)^2=96 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A moving body of mass $m$ and velocity $3 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ collides with a rest body of mass 2 m and stick to it. Now the combined mass starts to move. What will be the combined velocity?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A body of mass $m_1=4 \mathrm{~kg}$ moves at $5 \mathrm{i~} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ and another body of mass $m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ moves at $10 \mathrm{i~} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. The kinetic energy of centre of mass is
questions.solution:
$v_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{m_1 \frac{d r_1}{d t}+m_2 \frac{d r_2}{d t}}{m_1+m_2}=\frac{4 \times 5 \mathbf{i}+2 \times 10 \mathbf{i}}{4+2}$
$v_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{40 \mathbf{i}}{6}=\frac{20}{3} \mathbf{i}$
The kinetic energy
$K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times(4+2) \times \frac{20 \times 20}{3 \times 3}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times \frac{20 \times 20}{3 \times 3}$
$K=\frac{400}{3} \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: For inelastic collision between two spherical rigid bodies
options: the total kinetic energy is conserved
the total mechanical energy is not conserved
the linear momentum is not conserved
questions.solution: In an inelastic collision, the particles do not regain their shape and size completely after collision. Some fraction of mechanical energy is retained by the colliding particles in the form of deformation potential energy. Thus, the kinetic energy of particles no longer remains conserved. However in the absence of external forces, law of conservation of linear momentum still holds good.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A small disc of radius 2 cm is cut from a disc of radius 6 cm . If the distance between their centres is 3.2 cm , what is the shift in the centre of mass of the disc?
questions.solution: The situation can be shown as : Let radius of complete disc is $a$ and that of small disc is $b$. Also let centre of mass now shifts to $\mathrm{O}_2$ at a distance $x_2$ from original centre. The position of new centre of mass is given by $X_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{-\sigma \cdot \pi b^2 \cdot x_1}{\sigma \cdot \pi a^2-\sigma \cdot \pi b^2}$ Here, $a=6 \mathrm{~cm}, b=2 \mathrm{~cm}, x_1=3.2 \mathrm{~cm}$ Hence, $\quad X_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{-\sigma \times \pi(2)^2 \times 3.2}{\sigma \times \pi \times(6)^2-\sigma \times \pi \times(2)^2}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{12.8 \pi}{32 \pi} \\ & =-0.4 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bomb of mass 30 kg at rest explodes into two pieces of masses 18 kg and 12 kg. The velocity of 18 kg mass is $6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The kinetic energy of the other mass is
questions.solution:
The linear momentum of exploding part will remain conserved.
Applying conservation of linear momentum,
we write,
$m_1 u_1=m_2 u_2$
Here, 
$\begin{gathered}m_1=18 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=12 \mathrm{~kg} \\ u_1=6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}, u_2=?\end{gathered}$
$\therefore  18 \times 6=12 u_2$
$\Rightarrow u_2=\frac{18 \times 6}{12} = 9 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Thus, kinetic energy of 12 kg mass
$\begin{aligned} K_2 & =\frac{1}{2} m_1 u_2^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times(9)^2 \\ & =6 \times 81 \\ & =486 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Which of the following is not an inelastic collision?
options: A man jump on a cart
A bullet embedded in a block
questions.solution:
"Man jumping on cart" and "bullet imbedded in a block" are examples when two bodies have to move together after collision, therefore both of them are inelastic collision.
Where as it is not the case for "collision of two glass balls".

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides with a stationary particle of mass $2 m$. The speed of the system, will be
questions.solution:
According to conservation of momentum
$\begin{gathered}m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2=\left(m_1+m_2\right) v^{\prime} \\
m v+2 m \times 0=(m+2 m) v^{\prime}\end{gathered}$
So,
$v^{\prime}=\frac{v}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball of mass 10 g moving with acceleration of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ is hit by a force which acts on it for 0.1 sec . The impulsive force is
questions.solution:
Here: Mass of the ball $m=150 \mathrm{~g}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text {time }=0.15 \mathrm{~kg} \\& \text {Acceleration of the ball }=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\& \mathrm{dt} = 0.1 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$
Force, $\quad F=m a=0.15 \times 20=3 \mathrm{~N}$
Now impulsive force
$=F \times d t=3 \times 0.1=0.3 \mathrm{Ns}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: If a ball of mass 0.1 kg hits the ground from the height of 20 m and bounce back to the same height, then find out the force exerted on the ball if the time of impact is 0.04 sec . (Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
Here, mass of ball $m=0.1 \mathrm{~kg}$
Velocity attained by the ball before hitting the
$\begin{aligned}
\text { ground }(\mathrm{v}) & =\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\
& =\sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 20}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})=-20 \hat{\mathrm{j}} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
Velocity of ball when bounce back to the same height after hitting the ground,
$V^{\prime}=-v=-(-20 \hat{j})=20 \hat{j} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Change in velocity
$\Delta V=V^{\prime}-V=20 \hat{j}-(-20 \hat{j})=40 \hat{j} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Force exerted on the ball
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{f} & =\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{\mathrm{m} \Delta \mathrm{V}}{0.04}[\text { given } \Delta \mathrm{t}=0.04] \\
& =\frac{0.1 \times 40 \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{0.04}=100 \mathrm{~N}(\hat{\mathrm{j}})
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The density of a rod having length $l$ varies as $\rho=c+d x$, where $x$ is the distance from the left end. The centre of mass is
questions.solution:
Let the cross-sectional area is $\alpha$. The mass of an element of length $d x$ located at a distance $x$ away from the left end is $(C+D x) \alpha d x$. The $x$-coordinate of the centre of mass is given by
$X_{c m}=\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m}$
$\begin{aligned}= & \frac{\int_0^1 x(C+D x) \alpha d x}{\int_0^1(C+D x) \alpha d x} \\ = & \frac{C \frac{l^2}{2}+\frac{D l^3}{3}}{C l+\frac{D l^2}{2}}=\frac{3 C l+2 D l^2}{3(2 C+D l)}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Particles of masses $m, 2 m, 3 m, \ldots, n m$ are placed on the same line at distances $L, 2 L, 3 L, \ldots, n L$ from $O$. The distance of centre of mass from $O$ is
questions.solution: $\because \quad x_{C M}=\frac{m L+2 m \times 2 L+\ldots+n m \times n L}{m+2 m+\ldots+n m}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{L\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots+n^2\right)}{(1+2+\ldots+n)} \\ & =L \frac{\Sigma n^2}{\Sigma n}=L \frac{\frac{n(n+1)(2 n+1)}{6}}{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}} \\ & =\frac{(2 n+1) L}{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two rectangular blocks $A$ and $B$ of masses 2 kg and 3 kg respectively are connected by a spring of spring constant $10.8 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ and are placed on a frictionless horizontal surface. The block $A$ was given an initial velocity of $0.15 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ in the direction shown in the figure. The maximum compression of the spring during the motion is
questions.solution: As the block $A$ moves with velocity $0.15 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, it compresses the spring which pushes $B$ towards right. A goes on compressing the spring till the velocity acquired by $B$ becomes equal to the velocity of $A$, i.e., $0.15 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. Let this velocity be $v$. Now, spring is in a state of maximum compression. Let $x$ be the maximum compression at this stage. According to the law of conservation of linear momentum, we get $m_A u=\left(m_A+m_B\right) v$ or $v=\frac{m_A u}{m_A+m_B}$ $=\frac{2 \times 0.15}{2+3}=0.06 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ According to the law of conservation of energy. $\frac{1}{2} m_A u^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(m_A+m_B\right) v^2+\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ $\frac{1}{2} m_A u^2-\frac{1}{2}\left(m_A+m_B\right) v^2=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ $\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(0.15)^2-\frac{1}{2}(2+3)(0.06)^2=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ $0.0225-0.009=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ or $\quad 0.0135=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ or $\quad x=\sqrt{\frac{0.027}{k}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.027}{10.8}}=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The sound waves after being converted into electrical waves are not transmitted as such because
options: they travel with the speed of sound
they are heavily absorbed by the atmosphere
the height of antenna has to be increased several times
questions.solution: The electric waves do not have the capacity to travel through the atmosphere. They get immediately grounded and they become neutral as fast as possible. This makes electrical waves incapable to travel through the atmosphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What maximum frequency can be reflected from ionosphere?
questions.solution: The ionosphere extends from a height of 80 km to 300 km . The refractive index of ionosphere is less than its free space value. That is, it behaves as a rarer medium. As we go deep into the ionosphere, the refractive index keeps on decreasing. The bending of beam (away from the normal) will continue till it reaches critical angle after which it will be reflected back. The different points on earth receive signals reflected from different depths of the ionosphere. There is a critical frequency $f_c$ (5 to 100 MHz ) beyond which the waves cross the ionosphere and do not return back to earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The principle of LASER action involves
options: amplification of particular frequency emitted by the system
questions.solution:
Laser action involves all the given phenomena
(i) Amplification of particular frequency
(ii) Population inversion
(iii) Stimulated emission

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If resistivity of copper is $1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega-\mathrm{m}$ and number of free electrons in copper is $8.5 \times 10^{28} / \mathrm{m}^3$. Find the mobility.
questions.solution:
Given, Resistivity of copper
$\rho=1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega-\mathrm{m}$
Electrons density $\mathrm{n}=8.5 \times 10^{26} / \mathrm{m}^3$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \text { Mobility }(\mu) & =\frac{1}{\rho \text { ne }} \\
& =\frac{1}{1.72 \times 10^{-8}} \times 8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\
& =4.25 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{C} \Omega
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find $V_P-V_Q$ in the circuit shown in figure.
questions.solution: In the given circuit, net resistance between P and Q points is $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{PQ}}$ and net resistance between Q and R points is $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{QR}}$, then $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{10 \times 8}{10+8}=\frac{80}{18}=\frac{40}{9} \Omega$ $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{QR}}=\frac{10 \times 18 \times 25}{10 \times 18+18 \times 25+25 \times 10}=\frac{4500}{880}=\frac{225}{44} \Omega$ So, circuit will be In the series circuit, voltages will be divide in the ratio of their resistances. $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{PQ}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{QR}}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{PQ}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{QR}}} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\left(\frac{40}{9}\right)}{\left(\frac{225}{44}\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{PQ}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{QR}}} \text { or } \frac{1760}{2025}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{PQ}}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{QR}}} \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{1760}{3785}=\frac{V_{P Q}}{10} \Rightarrow V_{P Q}=4.65 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, if power rating of heater is 1000 Watt at 100 Volt, find the resistance R in figure so that heater produces power of 62.5 W.
questions.solution: Power rating of heater, $\mathrm{P}=1000 \mathrm{~W}$ Voltage rating of heater, $\mathrm{V}=100 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore$ Resistance of heater, $\mathrm{R}_1=\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{P}}=\frac{(100)^2}{1000}=10 \Omega$ According to question, power dissipated in heater, $\mathrm{P}^{\prime}=62.5 \mathrm{~W}$ $\therefore$ Voltage $\left(\mathrm{V}^{\prime}\right.$ ) across heater can be calculated as $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}^{\prime}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{V}^{\prime}\right)^2}{\mathrm{R}_1} \Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{V}^{\prime}\right)^2=\mathrm{P}^{\prime} \times \mathrm{R}_1=62.5 \times 10 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{V}^{\prime}=25 \mathrm{~V} \text { (across heater) } \\ & \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Voltage across $10 \Omega$ resistor, $\mathrm{V}^{\prime \prime}=100-25=75 \mathrm{~V}$ Current in $10 \Omega$ resistor $=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime \prime}}{10}=\frac{75}{10}=7.5 \mathrm{~A}$ Current in heater resistor $=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}{10}=\frac{25}{10}=2.5 \mathrm{~A}$ So, current in $\mathrm{R} \Omega=7.5-25=5 \mathrm{~A}$ Now, $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{IR} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{I}=\frac{25}{5}=5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find i in shown figure
questions.solution:
$\because 60 \Omega$ and $30 \Omega$ resistor are connected in
parallel. So, their net resistance
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_{\text {net }}=\frac{30 \times 60}{30+60}=\frac{1800}{90}=20 \Omega \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{i}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {net }}}=\frac{2}{20}=0.1 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer of $50 \Omega$ resistance has 25 divisions. A current of $4 \times 10^{-4}$ A gives a deflection of one division. To convert this galvanometer into a voltmeter having a range of 25 V , it should be connected with a resistance of
questions.solution: Given, resistance of galvanometer $\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)=50 \Omega$ Number of divisions $=25 \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{g}}=4 \times 10^{-4} \times 25=10^{-2} \mathrm{~A}$ R is the resistance connected in series of galvanometer to convert into voltmeter $\begin{aligned} \therefore V & =I_g\left(R_g+R\right) \\ 25 & =10^{-2}(50+R) \\ \Rightarrow R & =\frac{25}{10^{-2}}-50=2500-50 \Rightarrow R=2450 \Omega \text { in } \end{aligned}$ series.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To reduce the range of voltmeter, its resistance need to be reduced. A voltmeter has resistance $R_0$ and range $V$. Which of the following resistances when connected in parallel will convert it into a voltmeter of range $V / n$ ?
questions.solution:
When a resistance is connected in parallel, it reduces the resistance of combination but the range is not reduced. For the purpose of reducing the range the resistance in series with the galvanometer need to be reduced.
This cannot be achieved by connecting a resistance in parallel with voltmeter. It should be noted that the range of voltmeter can only be increased but cannot be decreased.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: You are given resistance wire of length 50 cm and a battery of negligible resistance. In which of the following cases is largest amount of heat generated?
options: When the wire is connected to the battery directly
When the wire is divided into two parts and both the parts are connected to the battery in parallel.
When the wire is divided into four parts and all the four parts are connected to the battery in parallel.
When only half of the wire is connected to the battery.
questions.solution:
Let $R$ be the resistance of the wire
(a) $H_1=\frac{V^2 t}{R}$
(b) Resistance of each part will be $R$ / 2. When they are connected in parallel, the resistance will be $R / 4$.
Hence, $H_2=\frac{4 v^2 t}{R}$
(c) In case of four wires connected in parallel, the resistance will be $R$ / 8
$\therefore H_3=\frac{8 V^2 t}{R}$
(d) $H_4=\frac{V^2 t}{[R / 2]}=\frac{2 V^2 \cdot t}{R}$
Hence, largest amount of heat will be generated in case of four parts connected in parallel.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two batteries of emf 3 V and 6 V with internal resistances $2 \Omega$ and $4 \Omega$ are connected in a circuit with resistance of $10 \Omega$ as shown in figure. The current and potential difference between the points $P$ and $Q$ are
questions.solution: Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law in the given loop, $\begin{aligned}-4 I+6-3-2 I-10 I & =0 \\ -16 I & =-3 \\ I & =\frac{3}{16} \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$ $\because$ Potential difference across $P Q=\frac{3}{16} \times 10=\frac{15}{8} \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod made up of metal is 1.2 m long and 0.8 cm in diameter. Its resistance is $3.5 \times 10^{-3} \Omega$. Another disc made of the same metal is 2.0 cm in diameter and 1.25 mm thick. What is the resistance between the round faces of the disc?
questions.solution:
Given, $I=12 \mathrm{~m}, d=0.8 \mathrm{~cm}$, $R=3.5 \times 10^{-3} \Omega$, thickness $=1.25 \mathrm{~mm}$, Resistivity of the material of the rod
$\begin{aligned} \rho & =\frac{R A}{I}=\frac{3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times \pi\left(0.4 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2}{1.2} \\ & =46.6 \times 10^{-9} \pi \Omega \mathrm{m}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Resistance of disc, } R=\frac{\rho \text { (Thickness) }}{\text { Area of cross-section }} \\ &= \frac{46.6 \times 10^{-9} \pi \times\left(1.25 \times 10^{-3}\right)}{\pi\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2} \\ &= 5.82 \times 10^{-7} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer of resistance $25 \Omega$ shows a deflection of 5 divisions when a current of 2 mA is passed through it. If a shunt of $4 \Omega$ is connected and there are 20 divisions on the scale, then the range of the galvanometer is
questions.solution:
There are 20 divisions on scale. Initially, current for 20 divisions $=\frac{2 \times 20}{5}=8 \mathrm{~mA}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & I g=8 \mathrm{~mA} \\ \because & \frac{I g}{I}=\frac{S}{S+G}\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad I & =\left(\frac{S+G}{S}\right) I_g \Rightarrow I=\left(\frac{4+25}{4}\right) \times 8 \\ & =29 \times 2=58 \mathrm{~mA}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of a given cylindrical wire is increased by $150 \%$. Due to the consequent decrease in diameter the change in the resistance of the wire will be
questions.solution:
If suppose initial length, $I_1=100$, then
$I_2=100+150=250$
$\because \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{100}{250}\right)^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{4}{25} \Rightarrow R_2=\frac{25}{4} R_1$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100 & =\frac{R_2-R_1}{R_1} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{\frac{25}{4} R_1-R_1}{R_1} \times 100=525 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of resistance $10 \Omega$ is bent to form a complete circle. Find its resistance between two diametrically opposite point.
questions.solution:
The wires $A D C$ and $A B C$ will have resistance $5 \Omega$ each. These two are joined in parallel between $A$ and $C$. The equivalent resistance $R$ between $A$ and $C$ is, therefore given by
$R=\frac{5 \Omega \times 5 \Omega}{5 \Omega+5 \Omega}=2.5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the resistance of a hollow cylindrical conductor of length 1.0 mm and 2.0 mm respectively. The resistivity of the material is $2.0 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{m}$.
questions.solution:
The area of cross-section of the conductor through which charges will be flow is
$\begin{aligned} A & =\pi(20 \mathrm{~mm})^2-\pi(1.0 \mathrm{~mm})^2 \\ & =3 \times \pi \mathrm{mm}^2\end{aligned}$
The resistance of the wire is therefore
$\begin{aligned} R & =\rho \frac{1}{A}=\frac{\left(2.0 \times 10^{-8}\right) \times(10)}{3.0 \times \pi \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =2.1 \times 10^{-3} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ammeter shown in figure consists of a $480 \Omega$ coil connected in parallel to a $20 \Omega$ shunt. The reading of ammeter is
questions.solution:
The equivalent resistance of the ammeter is
$\frac{(480)(20)}{480+20}=19.2 \Omega$
The equivalent resistance of the circuit is
$1408+19.2 \Omega=160 \Omega$
The current, $i=\frac{20 \mathrm{~V}}{160 \Omega}=0.125 \mathrm{~A}$
Hence, ammeter reading is 0.125 A .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper rod of length 20 cm and cross-sectional area $2 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ is joined with a similar aluminium rod as shown below The resistance of pair of rods is $\left(\rho_{\mathrm{Al}}=2.6 \times 10^{-8} \quad \Omega-\mathrm{m}\right.$ and $\quad \rho_{\mathrm{Cu}}=1.7 \times 10^{-8}$ $\Omega-\mathrm{m})$
questions.solution:
The resistance of the copper rod $=\rho \frac{1}{a}$
$=\frac{1.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 20 \times 10^{-2}}{2.0 \times 10^{-6}}=1.7 \times 10^{-3} \Omega$
Similarly the resistance of aluminium rod
$=2.6 \times 10^{-3} \Omega$
The equivalent resistance
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1.7 \times 10^{-3}}+\frac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & R=\frac{1.7 \times 2.6}{4.3} \times 10^{-3}=1.0 \mathrm{~m} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The galvanometer resistance is $30 \Omega$ and it is connected to 2 V battery along with a resistance $2000 \Omega$ in series. A full scale deflection of 25 divisions is obtained. In order to reduce this deflection to 20 divisions, the resistance in series should be
questions.solution:
Total initial resistance
$=G+R=30+2000=2030 \Omega$
Current $\quad i=\frac{2}{2030} \mathrm{~A}$
Let $x$ be the effective resistance of the circuit, then
$\begin{aligned} 2 V & =2030 \times \frac{2}{2030} \times 10^3 \\ & =x \times \frac{2}{2.5} \\ \Rightarrow \quad x & =\frac{2 \times 2.5}{2} \times 10^3 \\ & =2500 \Omega\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Resistance to be added $=2500-30=2470 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find equivalent resistance between $X$ and $Y$
questions.solution: The given circuit can be shown in the following way. No, current will be flown in the middle resistance. Equivalent resistence of $R$ and $R^{\prime}=2 R$ Total resistance $\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2 R}+\frac{1}{2 R}$ $R^{\prime}=R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A battery of emf 10 V and internal resistance of 0.5 ohm is connected across a variable resistance $R$. The maximum value of $R$ is given by
questions.solution: We know that for obtaining maximum power, the internal resistance must be equal to variable resistance so, the value of $R$ will be $0.5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The internal resistance of a primary cell is $4 \Omega$. It generates a current of 0.2 A in an external resistance of $21 \Omega$. The rate at which chemical energy to consumed in providing current is
questions.solution:
Rate of energy $\frac{H}{t}=i^2 R$
(But total Resistance $R=21+4=25 \Omega$ )
So, $\quad \frac{H}{t}=0.2 \times 0.2 \times 25=1 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer acting as a voltmeter should have
options: low resistance in series with its coil
low resistance in parallel with its coil
high resistance in series with its coil
high resistance in parallel with its coil
questions.solution: Voltmeter has high resistance and is always connected in parallel with the circuit. So to convert a galvanometer into voltmeter, a high resistance must be connected in series with it so that is draws negligible current from the circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find current (i) in circuit shown in figure.
questions.solution: Above circuit is satisfied, the Wheatstone bridge condition, so no current is flow in the branch BC and it behaves like an open circuit. So, circuit becomes as shown below Now, in the above circuit $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ABD}}=5+10=15 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ACD}}=10+20=30 \Omega\end{aligned}$ Resistance, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ABD}}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ACD}}$ in parallel, so $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{R}_{\text {net }} & =\frac{15 \times 30}{30+15}=\frac{450}{45}=10 \Omega \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{i} & =\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {net }}}=\frac{5}{10} \\ & =0.5 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the current in the $8 \Omega$ resistance in the given circuit.
questions.solution: The circuit can be redrawn as So, the circuit is break in two parts. As the other end of $8 \Omega$ resistor is grounded, is at zero potential, the potential difference across $8 \Omega$ resistor $=24-0=24 \mathrm{~V}$ So, current in $8 \Omega$ resistor $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{24-0}{8}=3 \mathrm{~A}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find $R_{\text {net }}$ between $A$ and $B$.
questions.solution: In the circuit, the branch EGHF have three resistance of $10 \Omega, 20 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$, respectively which are connected in series combination. So, their equivalent resistance is given by $\mathrm{R}_1=10+20+10=40 \Omega$ This $R_1$ resistance is parallel with $40 \Omega$ resistance which is connected in the branch EF. So, their equivalent resistance $\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{40 \times 40}{40+40}=20 \Omega$ Now, circuit becomes Now, t in the branch CEFD $10 \Omega, 20 \Omega$ and $10 \Omega$ resistance are connect in series combination their equivalent resistance is given by $\mathrm{R}_3=10 \Omega+20 \Omega+40 \Omega=40 \Omega$ This $R_3$ is parallel with $40 \Omega$ resistance which is present in branch CD. Their equivalent resistance, $\mathrm{R}_4=\frac{40 \times 40}{40+40}=20 \Omega$ Now, circuit becomes The net resistance between A and B , $\mathrm{R}_{\text {net }}=10+20+10=40 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equivalent resistance across $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: The equivalent circuit can be redrawn as We have, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}$ i.e, $\quad \frac{4}{4}=\frac{4}{4}$ So, the given circuit is a balanced Wheatstone's bridge. Hence, the equivalent resistance $\begin{aligned} R_{A B} & =\frac{(4+4) \times(4+4)}{(4+4)+(4+4)}=\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8} \\ & =\frac{64}{16}=4 \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circuit contain two resistance $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series. Find the ratio of input voltage to voltage of $\mathrm{R}_2$.
questions.solution: The situation is shown in the circuit diagram. Current flowing through the circuit, $I=\frac{V_i}{R_1+R_2}$ Voltage across $R_2, V_2=R_2, V_2=\frac{V_i R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}=\frac{V_i}{V_2} \Rightarrow \frac{V_i}{V_2}=\frac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, find voltage across $12 \Omega$ resistance.
questions.solution: If $\mathrm{R}_1$ be the equivalent resistance of parallel resistors $8 \Omega, 8 \Omega$ and $4 \Omega$, then $\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}=\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{4}{8} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_1=2 \Omega$ $\therefore$ Total resistance of upper branch, $\mathrm{R}_2=\mathrm{R}_1+4=2+4=6 \Omega$ Now circuit can be redraw of By current division rule, current through $12 \Omega$ resistor $\mathrm{I}_2=\frac{12 \times 6}{12+6}=12 \times \frac{1}{3}=4 \mathrm{~A}$ $\therefore$ Voltage across $12 \Omega$ resistor $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{I}_2 \mathrm{R}=4 \times 12=48 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At room temperature, copper has free electron density of $8.4 \times 10^{28} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The electron drift velocity in a copper conductor of cross-sectional area of $10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and carrying a current of 5.4 A , will be
questions.solution:
Drift velocity in a copper conductor
$\begin{aligned} v_d=\frac{i}{n e A} & =\frac{5.4}{8.4 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =0.4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \\ & =0.4 \mathrm{mms}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current $i$ is flowing through the wire of diameter (d) having drift velocity of electrons $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}}$ in it. What will be new drift velocity when diameter of wire is made $\mathrm{d} / 4$ ?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Current, $\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{nAev}_{\mathrm{d}}$
or $\quad \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{d}} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}}$
If diameter of wire is $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}$, then area will be $\frac{\mathrm{A}}{16}$, so new drift velocity $=16 v_d$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 5 amp fuse wire can withstand a maximum power of 1 W in circuit. The resistance of the fuse wire is
questions.solution:
Power $P=i^2 R$
$\Rightarrow \quad R=\frac{P}{i^2}$
given, $P=1 \mathrm{~W}$, and $i=5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore \quad R=\frac{1}{(5)^2}=0.04 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform wire of resistance $R$ and length $L$ is cut into four equal parts, each of length $L / 4$ which are then connected in parallel combination. The effective resistance of the combination will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}R=\frac{\rho l}{A} \text { or } R \propto l \\ \therefore & \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{L}{L / 4}=4 \\ \text { or } & R_2=\frac{R}{4}\left(\because R_1=R\right)\end{array}$
In parallel combination of such four resistance
$\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_2}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{16}{R}$
or $\quad R^{\prime}=R / 16$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer can be changed into an ammeter by using
questions.solution: Correct option is $3$. low resistance in parallel An ammeter is always connected in series to a circuit. Since Galvanometer is a very sensitive instrument therefore it can't measure heavy currents. In order to convert a Galvanometer into an Ammeter, a very low resistance known as "shunt" resistance is connected in parallel to Galvanometer. In short To convert a galvanometer into an ammeter a low value resistance is to be connected in parallel to it called shunt.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given figure, potential difference between $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: Equivalent resistance, $R=10+\frac{10}{2}=15 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ $\therefore \quad$ Current, $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{30}{15 \times 10^3}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$ Hence potential difference between A and B, $V=\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{2}\right) \times 10 \times 10^3=10 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform wire of resistance $R$ and length $L$ is cut into four equal parts, each of length $L / 4$, which are then connected in parallel combination. The effective resistance of the combination will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& R=\frac{\rho l}{A} \text { or } R \propto l \\
& \therefore \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{L}{L / 4}=4 \\
& \text { or } \quad R_2=\frac{R}{4} \quad\left(\because R_1=R\right) \\
&
\end{aligned}$
In parallel combination of such four resistances.
or
$\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_2}+\frac{1}{R_2}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}+\frac{1}{R / 4}$
or $\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}+\frac{4}{R}$
or $\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{16}{R}$
or $\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{R}{16}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of an ammeter is $13 \Omega$ and its scale is graduated for a current upto 100 A . After an additional shunt has been connected to this ammeter it becomes possible to measure currents upto 750 A by this meter. The value of shunt resistance is
questions.solution: Let $i_a$ is the current flowing through ammeter and $i$ is the total current. So, a current $i-i_a$ will flow through shunt resistance. Potential difference across ammeter and shunt resistance is same. ie, $\quad i_a \times R=\left(i-i_a\right) \times S$ or $S=\frac{i_a R}{i-i_a}\ldots(i)$ Given, $\quad i_a=100 \mathrm{~A}, i=750 \mathrm{~A}, R=13 \Omega$ Hence, $\quad S=\frac{100 \times 13}{750-100}=2 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equivalent resistance across $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: The equivalent circuit can be redrawn as We have, $\frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S}$ ie, $\frac{4}{4}=\frac{4}{4}$ So, the given circuit is a balanced Wheatstone's bridge. Hence, the equivalent resistance $R_{A B}=\frac{(4+4) \times(4+4)}{(4+4)+(4+4)}=\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8}=\frac{64}{16}=4 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three resistances $P, Q, R$ each of $2 \Omega$ and an unknown resistance $S$ form the four arms of a Wheatstone's bridge circuit. When a resistance of $6 \Omega$ is connected in parallel to $S$ the bridge gets balanced. What is the value of $S$ ?
questions.solution: The situation can be depicted as shown in figure. As resistances $S$ and $6 \Omega$ are in parallel their effective resistance is $\frac{6 S}{6+S} \Omega$ As the bridge is balanced, hence it is balanced Wheatstone's bridge. For balancing condition, $\begin{aligned} & & \frac{P}{Q} & =\frac{R}{\left(\frac{6 S}{6+S}\right)} \\ \text { or } & & \frac{2}{2} & =\frac{2(6+S)}{6 S} \\ \text { or } & & 3 S & =6+S \\ \text { or } & & S & =3 \Omega \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a metallic wire, the ratio $\frac{V}{i}$ ( $V=$ applied potential difference and $i=$ current flowing) is
options: increases or decreases as temperature rises depending upon the metal
questions.solution:
The resistance of a metallic wire at temperature $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is given by
$R_t=R_0(1+\alpha t)$
where $\alpha$ is coefficient of expansion.
Hence, resistance of wire increases on increasing the temperature. Also, from Ohm's law, ratio of $\frac{V}{i}$ is equal to $R$ ie,
$\frac{V}{i}=R$
Hence, on increasing the temperature the ratio $\frac{V}{i}$ increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steady current of 1.5 A flows through a copper voltameter for 10 min . If the electrochemical equivalent of copper is $30 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$, the mass of copper deposited on the electrode will be
questions.solution:
If $m$ is the mass of a substance deposited or liberated at an electrode during electrolysis when a charge $q$ passes through electrolyte, then according to Faraday's first law of electrolysis,
or
$\begin{aligned}
& m \propto q \\
& m=z q
\end{aligned}$
where $z$ is a constant of proportionality and is called electrochemical equivalent (ECE) of the the substance.
If an electric current $I$ flows through the electrolyte, then
$\therefore$
$\begin{aligned}
q & =I t \\
m & =z i t
\end{aligned}$
Here,
$\begin{aligned}
i=1.5 \mathrm{~A}, t & =10 \mathrm{~min}=10 \times 60 \mathrm{~s}, \\
z & =30 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{gC}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, mass of copper deposited on the electrode
$\begin{aligned}
m & =30 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 10 \times 60 \\
& =27 \times 10^{-2}=0.27 \mathrm{~g}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Flash light equipped with a new set of batteries, produces bright white light. As the batteries wear out
options: the light intensity gets reduced with no change in its colour
light colour changes first to yellow and then red with no change in intensity
it stops working suddenly while giving white light
colour changes to red and also intensity gets reduced
questions.solution: As batteries wear out, temperature of filament of flash light attains lesser value, therefore intensity of radiation reduces. Also dominating wavelength $\left(\lambda_m\right)$ in spectrum, which is the red colour, increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer having a resistance of $8 \Omega$ is shunted by a wire of resistance $2 \Omega$. If the total current is 1 A , the part of it passing through the shunt will be
questions.solution: Key Idea : Potential difference across galvanometer should be equal to potential difference across shunt. The shunt and galvanometer are connected as shown in figure. Let total current through the parallel combination is $i$, the current through the galvanometer is $i_g$ and the current through the shunt is $i-i_g$. The potential difference $V_{a b}\left(=V_a-V_b\right)$ is the same for both paths, so $\begin{array}{ll} & i_g G=\left(i-i_g\right) S \\ \text { or } & i_g(G+S)=i S \\ \text { or } & \frac{i_g}{i}=\frac{S}{S+G}\end{array}$ The fraction of current passing through shunt $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{i-i_g}{i}=1-\frac{i_g}{i} \\ & =1-\frac{S}{S+G}=\frac{G}{S+G} \\ & =\frac{8}{2+8}=\frac{8}{10} \\ & =0.8 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For the network shown in the figure, the value of the current $i$ is
questions.solution: The circuit given resembles the balanced Wheatstone Bridge as $\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3}$. Thus, middle arm containing $4 \Omega$ resistance will be ineffective and no current flows through it. The equivalent circuit is shown as below : Net resistance of $A B$ and $B C$, $R^{\prime}=4+2=6 \Omega$ Net resistance of $A D$ and $D C$, $R^{\prime \prime}=6+3=9 \Omega$ Thus, parallel combination of $R^{\prime}$ and $R^{\prime \prime}$ gives $\begin{gathered}R=\frac{R^{\prime} \times R^{\prime \prime}}{R^{\prime}+R^{\prime \prime}} \\=\frac{6 \times 9}{6+9}=\frac{54}{15}=\frac{18}{5} \Omega\end{gathered}$ Hence, current $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{V}{18 / 5}=\frac{5 V}{18}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform wire of length $l$ and having resistance $R$ is cut into $n$ equal parts and all parts are connected in parallel, then the equivalent resistance will be :
questions.solution:
Resistance of each part $R^{\prime}=\frac{R}{n}$
When, these parts are connected in parallel.
Then, $\frac{1}{R^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}+\frac{1}{R^{\prime}} \ldots . n$ time
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{n}{R^{\prime}} \\ \therefore \quad R^{\prime \prime} & =\frac{R^{\prime}}{n}=\frac{R}{n^2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $5 A$ fuse wire can withstand a maximum power of $1 W$ in circuit. The resistance of the fuse wire is
questions.solution:
Power $P=i^2 R$
$\Rightarrow R=\frac{P}{i^2}$
Given, $P=1 \mathrm{~W}, i=5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore R=\frac{1}{(5)^2}=0.04 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a wire of uniform cross-section $a$, length $l$ and resistance $R$ is bent into a complete circle, resistance between two of diametrically opposite points will be
questions.solution: When wire is bent to form a complete circle then $2 \pi r=R \Rightarrow r=\frac{R}{2 \pi}$ Resistance of each semicircle $=\pi r=\frac{\pi R}{2 \pi}=\frac{R}{2}$ Thus, net resistance in parallel combination of two semicircular resistance $R^{\prime}=\frac{\frac{R}{2} \times \frac{R}{2}}{\frac{R}{2}+\frac{R}{2}}=\frac{\frac{R^2}{4}}{R}=\frac{R}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown the value of $I$ in ampere is
questions.solution: So net resistance, $R=2.4+1.6=4.0 \Omega$ Therefore, current from the battery, $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{4}{4}=1 \mathrm{~A}$ Now from the circuit (b), $\begin{aligned} & 4 I^{\prime}=6 I \\ \Rightarrow \quad & I^{\prime}=\frac{3}{2} I\end{aligned}$ But, $\quad i=I+I^{\prime}=I+\frac{3}{2} I=\frac{5}{2} I$ $\begin{array}{lc}\therefore & 1=\frac{5}{2} I \\ \Rightarrow & I=\frac{2}{5}=0.4 \mathrm{~A}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential difference between points $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: From the given figure, current through lower branch of resistances which are joined in series is $i_1=\frac{10}{4+3}=\frac{10}{7} \mathrm{amp}$ Again current through upper branch of resistances which are also connected in series, is $i_2=\frac{10}{8+6}=\frac{10}{14} \mathrm{amp}$ Now according to the Kirchhoff's voltage law $\begin{aligned} V_B & -V_A=8 \times i_2-4 \times i_1 \\ & =8 \times \frac{10}{14}-4 \times \frac{10}{7}=0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a certain current is passed in the circuit as shown in figure, 10 kcal of heat is produced in $5 \Omega$ resistance. How much heat is produced in $4 \Omega$ resistance?
questions.solution:
Heat produced in $5 \Omega=10 \mathrm{kcal}$ Now from the relation. $H_1=i_1^2 R_1 t$ or $i_1^2=\frac{H_1}{R_1 t}$ or $\quad i_1^2=\frac{H_1}{5 t}$ ...(1) Again, $5 \times i_1=10 \times i_2$ or $i_2=\frac{i_1}{2}$
Now heat produced in $4 \Omega$ resistance is $\begin{aligned} H_2 & =i_2^2 R_2 t \\ i_2^2 & =\frac{H_2}{R_2 t}\end{aligned}$ or $\quad \frac{i_1^2}{4}=\frac{H_2}{4 t}$ or $i_1^2=\frac{H_2}{t}$ ...(2) From eqs. (1) and (2) $\begin{aligned} \frac{H_2}{t} & =\frac{H_1}{5 t} \\ \text { or } \quad H_2 & =\frac{H_1}{5}=\frac{10}{2}=2 \mathrm{kcal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two equal resistances $R$ are joined with voltage source $V$ in (i) series (ii) parallel, the ratio of electrical power consumed in two cases wll be
questions.solution:
(i) In series: total resistance $=R+R=2 R$
Power consumed $P_1=\frac{V^2}{2 R} \quad \ldots$ (i)
(ii) In parallel : potential difference across each resistance will be $V$
So, power consumed in each resistance is
$P=\frac{V^2}{R}$
So total power consumed in two resistance is
$P_2=\frac{V^2}{R}+\frac{V^2}{R}=\frac{2 V^2}{R}$ \quad \ldots$ (ii)
$\begin{array}{r}\text { Hence, required ratio }=\frac{P_1}{P_2} \\ =\frac{V^2 \times R}{2 R \times 2 V^2}=\frac{1}{4}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of 5 A is passing through a metallic wire of cross-sectional area $4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$. If the density of charge carriers of the wire is $5 \times 10^{26} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$, then the drift velocity of the electrons will be
questions.solution:
Drift velocity, $v_d=\frac{1}{n e A}$
$\Rightarrow v_d=\frac{5}{\left(5 \times 10^{26}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times\left(4 \times 10^{-6}\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{64}=1.56 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of 1.6A is passed through a solution of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$. How many $\mathrm{Cu}^{++}$ions are liberated in one minute? (Electronic charge $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ )
questions.solution:
Charge flowing in one minute
$q=I \times t=1.6 \times 60 \mathrm{C}$
Charge required to liberate one copper ion 2 C
Number of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ liberated $=\frac{1.6 \times 60}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$=3 \times 10^{20}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a galvanometer is $50 \Omega$. When 0.01 A current flows in it, full scale deflection is obtained in galvanometer, the resistance of shunt connected to convert galvanometer into an ammeter of range 5 A , will be
questions.solution:
Here: $I_g=0.01 \mathrm{~A}, G=50 \Omega$
