To convert the galvanometer in to an ammeter of range 5 A , a low resistance is to be connected in parallel with coil of galvanometer.
$S=\frac{i g \times G}{i-i_g}$
$=\frac{0.01 \times 50}{5-0.01}=\frac{0.5}{4.99}=0.1 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a resistance of $9 \Omega$ is connected at the ends of a battery, its potential difference decreases from 40 V to 30 V. The internal resistance of the battery is
questions.solution:
$E=40 \mathrm{~V}, V=30 \mathrm{~V}, R=9 \Omega$
Internal resistance
$r=\left(\frac{E}{V}-1\right) R=\left(\frac{40}{30}-1\right) 9$
$=\frac{1}{3} \times 9=3 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At room temperature, copper has free electron density of $8.4 \times 10^{28} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The electron drift velocity in a copper conductor of cross-sectional area of $10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and carrying a current of 5.4 A , will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor of capacitance $5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is connected as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the cell is $0.5 \Omega$. The amount of charge on the capacitor plates is
questions.solution:
In steady state, there will be no current in the capacitor branch.
Net resistance of the circuit
$R=1+1+0.5=2.5 \Omega$
Current drawn from the cell,
$i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{2.5}{2.5}=1 \mathrm{~A}$
Potential drop across two parallel branches
$\begin{aligned}
V=E-i r & =2.5-1 \times 0.5 \\
& =2.5-0.5 \\
& =2.0 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$
So, charge on the capacitor plates
$\begin{aligned}
q=C V & =5 \times 2 \\
& =10 \mu \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of resistance $R$ is divided in n equal parts, then these parts are joined in parallel, the equivalent resistance of the combination will be
questions.solution:
Resistance of one part $\frac{R}{n}$
Now equivalent resistance of $n$ parts each of resistance $\frac{R}{n}$ in parallel is given by
$R^{\prime}=\frac{R / n}{n}=\frac{R}{n^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Kirchhoff's law of junction, $\Sigma I=0$, is based on
options: conservation of energy as well as charge
questions.solution: Kirchhoff's first law states that in an electric circuit, the algebraic sum of the currents meeting at any junction in the circuit is zero. i.e., $\quad \Sigma i=0$ Hence, according to Kirchhoff's law $i_1+i_2=i_3+i_4+i_5$ Thus, the sum of currents flowing towards the junction is equal to the sum of the currents flowing away from the junction. In other words, when a steady current flows in a circuit, then there is neither any accumulation of charge at any point in the circuit, nor any charge is removed from there. Thus, Kirchhoff's first law expresses the conservation of charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer acting as a voltmeter should have
options: low resistance in series with its coil
low resistance in parallel with its coil
high resistance in series with its coil
high resistance in parallel with its coil
questions.solution: Voltmeter has high resistance and is always connected in parallel with the circuit. So, to convert a galvanometer into voltmeter, a high resistance must be connected in series with it so that it draws negligible current from the circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $N$ lamps each of resistance $r$, are fed by a machine of resistance $R$. If light emitted by any lamp is proportional to the square of the heat produced, prove that the most efficient way of arranging them is to place them in parallel arcs, each containing $n$ lamps, where $n$ is the integer nearest to
questions.solution:
As each are containing $n$ lamps, hence,
Resistance of each arc $=n r$
Number of arcs $=N / n$
Total resistance $S$ is given by
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{S} & =\Sigma \frac{1}{n r}=\frac{N}{n}\left(\frac{1}{n r}\right) \\ S & =\frac{n^2 r}{N}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Total resistance $=R+S=R+\left(n^2 r\right) N$
If $E$ is the emf of the machine, current entering the arcs is $E /(R+S)$ and in each arc is $n E /(R+S) N$.
Hence, current passing through each lamp
$I=\frac{n E}{N\left(R+n^2 r / N\right)}=\frac{E}{N}\left[\frac{R}{n}+\frac{n r}{N}\right]^{-1}$
Now, heat produced per second in the lamps is
$H=N_r I^2$
Since, light emitted is proportional to $H^2$ therefore light produced is maximum when $H^2$ and hence $H$ is maximum or $\left[\frac{R}{n}+\frac{n r}{N}\right]$ is minimum. Hence, we can write,
$\frac{R}{n}+\frac{n r}{N}=\left[\left(\frac{R}{n}\right)^{1 / 2}-\left(\frac{n r}{N}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]^2+2\left(\frac{R r}{N}\right)^{1 / 2}$
This is minimum when $\sqrt{R / n}-\sqrt{n r / N}=0$ or very small or $n$ is closely equal to $(N R / r)^{1 / 2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given circuit, what will be the equivalent resistance between the points $A$ and $B$ ?
questions.solution:
$\because \quad \frac{2}{3}=\frac{4}{6}$
$\therefore$ Given, circuit is a balanced Wheatstone's Bridge.
So, $10 \Omega$ resistance will be ineffective. Equivalent resistance of upper arms
$R_1=2+3=5 \Omega$
$\therefore$ Equivalent resistance of lower arms
$\begin{aligned} R_2 & =4+6=10 \Omega \\ R_{A B} & =\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\ & =\frac{5 \times 10}{5+10} \\ & =\frac{50}{10}=\frac{10}{3} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equilvalent resistance and potential difference between $A$ and $B$ for the circuit respectively are
questions.solution:
The equivalent resistance between $A$ and $B$ is
$\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{2}{3}$
or $\quad R^{\prime}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5 \Omega$
Now the equivalent resistance between $A$ and $B$ is
$R^{\prime \prime}=1.5+2.5=4 \Omega$
Now by Ohm's law, potential difference between $A$ and $B$ is given by
$\begin{aligned} V_A-V_B & =i R=2 \times 4.0 \\ & =8 \mathrm{volt}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The graph $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ and stopping potential $(V)$ of three metals having work function $\phi_1, \phi_2$ and $\phi_3$ in an experiment of photoelectric effect is plotted as shown in the figure. Which one of the following statement is/are correct? [Here $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the incident ray] (i) Ratio of work functions $\phi_1: \phi_2: \phi_3=1: 2: 4$ (ii) Ratio of work functions $\phi_1: \phi_2: \phi_3=4: 2: 1$ (iii) $\tan \theta \propto \frac{h c}{e}$, where $h=$ Planck's constant, $c=$ speed of light (iv) The violet colour-light can eject photoelectrons from metals 2 and 3
questions.solution:
According to Einstein photoelectric equation,
$\begin{aligned} E & =K_{\max }+\phi \\ \therefore \quad \frac{h c}{\lambda} & =\phi+e V_S \\ \therefore \quad V_S & =\left[\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi\right] \frac{1}{e}\end{aligned}$
$V_S$ versus $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ graph is a straight line.
Slope,
$\tan \theta=\frac{h c}{e}$
The curves intersect the $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ axis, where $V_S$ is zero.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{\lambda} & =\frac{\phi}{h c}\end{aligned}$
For metal $1 \quad$ For metal 2 For metal 3
$\begin{array}{cc} & \frac{\phi_1}{h c}=0.001 \quad \frac{\phi_2}{h c}=0.002 \quad \frac{\phi_3}{h c}=0.004 \\ \therefore \quad & \phi_1: \phi_2: \phi_3=1: 2: 4\end{array}$
For metal 2, threshold wavelength,
$\lambda \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{h c}{\phi}=\frac{1}{0.002}=500 \mathrm{~nm}$
Similarly, $\quad \lambda \mathrm{o}_3=250 \mathrm{~nm}$
$\lambda_{\text {violet }}=400 \mathrm{~nm}$ So $\lambda_{\text {violet }} \lt \lambda_{\text {threshold }}$ for metal 1 ,
$\lambda_{\text {violet }}^{\text {violet }}\gt\lambda_{\text {threshold }}$ for metal 3
Thus, violet light will eject photoelectron for metal 2 and not metal 3 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photo cell is illuminated by a small bright source placed 1m away. When the same source of light is placed 2 m away, the electrons emitted by photo cathode
options: carry one quarter of their previous energy
carry one quarter of their previous momenta
questions.solution:
$I \propto\left(\frac{1}{d^2}\right)$
when source is placed 2 m away, then $I^{\prime}=I / 4$. The number of electrons emitted is directly proportional to intensity. Hence, number of emitted electrons is reduced to one-fourth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A transverse wave is represented by the equation
$y=y_0 \sin \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(v t-k)$
For what value of $\lambda$ is the particle velocity equal to two times the wave velocity
questions.solution:
Comparing the given equation of the standard equation of wave
$\begin{aligned} w y_0 & =2 v \\ 2 \pi n y_0 & =2 n \lambda, \\ \lambda & =\pi y_0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In photoelectric effect, the number of photo-electrons emitted is proportional to
questions.solution: By increasing the intensity of incident beam, the number of emitted photo-electrons can be increased. Hence, number of photo-electrons emitted is proportional to the intensity of the incident beam.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelength $\lambda$ of a photon and the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron have the same value. Find the ratio of energy of photon to the kinetic energy of electron in terms of mass $m$, speed of light $c$ and planck constant.
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength
$h=\frac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow v=\frac{h}{m \lambda}...(i)$
$E_p=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$ (since $\lambda$ is same $)...(ii)$
Kinetic energy of photon $=\frac{E_p}{E_e}=\frac{h c / \lambda}{\frac{1}{2} 1 m u^2}=\frac{2 h c}{\lambda m v^2}$
Substituting value of $v$ from Eq. (i), we get
$\frac{E_p}{E_e}=\frac{2 h c}{\lambda_m\left(\frac{h}{m \lambda}\right)^2}=\frac{2 \lambda m c}{h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Minimum excitation potential of Bohr's first orbit in hydrogen atom is
questions.solution:
From the relation
$\begin{aligned} \Delta E & =13.6 \mathrm{eV}-\frac{13.6 \mathrm{eV}}{n^2} \\ & =13.6 \mathrm{eV}-\frac{13.6 \mathrm{eV}}{(2)^2}=10.2 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Therefore, excitation potential
$=\frac{10.2}{e} \mathrm{eV}=10.2 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of frequency $v$ falls on material of threshold frequency $v_0$. Maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is proportional to
questions.solution:
When light falls on a metallic surface, ejection of photoelectron results. In this process, conservation of energy holds.
Thus, from law of conservation of energy, Energy imparted by the photon
$=$ Maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron + work function of the metal
or $\quad h v=(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }+\phi$
but $\phi=h v_0, v_0$ being threshold frequency.
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & (\mathrm{KE})_{\max }=h v-h v_0 \\ \text { or } & (\mathrm{KE})_{\max } \propto v-v_0\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hard $X$-rays for the study of fractures in bones should have a minimum wavelength of $10^{-11} \mathrm{~m}$. The accelerating voltage for electrons in $X$-ray machine should be
questions.solution:
From conservation of energy the kinetic energy of electron equals the maximum photon energy (we neglect the work function $\phi$ because it is normally so small compared to $e V_0$ ).
$\therefore \quad e V_0=h v_{\max }$
or $e V_0=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min }}$
$\therefore \quad V_0=\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{\min }}$
or $\begin{aligned} V_0 & =\frac{12400 \times 10^{-10}}{10^{-11}} \\ & =124 \mathrm{kV}\end{aligned}$
Hence, accelerating voltage for electrons in $X$-ray machine should be less than 124 kV .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In photoelectric effect, the electrons are ejected from metals if the incident light has a certain minimum
questions.solution: In photoelectric effect for a given photosensitive material, there exists a certain minimum cut-off frequency, called the threshold frequency, below which no emission of photoelectrons takes place no matter how intense the light is.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photosensitive metallic surface has work function, $h v_0$. If photons of energy $2 h v_0$ fall on this surface, the electrons come out with a maximum velocity of $4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. When the photon energy is increased to $5 h v_0$, then maximum velocity of photoelectrons will be
questions.solution:
The solution to our problem consists in Einstein's photoelectric equation.
Einstein's photoelectric equation can be written as $\frac{1}{2} m v^2=h v-\phi$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} m \times\left(4 \times 10^6\right)^2=2 h v_0-h v_0...(i)$
and $\frac{1}{2} m \times v^2=5 h v_0-h v_0...(ii)$
Dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get
$\frac{v^2}{\left(4 \times 10^6\right)^2}=\frac{4 h v_0}{h v_0}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & v^2=4 \times 16 \times 10^{12} \\ \Rightarrow & v^2=64 \times 10^{12} \\ \therefore & v=8 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{array}$
NOTE: The efficiency of photoelectric effect is less than $1 \%$.ie, number of photons less than $1 \%$ are capable of ejecting photoelectrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If alpha, beta and gamma rays carry same momentum, which has the longest wavelength?
questions.solution:
On the basis of dual nature of light, Lious de-Broglie suggested that the dual nature is not only of light, but each moving material particle has the dual nature. He assumed a wave to be associated with each moving material particle which is called the matter wave. The wavelength of this wave is determined by the momentum of the particle. If $p$ is the momentum of the particle, the wavelength of the wave associated with it is
$\lambda=\frac{h}{p}$
where $h$ is Planck's constant.
Since, it is given that, alpha, beta and gamma rays carry same momentum, so they will have same wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The light rays having photons of energy 1.8 eV are falling on a metal surface having a work function $1 \cdot 2 \mathrm{eV}$. What is the stopping potential to be applied to stop the emitting electrons ?
questions.solution:
Maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron
$\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{KE})_{\max }=E-\phi \\ & =1.8-1.2 \\ & =0.6 \mathrm{eV} \\ & \text { i.e., } \quad e V_0=(\mathrm{KE})_{\max } \\ & \text { or } \quad e V_0=0.6 \mathrm{eV} \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Stopping potential $V_0=0.6 \mathrm{eV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If work function of a metal is 4.2 eV , the cut off wavelength is
questions.solution:
Suppose the cut off wavelength is represented by $\lambda_0$
So, $\frac{h c}{\lambda_0}=$ work function $=W_0$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{h c}{\lambda_0} & =4.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ \lambda_0 & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & =2946 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\ & \approx 2950 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work function of sodium is 2.3 eV . The threshold wavelength of sodium will be
questions.solution:
Here : work function
$W_0=2.3 \mathrm{eV}=2.3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}$
where $\lambda_0$ is the threshold wavelength for sodium
Then,
$\begin{aligned}
W_0 & =\frac{h c}{\lambda_0} \text { or } \lambda_0=\frac{h c}{W_0} \\
\lambda_0 & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{2.3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\
& =5380 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}=5380 Å
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy of an electron is 5 eV . Calculate the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it $\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right.$, $m_e=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ )
questions.solution:
Wavelength associated with an electron
$\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{19}}} \\ & =5.47 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}=5.47 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
If frequency of a photon is $6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$, then find its wavelength.
[Take, speed of light, $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ ]
questions.solution:
Given, Frequency of photon $(v)=6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Speed of light $(\mathrm{c})=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Wavelength of photon $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{v}}$
$=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{14}}=0.5 \times 10^{-6}=500 \times 10^{-9}=500 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Work function of a metal is $5.2 \times 10^{-18}$, then its threshold wavelength will be
questions.solution:
Work function $W=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$
$\begin{aligned} \lambda & =\frac{h c}{W} \\
& =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{5.26 \times 10^{-18}}=344.4 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which one of the following graph represents the variation of maximum kinetic energy $\left(E_k\right)$ of the emitted electrons with frequency $v$ in photoelectric effect correctly?
questions.solution:
According to photoelectric equation
$E_k=h v-h v_0$  ...(i)
If the energy of photon $(h v)$ is less than the work function $\left(h v_0\right)$ of metallic surface, then electrons will never be ejected from surface regardless of intensity of incident light.
Also, from Eq. (i), when
$v=v_0, E_k=0$
$\therefore$ Graph (4) represents variation of $E_k$ with $v$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work function for metals $A, B$ and $C$ are respectively $1.92 \mathrm{eV}, 2.0 \mathrm{eV}$ and 5 eV. According to Einstein's equation the metals which will emit photo, electrons for a radiation of wavelength $4100 Å$ is/are
questions.solution:
Work function for wavelength of $4100 Å$ is
$\begin{aligned} W & =\frac{h c}{\lambda} \\ & =\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4100 \times 10^{-10}} \\ & =4.8 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV} \\ & =3 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Now, we have
$\begin{aligned} & W_A=1.22 \mathrm{eV} \\ & W_B=2.0 \mathrm{eV} \\ & W_C=5 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Since, $W_A \lt W$
and $W_B \lt W$, hence $A$ and $B$ will emit photoelectrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Relation between wavelength of photon and electron of same energy is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Wavelength } \lambda=\frac{h c}{p} \\ & \therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{h c}{\sqrt{2 m E}} \therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Mass of photon is less than that of electron.
$\therefore$ Wavelength of photon is greater than that of electron $\lambda_{p h}\gt\lambda_e$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An isotropic point source emits light with wavelength 500 nm . The radiation power of the source is $P=10 \mathrm{~W}$. Find the number of photons passing through unit area per second at a distance of 3 m from the source.
questions.solution: Given, $\lambda=500 \mathrm{~nm}=500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$, As, $\begin{aligned} P & =n_0 \frac{h c}{\lambda} \\ n_0 & =\frac{P \lambda}{h c}...(i)\end{aligned}$ where, $n_0$ is number of photons per second At distance $r$ from point source, number of photons/area/time $n^{\prime}=\frac{n_0}{4 \pi r^2}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c \cdot 4 \pi r^2} \quad[$ from Eq. (i)] $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{10 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 4 \pi(3)^2} \\ & =2.23 \times 10^{17} / \mathrm{m}^2 . \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work functions for metals $A, B$ and $C$ are respectively $1.92 \mathrm{eV}, 2.0 \mathrm{eV}$ and $5 eV$ . According to Einstein's equation, the metals which will emit photoelectrons for a radiation of wavelength $4100 Å$ is/are
questions.solution:
That metal will emit photoelectrons which has work function lower than that obtained with the radiation of $4100 Å$.
Work function for wavelength of $4100 Å$ is
$\begin{aligned} W & =\frac{h c}{\lambda} \\ & =\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4100 \times 10^{-10}} \\ & =4.8 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =\frac{48 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=3 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Now, we have
$\begin{aligned}& W_A=1.92 \mathrm{eV} \\& W_B=2.0 \mathrm{eV} \\
& W_C=5 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Since, $W_A \lt W$ and $W_B \lt W$, hence, $A$ and $B$ will emit photoelectrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the given figure what will be the coefficient of mutual inductance
questions.solution: Magnetic field due to wire $\begin{aligned} B & =\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi x} \cdot i \\ d A & =a d x\end{aligned}$ and $\quad d \phi=B \cdot d A$ On integrating both sides, $\begin{aligned} \int d \phi & =\int B d A \\ \phi & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi} \int_b^{a+b} \frac{a d x}{x} \\ & =\left.\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi} a \ln x\right|_b ^{a+b} \\ & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi} a[\ln (a+b)-\ln b] \\ & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi} a\left[\ln \left(1+\frac{a}{b}\right)\right]=M i\end{aligned}$ where, $M=$ coefficient of mutual inductance $\therefore \quad M=\frac{\mu_0 a}{2 \pi} \ln \left(1+\frac{a}{b}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In 0.2 s, the current in a coil increases from 2.0 A to 3.0 A. If inductance of coil is 60 mH, then induced current in external resistance of $3 \Omega$ will be
questions.solution:
Induced emf
$\begin{aligned}|e| & =L \frac{d i}{d t}=\left(60 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times \frac{(3.0-2.0)}{0.2} \\ & =\frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}{0.2}=0.3 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
Induced current,
$\mathrm{t} i=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{0.3}{3}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of inductance 300 mH and resistance $2 \Omega$ is connected to a source of voltage 2 V . The current reaches half of its steady state value in
questions.solution: The current at any instant is given by $I=I_0\left(1-e^{-R t / L}\right)$ $\begin{aligned} \frac{I_0}{2} & =I_0\left(1-e^{-R t / L}\right) \\ \frac{1}{2} & =\left(1-e^{-R t / L}\right) \\ e^{-R t / L} & =1 / 2 \\ \frac{R t}{L} & =\ln 2\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad t=\frac{L}{R} \ln 2 & =\frac{300 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \times 0.693 \\ & =150 \times 0.693 \times 10^{-3} \\ & =0.10395 \mathrm{~s}=0.1 \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Induced emf in the coil depends upon
options: rate of change of linked flux
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law, "the induced emf in a closed loop equals the time rate of change of magnetic flux through the loop".
i.e., $e=-\frac{d \phi_B}{d t}$
Hence, induced emf in a coil depends on rate of change of flux.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The thermo emf of a hypothetical thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of hot junction as $E=a \theta+b \theta^2$ in volts, where the ratio $\frac{a}{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
options: no neutral temperature is possible for this thermocouple
questions.solution:
$E=a \theta+b \theta^2$ so $\frac{d E}{d \theta}=a+2 b \theta$
For neutral temperature $\theta=\theta_{\mathrm{n}}, \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=0$
or $a+2 b \theta_n=0$
or $\theta_{\mathrm{n}}=-\mathrm{a} / 2 \mathrm{~b}=-700 / 2=-350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
As cold junction is at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ so negative neutral temperature can not be possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a battery the electromotive force is 1.5 V . Terminal voltage is 1.25 V . Power supplied to external resistance is 2.5 W . Find the internal resistance of battery.
questions.solution:
EMF of battery, $\mathrm{E}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}$
Terminal voltage, $\mathrm{V}=1.25 \mathrm{~V}$
Power supplied to external resistance (R),
i.e. 
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{P} & =2.5 \mathrm{~W} \\
\mathrm{P} & =\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{\mathrm{R}} \\
2.5 & =\frac{(1.25)^2}{\mathrm{R}}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{R}=\frac{1.25 \times 1.25}{2.5} \\
\Rightarrow & \mathrm{R}=0.625 \Omega
\end{array}$
When battery is used to supply the power to load resistance, then $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{Ir}$
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow V & =E-\left(\frac{V}{R}\right) \cdot r \Rightarrow r=\left(\frac{E}{V}-1\right) R=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right) 0.625 \\
& =0.125 \Omega
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the current 3 A flowing in the primary coil is made zero in 0.1 sec, the emf induced in the secondary coil is 1.5 volt. The mutual inductance between the coils is
questions.solution:
Here: $e=1.5$ volt
$\Delta i=0-3=-3 \mathrm{~A}, \Delta t=0.1 \mathrm{sec}$
Now the emf induced in secondary coil
$e=\frac{M \Delta i}{\Delta t} \quad$ (numerically)
or $\quad 1.5=\frac{M \times 3}{0.1}$
or $\quad M=\frac{1.5 \times 0.1}{3}=0.05 \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Lenz law is based on principle of conservation of
questions.solution: Lenz law is based on the principle of conservation of energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic flux through each turn of a coil having 200 turns is given as $\left(t^2-2 t\right) \times 10^{-3}$ Wb , where $t$ is in second. The emf induced in the coil at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
questions.solution:
Given magnetic flux
$\phi=\left(t^2-2 t\right) \times 10^{-3}$
On differentiating with recpect to time $t$
$\frac{d \phi}{d t}=(2 t-2) \times 10^{-3}$
At time $\quad t=3 \mathrm{~s}$
$\begin{aligned}\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|_{t=3 \mathrm{~s}} & =(2 \times 3-2) \times 10^{-3} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~Wb} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ \text { Now, }|e| & =N\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right| \\ & =200 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \\ & =800 \times 10^{-3}=0.8 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In 0.1 s , the current in a coil increases from 1 A to 1.5A. If inductance of coil is 60 mH , then induced current in external resistance of $3 \Omega$ will be
questions.solution:
Induced emf,
$\begin{aligned}
|e|=L \frac{d i}{d t} & =\left(60 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times \frac{(1.5-1)}{0.1} \\
& =\frac{60 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{0.1}=0.3 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$
Induced current,
$i=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{0.3}{3}=0.1 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The impedance of a circuit, when a resistance $R$ and an inductor of inductance $L$ are connected in series in an AC circuit of frequency $f$, is
questions.solution:
In $L-R$ circuit,
Impedance $Z=\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2}$
Here, $\quad X_L=\omega L=2 \pi f L$
$\therefore \quad Z=\sqrt{R^2+4 \pi^2 f^2 L^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of mean radius of 7 cm and having 4000 turns is rotated at the rate of 1800 revolutions per minute in the earth's magnetic field $(B=0.5$ gauss), the emf induced in coil will be
questions.solution:
Here: $n=4000, B=0.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$
Rate of rotation of coil $=1800 \mathrm{rev} / \mathrm{min}$ $=\frac{1800}{60}=30 \mathrm{rev} / \mathrm{sec}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=2 \pi f=2 \pi \times 30=60 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}, \\ & r=7 \mathrm{~cm}=0.07 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
Now area of coil
$A=\pi r^2=\pi \times(0.07)^2=49 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$
Now the maximum energy induced is
$\begin{aligned}e & =B A n \omega \\& =0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 49 \pi \times 10^{-4} \times 4000 \times 60 \pi \\& =0.58 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Who discovered X-rays ?
questions.solution: Wilhelm Roentgen was already working on the effects of cathode rays during 1895, before he actually discovered X-rays.His experiments involved the passing of electric current through gases at extremely low pressure.So,the answer is option 1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A leaf which contains only green pigments, is illuminated by a laser light of wavelength $0.6328 \mu \mathrm{m}$. It would appear to be
questions.solution: If an object reflects the colour of light incident on it, it will appear with that colour but if object absorbs the colour of light, it will appear to be black. Since, the given wavelength does not belong to green, so it will be absorbed by the leaf and hence, it would appear to be black.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of electron falling on the target in an X-ray tube is $\lambda$. The cut-off wavelength of the emitted X-ray is
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength is given by
$\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}$ ...(i)
where, $E$ is the energy of the electron. The cut-off wavelength $\lambda_0$ is given by
$\lambda_0=\frac{h C}{E}$ ...(ii)
From Eq. (i),
$\lambda^2=\frac{h^2}{2 m E}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E=\frac{h^2}{2 m X^2}$ ... (iii)
Substituting the value of $E$ from Eq. (ii), we get
$\lambda_0=\frac{h c}{\frac{h^2}{2 m \lambda^2}}=\frac{2 m c \lambda^2}{h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Potential difference is given as $V(x)=-x^2 y$ volt. Find electric field at a point $(1,2)$ ?
questions.solution:
Potential difference
$\begin{aligned}
V(x) & =-x^2 y V \\
E & =-\Delta V=-\left[\hat{i} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-x^2 y\right)+\hat{j} \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-x^2 y\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =-\left[-2 x y \hat{i}-x^2 \hat{j}\right]=2 x y \hat{i}+x^2 \hat{j} \\ \text { E at }(1,2) & =2 \times 1 \times 2 \hat{i}+1^2 \hat{j} \\ & =4 \hat{i}+\hat{j} V / m\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A piece of blue glass heated to a high temperature and a piece of red glass at room temperature are taken inside a dimly-lit room. Then,
options: the blue piece will look blue and the red piece will look red as usual.
the red piece will look brighter red and the blue piece will look ordinary blue.
the blue will look brighter as compared to the red piece.
both the pieces will look equal red.
questions.solution: When blue glass is heated at high temperature, it absorbs all the radiation of higher wavelength except blue. If it is taken inside a dimly-lit room, it emits all the radiation of higher wavelength, hence it looks brighter as compared to the red piece.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam of light travelling along $X$-axis is described by the electric field $E_Y=600 \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{m}}$. $\sin \omega\left(t-\frac{x}{c}\right)$, the maximum magnetic force on a charge $q=2 e$, moving along $Y$-axis with the speed of $3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, electric field, $E_y=600 \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{m}} \cdot \sin \omega\left(t-\frac{x}{\mathrm{c}}\right)$...(i)
We know that,
$E=E_0 \sin \omega\left(t-\frac{x}{c}\right)$.....(ii)
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $E_0=600 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ and $C=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Maximum magnetic field,
$\begin{aligned} & B_0=\frac{E_0}{C} \\ & B_0=\frac{600}{3 \times 10^8}=2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad F_m & =q v B_0=2 \mathrm{ev} B_0 \\ & =2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \\ & =19.2 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 20 kV potential is applied across X-ray tube, the minimum wavelength of X-ray emitted will be
questions.solution:
From relation, $\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min }}=e V$ or $\quad \lambda_{\min }=\frac{h c}{e V}$
or $\begin{aligned} \lambda_{\min } & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 20 \times 10^3} \\ & =0.62 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}=0.62 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\lambda_v, \lambda_x$ and $\lambda_m$ represent the wavelengths of visible light, X-rays and microwaves respectively, then
questions.solution:
Wavelength order of given rays are listed below:
$\begin{array}{l|l}
\hline \text{Rays} & \text{Wavelength} \\
\hline \text{Visible light} & 4000-7900 \\
\text{X-rays} & 1-100 \\
\text{Microwaves} & 10^7-10^9 \\
\hline
\end{array}$
Obviously, $\lambda_x \lt \lambda_v \lt \lambda_m$
or $\lambda_m\gt\lambda_v\gt\lambda_x$
NOTE : Visible light, X-rays and microwaves are all electromagnetic waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum wavelength of X-ray emitted by X-ray tube is $0.4125 Å$. The accelerating voltage is
questions.solution:
From the formula
$\begin{aligned} e V & =h \frac{c}{\lambda} \\ V & =\frac{h c}{e \lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.4125 \times 10^{-10}} \\ & =30 \times 10^3 \text { volt }=30 \mathrm{kvolt}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force of attraction between two charges $8 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is 0.2 N . Find the distance of separation
questions.solution:
Given that, $F=0.2 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{Q}_1=8 \mu \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Q}_2=4 \mu \mathrm{C}$
$\because$ Force between two charges, $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{Q}_1 \mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
or $\quad 0.2=9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{8 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
$\therefore \quad r^2=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{0.2}$
or $\quad \mathrm{r}=1.2 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A non-conducting ring of radius 0.5 m carries a total charge of $1.11 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{C}$ distributed non-uniformly on its circumference producing on its circumference on electric field $\mathbf{E}$, everywhere in space.
The value of the line integral $\int_{l=\infty}^{l=0}(-\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d l})$ $(l=0$ being centre of ring) in volts is
questions.solution:
From definition of potential difference $\int_{l=\infty}^{l=0} \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d l}=$ potential difference at infinity and at centre of ring $=V_{\text {centre }}-V_{\text {infinity }}$
But by convention $V_{\text {infinity }}=0$ and $V_{\text {centre }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{R}$ $=9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{1 \cdot 11 \times 10^{-10}}{0.5}=2 \mathrm{volt}$ $\therefore \int_{l=\infty}^{l=0}(-\mathbf{E} \cdot \mathbf{d l})=2$ volt -0 volt $=2$ volt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical conducting balls $A$ and $B$ have positive charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ respectively. But $q_1 \neq q_2$. The balls are brought together so that they touch each other and then kept in their original positions. The force between them is
options: less than that before the balls touched
greater than that before the balls touched
same as that before the balls touched
questions.solution: According to coulomb's law, the force of repulsion between them is given by $F=\frac{q_1 q_2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}$ When the charged spheres $A$ and $B$ are brought in contact, each sphere will attain equal charge $q^{\prime}$ $q^{\prime}=\frac{q_1+q_2}{2}$ Now, the force of repulsion between them at the same distance $r$ is $F^{\prime}=\frac{q^{\prime} \times q^{\prime}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\left(\frac{q_1+q_2}{2}\right)\left(\frac{q_1+q_2}{2}\right)}{r^2}$ $=\frac{\left(\frac{q_1+q_2}{2}\right)^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}$ $\operatorname{As}\left(\frac{q_1+q_2}{2}\right)^2 \gt q_1 q_2$ $\therefore F^{\prime} \gt F$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positively charged ball hangs from a silk thread. We put a positive test charge $q_0$ at a point and measure $F / q_0$, then it can be predicted that the electric field strength $E$
questions.solution: Due to presence of test charge $q_0$ in front of positively charged ball, there would be a redistribution of charge on the ball. In the redistribution of charge, there will be less charge on front half surface and more charge on the back half surface. As a result, the net force $F$ between ball and charge will decrease, i.e. the electric field is decreased. Thus, actual electric field will be greater than $F / q_0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If same charge $q$ is placed inside a sphere and cube having radius 1 m and side 2 m respectively. What will be the ratio of flux passing through them?
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, electric flux passing through a closed surface is equal to $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}$ times the charge (q) enclosed by the surface
i.e.
$\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\varepsilon_0}$
Since, cube and sphere, both enclose same charge, therefore flux passing through cube and sphere is same. i.e. $\phi_{\text {sphere }}=\phi_{\text {cube }}$
$\therefore \quad \phi_{\text {sphere }}: \phi_{\text {cube }}=1: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If point charges $\mathrm{Q}_1=2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{Q}_2=3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C}$ are at 30 cm separation, then find electrostatic force between them
questions.solution:
$\because$ Electrostatic force, $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{Q}_1 \mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
$=9 \times 10^9 \times \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 10^{-7}}{\left(30 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2}=6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The charges on two spheres are $+7 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ respectively. They experience a force $F$. If each of them is given an additional charge of $-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$, then the new force of attraction will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } q_1=+7 g \mu \mathrm{C}=+7 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \\ & q_2=-5 \mu \mathrm{C}=-5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
New force $F^{\prime}=$ ?
We know that, $F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$
$\begin{aligned} F & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\left(+7 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(-5 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{r^2} \\ & =-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{35 \times 10^{-12}}{r^2} \mathrm{~N} \\ F^{\prime} & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\left(+5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(-7 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{r^2} \\ & =-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{35 \times 10^{-12}}{r^2} \mathrm{~N} \\ & \Rightarrow\end{aligned}$
$F^{\prime}=F$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle is accelerated by a potential of 200 V . If its velocity is $8.4 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then value of $e / m$ for that particle is
questions.solution:
If electron is accelerated by voltage $V$, then its kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=q V$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{q}{m} & =\frac{v^2}{2 V} \\ & =\frac{\left(8.4 \times 10^8\right)^2}{2 \times 200}=1.76 \times 10^{15}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three equal charges, each having a magnitude of $2.0 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C}$, are placed at the three corners of a right angled triangle of sides $3 \mathrm{~cm}, 4 \mathrm{~cm}$ and 5 cm . The force (in magnitude) on the charge at the right angled corner is
questions.solution: Consider the diagram The situation is shown in figure. The force on $A$ due to $B$ is $F_1=\frac{\left(2.0 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(2.0 \times 10^{-6}\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(4 \mathrm{~cm})^2}$ $\begin{aligned}=9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-12} & \times \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{-4}} \\ & =22.5 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$ As the force due to $C$ on $A$ is 40 N . Thus, net force (electric) $\begin{aligned} F & =\sqrt{F_1^2+F_1^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(22.5)^2+(40)^2}=45.9 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charges of $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $+20 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are separated by a distance 2 cm . The net potential (electric) due to the pair at the middle point of the line joining the two changes, is
questions.solution:
Using the equation $V=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 I}$
The potential due to $+10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is
$V_1=\frac{\left(10 \times 10^{-6}\right) \times\left(9 \times 10^9\right)}{1 \times 10^{-2}}=9 \mathrm{MV}$
The potential due to $+20 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is
$V_2=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^9}{1 \times 10^{-2}}=18 \mathrm{MV}$
The net potential at the given point is
$9 \mathrm{MV}+18 \mathrm{MV}=27 \mathrm{MV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $V=$ axy, then electric field at a point will be proportional t0
questions.solution: Electric field $E \propto r^2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field at point 20 cm away from the centre of dielectric sphere is $100 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, radius of sphere is 10 cm , then the value of electric field at a distance 3 cm from the centre is
questions.solution: Inside a dielectric sphere, electric field is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the electric flux entering and leaving an enclosed surface respectively are $\phi$, and $\phi_2$, the electric charge inside the surface will be
questions.solution:
According to Gauss theorem "the net electric flux through any closed surface is equal to the net charge inside the surface divided by $\varepsilon_0{ }^{\prime \prime}$.
Therefore, $\quad \phi \propto \frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$
Let $-q_1$ be the charge, due to which flux $\phi_1$ is entering the surface,
$\phi_1=\frac{-q_1}{\varepsilon_0}$
$-q_1=\varepsilon_0 \phi_1$
Let $+q_2$ be the charge, due to which flux $\phi_2$ is entering the surface.
$\phi_2=\frac{q_2}{\varepsilon_0}$
or $\quad q_2=\varepsilon_0 \phi_2$
So, electric charge inside the surface
$=q_2-q_1$
$\begin{aligned} & =\varepsilon_0 \phi_2+\varepsilon_0 \phi_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad=\varepsilon_0\left(\phi_2+\phi_1\right) \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three points charges $+q,-2 q$ and $+q$ are placed at points $(x=0, y=a, z=0),(x=0, y=0, z=0)$ and $\quad(x=a, y=0, z=0)$, respectively. The magnitude and direction of the electric dipole moment vector of this charge assembly are
options:
$\sqrt{2} q a$ along the line joining points
$(x=0, y=0, z=0) \text { and }(x=a, y=a, z=0)$
qa along the line joining points $(x=0, y=0, z=0)$ and $(x=a, y=a, z=0)$
questions.solution: $O$ is the origin at which $-2 q$ charge is placed. The system is equivalent to two dipoles along $x$ and $y$-directions respectively. The dipole moments of two dipoles are shown in figure. The resultant dipole moment will be directed along $O P$ where $P=(a, a, 0)$. The magnitude of resultant dipole moment is $p^{\prime}=\sqrt{p^2+p^2}=\sqrt{(q a)^2+(q a)^2}=\sqrt{2} q a$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $q$ is located at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face is
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's theorem, electric flux through the cube (closed surface),
$\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$
Since, cube has six surfaces and all the faces are symmetrical, therefore electric flux through any face
$\phi^{\prime}=\frac{q}{6 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{4 \pi q}{6\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_0\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shows three spherical and equipotential surfaces $A, B$ and $C$ round a point charge $q$. The potential difference $V_A-V_B=V_B-V_C$. If $t_1$ and $t_2$ be the distances between them, then
questions.solution:
Potential difference between two equipotential surfaces $A$ and $B$,
$V_A-V_B=k q\left(\frac{1}{r_A}-\frac{1}{r_B}\right)=k q\left(\frac{r_B-r_A}{r_A r_B}\right)=\frac{k q t_1}{r_A r_B}$
or $\quad t_1=\frac{\left(V_A-V_B\right) r_A r_B}{k q}$
or
$t_1 \propto r_A r_B$
Similarly, $t_2 \propto r_B r_C$
Since, $r_A \lt r_B \lt r_C$, therefore, $r_A r_B \lt r_B r_C$
$\therefore \quad t_1 \lt t_2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two spherical conductors $B$ and $C$ having equal radii and carrying equal charges in them repel each other with a force $F$ when kept apart at some distance. A third spherical conductor having same radius as that of $B$ but uncharged, is brought in contact with $B$, then brought in contact with $C$ and finally removed away from both. The new force of repulsion between $B$ and $C$ is
questions.solution:
Let the spherical conductors $B$ and $C$ have same charge as $q$. The electric force between them is
$F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{r^2}$
$r$, being the distance between them.
When third uncharged conductor $A$ is brought in contact with $B$, then charge on each conductor
$q_A=q_B=\frac{q_A+q_B}{2}=\frac{0+q}{2}=\frac{q}{2}$
When this conductor $A$ is now brought in contact with $C$, then charge on each conductor
$q_A=q_C=\frac{q_A+q_C}{2}=\frac{(q / 2)+q}{2}=\frac{3 q}{4}$
Hence, electric force acting between $B$ and $C$ is
$\begin{aligned}
F^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_B q_C}{r^2} & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(q / 2)(3 q / 4)}{r^2} \\
& =\frac{3}{8}\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{r^2}\right]=\frac{3 F}{8}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged oil drop is suspended in unifor m field of $3 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ so that it neither falls nor rises. The charge on the drop will be (Take the mass of the charge $=9.9 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { In steady state, } \\ & \text { electric force on drop } \\ & \quad \begin{aligned} \quad & =\text { weight of drop } \\ \begin{aligned} \therefore\end{aligned} \quad q E= & m g \\ & q=\frac{m g}{E} \\ & =\frac{9.9 \times 10^{-15} \times 10}{3 \times 10^4} \\ & =3.3 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is not true for equipotential surface for uniform electric field?
options: Electric lines are perpendicular to equipotential surface
questions.solution: An equipotential surface is a surface with a constant value of potential at all points on the surface. For a uniform electric field, say, along the $x$-axis, the equipotential surfaces are planes normal to the $x$-axis, $i e$, plane parallel to the $y$-z plane. Equipotential surfaces for a dipole and its electric field lines are shown in figure. As said above that on equipotential surface, potential at all points is constant, this means that on equipotential surface work done in moving a test charge from one point to other point is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As per this diagram a point charge $+q$ is placed at the origin $O$. Work done in taking another point charge $-Q$ from the point $A$ [coordinates $(0, a)]$ to another point $B[\operatorname{coordinates}(a, 0)]$ along the straight path $A B$ is
questions.solution: The work done in carrying a test charge consists in product of difference of potentials at points $A$ and $B$ and value of test charge. Potential at A $V_A=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{a}$ Potential at B $V_B=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{a}$ Thus, work done in carrying a test charge $-Q$ from $A$ to $B$ $W=\left(V_A-V_B\right)(-Q)=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two similar charges having mass $m$ and $2 m$ are placed in an electric field. The ratio of their kinetic energy is :
questions.solution:
Force on charged particle is electric field
$F=q E$
$\therefore$ Acceleration $a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{q E}{m}$
$\therefore$ Velocity
$\begin{aligned} v & =u+a t \\ & =0+\frac{q E}{m} t\end{aligned}$
So, kinetic energy
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\frac{1}{2} m_1\left(\frac{q E}{m_1} t\right)^2}{\frac{1}{2} m_2\left(\frac{q E}{m_2} t\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{m_2}{m_1}=\frac{2 m}{m} \\ & =\frac{2}{1} \\ & =2: 1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the correct diagram of electric lines of forces for negative charge.
questions.solution: Electric lines of forces always emerge from positive charge and enters into negative charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle ' $q$ ' is shot with speed $v$ towards another fixed charged particle $Q$. It approaches $Q$ upto a closest distance $r$ and then returns. If $q$ were given a speed $2 v$, the closest distance of approach would be
questions.solution:
At closest distance $r$ its whole KE is converted into PE
$\therefore \frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q \cdot q}{r} \Rightarrow r=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q \cdot q}{m v^2}$
In next case, $r^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q q}{m(2 v)^2}$
$\Rightarrow r^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q q}{m v^2}\right) \Rightarrow r^{\prime}=r / 4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $Q$ is distributed uniformly in a sphere (solid). Then, the electric field at any point $r$, where, $r \lt R$ ( $r$ is the radius of sphere) varies as :
questions.solution:
. Electric field intensity at a point inside the sphere $\quad E=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 R} r$
$\therefore \quad E \propto r$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Van De graff generator is used to
options: create a high potential of range of few million volts
create a low potential of range of few million volts
to de-accelerate projectile; like protons, deutrons etc.
it can not be used to study collision experiments in physics
questions.solution: Van De graff generator is a device used to create a high potential difference of range of few million volts.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two equal -ve charges $-q$ are fixed at the point $(0, a)$ and $(0, -a)$ on the $y$-axis. A positive charge $Q$ is released from rest at the poin $(2 a, 0)$ on the $x$-axis. The charge will :
options: move to the origin and remain at rest
questions.solution: Component of force on charge $+Q$ at $P$, along $x$-axis, $\begin{aligned} F_x & =\frac{2 Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)} \cos \theta \\ & =\frac{2 Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)} \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{a^2+x^2}} \\ & =\frac{2 Q q x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\end{aligned}$ Which is not directly proportional to $x$. So, motion is oscillatory but not SHM.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The concentric, conducting spherical shells $X, Y$ and $Z$ with radii $r, 2 r$ and $3 r$, respectively. $X$ and $Z$ are connected by a conducting wire and $Y$ is uniformly charged to charge $Q$ as shown in figure. Charges on shells $X$ and $Z$ will be
questions.solution: Let charges on $X$ and $Z$ be $q_x$ and $q_Z$ By conservation of charge, $q_x+q_z=0 \Rightarrow q_z=-q_x$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad V_x & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q_x}{r}+\frac{Q}{2 r}+\frac{q_z}{3 r}\right] \\ V_z & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{q_x}{3 r}+\frac{Q}{3 r}+\frac{q_z}{3 r}\right]\end{aligned}$ Since, $X$ and $Z$ are connected. $\therefore \quad V_x=V_Z$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \frac{q_X}{r}+\frac{Q}{2 r}+\frac{q_Z}{3 r} & =\frac{q_X}{3 r}+\frac{Q}{3 r}+\frac{q_Z}{3 r} \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{2 q_X}{3} & =\frac{-Q}{6} \\ & \therefore & q_X & =\frac{-Q}{4} \\ \because & & q_X+q_Z & =0 \Rightarrow q_Z=-q_X \\ & & & =\frac{Q}{4}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting sphere of radius $R=20 \mathrm{~cm}$ is given a charge $Q=16 \mu \mathrm{C}$. What is $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}$ at centre?
questions.solution: The value of $E$ at the centre of sphere is zero. Since, the centre of sphere is placed with in the Gaussian surface. Hence $E=0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If an insulated non-conducting sphere of radius $R$ has charge density $\rho$. The electric field at a distance $r$ from the centre of sphere $(r \gt R)$ will be
questions.solution:
Charge which is enclosed in the surface is given by $\quad \sigma=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho$
Now according to Gaussian law
$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon \phi E d s & =\sigma, \varepsilon_0 E 4 \pi r^2=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho \\ E & =\frac{\rho r}{3 \varepsilon_0}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The ratio of electrostatic and gravitational forces acting between electron and proton separated by a distance $5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~m}$, will be
(Charge on electron $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$, mass of electron $=9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$, mass of proton $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$, $\left.G=6.7 \times 10^{-11} \quad \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{kg}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Gravitational force $F=\frac{G M_1 M_2}{r^2}$
$F_G=\frac{6.7 \times 10^{-11} \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-11}\right)^2}$
$=3.9 \times 10^{-47} \mathrm{~N}$
Electrostatic force $F_E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$
$F_e=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{\left(5 \times 10^{-11}\right)^2}$
$=9.22 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~N}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\text { Electrostatic force }}{\text { Gravitational force }}=\frac{F_E}{F_G}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{9.22 \times 10^{-8}}{3.9 \times 10^{-47}} \\ & =2.36 \times 10^{39}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two small balls, each carrying a charge $q$ are suspended by equal insulator strings of length 1 m from the hook of a stand. This arrangement is carried in a satellite in space. The tension in each string will be
questions.solution:
Suppose, $T$ be the tension in the spring $T$. Also in the satellite, two balls will be in a straight line. So, force acting on the balls
$F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{(2 l)^2}$ $\ldots$ (i)
This force will be balanced by tension in the string.
i.e.,
$T=F$ $\ldots$ (ii)
Now, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$T=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q^2}{4 l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle is suspended in equilibrium in a uniform vertical electric field of intensity $20000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$. If mass of the particle is $9.6 \times 10^{-16} \mathrm{~kg}$, the charge on it and excess number of electrons on the-particle respectively are $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Charge on the particle is given by
$\begin{aligned}q E & =m g \text { or } q=\frac{m g}{E} \\& =\frac{9.6 \times 10^{-16} \times 10}{20000}=4.8 \times 10^{-19}
\end{aligned}$
When there is an excess of $n$ electrons on the particle, then
$q=n e$
So,
$n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A certain charge $Q$ is divided into two parts $q$ and $Q-q$. How the charge $Q$ and $q$ must be related so that when $q$ and $(Q-q)$ is placed at a certain distance apart experience maximum electrostatic repulsion?
questions.solution:
The electrostatic force of repulsion between the charge $q$ and $(Q-q)$ at separation $r$ is given by
$F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q(Q-q)}{r^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q Q-q^2}{r^2}$
If $F$ is maximum, then $\partial F / \partial q=0$ i.e. $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{(Q-2 q)}{r^2}=0$
As $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}$ is constant, therefore
$Q-2 q=0$ or $Q=2 q$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylinder of radius $R$ and length $L$ is placed in a uniform electric field $E$ parallel to the cylinder axis. The total flux for the surface of the cylinder is given by
questions.solution: Flux through surface $A$, $\begin{aligned} & \phi_A=E \times \pi R^2 \\ & \phi_B=-E \times \pi R^2\end{aligned}$ Flux through curved surface, $C=\int E \cdot d S$ $=\int E d S \cos 90^{\circ}=0$ $\therefore$ Total flux through cylinder $=\phi_A+\phi_B+\phi_C=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Universal time is based on
options: rotational effect of the earth about its axis
orbital motion of the earth around the sun
questions.solution: Time defined in terms of the rotation of the earth is called Universal Time (UT).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $2 m$ is placed on earth's surface. Calculate the change in gravitational potential energy, if this body is taken from earth's surface to a height of $h$, where $h=4 R$.
questions.solution:
Potential energy, $U=-\frac{G M m}{r}=\frac{-2 G M m}{r} \quad[\because m=2 m]$
At earth's surface, $r=R$
$\therefore \quad U_e=-\frac{2 G M m}{R}$
Now, if a body is taken to height, $h=4 R$, then the potential energy is given by
$\begin{aligned} U_h & =-\frac{2 G M m}{R+h} \quad[\because r=h+R] \\ & =\frac{-2 G M m}{5 R}\end{aligned}$
Thus, change in gravitational potential energy,
$\begin{aligned} \Delta U & =U_h-U_e \\ & =-\frac{2 G M m}{5 R}+\frac{2 G M m}{R}=\frac{8}{5} \frac{G M m}{R}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \Delta U & =\frac{8}{5} \frac{g R^2 m}{R} \quad\left[\because G M=g R^2\right] \\ & =\frac{8}{5} m g R\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If escape velocity on earth surface is $11.1 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$, then find the escape velocity on moon surface. If mass of moon is $\frac{1}{81}$ times of mass of earth and radius of moon is $\frac{1}{4}$ times radius of earth.
questions.solution:
Given, Escape velocity on the surface of earth, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=11.1 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$
i.e.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gR}_{\mathrm{e}}} \\
& \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}}
\end{aligned}$
Mass of moon, $M_m=\frac{M_e}{81}$
Radius of moon, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}{4}$
$\therefore$ Escape velocity on the surface of moon
$\begin{aligned}
& v_m=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M_m}{R_m}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G \frac{M e}{81}}{\frac{R_e}{4}}} \\
& =\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{9} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}}}=\frac{2}{9} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}}}=\frac{2}{9} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{2}{9} \times 11.1=2.46 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The planets with radii $R_1$ and $R_2$ have densities $\rho_1, \quad \rho_2$ respectively. Their atmospheric pressures are $p_1$ and $p_2$ respectively. Therefore, the ratio of masses of their atmospheres, neglecting variation of $g$ within the limits of atmosphere is
questions.solution:
Acceleration due to gravity,
$\begin{aligned}
& g=\frac{G M}{R^2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \rho G R \\
& \therefore \frac{g_1}{g_2}=\frac{\rho_1 R_1}{\rho_2 R_2}
\end{aligned}$
Atmospheric pressure can be given by $p=\frac{W}{S}$
where, $W=$ Weight of atmosphere,
$S=$ Surface area of the planet
$\therefore \frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{\rho_1 S_1 g_2}{\rho_2 S_2 g_1}=\frac{p_1 \cdot\left(4 \pi R_1^2\right)}{p_2\left(4 \pi R_2^2\right)} \cdot \frac{\rho_2 R_1}{\rho_1 R_1}=\frac{p_1 R_1 \rho_2}{p_2 R_2 \rho_1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rocket is intended to leave the Earth's gravitational field. The fuel in its main engine is a little less than the amount that is necessary and an auxiliary engine, (only capable of operating for a short time) has to be used as well. When is it best to switch on the auxiliary engine?
options: when the rocket has nearly stopped with respect to the Earth.
questions.solution:
The rocket has to reach the highest possible total energy. If the zero level of gravitational potential energy is 'infinitely' far away, then the energy of the rocket standing on the surface of the earth is negative. The energy released during the operation of the engines increases the total energy of the rocket, and the rocket can leave, the earth's gravitational field if the sum of its potential and kinetic energies becomes positive.
The energy released in the course of the operation of the principal and auxiliary engines increases the total energy of the rocket and its ejected combustion products by a fixed value; this increase is independent of the moment when the engines are scratched on. However, the speed at which the combustion products fall back to the earth does depend on the timing of the rocket's operations. Indeed, if the auxiliary engine starts working when the rocket is at a greater height, the combustion products fall further and their speed and total energy are higher when they hit the ground. This means that the sooner the auxiliary engine is switched on, the higher the energy ultimately acquired by the rocket. The same argument is valid for the principal engine, and if the only energy consideration apply, it is best to operate the engines for the shortest time and at the highest thrust.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At what distance (in metre) from the centre of the Moon, the intensity of gravitational field will be zero? (Take, mass of Earth and Moon as $5.98 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $7.35 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~kg}$ respectively and the distance between Moon and Earth is $3.85 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m}$.)
questions.solution:
Let X be the distance of the point from the centre of earth where gravitational intensity is zero. Therefore,
$\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{x}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{GM}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\left(3.85 \times 10^8-\mathrm{x}\right)^2}$
or $\frac{\mathrm{x}}{3.85 \times 10^8-\mathrm{x}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{m}}}}=\sqrt{\frac{5.98 \times 10^{24}}{7.35 \times 10^{22}}} \cong 9$
or $\quad \frac{x}{9}+x=3.85 \times 10^8$
or $\quad \mathrm{x}=9 \times 3.85 \times 10^8 / 10=3.46 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m}$
Distance from Moon $=3.85 \times 10^8-3.46 \times 10^8$
$=3.9 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} \cong 3.85 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The escape velocity for the earth is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. The mass of another planet 100 times mass of earth and its radius is 4 times radius of the earth. The escape velocity for the planet is
questions.solution:
The escape velocity of the the planet
$\begin{aligned} v_{\text {escape }} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 G m}{R}} \propto \sqrt{\frac{M}{R}} \\ \frac{v_p}{v_e} & =\sqrt{\frac{M_p}{R_p} \times \frac{R_e}{M_e}} \\ \frac{v_p}{v_e} & =\sqrt{100 \times \frac{1}{4}}=5 \\ v_p & =5 \times 11.2=56 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Change in acceleration due to gravity is same upto a height $h$ from each other the earth surface and below depth $x$, then relation between $x$ and $h$ is ( $h$ and $x\lt\lt \lt R_e$ )
questions.solution:
Variation in $g$ at a height $h$ from the earth surface $g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R_e}\right)$
Variation in $g$ at a depth $x$ below the earth surface $g^{\prime \prime}=g\left(1-\frac{x}{R_b}\right)$
But variations are same
$\therefore \quad g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R_\theta}\right)=g\left(1-\frac{x}{R_\theta}\right)$
$\therefore \quad x=2 h$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two spherical bodies of masses $M$ and $5 M$ and radii $R$ and $2 R$ respectively are released in free space with initial separation between their centres equal to $12 R$. If they attract each other due to gravitational force only,then the distance covered by the smaller body just before collision, is
questions.solution: Let at $O$ there will be a collision. If smaller sphere moves $x$ distance to reach at $O$, then bigger sphere will move a distance of $(9 R-x)$. $\begin{aligned} F & =\frac{G M \times 5 M}{(12 R-x)^2} \\ a_{\text {small }} & =\frac{F}{M}=\frac{G \times 5 M}{(12 R-x)^2} \\ a_{\mathrm{big}} & =\frac{F}{5 M}=\frac{G M}{(12 R-x)^2} \\ x & =\frac{1}{2} a_{\text {small }} t^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G \times 5 M}{(12 R-x)^2} t^2 \ldots(i)\\ (9 R-x) & =\frac{1}{2} a_{\text {big }} t^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M}{(12 R-x)^2} t^2\ldots(ii) \end{aligned}$ Thus, dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get $\frac{x}{9 R-x}=5$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad x=45 R-5 x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 6 x=45 R \\ & \therefore \quad x=7.5 R \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite orbiting the earth in a circular orbit of radius $R$ completes one revolution in 3h. If orbital radius of geostationary satellite is 36000 km , orbital radius of earth is
questions.solution:
Time period of satellite
$T^2=\frac{4 \pi^2}{G M} r^3 \text { or } T^2=\frac{4 \pi^2}{g R^2} r^3 \quad\left[\because G M=g R^2\right]$
or $\quad T=\frac{2 \pi}{R} \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{g}}$ or $T \propto r^{3 / 2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^{3 / 2}$
Given, $\quad T_1=3 \mathrm{~h}, T_2=24 \mathrm{~h}$ (geostationary
satellite) $r_1=R, r_2=36000 \mathrm{~km}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{3}{24}=\left(\frac{R}{36000}\right)^{3 / 2}$
or $\quad R=\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^{2 / 3} \times 36000$ or $\quad R=\frac{1}{4} \times 36000=9000 \mathrm{~km}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two satellites of earth, $S_1$ and $S_2$, are moving in the same orbit. The mass of $S_1$ is four times the mass of $S_2$. Which one of the following statements is true?
options: The time period of $S_1$ is four times that of $S_2$
The potential energies of earth and satellite in the two cases are equal
$S_1$ and $S_2$ are moving with the same speed
The kinetic energies of the two satellites are equal
questions.solution:
When two satellites of earth are moving in same orbit, then time period of both are equal. From Kepler's third law
$T^2 \propto r^3$
Time period is independent of mass, hence their time periods will be equal.
The potential energy and kinetic energy are mass dependent, hence the PE and KE of satellites are not equal.
But, if they are orbiting in a same orbit, then they have equal orbital speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The speed of earth's rotation about its axis is $\omega$. Its speed is increased to $x$ times to make the effective acceleration due to gravity equal to zero at the equator. Then $x$ is
questions.solution:
Earth rotates about its own axis in 24 h .
So, $\quad T=24 \times 60 \times 60 \mathrm{~s}$
So, angular speed of earth about its own axis
$\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{24 \times 60 \times 60} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
At equator, $g^{\prime}=g-R_e \omega^{\prime 2}$
or $\quad 0=g-R_e \omega^{\prime 2}$
or $\omega^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{g}{R_e}}=\frac{2 \pi}{84.6 \times 60}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\omega^{\prime}}{\omega}=\frac{24 \times 60 \times 60}{84.6 \times 60} \approx 17$
$\Rightarrow \quad \omega^{\prime}=17 \omega$
So, value of $x$ is 17 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Imagine a new planet having the same density as that of earth but it is $3$ times bigger than the earth in size. If the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is $g$ and that on the surface of the new planet is $g^{\prime}$, then
questions.solution:
The acceleration due to gravity on the new planet can be found using the relation $g=\frac{G M}{R^2}...(i)$
but $M=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho, \rho$ being density.
Thus, Eq. (i) becomes
$\therefore  g=\frac{G \times \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho}{R^2}$
$=G \times \frac{4}{3} \pi R \rho$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & g \propto R \\ \therefore & \frac{g^{\prime}}{g}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{R} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{g^{\prime}}{g}=\frac{3 R}{R}=3 \\ \Rightarrow & g^{\prime}=3 g\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two planets are revolving around the earth with velocities $v_1$ and $v_2$ and in radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ $\left(r_1 \gt r_2\right)$ respectively. Then:
questions.solution:
Orbital velocity $v=\sqrt{g r}$
$\frac{v_1}{v_2}=\sqrt{\frac{r_1}{r_2}}$
As, $r_1 \gt r_2$ so, $v_1 \gt v_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Earth is revolving around the sun. If the distance of the earth from the sun is reduced to $1 / 4$ th of the present distance then the length of present day will be reduced by :
questions.solution:
From Kepler's law
$\begin{aligned} & T^2 \propto R^3 \\ & \therefore \quad\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^3 \\ & \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(\frac{R}{R / 4}\right)^{3 / 2} \\ & =(4)^{3 / 2}=(2)^3=8 \\ & \therefore \quad T_2=\frac{T_1}{8} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Hence, the length of the day is reduced by $\frac{1}{8}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are two planets and the ratio of radius of the two planets is $K$ but ratio of acceleration due to gravity of both planets is $g$. What will be the ratio of their escape velocity?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} v_e & =\sqrt{R g} \\ \frac{v_1}{v_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{R_1}{R_2} \cdot \frac{g_1}{g_2}} \\ & =\sqrt{K g} \\ & =(K g)^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radius of orbit of satellite of earth is $R$. Its kinetic energy is proportional to
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy of the satellite
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2$ ...(1)
where $v_0=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{R}}$
Now putting the value of $v_0$ is eq. (1), we get
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\sqrt{\frac{G M}{R}}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m G M}{R}$
Hence, $\mathrm{KE} \propto \frac{1}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: R is the radius of Earth and $\omega$ is its angular velocity and $g_p$ is the value of $g$ at the poles. The effective value of $g$ at a latitude $\lambda=60^{\circ}$.
questions.solution:
The acceleration due to gravity at a latitude $\lambda$ is
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\mathrm{g}-\mathrm{R} \omega^2 \cos ^2 \lambda$
At the poles, $\lambda=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\mathrm{g}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{p}}$
For $\quad \lambda=60^{\circ}$,
$\mathrm{g}^{\prime}=\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{p}}-\mathrm{R} \omega^2 \cos ^2 \lambda$
or $\quad g^{\prime}=g_p-R \omega^2 \cos ^2 60^{\circ}$
$=g_p-R \omega^2\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=g_p-\frac{1}{4} R \omega^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform ring of mass $m$ and radius $a$ is placed directly above a uniform sphere of mass $M$ and of equal to radius. The centre of the ring is at a distance $\sqrt{3} a$ from the centre of the sphere. The gravitational force $(F)$ exerted by the sphere on the ring is
questions.solution: Now, the gravitational field due to the ring at a distance $d=\sqrt{3} a$ on its axis is $E=\frac{G m d}{\left(a^2+d^2\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\sqrt{3} G m}{8 a^2}$ The force in a particle of mass $M$ placed here is $F=M E=\frac{\sqrt{3} G M m}{8 a^2}$ This is also the force due to the sphere in the ring.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A planet of radius R has a time period of revolution T. Find time period of a planet of radius 9 R ?
questions.solution:
By Kepler's third law of planetory motion,
Given,
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_1}{T_2} & =\left(\frac{a_1}{a_2}\right)^{3 / 2} \\
T_1 & =T, a_1=R, T_2=?, a_2=9 R \\
\frac{T}{T_2} & =\left(\frac{R}{9 R}\right)^{3 / 2}, \frac{T}{T_2}=\left[\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2\right]^{3 / 2} \\
\frac{T}{T_2} & =\frac{1}{27} \Rightarrow T_2=27 \mathrm{~T}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A launching vehicle carrying an artificial satellite of mass $m$ is set for launch on the surface of the earth of mass $M$ and radius $R$. If the satellite is intended to move in a circular orbit of radius $7 R$, the minimum energy required to be spent by the launching vehicle on the satellite is (Gravitational constant $=G$ )
questions.solution:
The energy of artificial satellite at the surface of the earth
$E_1=-\frac{G M m}{R}$
When the satellite is intended to move in a circular orbit of radius $7 R$, then energy of artificial satellite
$E_2=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M m}{7 R}$
The minimum energy required
$E=E_1-E_2$
$=-\frac{G M m}{R}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{G M m}{7 R}\right)$
$=\frac{-14 G M m+G M m}{14 R}$
$=-\frac{13 G M m}{14 R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a satellite moving in an orbit around the earth, the ratio of kinetic energy to potential energy is
questions.solution:
Key Idea : Kinetic energy of satellite is half of its potential energy.
Potential energy of satellite
$U=-\frac{G M_e m}{R_e}$
where $R_e$ is radius of earth, $M_e$ the mass of earth, $m$ the mass of satellite and $G$ the gravitational constant.
$|U|=\frac{G M_e m}{R_e}$
Kinetic energy of satellite
$K=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_e m}{R_e}$
Thus,$\frac{K}{|U|}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_e m}{R_e} \times \frac{R_e}{G M_e m}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A balloon contains $500 \mathrm{~m}^3$ of helium at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atmosphere pressure. The volume of the helium at $-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature and 0.5 atmosphere pressure will be
questions.solution:
Using gas equation law
$\begin{aligned} \frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} & =\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2} \\ \text { or } \frac{1 \times 500}{300} & =\frac{0.5 \times V_2}{270} \\ V_2 & =900 \mathrm{~m}^3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A graph between pressure $P$ (along $y$-axis) and absolute temperature, $T$ (along $x$-axis) for equal moles of two gases has been drawn. Given that volume of second gas is more than volume of first gas. Which of the following statement is correct?
options: Slope of gas 1 is less than gas 2
Slope of gas 1 is more than gas 2
questions.solution:
According to ideal gas equation, $p V=n R T$ or $\frac{p}{T}=\frac{n R}{V}, \frac{p}{T}$ represents slope of the graph As the number of moles are the same for the two gases,
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{p}{T} \propto \frac{1}{V} \\ & \because \quad V_2 \gt V_1 \text { (Given) } \\ & \therefore \quad(\text { Slope })_2 \lt (\text { Slope })_1 \\ & \text { or } \quad(\text { Slope })_1 \gt (\text { Slope })_2 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of uniform cross-sectional area $A$ is fitted with two air tight frictionless pistons. The pistons are connected to each other by a metallic wire. Initially, the pressure of the gas is $p_0$ and temperature is $T_0$, atmospheric pressure is also $p_0$. Now, the temperature of the gas is increased to $2 T_0$, the tension of wire will be
questions.solution: Volume of the gas is constant i.e. $V$ = constant $\therefore p \propto T$ i.e. pressure will be doubled if temperature is doubled. Let $F$ be the tension in the wire. Then, equilibrium of any one pipes gives. $F=\left(p-p_0\right) A=\left(2 p_0-p_0\right) A=p_0 A$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of a gas is raised from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The root mean square speed
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} T_1 & =27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ T_2 & =927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=1200 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$
$v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{3 k T}{m}\right)}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad v_{\mathrm{rms}} \propto \sqrt{T} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\left(v_{\text {rms }}\right)_1}{\left(v_{\text {rms }}\right)_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}} \\ & \frac{\left(v_{\text {rms }}\right)_1}{\left(v_{\text {rms }}\right)_2}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{1200}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(v_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_2=2\left(v_{\mathrm{rms}}\right)_1 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rms speed (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of oxygen molecules of the gas at temperature 300 K , is
questions.solution:
The rms speed is given by $v_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M_0}}$
where, $\begin{aligned} R & =\text { gas constant } \\ T & =\text { Temperature of the gas } \\ \text { and } M_0 & =\text { Molecular weight of the gas } \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8.3 \times 300}{32 \times 10^{-3}}} \\ & =483 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical glass spheres filled with air are connected by a horizontal glass tube. The glass tube contains a pellet of mercury at its mid-point. Air in one sphere is at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the other is at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If both the vessels are heated through $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then neglecting the expansions of the bulbs and the tube
options: the mercury pellet gets displaced towards the sphere at lower temperature.
the mercury pellet gets displaced towards the sphere at higher temperature.
the mercury pellet does not get displaced at all.
the temperature rise causes the pellet to expand without any displacement.
questions.solution:
Let $n_1$ and $n_2$ be the number of moles in the bulbs at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. Then,
$p V=273 n_1 R=293 n_2 R$
$\therefore \frac{n_1}{n_2}=\frac{293}{273}...(i)$
When the vessels are heated, let the volume of low temperature be $V_1$ and that of the other be $V_2$. Since, pressure are same, hence
$V_1 p=283 n_1 R$ and $V_2 p=303 n_2 R$
$\therefore \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{283 n_1}{303 n_2}...(ii)$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{283}{303} \times \frac{293}{273}=1$
Thus, the mercury pellet remains at the same position.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Mercury boils at $367^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. However, mercury thermometers are made such that they can measure temperature upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This is done by
options: maintaining vacuum above mercury column in the stem of the thermometer
filling nitrogen gas at high pressure above the mercury column
filling oxygen gas at high pressure above the mercury column
filling nitrogen gas at low pressure above the mercury column
questions.solution: If we fill nitrogen gas at high pressure above mercury level, the boiling point of mercury is increased which can extend the range upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Pressure of an ideal gas is increased by keeping temperature constant. What is the effect on kinetic energy of molecules?
questions.solution: Kinetic energy of ideal gas depends only on its temperature. Hence, it remains constant whether its pressure is increased on decreased.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When temperature of an ideal gas is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its rms speed is changed from $400 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ to $v_s$. Then, $v_s$ is
questions.solution:
We know that rms speed is directly proportional to square root of temperature
$\quad \qquad  v_{\mathrm{rms}} \propto \sqrt{T}$
Hence,$\frac{v_{\mathrm{rms}(1)}}{v_{\mathrm{rms}(2)}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{400}{v_{\mathrm{rms}(2)}}=\frac{\sqrt{27+273}}{\sqrt{227+273}}=\sqrt{\frac{300}{500}} \\ & V_{\mathrm{rms}(2)}=\sqrt{\frac{500}{300}} \times 400 \\ & =1.29 \times 400=516.39 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \approx 516 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylinder contains 10 kg of gas at pressure of $10^7 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The quantity of gas taken out of the cylinder, if final pressure is $2.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ will be (temperature of gas is constant)
questions.solution:
Intital mass of the gas $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$
Initial pressure $P_1=10 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
Final pressure $P_2=2.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
According to kinetic theory of gases
$P V=\frac{1}{3} m c^{-2}$
or $\quad P \propto m$
Hence, $\quad \frac{P_1}{P_2}=\frac{m_1}{m_2}$
or $\quad \frac{10^7}{2.5 \times 10^6}=\frac{10}{m_2}$
or $\quad m_2=2.5 \mathrm{~kg}$
Hence, quantity of gas taken out from the cylinder is $10-2.5=7.5 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Mercury boils at $367^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. However, mercury thermometers are made such that they can measure temperature upto $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This is done by
options: maintaining vacuum aboves mercury column in the stem of the thermometer.
filling nitrogen gas at high pressure above the mercury column.
filling oxygen gas at high pressure above the mercury column.
filling nitrogen gas at low pressure above the mercury column.
questions.solution: If we fill nitrogen gas at high pressure above mercury level, the boiling point of mercury is increased which can extend to the range upto $500^{\circ}(C)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a gas $\frac{R}{C_v}=0.67$. This gas is made up of molecules which are
options: mixture of diatomic and poly atomic molecules
questions.solution:
Given, $\frac{R}{C_V}=0.67$
So, $\quad C_V=\frac{R}{0.67}=1.5 R=\frac{3}{2} R$
This value corresponds to monoatomic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of a monoatomic ideal gas undergoes the process $A \rightarrow B$ in the given $p-V$ diagram. The molar heat capacity for this process is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} W_{A \rightarrow B} & =\text { Area under } p-V \text { diagram } \\ & =4 V_0 \times 3 p_0+\frac{1}{2} 4 V_0 \times 3 p_0=18 p_0 V_0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \Delta U & =n C_V \Delta T \\ & =1 \cdot \frac{3}{2} R\left(T_B-T_A\right) \\ & =\frac{3}{2} R\left(\frac{30 p_0 V_0}{R}-\frac{3 p_0 V_0}{R}\right) \\ & =\frac{81}{2} p_0 V_0\end{aligned}$
