questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the height of liquid in capillary tube, if surface tension of liquid $=\mathrm{S}$, radius of capillary tube $=\mathrm{r}$ and acceleration due to gravity $=\mathrm{g}$.
questions.solution:
Height of liquid in a capillary tube (Ascent formula) is given by $\mathrm{H}=\frac{2 S \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{rg}}$
where, $S=$ surface tension, $\rho=$ density of liquid, $r=$ radius of capillary and $\theta=$ angle of contact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two glass plates are separated by water. If surface tension of water is 75 dyne/cm and area of each plate wetted by water is $8 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and the distance between the plates is 0.12 mm , then the force applied to separate the two plates is
questions.solution: The shape of water layer between the two plates is shown in the figure. Thickness $d$ of the film $=0.12 \mathrm{~mm}$ $=0.012 \mathrm{~cm}$ Radius $R$ of the cylindrical face $=\frac{d}{2}$ Pressure difference across the surface $=\frac{T}{R}=\frac{2 T}{d}$ Area of each plate wetted by water $=A$ Force $F$ required to separate the two plates is given by $F=$ pressure difference $\times$ area $=\frac{2 T}{d} A$ Putting the given values, we get $F=\frac{2 \times 75 \times 8}{0.012}=10^5$ dyne

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done in blowing a soap bubble of surface tension $0.06 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ from 2 cm radius to 5 cm radius is
questions.solution:
Here, $S=0.06 \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$,
$\mathrm{r}_1=2 \mathrm{~cm}=0.02 \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{r}_2=5 \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$
Since, bubble has two surfaces, initial surface area of the bubble
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 4 \pi r_1^2=2 \times 4 \pi(0.02)^2 \\ & =32 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$
Final surface area of the bubble
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 4 \pi r_2^2=2 \times 4 \pi(0.05)^2 \\ & =200 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$
Final surface area of the bubble
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 4 \pi \mathrm{r}_2^2=2 \times 4 \pi(0.05)^2 \\ & =200 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$
Increase in surface area
$\begin{aligned} & =200 \pi \times 10^{-4}-32 \pi \times 10^{-4} \\ & =168 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Work done $=\mathrm{S} \times$ Increase in surface area
$=0.06 \times 168 \pi \times 10^{-4}=0.003168 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius $R$ of the soap bubble is doubled under isothermal condition. If $T$ be the surface tension of soap bubble. The work done in doing so is given by
questions.solution:
Initial surface energy
$w_1=2 \times 4 \pi R^2 T=8 \pi R^2 T$
Final surface energy
$w_2=2 \times 4 \pi(2 R)^2 T=32 \pi R^2 T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cone filled with water is revolved in a vertical circle of radius 4 m and the water does not fall down. What must be the maximum period of revolution?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A raft of wood of mass 120 kg floats in water. The weight that can be put on the raft to make it just sink, should be $\left(d_{\text {raft }}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3\right)$
questions.solution:
Volume of raft is given by
$V=\frac{m}{d}=\frac{120}{600}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}^3$
If the raft is immersed fully in water.
Then weight of water displaced is $=V d_{\text {water }}$
$=0.2 \times 10^3=200 \mathrm{~kg}$
Hence, the additional weight which is supported by raft
$=200 \mathrm{~kg}-120 \mathrm{~kg}=80 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Horizontal tube of non-uniform cross-section has radii of 0.1 m and 0.05 m respectively at $M$ and $N$. For a streamline flow of liquid, the rate of liquid flow is
questions.solution: The velocity of flow will increase if cross-section decreases and vice-versa. i.e., $\quad A_1 v_1=A_2 v_2$ or $A v=$ constant Therefore, the rate of liquid flow will be greater at $N$ than at $M$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If two soap bubbles of different radii are connected by a tube, then
options: air flows from bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till sizes become equal
air flows from bigger bubble to the smaller bubble till sizes are interchanged
air flows from smaller bubble to bigger
there is no flow of air
questions.solution:
Air flows from smaller bubble to bigger as the pressure in smaller bubble is higher.
Pressure inside a bubble \(=P_o+\frac{4 S}{R}\)
When they are connected using a tube then, as smaller bubbles {due to small radius} has higher pressure, the air flows from smaller bubble to bigger bubble until the smaller bubble vanishes completely.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ice-cube of density $900 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ is floating in water of density $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The percentage of volume of ice-cube outside the water is
questions.solution:
The percentage of volume of ice cube outside the water is
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\rho_{\text {water }}-\rho_{\text {ice }}}{\rho_{\text {water }}} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{1000-900}{1000} \times 100=10 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A tank is filled with water upto height $H$. When a hole is made at a distance $h$ below the level of water. What will be the horizontal range of water jet?
questions.solution: Applying Bernoullis' theorem, the velocity of water at point $v=\sqrt{2 g h}$ Time take on to reach point $C$ is $t$ So, $\quad H-h=\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ $t=\sqrt{\frac{2(H-h)}{g}}$ ...(1) Now horizontal range $\begin{aligned} R & =v t \\ & =\sqrt{2 g h} \times \sqrt{\frac{2(H-h)}{g}}=2 \sqrt{(H-h) h}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A liquid is allowed into a tube of truncated cone shape. Identify the correct statement from the following.
options: The speed is high at the wider end and low at the narrow end.
The speed is low at the wider end and high at the narrow end.
The speed is same at both ends in a streamline flow.
The liquid flows with uniform velocity in the tube.
questions.solution:
For an incompressible liquid equation of continuity
$A V=\text { constant or } A \propto \frac{1}{V}$
Therefore, at the wider end speed below and at the narrow end speed will be high.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire can be broken by applying load of 200 N . The force required to break another wire of the same length and same material, but double in diameter is
questions.solution:
Yaung's modulus
$Y=\frac{F L}{A l} \text { or } F=\frac{Y A l}{L}$
or $F \propto A$ or $F \propto r^2$ or $F \propto d^2$
$\therefore \quad \frac{F_1}{F_2}=\frac{d_1^2}{d_2^2}$
Given, $d_1=d, d_2=2 d, F_1=200 \mathrm{~N}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & \frac{200}{F_2}=\frac{(d)^2}{(2 d)^2}=\frac{1}{4} \\
\text { or } & F_2=4 \times 200=800 \mathrm{~N}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the increase in pressure required to decrease the volume of water sample by $0.01 \%$. Bulk modulus of water $=2.1 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{As}, \beta=2.1 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$
Also given that $\frac{\Delta V}{V}=0.1 \%=10^{-4}$
We know that, $B=\frac{p V}{\Delta V}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad p & =B\left(\frac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \\ & =2.1 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-4} \\ & =2.1 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the ratio of lengths, radii and Young's modulus of steel and brass wires shown in the figure are $a, b$ and $c$ respectively, the ratio between the increase in lengths of brass and steel wires would be
questions.solution: Free body diagram of the two blocks are Given, $\quad \frac{l_1}{l_2}=a, \frac{r_1}{r_2}=b, \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}=c$ Let Young's modulus of steel is $Y_1$ and of brass is $Y_2$ $\therefore \quad Y_1=\frac{F_1 \cdot l_1}{A_1 \cdot \Delta l_1}$...(i) and $\quad Y_2=\frac{F_2 \cdot l_2}{A_2 \Delta l_2}$...(ii) Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get $\frac{Y_1}{Y_2}=\frac{\frac{F_1 \cdot l_1}{A_1 \cdot \Delta l_1}}{\frac{F_2 \cdot l_2}{A_2 \cdot \Delta l_2}}$ or $\quad \frac{Y_1}{Y_2}=\frac{F_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot l_1 \cdot \Delta l_2}{F_2 \cdot A_1 \cdot l_2 \cdot \Delta l_1}$...(iii) Force on steel wire from free body diagram $T=F_1=2 g \mathrm{~N}$ Force on brass wire from free body diagram $F_2=T^{\prime}=T+2 g=4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~N}$ Now, putting the values of $F_1, F_2$ in Eq. (iii), we get $\frac{Y_1}{Y_2}=\left(\frac{2 g}{4 g}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{\pi r_2^2}{\pi r_1^2}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{l_1}{l_2}\right] \cdot\left(\frac{\Delta l_2}{\Delta l_1}\right)$ or $\quad c=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{b^2}\right) \cdot a\left(\frac{\Delta l_2}{\Delta l_1}\right)$ or $\quad \frac{\Delta l_1}{\Delta l_2}=\left(\frac{a}{2 b^2 c}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the change in the volume of 1.0 L kerosene, when it is subjected to an extra pressure of $2.0 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}$ from the following data? Density of kerosene $=800 \mathrm{~kg}$ $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ and speed of sound in kerosene $=1330 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
As, we know that
$v=\sqrt{\frac{R}{\rho}}$
where, $k=$ bulk modulus of elasticity
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & k & =v^2 \rho=(1330)^2 \times 800 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ \text { Also, } & k & =\frac{F / A}{\Delta V / V} \\ \Rightarrow & \Delta V & =\frac{\text { pressure }}{k}=\frac{2 \times 10^5}{1330 \times 1330 \times 800} \\ & & =0.15 \mathrm{~cm}^3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wire of same material having radius in ratio $2: 1$ and lengths in ratio $1: 2$. If same force is applied on them, then ratio of their change in length will be
questions.solution:
Given, ratio of radius of two wires $r_1: r_2=2: 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}=\frac{2}{1}$
and ratio in their lengths, $1_1: 1_2=1: 2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{l_1}{1_2}=\frac{1}{2}$
When same force is applied on them, then ratio r change in their length $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}_1}{\Delta \mathrm{l}_2}=$ ?
We know that, Young's modulus
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Y}=\frac{\mathrm{Fl}}{\mathrm{A} \Delta \mathrm{l}} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{l}=\frac{\mathrm{Fl}}{\mathrm{AY}} \\
& \Delta \mathrm{l} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~A}} \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{I}_1}{\Delta \mathrm{I}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{l}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_1} / \frac{\mathrm{l}_2}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{l}_1 \mathrm{~A}_2}{\mathrm{l}_2 \mathrm{~A}_1} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}_1}{\Delta \mathrm{I}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{l}_1}{\mathrm{l}_2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{r}_2^2}{\mathrm{r}_1^2}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{8}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cube of side 40 mm has its upper face displaced by 0.1 mm by a tangential force of 8 kN . The shearing modulus of cube is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Bulk modulus for an incompressible liquid is
questions.solution:
Since liquid is incompressible there is no change in volume.
When strain is small the ratio of the normal stress to the volume strain is called the bulk modulus of material of the body.
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{\text { normalstress }}{\text { volumestrain }}$
=normal stress
change in volume/ original volume since, the liquid is incompressible change in
$\mathrm{B}=\frac{\text { normalstress }}{\mathrm{o}}=\infty$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A steel wire of 1 m long and $1 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ cross section area is hanged from rigid end when weight of 1 kg is hanged from it, then change in length will be
(Young's coefficient for wire $Y=2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$)
questions.solution:
Young's coefficient of elasticity
$Y=\frac{m g L}{\pi r^2 \Delta L}$
Substituting the values, we get
$\Delta L=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If compressibility of material is $4 \times 10^{-5}$ per atm, pressure is 100 atm and volume is $100 \mathrm{~cm}^3$, then find $\Delta \mathrm{V}=$ ?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Compressibility
$|\beta|=\frac{1}{\mid \text { Bulk modulus of elasticits } \mid}$
$|\beta|=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{pV}}{\Delta \mathrm{V}}$
or $\quad \beta=\frac{l}{\left(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{pV}}{\Delta \mathrm{V}}\right)}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\Delta \mathrm{pV}}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \Delta \mathrm{V}=\beta(\Delta \mathrm{p} . \mathrm{V}) & =4 \times 10^{-5}\left(100 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\ & =0.4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3=0.4 \mathrm{~cm}^3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves towards east for 2 s with velocity $15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and move towards north for 8 s with velocity $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Then, average velocity of the particle is
questions.solution:
Distance covered towards east
$=2 \times 15=30 \mathrm{~m}$
Distance covered towards north
$=8 \times 5=40 \mathrm{~m}$
Total displacement
$=\sqrt{40^2+30^2}=50 \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Average velocity } & =\frac{\text { Total displacement }}{\text { Total time }} \\ & =\frac{50}{2+8}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A body is projected vertically upwards. The times corresponding to height $h$ while ascending and while descending are $t_1$ and $t_2$, respectively.
Then, the velocity of projection will be (take $g$ as acceleration due to gravity)
questions.solution:
Let $v$ be initial velocity of vertical projection and $t$ be the time taken by the body to reach a height $h$ from ground.
Here, $u=u, a=-g, s=h, t=t$
Using, $s=u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2$, we have
$\begin{aligned}& h=u t+\frac{1}{2}(-g) t^2 \text { or } g t^2-2 u t+2 h=0 \\& \therefore t=\frac{2 u \pm \sqrt{4 u^2-4 g \times 2 h}}{2 g}=\frac{u \pm \sqrt{u^2-2 g h}}{g}\end{aligned}$
It means $t$ has two values, i.e.
$\begin{aligned}& t_1=\frac{u+\sqrt{u^2-2 g h}}{g} \\
& t_2=\frac{u-\sqrt{u^2-2 g h}}{g} \\& t_1+t_2=\frac{2 u}{g} \text { or } u=\frac{g\left(t_1+t_2\right)}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A runner starts from O and goes to O following path OQRO in lhr. What is net displacement and average speed?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Runner starts from O and goes to O .
So, net displacement is zero.
$\begin{aligned} \because \text { Average speed } & =\frac{\text { Total distance }}{\text { Total time }} \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{OQ}+\mathrm{QR}+\mathrm{RO}}{\text { total time }}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1 \mathrm{~km}+(2 \pi \mathrm{r})\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+1 \mathrm{~km}}{1 \mathrm{hr}} \\ = & 2+\frac{\pi}{2}=3.57 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the average velocity when a particle complete the circle of radius 1 m in 10 sec .
questions.solution:
When particle completes one revolution in circular motion, then average displacement travelled by particle is zero.
Hence, Average velocity
$=\frac{\text { average displacement }}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{0}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is thrown upwards with a speed $u$ from a height h above the ground. The time taken by the ball to hit the ground is
questions.solution:
Time taken to reach the highest point from the height h is obtained from equation, $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}-\mathrm{gt}$
$\therefore \quad 0=\mathrm{u}-\mathrm{gt}$ or $\mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{g}}$
Height attained above h is obtained from
$\mathrm{v}^2-\mathrm{u}^2=2(-\mathrm{g}) \mathrm{h}_1$
or $\quad 0-\mathrm{u}^2=2(-\mathrm{g}) \mathrm{h}_1$ or $\mathrm{h}_1=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$
Total height, $\mathrm{h}_2=\left[\mathrm{h}_1+\mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}+\mathrm{h}\right]$
Time taken to hit the ground is obtained from,
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{h}_2 & =\mathrm{ut}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^2 \\ \frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}+\mathrm{h} & =0+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Total time taken, $\mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(\mathrm{u}^2+2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~h}\right)}{\mathrm{g}^2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An observer is approaching with velocity $v$ towards a light source. If the velocity of light is $c$, then velocity of light with respect to observer will be
questions.solution: Observer and light are moving towards each other, so relative velocity is $c+v$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Speeds of a particle at 3rd and 8th sec are $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and zero respectively, then average acceleration between 3 rd and 8th sec will be
questions.solution:
Time interval between 8 th sec. and 3rd sec $\Delta \mathrm{t}=8-3=5 \mathrm{~s}$
i.e. $\Delta t=5 \mathrm{~s}$
Change in velocity $\Delta \mathrm{V}=20-0=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Average acceleration $=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{V}}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{20}{5}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block is kept on the floor of an elevator at rest. The elevator starts descending with an acceleration of $12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. Find the displacement of the block during the first 0.2 s after the start. (Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
The acceleration (downward) of the elevator is $12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ which is greater than gravitational acceleration $g$. So, the block will act as a freely falling body.
From the motion of the block
$\begin{aligned} s & =u t+\frac{1}{2} g t^2 \\ & =0 \times t+\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times(0.2)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 0.2 \times 0.2 \\ & =20 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A police party is moving in a jeep at a constant speed $v$. They saw a thief at a distance $x$ on a motorcycle which is at rest. The moment the police saw the thief, the thief started at constant acceleration $a$. Which of the following relations is true if the police is able to catch the thief?
questions.solution: Let the police party catch the thief after $t \mathrm{~s}$ $\therefore$ Distance travelled by police party in $t \mathrm{~s}=v t$ and distance travelled by thief $=x+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$ So, $\quad x+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \leq v t$ or $\quad \frac{\alpha t^2}{2}-v t+x=0$ or $\quad t=\frac{v \pm \sqrt{v^2-2 \alpha x}}{\alpha}$ For $t$ to be real, $v^2 \geq 2 \alpha x$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two boys are standing at the ends $A$ and $B$ of a ground, where $A B=a$. The boy at $B$ starts running in a direction perpendicular to $A B$ with velocity $v_1$. The boy at $A$ starts running simultaneously with velocity $v$ and catches the other boy in a time $t$, where $t$ is
questions.solution: Distance covered by boy $A$ in time $t$ $A C=v t\ldots(i)$ Distance covered by boy $B$ in time $t$ $B C=v_1 t\ldots(ii)$ Using Pythagoras theorem $\begin{aligned} A C^2 & =A B^2+B C^2 \\ \text { or } \quad(v t)^2 & =a^2+\left(v_1 t\right)^2\end{aligned}$ or $\quad v^2 t^2-v_1^2 t^2=a^2$ or $\quad t^2\left(v^2-v_1^2\right)=a^2 \therefore t=\sqrt{\frac{a^2}{\left(v^2-v_1^2\right)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coin is dropped in a lift. It takes time $t_1$ to reach the floor when lift is stationary. It takes time $t_2$ when lift is moving up with constant acceleration. Then,
questions.solution:
For stationary lift $t_1=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}$
and when the lift is moving up with constant acceleration
$t_2=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}$
$t_1 \gt t_2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train is moving towards east and a car is along north, both with same speed. The observed direction of car to the passenger in the train is
questions.solution: Let $O$ be the origin, then passenger in the train at $P$ observes the car at $Q$ along direction $P Q$, where direction $P Q$ is west north direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The numerical ratio of displacement to the distance covered is always
options: equal to or less than one
equal to or greater than one
questions.solution:
The correct option is $\mathbf{4}$. Equal to or less than one Displacement is the shortest path between two points whereas distance is the length travelleded actually while moving from one point to another point.
So, distance is always greater than or equal to displacement. Thus the numerical ratio of displacement to distance covered is always equal to or less than one.
Hence, option 4 is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle starting from the origin $(0,0)$ moves in a straight line in the $(x, y)$ plane. Its coordinates at a later time are $(\sqrt{3}, 3)$. The path of the particle makes with the $x$-axis an angle of
questions.solution: Draw the situation as shown. OA represents the path of the particle starting from origin $O(0,0)$ Draw a perpendicular from point $A$ to x-axis. Let path of the particle makes an angle $\theta$ with the x -axis, then $\begin{aligned} \tan \theta & =\text { slope of line } O A \\ & =\frac{A B}{O B}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3} \\ \text { or } \quad \theta & =60^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metro train starts from rest and in 5 s achieves $108 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. After that it moves with constant velocity and comes to rest after travelling 45 m with uniform retardation. If total distance travelled is 395 m, find total time of travelling.
questions.solution:
Given, $v=108 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
For first equation of motion
$v=u+a t$
$\therefore \quad 30=0+a \times 5 \quad(\because u=0)$
or $\quad a=6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
So, distance travelled by metro train in 5 s
$s_1=\frac{1}{2} a t^2=\frac{1}{2} \times(6) \times(5)^2=75 \mathrm{~m}$
Distance travelled before coming to rest
$=45 \mathrm{~m}$
So, from third equation of motion
$\begin{aligned} & 0^2=(30)^2-2 a^{\prime} \times 45 \\ & \text { or } \quad a^{\prime}=\frac{30 \times 30}{2 \times 45}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \end{aligned}$
Time taken in travelling 45 m is
$t_3=\frac{30}{10}=3 \mathrm{~s}$
Now, total distance $=395 \mathrm{~m}$
ie, $\quad 75+s^{\prime}+45=395 \mathrm{~m}$
or $s^{\prime}=395-(75+45)=275 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad t_2=\frac{275}{30}=9.2 \mathrm{~s}$
Hence, total time taken in whole journey
$\begin{aligned} & =t_1+t_2+t_3 \\ & =5+9.2+3 \\ & =17.2 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A boy on a cycle pedals around a circle of 20 metres radius at a speed of $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The combined mass of the body and the cycle makes with the vartical so that it may not fall is $\left(g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A particle moves along a straight line $O X$. At a time $t$ (in second) the distance $x$ (in metre) of the particle from $O$ is given by
$x=40+12 t-t^3$
How long would the particle travel before coming to rest?
questions.solution:
Key Idea : Speed is rate of change of distance. Distance travelled by the particle is
$x=40+12 t-t^3$
We know that, speed is rate of change of distance $i e, v=\frac{d x}{d t}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad v & =\frac{d}{d t}\left(40+12 t-t^3\right) \\ & =0+12-3 t^2\end{aligned}$
but final velocity $v=0$
$\therefore \quad 12-3 t^2=0$
or $\quad t^2=\frac{12}{3}=4$
or $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$
Hence, distance travelled by the particle before coming to rest is given by
$\begin{aligned} x & =40+12(2)-(2)^3 \\ & =40+24-8=64-8 \\ & =56 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car moves from $X$ to $Y$ with a uniform speed $v_u$ and returns to $Y$ with a uniform speed $v_d$. The average speed for this round trip is
questions.solution:
Let $t_1$ and $t_2$ be times taken by the car to go from $X$ to $Y$ and then from $Y$ to $X$ respectively. Then, $t_1+t_2=\frac{X Y}{v_u}+\frac{X Y}{v_d}=X Y\left(\frac{v_u+v_d}{v_u v_d}\right)$
Total distance travelled
$=X Y+X Y=2 X Y$
Therefore, average speed of the car for this round trip is
$\begin{aligned} v_{\mathrm{av}} & =\frac{2 X Y}{X Y\left(\frac{v_u+v_d}{v_u v_d}\right)} \\ \text { or } \quad v_{\mathrm{av}} & =\frac{2 v_u v_d}{v_u+v_d}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement $x$ of a particle varies with time $t$ as $x=a e^{-\alpha t}+b e^{\beta t}$, where $a, b, \alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. The velocity of the particle will
questions.solution:
Given, $x=a e^{-\alpha t}+b e^{\beta t}$
So, velocity 
$\begin{aligned} v & =\frac{d x}{d t} \\ & =-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}+b \beta e^{\beta t} \\ & =A+B\end{aligned}$
Where, $A=-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}, B=b \beta e^{\beta t}$
The value of term $A=-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}$ decreases and of term $B=b \beta e^{\beta t}$ increases with increase in time.
As a result, velocity goes on increasing with time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car starts from rest, moves with an acceleration $a$ and then decelerates at a constant rate $b$ for sometimes to come to rest. If the total time taken is $t$. The maximum velocity of car is given by :
questions.solution:
Let car acceleates for time $t_1$ and decelerates for time $t_2$ then
$t_1+t_2=t$.....(1)
From
v=u+a t
$\begin{aligned} & v =u+a t_1 \\ \Rightarrow v & =a t_1\end{aligned}$
For deceleration
$\begin{aligned} & v=u-a t \\ & 0=a t_1-b t_2 (\because u=v)\\ & a t_1=b t_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t_2=\frac{a t_1}{b} \\ & \therefore \quad t_1+\frac{a t_1}{b}=1 [From Eq. (1)]\\ & \Rightarrow \quad t_1\left(1+\frac{a}{b}\right)=t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t_1=\frac{b t}{a+b} \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Maximum velocity of car
$v=a t_1=\frac{a b t}{a+b}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A police jeep is chasing with velocity of $45 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. A thief in another jeep moving with velocity $153 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. Police fires a bullet with muzzle velocity of $180 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity it will strike the car of the thief is
questions.solution:
Effective speed of bullet is \(=\) Speed of bullet + Speed of police jeep
\(\begin{aligned}
& =18 \mathrm{om} / \mathrm{s}+45 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr} \\
& =180+45 \times \frac{5}{18} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =192.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}\)
Speed of theifs jeep \(=153 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=42.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
Velocity of bullet with respect to theif's jeep \(=192.5-42.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
\(=150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Velocity is given by $v=4 t(1-2 t)$, then find time at which velocity is maximum
questions.solution:
To find value of time at which velocity is maximum, taking differentiation of v with respect to time
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$
Here, $v=4 t(l-2 t)$
$\begin{array}{lc} & \mathrm{v}=4 \mathrm{t}-8 \mathrm{t}^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dt}}\left(4 \mathrm{t}-8 \mathrm{t}^2\right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 4-16 \mathrm{t}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~s}=0.25 \mathrm{~s}\end{array}$
Again taking differentiation,
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 v}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-16 \lt 0$
So, at $\mathrm{t}=0.25 \mathrm{~s}$ velocity is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The distance $x$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}$ ) covered by a molecule starting from point $A$ at time $t=0$ and stopping at another point $B$ in given by the equation
$x=t^2\left(2-\frac{t}{3}\right)$
The distance between $A$ and $B$ (in $\mu \mathrm{m}$ ) is closed to
questions.solution:
Given that distance
$x=t^2(2-t / 3)$
On differentiating
$\frac{d x}{d t}=4 t-t^2$
For $x$ to be maximum, $\frac{d x}{d t}=0$
$\begin{array}{rlr}
\Rightarrow & 4 t-t^2=0 \\
\Rightarrow & t=4
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Maximum distance
$\begin{aligned} x_{\max } & =4^2(2-4 / 3) \\ 16 & \times\left[\frac{6-4}{3}\right] \\ = & 16 \times \frac{2}{3} \\ & =10.7\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement $x$ of a particle varies with time $t$ as $x=a e^{-\alpha t}+b e^{\beta t}$, where $a, b, \alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. The velocity of the particle will
questions.solution:
Given,
$x=a e^{-\alpha t}+b e^{\beta t}$
We know that, velocity, $v=\frac{d x}{d t}$
$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{d}{d t}\left(a e^{-\alpha t}+b e^{\beta t}\right) \\ &=-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}+b \beta e^{\beta t} \\ &= A+B\end{aligned}$
where, $A=-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}, B=b \beta e^{\beta t}$
The value of term $A=-a \alpha e^{-\alpha t}$ increases and of term $B=b \beta e^{-\beta t}$ increases with time. As a result, velocity goes on increasing with time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod $P Q$ of length $l$ is moving with ends remaining in contact with frictionless wall and floor. If at the instant, shown the velocity of end $B$ is $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards negative direction of $x$. The speed of end $A$ will be
questions.solution:
From the diagram in question,
$\begin{array}{rlrl}x & =1 \cos \theta \text { and } y=1 \sin \theta \\ \therefore \quad & v_x & =\frac{d x}{d t}=-1 \sin \theta \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d t}\end{array}$
and $\quad v_y=\frac{d y}{d t}=1 \cos \theta \frac{d \theta}{d t}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_y}{v_x}=-\cot \theta$ or $v_y=-v_x \cot \theta$
Since, $\quad v_x=-2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Hence, $v_y=(-2) \cot 60^{\circ}=-2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
The negative sign indicates the motion towards negative direction of $Y$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
If we throw a body upwards with velocity of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, at what height does its kinetic energy reduce to half of the initial value?
(Take $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
Key Idea : At a given height the half of the kinetic energy of the body is equal to its potential energy. Initial kinetic energy of the body
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m(4)^2=8 m\end{aligned}$
Let at height $h$, the kinetic energy reduces to half, ie, it becomes 4 m . It is also equal to potential energy. Hence,
$m g h=4 \mathrm{~m}$
or $h=\frac{4}{g}=\frac{4}{10}=0.4 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The displacement of particle is given by
$x=a_0+\frac{a_1 t}{2}-\frac{a_2 t^2}{3}$
What is its acceleration?
questions.solution:
Key Idea : Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity and velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
The displacement equation is given by
$x=a_0+\frac{a_1 t}{2}-\frac{a_2 t^2}{3}$
Velocity $=$ rate of change of displacement
ie, $\quad v=\frac{d x}{d t}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{d}{d t}\left(a_0+\frac{a_1 t}{2}-\frac{a_2 t^2}{3}\right) \\ & =0+\frac{a_1}{2}-\frac{2 a_2 t}{3} \\ & =\frac{a_1}{2}-\frac{2 a_2 t}{3}\end{aligned}$
Acceleration $=$ rate of change of velocity
$i e$, 
$\begin{aligned} a & =\frac{d v}{d t} \\ & =\frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{a_1}{2}-\frac{2 a_2}{3} t\right) \\ & =0-\frac{2 a_2}{3} \\ & =-\frac{2 a_2}{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two boys are standing at the ends $A$ and $B$ of a ground, where $A B=a$. The boy at $B$ starts running in a direction perpendicular to $A B$ with velocity $v_1$. The boy at $A$ starts running simultaneously with velocity $v$ and catches the other boy in a time $t$, where $t$ is
questions.solution: Distance covered by boy $A$ in time $t$ $A C=v t...(i)$ Distance covered by boy $B$ in time $t$ $B C=v_1 t...(ii)$ Using Pythagorus theorem $\begin{aligned}& & A C^2 & =A B^2+B C^2 \\& \text { or } & (v t)^2 & =a^2+\left(v_1 t\right)^2 \\& \text { or } & v^2 t^2-v_1^2 t^2 & =a^2 \\& \text { or } & t^2\left(v^2-v_1^2\right) & =a^2\end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ $t=\sqrt{\frac{a^2}{\left(v^2-v_1^2\right)}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile of mass 1 kg is projected with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ from the horizontal when projectile is at its highest point, its magnitude of angular momentum (about point of projection)
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {Given, mass of projectile, } \mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \text { Speed of projectile, } \mathrm{v}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \theta=60^{\circ} \\ & \text {Magnitude of angular momentum at highest point, } \\ & \begin{array}{r}\mathrm{L}=\text { mass } \times \text { velocity at highest point } \times \text { perpendicular distance }\end{array} \\ & =\mathrm{m} \times \text { horizontal component of velocity } \times \text { maximum height } \\ & =1 \times 10 \cos 60 \times \frac{\mathrm{v}^2 \sin ^2 60^{\circ}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=10 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{10 \times 10}{2 \times 10} \times \frac{3}{4} \\ & =\frac{75}{4} \mathrm{Nm} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is projected at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal with a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ on the earth. It's time of flight, range, velocity of impact are respectively $\mathrm{T}, \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{V}$. If this projectile is projected on the moon then its time of flight, range and velocity of impact is (assume gravitational field on moon is $\mathrm{g} / 6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
Given, angle of projection, $\theta=30^{\circ}$ Initial speed of particle, $\mathrm{v}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Time of flights, range and velocity of impact on the surface of earth are T, R and V respectively
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \mathrm{v} \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}, \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$
Gravitational acceleration on moon's surface is equal to $\frac{1}{6}$ part of gravitational acceleration on the earth's surface.
Hence,
$\begin{aligned}
& g_m=\frac{g_e}{6} \\
& T_m=\frac{2 v \sin \theta}{\frac{g}{6}}=\frac{6 \times 2 v \sin \theta}{g}=6 \mathrm{~T} \\
& R_m=\frac{v^2 \sin 2 \theta}{\frac{g}{6}}=\frac{6 v^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g}=6 R
\end{aligned}$
Velocity of impact will be same at both places of moon and earth.
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{m}}=\mathrm{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The $x$ and $y$ coordinates of a particle moving in a plane are given by $x(t)=a \cos (p t)$ and $y(t)=b \sin (p t)$ where $a, b( \lt a)$ and $p$ are positive constants of appropriate dimensions and $t$ is time. Then, which of the following is not true?
options: The path of the particle is an ellipse.
Velocity and acceleration of the particle are perpendicular to each other at $t=\frac{\pi}{2 p}$
Acceleration of the particle is always directed towards a fixed point.
Distance travelled by the particle in time interval between $t=0$ and $t=\frac{\pi}{2 P}$ is a
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& x=a \cos (p t), y=b \sin (p t) \\
& \therefore \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1, \text { i.e. equation of ellipse } \\
& \text { Now, } r=x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}=a \cos (p t) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b \sin (p t \hat{\mathbf{j}} \\
& v=\frac{d r}{d t}=-p \operatorname{asin}(p t) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+p b \cos (p t) \hat{\mathbf{j}} \\
& a=\frac{d v}{d t}=-p^2 a \cos (p t) \hat{\mathbf{i}}-p^2 b \sin (p t) \hat{\mathbf{j}} \\
& \text { at } t=\frac{\pi}{2 p} \\
& v=-p a \hat{\mathbf{i}} \text { and } \mathbf{a}=-p^2 b \hat{\mathbf{j}}
\end{aligned}$
$v \text { perpendicular to a }$
Also, $a=-p^2 r$, i.e. directed towards a fixed point

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is thrown with a velocity of $9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ making an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. It will hit the ground after a time
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { The time of flight }=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g} \\ & =\frac{2 \times 9.8 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{9.8}=1 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone projected with a velocity $u$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal reaches maximum height $H_1$. When it is projected with velocity $u$ at an angle $\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ with the horizontal, it reaches maximum height $H_2$. The relation between the horizontal range $R$ of the projectile, $H_1$ and $H_2$ is
questions.solution:
We know that, $H_1=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { and } \quad H_2=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2(90-\theta)}{2 g} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad H_2=\frac{u^2 \cos ^2 \theta}{2 g} \\ & \because \quad H_1 H_2=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g} \times \frac{u^2 \cos ^2 \theta}{2 g}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{4}{4} \times \frac{U^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g} \times \frac{U^2 \cos ^2 \theta}{2 g}$
$=\frac{\left(u^2 \sin 2 \theta\right)^2}{16 g^2} \quad\left[\because \sin ^2 \theta=2 \sin \theta \cos \theta\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{R^2}{16} \\ R^2 & =6 H_1 H_2 \\ \therefore \quad R & =4 \sqrt{H_1 H_2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the radius of curvature of the parabola traced out by the projectile in the previous problem at a point where the particle velocity makes an angle $\frac{\theta}{2}$ with the horizontal?
questions.solution: Let $v$ be the velocity at the point where it makes an angle $\frac{\theta}{2}$ with the horizontal. The horizontal component remains unchanged.So, $v_m \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=u \cos \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{u \cos \theta}{\cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)}$ $\ldots$(i) From figure, $m g \cos \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{m v^2}{r}$ $r=\frac{v^2}{g \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}$ Putting the value of $v$ from Eq. (i), we get $r=\frac{v^2 \cos ^2 \theta}{g \cos ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If object starts from rest and covers angle of 60 rad in 10 sec in circular motion magnitude of angular acceleration will be
questions.solution:
Given, initial angular velocity of object $w_0=0$
Angular displacement $\theta=60 \mathrm{rad}$
and
$\Delta \mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{sec}$
From equation of rotational motion,
$\begin{aligned}
\theta & =\mathrm{w}_0 \mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{2} \alpha \mathrm{t}^2 \\
60 & =0 \times \mathrm{t}+\frac{1}{2} \times \alpha \times 10^2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \alpha & =\frac{60}{50} \Rightarrow \alpha=1.2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Velocity-time curve for a body projected vertically upwards is
questions.solution: For a body projected vertically upwards, Velocity at any instant of time is given by $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{u}_0-\mathrm{gt}$, which is a straight line with slope '-g' and intercept $\mathrm{u}_0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is thrown in the upward direction making an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal direction with a velocity of $147 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. Then the time after which its inclination with the horizontal is $45^{\circ}$, is
questions.solution:
Key Idea : At the two points of the trajectory during projection, the horizontal component of the velocity is the same.
Horizontal component of velocity at angle $60^{\circ}$
$=$ Horizontal component of velocity at $45^{\circ}$
$i e, \quad u \cos 60^{\circ}=v \sin 45^{\circ}$
or $\quad 147 \times \frac{1}{2}=v \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
or $\quad v=\frac{147}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Vertical component of $u=u \sin 60^{\circ}$
$=\frac{147 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~m}$
Vertical component of $v=v \sin 45^{\circ}$
$=\frac{147}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{147}{2} \mathrm{~m}$
but $\quad v_y=u_y+a t$
$\therefore \quad \frac{147}{2}=\frac{147 \sqrt{3}}{2}-9.8 t$
or $\quad 9.8 t=\frac{147}{2}(\sqrt{3}-1)$
$\therefore \quad t=5.49 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is thrown vertically upward. It has a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when it has reached one-half of its maximum height. How high does the ball rise? (Take, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution: The problem can be solved using third equation of motion at $A$ and $O$ '. Let maximum height attained by the ball be H.Third equation of motion gives $v^2=u^2-2 g h$ At $A$, $(10)^2=u^2-2 \times 10 \times \frac{H}{2}$ $\Rightarrow u^2=100+10 H...(i)$ At $O^{\prime},(0)^2=u^2-2 \times 10 \times H$ $\Rightarrow u^2=20 H...(ii)$ Thus, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $20 H=100+10 H$ $\Rightarrow 10 H=100 \quad \therefore H=10 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum range of a gun on horizontal terrain is 16 km , if $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. What must be the muzzle velocity of the shell?
questions.solution:
We know that in projection of the particle, for maximum range, $\theta=45^{\circ}$
Now maximum range
$\begin{aligned} & R=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g}=\frac{u^2 \sin 90^{\circ}}{g} \\ & u=\sqrt{R g} & ...(1) \end{aligned}$
Here : $R_{\max }=16 \mathrm{~km}=16 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m}$,
$g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Now from eq. (1), we get
$u=\sqrt{16 \times 10^3 \times 10}=400 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The earth of mass $=6 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$ revolves around the sun with an angular velocity of $2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~km}$. The force exerted by the sun on earth is :
questions.solution:
Here: Mass of earth $M_e=6 \times 10^{24} \mathrm{~kg}$
Angular velocity of earth $\omega=2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Radius of circular orbit
$\begin{aligned}R & =1.5 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~km} \\& =1.5 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
Force exerted on the earth by the sun is given by
$\begin{aligned}F & =M_e R \omega^2 \\& =6 \times 10^{24} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11} \times\left(2 \times 10^{-7}\right)^2 \\& =36 \times 10^{21} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone is thrown with an initial speed of $4.9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ from a bridge in vertically upward direction. It falls down in water after 2 sec . The height of bridge is
questions.solution:
Here: Initial velocity $u=-4.9 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ (- ve is due to vertically upward motion)
Total time $t=2 \mathrm{sec}$
The height of the bridge is given by
$s=u t+\frac{1}{2} g t^2$
$\begin{aligned} & =-4.9 \times 2+\frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times(2)^2 \\ & =-9.8+19.6=9.8 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A river is flowing from west to east with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$. A man can swim in still water with a velocity $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$. In which direction should the man swim so, as to take the shortest possible path to go to the south?
questions.solution: Let the swimmer swims at an angle $\theta$ with the vertical. $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \sin \theta & =\frac{v_r}{v_s}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2}=\sin 30^{\circ} \\ \theta & =30^{\circ}\end{aligned}$ The swimmer should swim $30^{\circ}$ east of south to take the shortest possible path to go to the south.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One end of a massless spring of constant $100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ and natural length 0.5 m is fixed and the other end is connected to a particle of mass 0.5 kg lying on a frictionless horizontal table. The spring remains horizontal. If the mass is made to rotate at angular velocity of $2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$, then elongation of spring is
questions.solution:
For the circular motion acceleration towards the centre is $\frac{v^2}{r}$. The horizontal force on the particle is due to the spring and $k l$, where $l$ is the elongation and $k$ is spring constant of spring.
$\begin{aligned} & k l=\frac{m v^2}{r} & =m \omega^2 r=m \omega^2\left(l_0+l\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \left(k-m \omega^2\right) l & =m \omega^2 l_0 \\ \Rightarrow & l & =\frac{m \omega^2 l_0}{k-m \omega^2}\end{aligned}$
Putting the values $1=\frac{0.5 \times 4 \times 0.5}{100-0.5 \times 4}=\frac{1}{98} \mathrm{~m}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is fired with a velocity $u$ at angle $\theta$ with the ground surface. During the motion at any time it is making an angle $\alpha$ with the ground surface. The speed of particle at this time will be
questions.solution:
Horizontal component of velocity is always same
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad v \cos \alpha=u \cos \theta \\ & \text { or, } \quad v=\frac{u \cos \theta}{\cos \alpha} \\ & \text { or, } \quad V=u \cos \theta \sec \alpha \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If three objects are thrown with velocity $\sqrt{\frac{2 g H}{3}}, \sqrt{2 \mathrm{gH}}, \sqrt{\frac{g H}{3}}$, then find the ratio of their maximum heights.
questions.solution:
Maximum height of projectile $\mathrm{H}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{u}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_1:\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_2:\left(\mathrm{H}_{\max }\right)_3 \\
&=\frac{\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{gH}}{3}}\right)^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}: \frac{(\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gH}})^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}: \frac{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{gH}}{3}}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=\frac{2}{3}: 2: \frac{1}{3} \\
& \quad=2: 6: 1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is whirled in a horizontal circle of radius 25 cm . It has an angular velocity of $13 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. What is its linear velocity at any point on circular path?
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} r & =25 \mathrm{~cm}=0.25 \mathrm{~m} \\ \omega & =13 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\because$ Linear speed $=$ Radius $\times$ Angular speed
$\begin{array}{llrl}\text { or } & & v & =r \omega \\ \therefore & & & =0.25 \times 13 \\ \Rightarrow & & v & =3.25 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the range of a projectile thrown with velocity $98 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ with angle $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal ?
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{U}=98 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \theta=30^{\circ}$
$\because$ Range of a projectile, $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{U}^2 \sin (2 \theta)}{\mathrm{g}}$
$=\frac{98 \times 98 \times \sin 60^{\circ}}{9.8}=490 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half-life period of a radioactive element $X$ is same as the mean life of another radioactive element $Y$. Initially, both of them have the same numbers of atoms then,
options: $X$ and $Y$ have the same decay rate initially.
$X$ and $Y$ decay at the same rate always
$Y$ will decay at a faster rate than $X$
$X$ will decay at a faster rate than $Y$
questions.solution:
Half life of $x=$ mean life of $y$
Let $\lambda_x$ and $\lambda_y$ be decay constants of $x$ and $y$, then condition (1) implies
$\begin{aligned} \frac{0.693}{\lambda_x} & =\frac{1}{\lambda_y} \\ \lambda_y & =\frac{\lambda_x}{0.693} \Rightarrow \lambda_y\gt\lambda_x\end{aligned}$
As rate of decay $\alpha \lambda$; hence decay of $y$ will be faster than that of $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Radioactive decay will occur as follows
$\begin{aligned}
& { }_{86}^{220} \mathrm{Rn} \longrightarrow{ }_{84}^{216} \mathrm{PO}+{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He} \text { Half life }=55 \mathrm{~s} \\
& { }_{84}^{816} \mathrm{Po} \longrightarrow{ }_{82}^{212} \mathrm{~Pb}+{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He} \text { Half life }=0 \cdot 66 \mathrm{~s} \\
& { }_{82}^{212} \mathrm{BL}+\lambda^{\circ} e \text { Half life }=10 \cdot 6 \mathrm{~h}
\end{aligned}$
If a certain mass of radon $(R n=220)$ is allowed to decay in a certain container; then after 5 minutes the element with the greater mass will be
questions.solution:
Let $M_0$ be initial mass of $R n-220$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Number of half-lives of } R n \text { in } 5 \text { minutes }=\frac{5 \mathrm{~min}}{55 \mathrm{~s}} \\
& =\frac{5 \times 60}{55} \approx 5.5
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $R n-220$ (left) $=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5.5} M_0=\frac{M_0}{45}$
$\therefore$ The mass converted into Po -216 is (44/45)
$M_0$, Number of half-lives of $P o-216$ in 5 minutes $=\frac{5 \mathrm{~min}}{0.66}=\frac{300}{0.66} \approx 455$
$\therefore$ Mass of $P o-216$ left $=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{455} \times \frac{44}{45} M_0 \longrightarrow 0$
This means that $P o-216$ formed will soon decay to lead. Hence, lead will have maximum mass nearly $\left(\frac{44}{45}\right) M_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ba-122 has half life of 2 min. Experiment has to be done using Ba-122 and it takes 10 min to set up the experiment. If initially 80 g of Ba-122 was taken, how much Ba was left when experiment was started?
questions.solution:
As $\mathrm{T}_{\frac{1}{2}}=2 \mathrm{~min} \Rightarrow 10 \mathrm{~min}=5$ half-lives
We know, $\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_0}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$
where, $\quad \mathrm{N}=$ remaining of nuclei
$\mathrm{N}_0=$ initial number of nuclei
$\mathrm{n}=$ number of half lives
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{N}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{2^n}=\frac{80}{2^5}=\frac{80}{32}=2.5 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An atomic power nuclear reactor can deliver 300 MW. The energy released due to fission of each nucleus of uranium atoms $\mathrm{U}^{238}$ is 170 MeV . The number of uranium atoms fissioned per hour will be
questions.solution:
Power $=\frac{\text { Energy }}{\text { Time }}=300 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~W}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}$
Energy released due to fission
$\begin{gathered}=170 \mathrm{MeV}=170 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ =27.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J}\end{gathered}$
Number of atoms fissioned per second
$=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{27.2 \times 10^{-12}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{20}}{27.2}$
Number of atoms fissioned per hour
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{3 \times 10^{20} \times 3600}{27.2} \\ & =\frac{3 \times 36}{27.2} \times 10^{22}=4 \times 10^{22} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In which radiation atomic number and mass number are not affected?
questions.solution:
In $\gamma$-radiation, no change occurs in mass number and atomic number because its rest mass is zero and it is a neutral for electric and magnetic field.
In $\alpha$-radiation, atomic number is decreased by 2 units and atomic mass is decreased by 4 units. In $\beta$-radiation, no change occurs in atomic mass but atomic number increases by 1 unit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $\mathrm{A}^7 \mathrm{Li}$ target is bombarded with a proton beam current of $10^{-4} \mathrm{~A}$ for one hour to produce ${ }^7 \mathrm{Be}$ of activity $1.8 \times 10^8$ disintegrations per second. Assuming that one ${ }^7$ Be radioactive nuclei is produced by bombarding 1000 protons, its half-life is
questions.solution: Given, $i=10^{-4} \mathrm{~A}, t=1 \mathrm{~h}=3600 \mathrm{~s}$ We know that, $q=$ it $=10^{-4} \times 1 \times 3600=0.36 \mathrm{C}$ $\therefore$ Number of protons $=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{0.36}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$ $\left[\because e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$ $=2.25 \times 10^8$ Number of ${ }^7$ Be nuclei produced $=\frac{2.25 \times 10^{18}}{1000}=2.25 \times 10^{15}$ Activity of ${ }^7 \mathrm{Be}=1.8 \times 10^8$ disintegration $/ \mathrm{s}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Activity, } A & =\lambda N \\ & =\frac{\ln 2}{t_{1 / 2}} N\end{aligned}$ $\left[\because \lambda=\frac{\ln 2}{t_{1 / 2}}\right]$ $1.8 \times 10^8=\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}} \times 2.25 \times 10^{15}$ $\therefore \quad t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.693 \times 2.25 \times 10^{15}}{1.8 \times 10^8}=0.87 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half-life of ${ }^{198} \mathrm{Au}$ is 2.7 days. The average life is
questions.solution:
The half-life and the decay constant are related as
$\begin{aligned} t_{12} & =\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}=\frac{0.693}{\lambda} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \lambda & =\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.693}{2.7 \mathrm{day}} \\ & =\frac{0.693}{2.7 \times 24 \times 3600 \mathrm{~s}} \\ & =2.9 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
Now average life
$t_{a v}=\frac{1}{\lambda}=3.9$ days

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts which have their velocities in the ratio $2: 1$. The ratio of their nuclear size will be
questions.solution:
According to the law of conservation of momentum, we get
$m_1 V_1=m_2 V_2$
or $\left(\frac{m_1}{m_2}\right)=\left(\frac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$
But $m=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho \text { or } m \propto r^3$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{r_1^3}{r_2^3} \\ \therefore & \frac{r_1^3}{r_2^3}=\frac{V_2}{V_1}=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{r_1}{r_2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3} \\ \therefore & r_1: r_2=1:(2)^{1 / 3}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Remaining quantity (in\%) of radioactive element after 5 half lives is
questions.solution:
Remaining quantity
$N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^5=\frac{N_0}{32}$
In percentage, $N=\frac{N_0}{30 \times N_0} \times 100=3.125 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A radioactive element ${ }_{90} X^{238}$ decays into ${ }_{83} Y^{222}$. The number of $\beta$-particles emitted are
questions.solution:
$\alpha$-particles are emitted $=\frac{238-222}{4}=4$
The atomic number is decreased
$90-4 \times 2=82$
As atomic number of ${ }_{83} Y^{222}$, So, atomic number is increased by 1 , therefore, one $\beta$-particle is emitted.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The binding energy per nucleon is maximum in the case
questions.solution: We know that binding energy per nucleon increases with atomic number binding energy for iron is maximum, after that it decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half-life of radio isotope is 4 h . If initial mass of the isotope was 200 g , then mass remaining after 24 h will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & M=M_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / t_{1 / 2}} \\ & \therefore \\ & M=200\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{24 / 2}=200\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^6=\frac{200}{64}=3.125 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Activity of a radioactive sample decreases to (1/3)rd of its original value in 3 days. Then, in 9 days its activity will become
questions.solution:
$R=R_0 e^{-\lambda t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)=e^{-\lambda \times 3}=e^{-3 \lambda}$   ...(i)
Again, $\frac{R^{\prime}}{R_0}=e^{-\lambda \times 9}=e^{-9 \lambda}=\left(e^{-3 \lambda}\right)^3$
$=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^3$ [From Eq. (i)]
$=\frac{1}{27}$
$\therefore \quad R^{\prime}=\frac{R_0}{27}$
Hence, in 9 days activity will become $\left(\frac{1}{27}\right)$ of the original value.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $r_1$ and $r_2$ are the radii of the atomic nuclei of mass numbers 64 and 125 respectively, then the ratio $\left(r_1 / r_2\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$r=r_0(A)^{1 / 3}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{r_1}{r_2}=\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\frac{64}{125}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$=\left[\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^3\right]^{1 / 3}=\frac{4}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ionization energy of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Ionization energy $=\operatorname{Rch} Z^2$
$Z=3$ for $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$
$\therefore$ Ionization energy $=(3)^2 R c h$
$=9 R c h$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: X-rays are used in determining the molecular structure of crystalline because
options: its wavelength is comparable to interatomic distance
questions.solution: Crystal structure is explored through the diffraction of waves having a wavelength comparable with the interatomic spacing $\left(10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}\right)$ in crystals. Radiation of longer wavelength cannot resolve the details of structure, while radiation of much shorter wavelength is diffracted through in conveniently small angles. Usually diffraction of X -rays is employed in the study of crystal structure as X-rays have wavelength comparable to interatomic spacing.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half-life of a radio-isotope is 4 h . If initial mass of the isotope was 200 g , then mass remaining after 24 h will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & M=M_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / 1 / 2} \\ & \therefore \quad M=200\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{24 / 4}=200\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^6=\frac{200}{64}=3.125 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}$ emits $8 \alpha$-particles and $6 \beta$-particles, then the resulting nucleus is
questions.solution:
After one $\alpha$-emission, the daughter nucleus reduces in mass number by 4 unit and in atomic number by 2 unit. In $\beta$-emission the atomic number of daughter nucleus increases by 1 unit. The reaction can be written as
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238} \xrightarrow{-8 \alpha}{ }_{76} X^{206} \xrightarrow{-6 \beta}{ }_{82} Y^{206}$
Thus, the resulting nucleus is ${ }_{82} Y^{206}, i e,{ }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{206}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ratio of charge on positron to mass of positron is approximately
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  Charge on positron }(\mathrm{q})=\mathrm{e}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C} \\ & \text { Mass of positron }(\mathrm{m})=\text { mass of electron } \\ & =9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}=1.75 \times 10^{11} \\ & \simeq 2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block having 12 g of element is placed in a room. This element is a radioactive element with half-life of 15 yr . After how many years will there be just 1.5 g of the element in the box?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } T_{1 / 2}=15 \mathrm{yr} \\ & N_0=12 \mathrm{~g} \\ & N=1.5 \mathrm{~g} \\ & \therefore \quad N=N_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda t} \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & & e^{-\lambda t} & =\frac{N}{N_0} \\ \Rightarrow & -\lambda t & =\ln \left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & t & =\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln \left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)=\frac{T_{1 / 2}}{0.693} \ln \left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)\end{array}$
$\left[\because \lambda=\frac{0.693}{T_{1 / 2}}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{15}{0.693} \times \ln \left(\frac{12}{1.5}\right) \\ & =45 \mathrm{yr}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a proton and anti-proton come close to each other and annihilate, how much energy will be released?
questions.solution:
Mass of proton $=$ mass of anti-proton
$=1.67 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}=1 \mathrm{amu}$
Energy equivalent to $1 \mathrm{amu}=931 \mathrm{MeV}$.
So, energy equivalent to $2 \mathrm{amu}=2 \times 931 \mathrm{MeV}$
$\begin{aligned} & =1862 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ & =2.97 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =3 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two radioactive substances $A$ and $B$ have decay constants $5 \lambda$ and $\lambda$ respectively. At $t=0$ they have the same number of nuclei. The ratio of number of nuclei of $A$ to those of $B$ will be $\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^2$ after a time interval
questions.solution:
Number of nuclei remained after time $t$ can be written as
$N=N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$
where $N_0$ is initial number of nuclei of both the substances.
and
$\begin{aligned}
& N_1=N_0 e^{-5 \lambda t} \ldots(i)\\
& N_2=N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \ldots(ii)
\end{aligned}$
Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we obtain
$\frac{N_1}{N_2}=e^{(-5 \lambda+\lambda) t}=e^{-4 \lambda t}=\frac{1}{e^{4 \lambda t}}$
But, we have given
$\frac{N_1}{N_2}=\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^2=\frac{1}{e^2}$
Hence, $\quad \frac{1}{e^2}=\frac{1}{e^{4 \lambda t}}$
Comparing the powers, we get
or
$\begin{aligned}
& 2=4 \lambda t \\
& t=\frac{2}{4 \lambda}=\frac{1}{2 \lambda}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In radioactive decay process, the negatively charged emitted $\beta$-particles are
options: the electrons present inside the nucleus
the electrons produced as a result of the decay of neutrons inside the nucleus
the electrons produced as a result of collisions between atoms
the electrons orbiting around the nucleus
questions.solution:
Beta decay can involve the emission of either electrons or positrons. The electrons or positrons emitted in a $\beta$-decay do not exist inside the nucleus. They are only created at the time of emission, just as photons are created when an atom makes a transition from higher to a lower energy state.
In negative $\beta$-decay a neutron in the nucleus is transformed into a proton, an electron and an antineutrino. Hence, in radioactive decay process, the negatively charged emitted $\beta$-particles are the electrons produced as a result of the decay of neutrons present inside the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the radioactive isotope ${ }_{88} R a^{226}$ decays in a series by emission of three alpha $(\alpha)$ and a beta ( $\beta$ ) particle, the isotope $X$ which remains undecay is
questions.solution: Due to emission of $3 \alpha$ particles, the mass number is reduced by 12 while atomic number is decreased by 6 . Due to emission of $\beta$ particle, the atomic number is increased by one while there is no change in mass number. Now, resulting mass will be decreased by 12 and atomic number is decreased by 5. So, isotope $X$ is ${ }_{83} X^{214}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nucleus ${ }_Z^A X$ has mass represented by $m(A, Z)$. If $m_p$ and $m_n$ denote the mass of proton and neutron respectively and $B E$ the binding energy (in MeV ) then,
questions.solution:
In the case of formation of nucleus the evolution of energy equals to the binding energy of the nucleus takes place due to disappearance of a fraction of total mass. If the quantity of mass disappearing is $\Delta m$, then the binding energy is
$B E=\Delta m c^2$
cFrom the above discussion, it is clear that the mass of the nucleus must be less than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons. We can then write
$\Delta m=Z m_p-N m_n-m(A, Z)$
where $m(A, Z)$ is the mass of the atom of mass number $A$ atomic number $Z$. Hence, the binding energy of nucleus is
$\begin{aligned} & B E=\left[Z m_p+N m_n-m(A, Z)\right] c^2 \\ & B E=\left[Z m_p+(A-Z) m_n-m(A, Z)\right] c^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In gamma ray emission from a nucleus
options: both the neutron number and the proton number change
there is no change in the proton number and the neutron number
questions.solution: In gamma ray emission the energy is released from nucleus, so that nucleus get stablished.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $M_O$ is the mass of an oxygen isotope ${ }_8 \mathrm{O}^{17}, M_p$ and $M_n$ are the masses of a proton and a neutron, respectively, the nuclear binding energy of the isotope is
questions.solution:
Binding energy
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{BE} & =\left(M_{\text {nucleus }}-M_{\text {nucleons }}\right) c^2 \\
& =\left(M_O-8 M_p-9 M_n\right) c^2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hydrogen bomb is based upon
questions.solution: The working of hydrogen bomb is based on nuclear fusion. But for the process of nuclear fusion, a very high temperature ( $\approx 10^7 \mathrm{~K}$ ) and a very high pressure is required, hence to construct a hydrogen bomb, first atom bomb is constructed. The atom bomb is then covered from all sides by an enclosure of a compound of heavy hydrogen (deuteron) such as lithium hydride. First the atom bomb is exploded which produces such a high temperature and pressure that deuteron nuclei get fused and a huge amount of energy is released.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $M_{\mathrm{O}}$ is the mass of an oxygen isotope ${ }_8 \mathrm{O}^{17}$, $M_p$ and $M_n$ are the masses of a proton and a neutron, respectively, the nuclear binding energy of the isotope is
questions.solution:
The binding energy is given as
$\mathrm{BE}=\Delta m c^2$
where, $\Delta m=$ mass defect
and $\quad C=$ speed of light
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{BE}=\left(8 M_{\mathrm{p}}+9 M_{\mathrm{n}}-M_{\mathrm{O}}\right) c^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The nuclei of which one of the following pairs of nuclei are isotones ?
questions.solution: The nuclei which have same number of neutrons but different atomic number and mass number are known as isotones. In choice (a) nuclei of ${ }_{34} \mathrm{Se}^{74}$ and ${ }_{31} \mathrm{Ga}^{71}$ are isotones as $A-Z=74-34=71-31=40$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the reaction given below
${ }_{86} A^{222} \longrightarrow{ }_{84} B^{210}$
how many $\alpha$ and $\beta$-particles are emitted?
questions.solution:
${ }_{86} A^{222} \longrightarrow{ }_{84} B^{210}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Decrease in mass number } & =222-210 \\ & =12\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Number of $\alpha$-particles emitted
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{12}{4}=3 \\ { }_{86} A^{222} \longrightarrow{ }_{80} X^{210} & \longrightarrow 84 B^{210}\end{aligned}$
Increase in atomic number $=84-80=4$
$\therefore$ Number of $\beta$-particles emitted $=4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The phenomenon of radioactivity is :
options: exothermic change which increases or decreases with temperature
nuclear process does not depend on external factors
questions.solution: Radioactivity is a natural process which does not depend upon external factors.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The element ${ }_{90} \mathrm{Th}^{232}$ belongs to thorium series. Which of the following will act as the end product of the series ?
questions.solution: The end product of thorium series is $\mathrm{Pb}-208$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The energy in MeV is released due to transformation of 1 kg mass completely into energy, is $\left(c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's mass energy equivalence
$E=m c^2=1 \times\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2=9 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~J}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{9 \times 10^{16}}{1.6 \times 10^{-13}} \mathrm{MeV} \\ & \quad \text { (since } 1 \mathrm{MeV}=1.6 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~J} \text { ) }\end{aligned}$
$=5.625 \times 10^{29} \mathrm{MeV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The half life of a radioactive substance is 3.6 days. How much of 20 mg of this radioactive substance will remain after 36 days?
questions.solution:
Half life $T_{1 / 2}=3.6$ days
Initial quantity $N_0=20 \mathrm{mg}$
Total time $=36$ days
The number of half lives
$n=\frac{t}{T_{1 / 2}}=\frac{36}{3.6}=10$
Hence, mass of radioactive substance left after 10 half lives
$N=N_0 \times\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n=20 \times \frac{1}{1024}=0.019 \mathrm{mg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following electromagnetic waves have the smallest wavelength?
questions.solution: $\gamma$-rays have the smallest wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The operation of a nuclear reactor is said to be critical, if the multiplication factor $(k)$ has a value
questions.solution:
The multiplication factor $(k)$ is an important reactor parameter and is the ratio of number of neutrons present at the beginning of a particular generation to the number present at the beginning of the next generation. It is a measure of the growth rate of the neutrons in the reactor. For $k=1$, the operation of the reactor is said to be critical.
Note: If $k$ becomes greater than one, the reaction rate and the reactor power increase exponentially. Unless the factor $k$ is brought down very close to unity, the reactor will become supercritical and can even explode.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Half-lives of two radioactive substances $A$ and $B$ are respectively 20 min and 40 min . Initially the samples of $A$ and $B$ have equal number of nuclei. After 80 min the ratio of remaining number of $A$ and $B$ nuclei is
questions.solution:
Key Idea : Total number of nuclei remained after $n$ half-lives is $N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$.
Total time given $=80 \mathrm{~min}$
Number of half-lives of $A, n_A=\frac{80 \mathrm{~min}}{20 \mathrm{~min}}=4$
Number of half-lives of $B, n_B=\frac{80 \mathrm{~min}}{40 \mathrm{~min}}=2$
Number of nuclei remains undecayed
$N=N_0\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$
where $N_0$ is initial number of nuclei.
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & \frac{N_A}{N_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n_A}}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n_B}} \\ \text { or } & \frac{N_A}{N_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)} \\ \text { or } & \frac{N_A}{N_B}=\frac{1}{4}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In any fission process the ratio
is
options: depends on the mass of parent nucleus
questions.solution:
In fission process, when a parent nucleus breaks into daughter products, then some mass is lost in the form of energy. Thus, mass of fission products $ \lt $ mass of parent nucleus
$\Rightarrow \frac{\text { Mass of fission Products }}{\text { Mass of parent nucleus }} \lt 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the reaction ${ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }_0^1 n$, if the binding energies of ${ }_1^2 \mathrm{H},{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}$ and ${ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}$ are respectively $a, b$ and $c$ (in MeV ), then the energy (in MeV ) released in this reaction is
questions.solution:
The energy released per nuclear reaction is the resultant binding energy.
Binding energy of $\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}\right)=a+b$
Binding energy of ${ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}=c$
In a nuclear reaction the resultant nucleus is more stable than the reactants. Hence, binding energy of ${ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}$ will be more than that of $\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^3 \mathrm{H}\right)$
Thus, energy released per nucleon
$\begin{aligned}& =\text { resultant binding energy } \\& =c-(a+b)=c-a-b\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Fission of nuclei is possible because the binding energy per nucleon in them
options: increases with mass number at high mass numbers
decreases with mass number at high mass numbers
increases with mass number at low mass numbers
decreases with mass number at low mass numbers
questions.solution:
The binding energy per nucleon for the middle nuclides (from $A=20$ to $A=56$ ) is maximum. Hence, these are more stable.
As the mass number increases, the binding energy per nucleon gradually decreases and ultimately binding energy per nucleon of heavy nuclides (such as uranium etc.) is comparatively low. Hence, these nuclides are relatively unstable. So, they can be fissioned easily.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: India conducted an underground nuclear test at
questions.solution: India conducted underground nuclear test at Pokharan.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following is fusion process?
questions.solution:
When two lighter nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus, the process is called nuclear fusion. e.g.,
$\because \quad{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the fusion reaction ${ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H} \longrightarrow$ ${ }_2^3 \mathrm{He}+{ }_0^1 \mathrm{n}$, the masses of deuteron, helium and neutron expressed in amu are $2.015,3.017$ and 1.009 , respectively. If 1 kg of deuterium undergoes complete fusion, find the amount of total energy released. (1 amu $=931.5 \mathrm{MeV})$.
questions.solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{m}=2(2.015)-(3.017+1.009)=0.004 \mathrm{amu}$
$\therefore$ Energy released $=(0.004 \times 931.5) \mathrm{MeV}$
$=3.726 \mathrm{MeV}$
Energy released per deuteron
$=\frac{3.726}{2}=1.863 \mathrm{MeV}$
Number of deuterons in 1 kg
$=\frac{6.02 \times 10^{26}}{2}=3.01 \times 10^{26}$
$\therefore$ Energy released per kg of deuterium fusion
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(3.01 \times 10^{26} \times 1.863\right) \\ & =5.6 \times 10^{26} \mathrm{MeV} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid cylinder is attached to a horizontal massless spring as shown in figure. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, the time period of oscillation of the cylinder is
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the extension in the spring, Then, $x=2 R \theta$
$\therefore$ Torque acting on the cylinder about point of contact,
$T=F \times 2 R=-K x(2 R)=-4 R^2 K \cdot \theta$
$\therefore T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{K e}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\frac{3}{2} M R^2}{4 R^2 K}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{3 M}{8 K}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $Y=5 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}(100 t-2 x)$, what is time period?
questions.solution:
Given that,
$Y=5 \sin (50 \pi t-\pi x)$  ...(i)
Equation of displacement,
$Y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)$  ...(ii)
After comparing with Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$\omega=50 \pi$
$\because$ Time period, $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi}{50 \pi}=0.04 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A particle is subjected to two simple harmonic motions along $X$-axis while other is along a line making angle $45^{\circ}$ with the $X$-axis. The two motions are given by $x=x_0 \sin \omega t$ and $s=s_0 \sin \omega t$.
The amplitude of resultant motion is
questions.solution:
Two SHMs are separated at angle $45^{\circ}$. So, resultant amplitude is
$\begin{aligned} & A_R=\sqrt{\left(s_0+x_0 \cos 45^{\circ}\right)^2+\left(x_0 \sin 45^{\circ}\right)^2} \\ & \quad=\sqrt{\left(s_0^2+x_0^2 \cos ^2 45^{\circ}+2 x_0 s_0 \cos 45^{\circ}+x_0^2 \sin ^2 45^{\circ}\right)} \\ & \quad=\sqrt{s_0^2+x_0^2+\sqrt{2} x_0 s_0}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shows spring + block + pulley system which are light. The time period of mass would be
questions.solution:
Let when $m$ is hanging, the extension in the spring is $l$, then tension in the string is,
$T_1=k l=m g$
when force is applied, let the further extension is x . The tension in the spring is
$T_2=k(x+l)$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Driving force }=T_2-T_1=k(x+l)-k l=k x \\ & \therefore \text { Acceleration }=\frac{k l}{m}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad T & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\text { displacement }}{\text { acceleration }}} \\ & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{x}{\frac{k x}{m}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass of Hg is executing SHM which is given by $x=6.0 \cos \left(100 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \mathrm{cm}$. What is the maximum kinetic energy?
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{m}=1 \mathrm{~kg}$
The given equation of SHM is
$x=6.0 \cos \left(100 t+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
Comparing it with equation of SHM,
$\begin{aligned} x & =A \cos (\omega t+\phi), \text { we have } \\ A & =6.0 \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{6}{100} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
and $\quad \omega=100 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Maximum kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\mathrm{v}_{\max }\right)^2$
$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{~A} \omega)^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times\left[\frac{6}{100} \times 100\right]^2=18 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle executes simple harmonic oscillation with an amplitude $a$. The period of oscillation is $T$. The minimum time taken by the particle to travel half of the amplitude from the equilibrium position is
questions.solution:
Let displacement equation of particle executing SHM is
$y=a \sin \omega t$
As particle travels half of the amplitude from the equilibrium position, so
$y=\frac{a}{2}$
Therefore,
$\frac{a}{2}=a \sin \omega t$
or
$\sin \omega t=\frac{1}{2}=\sin \frac{\pi}{6}$
or
$\omega t=\frac{\pi}{6}$
$or$
$t=\frac{\pi}{6 \omega}$
or
$t=\frac{\pi}{6\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)} \quad\left(\text { as } \omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)$
or $\quad t=\frac{T}{12}$
Hence, the particle travels half of the amplitude from the equilibrium in $\frac{T}{12} \mathrm{~s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The circular motion of a particle with constant speed is
questions.solution: In a circular motion particle repeats after equal intervals of time. So, particle motion on a circular path is periodic but not simple harmonic as it does not execute to and fro motion about a fixed point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle executing simple harmonic motion of amplitude $5$ cm has maximum speed of $31.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of its oscillation is
questions.solution:
Maximum speed of a particle executing SHM is $u_{\max }=a \omega=a(2 \pi n)$
$\Rightarrow n=\frac{u_{\max }}{2 \pi a}$
Here, $u_{\max }=31.4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}, a=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Substituting the given values, we have
$n=\frac{31.4}{2 \times 3.14 \times 5}=1 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pendulum is undergoing SHM with frequency $f$. What is the frequency of its kinetic energy ?
questions.solution:
Let, $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}$ $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{A} \omega \cos \omega \mathrm{t}$ Kinetic energy, $\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2 \cos ^2 \omega \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2\left(\frac{1+\cos 2 \omega \mathrm{t}}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2\left(1+\cos ^2 \omega \mathrm{t}\right) \\ & \therefore \omega_{\mathrm{K}}=2 \omega\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Frequency of oscillation of $\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}=2 \mathrm{f} \quad\left[\mathrm{f}=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is executing the motion $x=a \cos (\omega t-\theta)$. The velocity of the particle is
questions.solution:
Given equation is $x=a \cos (\omega t-\theta)$
So, velocity $\left|\frac{d x}{d t}\right|=a \omega \sin (\omega t-\theta)$
At $(\omega t-\theta)=90^{\circ}, \quad$ the velocity will be maximum, therefore the maximum velocity is
$v=a \omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The spring extends by $x$ on loading, then energy stored by the spring is (if $T$ is the tension in spring and $k$ is spring constant)
questions.solution:
The energy stored in the spring is
$E=\frac{1}{2} k x^2 \quad \ldots$ (i)
and expansion in the spring is
$x=\frac{T}{k}  \quad \ldots$ (ii)
From equations (i) and (ii), we get
$E=\frac{1}{2} k \frac{T^2}{k^2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{T^2}{k}=\frac{T^2}{2 k}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is executing two different simple harmonic motions, mutually perpendicular, of different amplitudes and having phase difference of $\frac{\pi}{2}$. The path of the particle will be
questions.solution: As the different amplitudes are mutually perpendicular which have phase difference of $\frac{\pi}{2}$. Therefore, particle will follow elliptical path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle doing SHM having amplitude 5 cm , mass 0.5 kg and angular frequency $5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ is at 1 cm from mean position. Find potential energy and kinetic energy.
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{m}=0.5 \mathrm{~kg} . \omega=5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{x}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$,$\mathrm{A}=5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$\because$ Potential energy, $\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{x}^2$
or $\quad \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times(5)^2 \times\left(10^{-2}\right)^2$
$=\frac{25}{4} \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}=6.25 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$
$\because$ Kinetic energy, $\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2\left(\mathrm{~A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2\right)$
or $\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times(5)^2(25-1) \times 10^{-4}$
$=150 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass of 1 kg is suspended from a spring of force constant 400 N , executing SHM total energy of the body is 2 J , then maximum acceleration of the spring will be
questions.solution:
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{R}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{400}}=\frac{2 \pi}{20}$
Frequency $n=\frac{20}{2 \pi}$
$\omega=2 \pi n=2 \pi \times \frac{20}{2 \pi}=20$
Total energy
$\begin{aligned}& E=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 r^2 \\& 2=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 400 \times r^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{gathered}r=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m} \\ \text { Acceleration }=\omega^2 r=400 \times \frac{1}{10}=40 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Maximum acceleration in SHM
questions.solution:
Acceleration of a particle in S.H.M is given by
$a=-\omega^2 X$
