Where, x is the displacement from mean position Acceleration will be maximum, When $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A}$, where A is amplitude
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad & \mathrm{a}_{\max }=-\omega^2 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \left|\mathrm{a}_{\max }\right|=\omega^2 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement of a particle along the $x$-axis is given by $x=a \sin ^2 \omega t$. The motion of the particle corresponds to
questions.solution:
Given, $X=\operatorname{asin}^2 \omega t$
or $X=a\left(\frac{1-\cos 2 \omega t}{2}\right) \quad\left[\because \cos 2 \theta=1-2 \sin ^2 \theta\right]$ 
or $X=\frac{a}{2}-\frac{a \cos 2 \omega t}{2}$
Now, $V=\frac{d x}{d t}=a \omega \sin 2 \omega t$ 
and $a=d v / d t=2 a \omega^2 \cos 2 \omega t$
Here, $a$ is not directly proportional to $(-x)$ which is a condition for SHM.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two equal negative charges $-q$ are fixed at the point $(0, a)$ and $(0,-a)$ on the $y$-axis. A positive charge $Q$ is released from rest at the point $(2 a, 0)$ on the $x$-axis. The charge will
options: move to the origin and remain at rest
questions.solution: Component of force on charge of $+Q$ at $P$, along $x$-axis, $\begin{aligned} F_x & =\frac{2 Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)} \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{a^2+x^2}} \\ & =\frac{2 Q q x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)} \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{a^2+x^2}} \\ & =\frac{2 Q q x}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0\left(a^2+x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\end{aligned}$ Which is not directly proportional to $x$. So, motion is oscillatory but not SHM.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the length of a pendulum is made 9 times and mass of the bob is made 4 times, then the value of time period becomes :
questions.solution:
Time period $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}$
i.e., time period does not depend on the mass of the bob.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore & & T & \propto \sqrt{l} \\ & & \frac{T_1}{T_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{l_1}{l_2}} \\ & & \frac{T}{T_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{l}{9 l}} \\ & \therefore & T_2 & =3 T\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A simple pendulum of length $l$ has a maximum angular displacement $\theta$. The maximum kinetic energy of the bob is
questions.solution: Here : length of pendulum $=l$ maximum angular displacement $=\theta$ mass of the bob $=m$ Now height of the bob at maximum angular displacement is $h=l-l \cos \theta=l(1-\cos \theta)$ Also at the end of the displacement kinetic energy of the bob = potential energy of the bob $=m g h=m g l(1-\cos \theta)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two simple pendulums of length 0.5 m and 20 m respectively are given small linear displacement in one direction at the same time. They will again be in the phase when the pendulum of shorter length has completed $x$ oscillations, where $x$ is
questions.solution:
Time period of simple pendulum
For Ist pendulum $\begin{aligned} T_1 & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{5}{g}} \\ & =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{5}{9.8}}=\sqrt{20} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$
For IInd pendulum $T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{20}{g}}$
$=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{20}{9.8}}=2 \sqrt{20} \mathrm{~s}$
Suppose the time will be in same phase is $t$ then
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{t} & =\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{20}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{20}}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{20}}$
So, $\quad t=2 \sqrt{20}$
Hence, the number of oscillation of simple pendulum of shorter length $(x)=\frac{2 \sqrt{20}}{\sqrt{20}}=2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pan with a set of weights is attached to a light spring. The period of vertical oscillation is 0.5 s . When some additional weights are put in pan, then the period of oscillations increases by 0.1 s . The extension caused by the additional weight is
questions.solution:
Given, $T=0.5 \mathrm{~s}$,
We know that, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \Rightarrow 0.5=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$
$\frac{1}{4 \pi}=\sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$
On squaring both sides, we get
$\frac{m}{k}=\frac{1}{16 \pi^2}$...(i)
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad T+0.1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m+m^{\prime}}{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 0.5+0.1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m+m^{\prime}}{k}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{0.6}{2 \pi}\right)^2=\frac{m+m^{\prime}}{k}=\frac{m}{k}+\frac{m^{\prime}}{k} \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{0.6}{2 \pi}\right)^2=\frac{1}{16 \pi^2}+\frac{m^{\prime}}{k} \quad$ [using Eq. (i)]
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(0.6)^2}{4 \pi^2}-\frac{1}{16 \pi^2}=\frac{m^{\prime}}{k}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{m^{\prime}}{k}=\frac{1.44-1}{16 \pi^2} \Rightarrow \frac{m^{\prime}}{k}=\frac{0.44}{16 \pi^2}$....(ii)
$\begin{aligned} \because & m^{\prime} g & =k x \\ \therefore & x & =\frac{m^{\prime}}{k} g\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{0.44}{16 \pi^2} \times 10 \quad$ [using Eq. (ii)]
$\begin{aligned} & =0.0279 \\ & =2.79 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =2.7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If minimum deviation $=30^{\circ}$, then speed of light in shown prism will be
questions.solution:
Given, $\mathrm{A}=60^{\circ}, \delta_{\mathrm{m}}=30^{\circ}$
According to prism formula,
$\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_{\mathrm{m}}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}$
or $\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{60^{\circ}+30^{\circ}}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{60^{\circ}}{2}}=\frac{\sin 45^{\circ}}{\sin 30^{\circ}}$
or $\quad \mu=\sqrt{2}$
$\begin{aligned} \because \quad \mu & =\frac{\text { Speed of light in air (c) }}{\text { Speed of light in prism (v) }} \\ v & =\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mu}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the value of $\theta$ in the given diagram.
questions.solution: Key Idea The angle of incidence in a denser medium for which the angle of refraction in rarer medium becomes $90^{\circ}$ is known as critical angle. $\mathrm{C}=\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{\mu}$ where, $\mu=$ refractive index of denser to rarer medium. Since, BC grazes along sarface $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \text { angle }\left(90-\theta_1\right)=\text { critical angle } C \\ & \therefore \quad \sin C=\frac{1}{\mu} \\ & \sin \left(90-\theta_1\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \cos \theta_1=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1=30^{\circ} \\ & \end{aligned}$ Now, using Snells' law on lst interface, $\begin{array}{rlrl} \mathrm{n}_1 \sin \theta & =\mathrm{n}_2 \sin \theta_1 \\ \Rightarrow & & 1 \sin \theta=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \sin 30^{\circ} & =\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & & \sin \theta & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \Rightarrow & & \theta & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The refractive index of glass is 1.5 . The speed of light in glass is
questions.solution:
From Snell's law of refraction,
$\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}=\frac{v_1}{v_2}$
Given, $v_1=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl} & & \frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1} & ={ }^1 \mu_2=1.5 \\ \Rightarrow & & v_2 & =\frac{v_1}{{ }^1 \mu_2}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ \therefore & & v_2=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 / 2}=2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin symmetrical double convex lens of refractive index $\mu_2=1.5$ is placed between a medium of refractive index $\mu_1=1.4$ to the left and another medium of refractive index $\mu_3=1 \cdot 6$ to the right. Then, the system behaves as
questions.solution:
For first refracting surface,
For parallel beam of light; $-\frac{\mu_1}{u}+\frac{\mu_2}{V}=\frac{\mu_2-1}{R}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow-\frac{1 \cdot 4}{-\infty}+\frac{1 \cdot 5}{V}=\frac{1 \cdot 5-1.4}{R} \\
& V=15 R\end{aligned}$
For second refracting surface,
$\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{\mu_2}{1.5 R}+\frac{\mu_3}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{\mu_3-\mu_2}{R} \\
& \Rightarrow-\frac{1.5}{15 R}+\frac{1.6}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{1.6-1.5}{R} \Rightarrow V^{\prime}=\infty\end{aligned}$
Hence, the combination behaves as glass plate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An isotropic point source of light is suspended $h$ metre vertically above the centre of circular table of radius $r$ metre. Then, the ratio of illumenances at the centre to that at the edge of the table is
questions.solution: According to question, the situation is shown in the figure below $E_1=\frac{I}{(L O)^2}=\frac{I}{h^2}...(i)$ The illumination at the edge $A$ is given by $E_2=\frac{I \cos \theta}{(L A)^2}=\frac{I \cos \theta}{\left(h^2+r^2\right)}...(ii)$ From figure, $\cos \theta=\frac{h}{\sqrt{\left(h^2+r^2\right)}}$ $\therefore E_2=\frac{I h}{\left(h^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}}$ Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get $\begin{aligned}\frac{E_1}{E_2} & =\frac{I / h^2}{I h /\left(h^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}}=\frac{\left(h^2+r^2\right)^{3 / 2}}{h^3} \\ & =\left(\frac{h^2+r^2}{h^2}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(1+\frac{r^2}{h^2}\right)^{3 / 2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the position of final image from first lens. Given focal length of each lens is 10 cm .
questions.solution: (i) For refraction from lst lens, $\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}+\frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{40}=\frac{3}{40} \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{40}{3} \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$ This image acts as object for 2nd lens. $\therefore$ Object distance for 2 nd lens $\mathrm{L}_2=30-\frac{40}{3}=\frac{50}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ (ii) Now for refraction from $L_2, \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{3}{50}=\frac{2}{50} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=25 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$ This image is object for 3rd lens, $\text { ( } \mathrm{v}=30-25=5 \mathrm{~cm} \text { ) }$ $\Rightarrow$ For $\mathrm{L}_3, \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{5}$ or $\mathrm{v}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$ (location of final image $=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ to left of $\mathrm{L}_3$ ) $\therefore$ Distance from $\mathrm{L}_1=30+(30-10)=50 \mathrm{~cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A prism of crown glass with refracting angle of $5^{\circ}$ and mean refractive index $=1.51$ is combined with a flint glass prism of refractive index $=1.65$ to produce no deviation. Find the angle of flint glass.
questions.solution:
Let $A^{\prime}$ be the angle of flint glass prism.
Here, $\mathrm{A}=5^{\circ}$ and $\mu=1.51$ for crown glass prism. Deviation produced by flint glass,
$\delta^{\prime}=(\mu-1) \mathrm{A}=(1.51-1) \times 5=2.55^{\circ}$
For no deviation, $\delta^{\prime}=\delta$
Or $0.65 \mathrm{~A}^{\prime}=2.55$
$\mathrm{A}^{\prime}=\frac{2.55}{0.65}=3.92^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a light ray enters from oil to glass on oil- glass interface, then velocity of light changes by a factor of [given, $\mu_{\text {oil }}=2, \mu_{\text {glass }}=3 / 2$ ]
questions.solution:
Given, $\mu_{\text {oil }}=2$ and $\mu_{\text {glass }}=3 / 2$
velocity of light in oil is given by
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{V}_{\text {oil }}=\frac{\text { Velocity of light (c) }}{\mu_{\text {oil }}}=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mu_{\text {oil }}} \\
\text { Similarly, } \mathrm{V}_{\text {glass }} & =\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mu_{\text {glass }}} \\
\therefore \quad & \frac{V_{\text {glass }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {oil }}}=\frac{\mathrm{c} / \mu_{\text {glass }}}{\mathrm{c} / \mu_{\text {oil }}}=\frac{\mu_{\text {oil }}}{\mu_{\text {glass }}}=\frac{2}{3 / 2}=\frac{4}{3}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fish rising vertically to the surface of water in a lake uniformly at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ observes a kingfisher diving vertically towards the water at a rate of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If refractive index of water $n=\frac{4}{3}$, what will be the actual velocity of the kingfisher
questions.solution: $\because$ Velocity of bird w.r.t. fish, $v_{B \rightarrow F}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and velocity of fish w.r.t. ground $V_{F \rightarrow G}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\therefore$ Distance between bird and fish, as seen by fish $S=x+n y$ on differentiating w.r.t. time, we get $\begin{aligned} & \frac{d S}{d t}=\frac{d x}{d t}+n \frac{d y}{d t} \\ & v_{B \rightarrow F}=v_{F \rightarrow G}+n \cdot v_{B \rightarrow G} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 10=2+\frac{4}{3} v_{B \rightarrow G} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v_{B \rightarrow G}=\frac{8}{4 / 3}=6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A quarter cylinder of radius $R$ and refractive index 1.5 is placed on a table. A point object $P$ is kept at a distance of $m R$ from it as shown in figure. For what value of $m$ for which a ray from $P$ will emerge parallel to the table?
questions.solution: Refraction at plane surface (1), $n_1=1, n_2=1.5=\frac{3}{2}, u=-m R, v=?$ Using formula, $\frac{n_2}{v}-\frac{n_1}{u}=\frac{n_2-n_1}{R}$ $\begin{aligned} & \because \quad R=\infty \text { for plane surface. } \\ & \because \frac{3 / 2}{v}-\frac{1}{(-m R)}=0 \\ & \therefore \quad v=-\frac{3}{2} m R\end{aligned}$ Refraction at spherical surface (2) $\begin{aligned} & u=-\left(\frac{3}{2} m R+R\right) \text { and } R=-R, v=\infty, \\ & n_1=\frac{3}{2}, n_2=1\end{aligned}$ $\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{3 / 2}{\left[-\left(\frac{3}{2} m R+R\right)\right]}=\frac{1-3 / 2}{-R}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 / 2}{\frac{R(3 m+2)}{2}}=\frac{1 / 2}{R} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{6}{3 m+2}=1 \Rightarrow 3 m+2=6 \\ & m=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square wire of side 2.0 cm is placed 20 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm with its centre on the axis of the mirror and its plane normal to the axis. The area enclosed by the image of wire is
questions.solution:
Given, square wire of side $=2 \mathrm{~cm}$, $u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$,
Area of second wire $=$ ?
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u} & =\frac{1}{f} \\ & \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{(-20)} & =\frac{1}{-10} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{20} \\ & \Rightarrow & v & =-20 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
We know that, $m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-(-20)}{-20}=-1$
$\because \quad \frac{\text { Area of image }}{\text { Area of object }}=m^2 \Rightarrow \frac{A_i}{A_0}=(-1)^2$
$\frac{A_i}{2 \times 2}=1$
$\Rightarrow \quad A_i=4 \mathrm{~cm}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A prism of a certain angle deviates the red and blue rays by 8 and 12 , respectively. Another prism of the same angle deviates the red and blue rays by 10 and 14 , respectively. The prisms are small angled and made of different materials. The dispersive power of the materials of the prisms are in the ratio
questions.solution:
For prism 1,
$\begin{aligned} & \delta=\frac{\delta_B+\delta_R}{2}=\frac{12+8}{2}=10 \\ & \text { [given, } \delta_R=8, \delta_B=12 \text { ] } \\ & \therefore \quad \omega_1=\frac{\delta_B-\delta_R}{\delta}=\frac{12-8}{10}=\frac{4}{10}=\frac{2}{5} \\ & \end{aligned}$
For prism 2,
$\begin{aligned} & \delta=\frac{\delta_B+\delta_R}{2}=\frac{14+10}{2}=12 \\ \therefore \quad & \omega_2=\frac{\delta_B-\delta_R}{\delta}=\frac{14-10}{12}=\frac{1}{3}\end{aligned}$
So, $\quad \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\frac{2 / 5}{1 / 3}=\frac{6}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A luminous object is placed at a distance of 30 cm from the convex lens of focal length 20 cm . On the other side of the lens, at what distance from the lens a convex mirror of radius of curvature 10 cm be placed in order to have an upright image of the object coincident with it
questions.solution: Using the formula $\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} & =\frac{1}{f} \\ \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-30} & =\frac{1}{20} \\ v & =60 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ If the image is formed at the centre of curvature of the mirror, the coincidence is possible. Then the rays refracting through the lens will fall normally on the convex mirror and retrace their path of form the image at $O$. Hence, the distance between lens and mirror will be $60-10=50 \mathrm{~cm}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To get three images of a single object, we should have two plane mirrors at an angle of
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} n=\frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta}-1 & \\ & 3=\frac{360^{\circ}}{\theta}-1 \\ \theta & =90^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel of depth $t$ is half filled with a liquid having refractive index $n_1$ and the other half is filled with water of having refractive index $n_2$. The apparent depth of the vessel as viewed from top is
questions.solution:
The apparent depth is given by
$\begin{aligned} I^{\prime} & =\frac{t_1}{n_1}+\frac{t_2}{n_2} \\ & =\frac{t / 2}{n_1}+\frac{t / 2}{n_2}=\frac{t\left(n_1+n_2\right)}{2 n_1 n_2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnification produced by a astronomical telescope for normal adjustment is 10 and the length of the telescope is 1.1 m . The magnification, when the image is formed atleast distance of distinct vision is
questions.solution:
Given, $m=10$, length of telescope $=1.1 \mathrm{~m}$
We know that,
Magnification, $m=\frac{t_0}{t_e}$
$\begin{aligned} & 10=\frac{t_0}{t_e} \\ & f_0=10 f_e \\ & \because \quad f_e+f_0=1.1 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t_e+10 t_e=1.1 \mathrm{~m} \\ & {\left[\because f_0=10 f_e\right]} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f_e(1+10)=1.1 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f_e=0.1 \mathrm{~m} \text { or } 10 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Magnification least distance of distinct vision,
$\begin{aligned} M_b & =\frac{f_o}{f_e}\left(1+\frac{f_e}{D}\right) \\ & =10\left(1+\frac{10}{25}\right) \quad[\because D=25 \mathrm{~cm}] \\ & =10 \times \frac{35}{25} \\ & =14\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point source of light is placed 4 m below the surface of water of refractive index $5 / 3$. The minimum diameter of a disc which should be placed over the source on the surface of water to cut-off all light coming out of water is
questions.solution:
Given, $\sin i=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{3}{5}$
So, $\quad \tan i=\frac{3}{4}=\frac{r}{4}$
It gives $\quad r=3 \mathrm{~m}$
So, the diameter $=6 \mathrm{~m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vessel of height $2 d$ is half filled with a liquid of refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ and the other half with a liquid of refractive index $n$ (the given liquids are immiscible). Then, the apparent depth of the inner surface of the bottom of the vessel (neglecting the thickness of the bottom of the vessel) will be
questions.solution:
Refractive index $\mu=\frac{\text { Real depth }(d)}{\text { Apparent depth }(x)}$
For 1st liquid, $\sqrt{2}=\frac{d}{x_1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x_1=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$
Similarly, for 2nd liquid,
$n=\frac{d}{x_2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x_2=\frac{d}{n}$
Total apparent depth $=x_1+x_2$
$=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{d}{n}=\frac{d(n+\sqrt{2})}{n \sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The two lenses of an achromatic doublet should have
options: equal ratio of their power and dispersive power
sum of the product of their powers and dispersive power equal to zero
questions.solution:
An achromatic doublet is a pair of two lenses such that the combination does not split light into its constituent colors.
Therefore, the net dispersive power of the system must be zero. The mathematical condition then becomes that sum of product of their powers and dispersive powers equal to zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plane mirror produces a magnification of
questions.solution: In a plane mirror, the image formed is erect and of same size as of object. Thus, magnification of plane mirror is 1 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light movs from denser to rarer medium, which of the following is correct?
questions.solution:
When a ray of light moves from one medium to other, its velocity changes. This change depends on refractive index of the medium.
Light travels from denser to rarer medium, $i e$, from medium of higher refractive index to lower refractive index. So, in second (rarer) medium, its velocity increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of a light wave in a material is $2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and wavelength is $5000 Å$. The refractive index of material will be
questions.solution:
Velocity of light waves in material is
$v=\mathrm{v} \lambda\ldots(i)$
Refractive index of material is
$\mu=\frac{c}{v}\ldots(ii)$
where $c$ is speed of light in vacuum or air.
or
$\mu=\frac{c}{v \lambda}\ldots(iii)$
Given, $\quad v=2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$,
$\begin{aligned}
\lambda & =5000 Å=5000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}, \\
c & =3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, from Eq. (iii), we get
$\mu=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^{14} \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}=3.00$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The refractive index of glass is 1.520 for red light and 1.525 for blue light. Let $D_1$ and $D_2$ be angles of minimum deviation for red and blue light respectively in a prism of this glass. then,
options: $D_1$ can be less than or greater than $D_2$ depending upon the angle of prism
questions.solution:
$D=(\mu-1) A$
For blue light $\mu$ is greater than that for red light, so $D_2 \gt D_1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A symmetric double convex lens is cut in two equal parts by a plane perpendicular to the principal axis. If the power of the original lens is $4 D$, the power of a cut lens will be
questions.solution: Biconvex lens is cut perpendicularly to the principal axis, it will become a plano-convex lens. Focal length of biconvex lens $\begin{gathered} \frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \\ \frac{1}{f}=(n-1) \frac{2}{R} \quad\left(\because R_1=R, R_2=-R\right) \end{gathered}$ $\Rightarrow \quad f=\frac{R}{2(n-1)}\ldots(i)$ For plano-convex lens $\begin{gathered} \frac{1}{f_1}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \\ f_1=\frac{R}{(n-1)}\ldots(ii) \end{gathered}$ Comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we see that focal length becomes double. As power of lens $P \propto \frac{1}{\text { focal length }}$ Hence, power will become half. $\text { New power }=\frac{4}{2}=2 \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a laboratory four convex lenses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and $L_4$ of focal lengths $2,4,6$ and 8 cm , respectively are available. Two of these lenses form a telescope of length 10 cm and magnifying power 4. The objective and eye lenses are respectively
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}
\text { Length of tube }=10 \mathrm{~cm} \\
\qquad \begin{array}{c}
f_o+f_e=10 \mathrm{~cm} \\
\text { Magnification } m=\frac{f_o}{f_e}=4 \\
f_o=4 f_e
\end{array}
\end{gathered}$
Putting in Eq. (i),
or
$5 f_{\varepsilon}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
and
$\begin{aligned}
& f_e=2 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& f_o=8 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& f_o=8 \mathrm{~cm}, f_e=2 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $L_4$ and $L_1$ will be used.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A telescope has focal length of objective and eye-piece as 200 cm and 5 cm respectively. What is magnification of telescope?
questions.solution:
In relaxed eye state of telescope or when final image is formed at infinity, the magnification of telescope is given by
$|M|=\frac{f_o}{f_e}$
where $\begin{aligned} f_o & =\text { focal length of objective }=200 \mathrm{~cm} \\ f_e & =\text { focal length of eye-piece }=5 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Hence, $|M|=\frac{200}{5}=40$
$|M|=\frac{200}{5}=40$
Note : The aperture of the objective lens is kept large so that more and more rays coming from the heavenly body may enter the telescope and a bright image is formed by the objective lens. The aperture of the eye lens is kept comparatively small so that all the rays may enter the eye.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Calculate the dispersive power for crown glass from the given data
$\begin{array}{rll} & \mu_{\mathrm{V}}=1.523 \\ \text { and } & \mu_{\mathrm{R}}=1.5145\end{array}$
questions.solution:
Here, $\mu_V=1.523$ and $\mu_R=1.5145$
Mean refractive index,
$\mu=\frac{1.523+1.5145}{2}=1.51875$
Dispersive power is given by,
$\begin{aligned} \omega & =\frac{\left(\mu_V-\mu_R\right)}{(\mu-1)} \\ & =\frac{1.523-1.5145}{(1.51875-1)}=0.01639\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If velocity of light in air is c, what will be the velocity of light in a medium of refractive index 1.4 ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  Refractive index of medium } \\ & \qquad=\frac{\text { speed of light in vacuum }}{\text { speed in medium }} \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Speed of light in medium } \\ & =\frac{\text { speed of light in vacuum }}{\text { refractive index }} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{3 \times 8^8}{1.4}=2.14 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The plane face of a plano convex lens is silvered. If $\mu$ be the refractive index and $R$, the radius of curvature of curved surface, then system will behave like a concave mirror of curvature
questions.solution:
When an object is placed in front of such a lens,the rays first of all refracted from the convex surface, then refract from the polished plane surface and again refracts from convex surface. If $f_l$ and $f_m$ be the focal lengths of lens (convex surface) and mirror (plane polished surface) respectively, then effective focal length $F$ is given by
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{f_l}+\frac{1}{f_m}+\frac{1}{f_l}=\frac{2}{f_l}+\frac{1}{f_m}=\frac{2}{f_l} \quad\left(\because f_m=\frac{R}{2}=\infty\right) \\ & \frac{1}{f_l}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R}\right) \\ & \therefore \frac{1}{F}=\frac{2(\mu-1)}{R} \text { or } F=\frac{R}{2(\mu-1)} \text { or } R=2 F=\frac{R}{(\mu-1)}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following is true for rays coming from infinity?
options: Continuous image is formed between focal points of upper and lower lens
questions.solution:
Since, lens is made of two layers of different refractive indices, for a given wavelength of light it will have two focal lengths or will form two images at two different points as there are $\mu$ 's as
$\frac{1}{f} \propto(\mu-1)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular resolution of a 10 cm diameter telescope at a wavelength of $5000 Å$ is of the order of
questions.solution:
Angular resolution $=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{d}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1.22 \times 5000 \times 10^{-10}}{10 \times 10^{-2}} \\ & =6.1 \times 10^{-6} \\ & \approx 10^{-6} \mathrm{rad}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a convex lens of focal length 80 cm and a concave lens of focal length 50 cm are combined together, what will be their resultant power ?
questions.solution:
$f_1=+80 \mathrm{~cm}, f_2=-50 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Resultant focal length
$\begin{aligned} F & =\frac{f_1 f_2}{f_1+f_2} \\ & =\frac{80 \times-50}{80-50}=\frac{-4000}{30} \\ & =-\frac{4000}{3} \mathrm{~cm} .\end{aligned}$
Resultant power $\begin{aligned} P & =\frac{100}{F}=\frac{100}{-400 / 3} \\ & =-\frac{3}{4} \\ & =-0.75 \mathrm{D} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time taken by light to pass through 4 mm thick glass slab of refractive index 1.5 will be : (velocity of light in air $=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
questions.solution:
Time taken by light
$\begin{aligned} t & =\frac{\mu d}{c} \\ & =\frac{1.5 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 10^8}=2 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A convex lens forms a full image of the object on a screen. If half of the lens is covered with an opaque object, then :
options: half of the image is seen
full image is seen and of same intensity
full image is seen and of decreased intensity
questions.solution: If half of the lens is covered with an opaque object, then, full image is seen but intensity of image decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A prism of refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ has a refracting angle of $60^{\circ}$. At what angle a ray must be incident on it so that it suffers a minimum deviation.
questions.solution:
The relation for refractive index of prism is
$\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$ $\ldots(1)$
The condition for minimum deviation is
$r=\frac{A}{2}=\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}=30^{\circ}$
Putting the given value of $\mu=\sqrt{2}$ and $r=30^{\circ}$ in eq (1), we get
$\begin{aligned} \sqrt{2} & =\frac{\sin i}{\sin 30^{\circ}} \\ \text { So, } \quad \sin i & =\sqrt{2} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ \sin i & =\sin 45^{\circ} \\ i & =45^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius of the convex surface of planoconvex lens is 20 cm and the refractive index of the material of the lens is 1.5. The focal length is
questions.solution:
Using the relation for focal length of planoconvex lens
$=\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
(Refractive index of material of lens $\mu=1.5$, $\left.R_1=20 \mathrm{~cm}, R_2=\infty\right)$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{f} & =(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \\ \frac{1}{f} & =\frac{1}{40} \\ \text { or } \quad f & =40 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short linear object of length $b$ lies along the axis of a concave mirror of focal length $f$ at a distance $u$ from the pole of the mirror, what is the size of image?
questions.solution:
Using the relation for the focal length of concave mirror
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$ ...(1)
Differentiating equation (1), we obtain
$0=-\frac{1}{v^2} d v-\frac{1}{u^2} d u$
So, $\quad d v=-\frac{v^2}{u^2} \times b$ $\ldots(2)$
(Here : $d u=b$ )
From equation (1)
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v} & =\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{u-f}{f u} \\
\frac{u}{v} & =\frac{u-f}{f} \\
\end{aligned}$
$\frac{v}{u}=\frac{f}{u-f}$ $\ldots(3)$
Now, from equations (2) and (3), we get
$d v=-\left(\frac{f}{u-f}\right)^2 b$
Therefore, size or image is $=\left(\frac{f}{u-f}\right)^2 b$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The refractive index of the material of the prism and liquid are 1.56 and 1.32 respectively. What will be the value of $\theta$ for the following refraction?
questions.solution:
Using the formula
$\sin (\theta)=\frac{\mu_{\text {liquid }}}{\mu_{\text {prism }}}=\frac{1.32}{1.56}=\frac{11}{13}$ ...(1)
Now the condition for total internal reflection, occur when $\sin \theta \geq \mu$
So,
$\sin \theta \geq \frac{11}{13}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person who can see things most clearly at a distance of 10 cm , requires spectacles to enable to see clearly things at a distance of 30 cm . What should be the focal length of the spectacles?
questions.solution:
A concave lens is required by the person so that the lens forms the image of object distant 30 cm , at 10 cm it means
$\begin{aligned} u & =-30 \mathrm{~cm}, v=-10 \mathrm{~cm} \\ \frac{1}{f} & =\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \\ & =\frac{1}{-10}-\frac{1}{-30}=-\frac{2}{30} \\ \text { or } \quad \frac{1}{f} & =\frac{-1}{15} \\ \text { So } \quad f & =-15 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Minus sign singnifies that lens used is concave

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What should be the minimum value of refractive index of the material of the prism for the reflections to take place as shown in the figure?
questions.solution:
From the formula, the minimum refractive index is
$\begin{aligned} \mu_m & =\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin 90^{\circ}}{\sin 45^{\circ}} \\ & =\frac{1}{1 / \sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{2}=1.414 \\ & \approx 1.4\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A student can distinctly see the object upto a distance 15 cm. He wants to see the black board at a distance of 3 m. Focal length and power of lens used respectively will be :
questions.solution:
The student should use a lens which forms image at distance of 15 cm of the object placed at 3 m i.e., object distance $u=-3 \mathrm{~m}=-300 \mathrm{~cm}$, image distance $v=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$
From lens formula $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$ 
we get $\quad \frac{1}{-15}-\frac{1}{-300}=\frac{1}{f}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{15}=-\frac{19}{300}$
or $\quad f=\frac{-300}{19}=-15.8 \mathrm{~cm} \quad$ (concave lens)
Now power of the lens is
$P=\frac{100}{f(\mathrm{~cm})}=\frac{100}{-15.8}=-6.33 \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A this glass prism $(\mu=1.5)$ in the position of minimum deviation deviates the monochromatic light ray by $10^{\circ}$, the refracting angle of prism is
questions.solution:
Given : $\delta_m=10^{\circ}, \mu=1.5$
For a thin prism of minimum deviation
$\delta_m=(\mu-1) \mathrm{A}$
$10^{\circ}=(1.5-1) \mathrm{A}$ or $A=\frac{10}{0.5}=20^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two thin lenses of powers 12 D and -2 D respectively are placed in contact, the power, focal length and nature respectively will be
questions.solution:
Given: $P_1=12 \mathrm{D}, P_2=-2 \mathrm{D}$
Power of combination
$P=P_1+P_2=12-2=10 \mathrm{D}$
Focal length $f=\frac{1}{P}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}$
The combined lens will be convex (convergent)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a given lens, the magnification was found to be twice as large as when the object was 0.15 m distant from it as when the distance was 0.2 m . The focal length of the lens is
questions.solution: Let as shown, 1 and 2 are positions of objects and images in two different situations. It is given $\left|\frac{v_1}{u_1}\right|=2\left|\frac{v_2}{u_2}\right|$ Here, $u_1=-15 \mathrm{~cm}, u_2=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\therefore \quad v_1=2 v_2 \times \frac{u_1}{u_2}$ $=2 v_2 \times \frac{15}{20}=\frac{3}{2} v_2$ Now, $\quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u_1}$ and $\quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}$ So, $\frac{1}{v_1}-\frac{1}{u_1}=\frac{1}{v_2}-\frac{1}{u_2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3 v_2}+\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v_2}+\frac{1}{20}$ $\Rightarrow \quad v=20 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The tip of a needle does not give a sharp image on a screen. This is due to
questions.solution: Image is not sharp because of deviation of light from straight line path account of diffraction of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In refraction, light waves are bent on passing from one medium to the second medium, because, in the second medium
options: the coefficient of elasticity is different
questions.solution:
When a ray of light travels from one medium to the second medium, it bends. If first medium is
rarer and second medium is denser, it bends towards the normal while if first medium is denser and second medium is rarer, it bends away from the normal. This bending is due to refractive index of second medium with respect to first.
The refractive index is related to the speed of light in the two media as below :
${ }_1 \mu_2=\frac{\text { speed of light in first medium }}{\text { speed of light in second medium }}$
or speed of light in second medium
$=\frac{\text { speed of light in first medium }}{{ }_1 \mu_2}$
where ${ }_1 \mu_2$ is refractive index of second medium relative to the first medium.
Hence, we see that on passing through another medium speed of light changes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
If the focal length of a lens of a camera is $5 f$ and that of another is $2.5 f$, what is the time of exposure for the second, if for the first one is $\frac{1}{200} \mathrm{~s}$ ?
(where $f$ is focal length/unit aperture)
questions.solution:
Ratio of time of expoiusers
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{t_1}{t_2}=\left(\frac{f_1}{f_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{5 f}{2.5 f}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1 / 200}{t_2}=(2)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t_2=\frac{1}{800} \mathrm{~s} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lens acts as converging in air and diverging when immersed in water. Then, refractive index of lens is :
questions.solution: When lens is immersed in water then its nature changes. The refractive index of lens is less than that of wateri.e., less than 1.33 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A plane glass slab is kept over various colour letters, the letter which appears least raised is :
questions.solution:
Refractive index
$\mu=\frac{\text { real depth }}{\text { apparent depth }}$
For red colour, refractive index is minimum, i.e., it will have maximum apparent depth and hence, appears least raised.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light passing through air has wavelength $6000 Å$. The wavelength when same ray passes through a glass slab of refractive index 1.5 will be :
questions.solution:
When light ray passes through a glass slab, then new wavelength
$\begin{aligned} \lambda^{\prime} & =\frac{\lambda}{\mu}=\frac{6000}{1.5} \\ & =4000 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam of light composed of red and green rays is incident obliquely at a point on the face of a rectangular glass slab. When coming out on the opposite parallel face, the red and green rays emerge from
options: two points propagating in two different non-parallel directions.
two points propagating in two different parallel directions.
one point propagating in two different directions.
one point propagating in the same direction.
questions.solution: We know that in any medium except vacuum or air, the velocities of different colours are different. Therefore, both red and green colours are refracted at different angles of refraction. So, after emerging from glass slab through opposite parallel faces, they appear at two different points and move in two different parallel direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball of radius $R$ rolls without slipping. Find the fraction of total energy associated with its rotational energy, if the radius of the gyration of the ball about an axis passing through its centre of mass is $K$.
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy of rotation is
$K_{\text {rot }}=\frac{1}{2} l \omega^2=\frac{1}{2} M K^2 \frac{v^2}{R^2}$
where, $k$ is radius of gyration.
Kinetic energy of translation is $K_{\text {trans }}=\frac{1}{2} M v^2$
Thus total energy,
$\begin{aligned} E & =K_{\text {rot }}+K_{\text {trans }} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} M K^2 \frac{v^2}{R^2}+\frac{1}{2} M v^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} M v^2\left(\frac{K^2}{R^2}+1\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{M v^2}{R^2}\left(K^2+R^2\right)\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\begin{aligned} \frac{K_{\text {rot }}}{\text { Total energy, } E} & =\frac{\frac{1}{2} M K^2 \frac{v^2}{R^2}}{\frac{1}{2} \frac{M v^2}{R^2}\left(K^2+R^2\right)} \\ & =\frac{K^2}{K^2+R^2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rod of mass m and length $l_0$ is pivoted at one end and is hanging in the vertical direction. The period of small angular oscillations of the rod is
questions.solution:
Here, the rod is oscillating about an end point $O$. Hence, moment of inertia of rod about the point of oscillating is
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~m} l_0^2$
Moreover, length $l$ of the pendulum = distance from the oscillation axis to centre of mass of rod
$=l_0 / 2$
$\therefore$ Time period of oscillation,
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{mg} l}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\frac{\mathrm{l}}{3} \mathrm{~m} l_0^2}{m g\left(\frac{l_0}{2}\right)}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 l_0}{3 \mathrm{~g}}} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find ratio of radius of gyration of a disk and ring of same radii at their tangential axis in plane.
questions.solution:
For disk, moment of inertia about the diameter, $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\mathrm{mr}^2}{4}$ and moment of inertia about the tangential axis
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_{\text {disc }}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{d}}+\mathrm{mr}^2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\text {disc }}=\frac{\mathrm{mr}^2}{4}+\mathrm{mr}^2 \\ & \mathrm{I}_{\text {disc }}=\frac{5 \mathrm{mr}^2}{4}\end{aligned}$
Let the radius of gyration of disc is $\mathrm{k}_{\text {disc }}$.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I}_{\text {disc }} & =\mathrm{mk}_{\text {disc }}^2 \\ \frac{5 \mathrm{mr}^2}{4} & =\mathrm{mk}_{\text {disc }}^2 \\ \mathrm{k}_{\text {disc }}^2 & =\sqrt{\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{r}}\end{aligned}$
For ring, moment of inertia about the diameter
$\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{a}}^{\prime}=\mathrm{mr}^2 / 2$
and moment of inertia about the tangential axis
$\mathrm{I}_{\text {ring }}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{a}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{mr}^2=\frac{\mathrm{mr}^2}{2}+\mathrm{mr}^2=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{mr}^2$
Let the radius of gyration of ring $\mathrm{k}_{\text {ring }}$.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{I}_{\text {ring }} & =\mathrm{mk}^2 \text { ring } \\ \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{mr}^2 & =\mathrm{mk}^2 \text { ring } \\ \mathrm{k}_{\text {ring }} & =\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \mathrm{r} \\ \frac{\mathrm{k}_{\text {disc }}}{\mathrm{k}_{\text {ring }}} & =\frac{\sqrt{\frac{5}{4} \mathrm{r}}}{\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} \mathrm{r}}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{6}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bicycle wheel rolls without slipping on a horizontal floor. Which one of the following is true about the motion of points on the rim of the wheel, relative to the axis at the wheel's centre?
options: Points near the top move faster than points near the bottom
Points near the bottom move faster than points near the top
All points on the rim move with the same speed
All points have the velocity vectors that are pointing in the radial direction towards the centre of the wheel
questions.solution: We have, $V_A=2 v \sin \theta / 2$ Hence, velocity of point on rim increases with $\theta$ for $0^{\circ} \lt \theta \lt 180^{\circ}$ and decreases with $\theta$ for $180^{\circ} \lt \theta \lt 30^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A tangential force acting on the top of sphere of mass $m$ kept on a rough horizontal place as shown in figure. If the sphere rolls without slipping, then the acceleration with which the centre of sphere moves, is
questions.solution:
Let $a$ be the acceleration of centre of sphere. The angular acceleration about the centre of the sphere is $\alpha=\frac{a}{r}$, as there is no slipping.
For the linear motion of the centre
$f+F=m a$ ...(i)
and for the rotational motion about the centre
$F r-f r=I \alpha=\left(\frac{2}{5} m r^2\right)\left(\frac{a}{r}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad F-f=\frac{2}{5} m a$ ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii)
$2 F=\frac{7}{5} m a \Rightarrow a=\frac{10 F}{7 m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $2 R$ rolls down an inclined plane of height $h$ without slipping. The speed of its centre of mass when it reaches the bottom is
questions.solution:
When solid sphere rolls down on an inclined plane, then it has both rotational and translational kinetic energy
or $\quad \begin{array}{ll} & K=K_{\text {rot }}+K_{\text {trans }} \\ & K=\frac{1}{2} l \omega^2+\frac{1}{2} M v^2\end{array}$
where, $I=$ moment of inertia of solid sphere $=\frac{2}{5} M R^2$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad K & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2}{5} M(2 R)^2\right) \omega^2+\frac{1}{2} M v^2 & {[\because R=2 R] } \\ & =\frac{4}{5} M R^2\left(\frac{v}{2 R}\right)^2+\frac{7}{2} M v^2 & {[\because v=r \omega] }\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{1}{5} M v^2+\frac{1}{2} M v^2=\frac{1}{10} M v^2$
Now, gain in $\mathrm{KE}=$ loss in PE
$\begin{aligned} \frac{7}{10} M v^2 & =M g h \\ v & =\sqrt{\frac{10}{7} g h}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If applied torque on a system is zero, i.e., $\tau=0$, then for that system
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \tau= & l \alpha \\ & I=\text { moment of inertia } \\ & \alpha=\text { angular acceleration }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { If } & \tau=0 \Rightarrow l \alpha=0 \\ \text { But } & I \neq 0\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \alpha=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two rigid bodies $A$ and $B$ rotate with rotational kinetic energies $E_A$ and $E_B$ respectively. The moments of inertia of $A$ and $B$ about the axis of rotation are $I_A$ and $I_B$ respectively.
If $I_A=\frac{I_B}{4}$ and $E_A=100=E_B$, the ratio of angular momentum $\left(L_A\right)$ of $A$ to the angular momentum $\left(L_B\right)$ of $B$ is
questions.solution:
From, the formula, rotational KE
$E_{\text {rotational }}=\frac{1}{2} l \omega^2=\frac{L^2}{2 \mid}(\because L=\mid \omega)$
Therefore, $\quad L^2=2 E l$
Hence
$L_A^2=2 E_A I_A\ldots(i)$
and
$L_B^2=2 E_B I_B\ldots(ii)$
From Eq. (i) and (ii)
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{L_A}{L_B}=\sqrt{\frac{2 E_A I_B}{2 E_B I_A}}=\sqrt{\frac{100}{4}}=5 \\ & \quad\left(\because \frac{E_B}{E_A}=100 \text { and } \frac{I_A}{I_B}=4\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel of radius 0.4 m can rotate freely about its axis as shown in the figure. A string is wrapped over its rim and a mass of 4 kg is hung. An angular acceleration of $8 \mathrm{~rad/s}^{-2}$ is produced in it due to the torque. Then, moment of inertia of the wheel is $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\right)$
questions.solution: Given, $\quad r=0.4 \mathrm{~m}$ $\alpha=8 \mathrm{~rad}/ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ $m=4 \mathrm{~kg}, \quad I=$ ? Torque, $\quad \tau=I \alpha$ $m g r=I \cdot \alpha$ or $\quad 4 \times 10 \times 0.4=I \times 8$ $\Rightarrow \quad I=\frac{16}{8}=2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$ or $I=2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a ceiling fan is switched on, it makes 10 revolution in the first 3 s. Assuming a uniform angular acceleration, how many rotation it will make in the next 3 s ?
questions.solution:
Angle turned in three second and $\theta_3 s=2 \pi \times 10$
$=20 \pi \mathrm{rad}$
$\theta_1=\omega_0 t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$
$\alpha=\frac{49 \pi}{9} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Now, angle turned in 6 s from the starting
$\theta_2=\omega_0 t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$
$=0+\frac{1}{2} \times\left(\frac{40 \pi}{9}\right)+(6)^2$
$=80 \pi \mathrm{rad}$
Angle turned between $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ to $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$
$\theta_{\text {lost }}=\theta_2-\theta_3=80 \pi-20 \pi$
$=60 \pi$
Number of revolution $=\frac{60 \pi}{2 \pi}=30$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Point masses $1,2,3$ and 4 kg are lying at the points $(0,0,0),(2,0,0),(0,3,0)$ and $(-2,-2,0)$ respectively. The moment of inertia of this system about $X$-axis will be
questions.solution: Moment of inertia of the whole system about the axis of rotation will be equal to the sum of the moments of inertia of all the particles. $I=I_1+I_2+I_3+I_4$ $\therefore I=m_1 r_1^2+m_2 r_2^2+m_3 r_3^2+m_4 r_4^2$ $I=(1 \times 0)+(2 \times 0)+\left(3 \times 3^2\right)+4(-2)^2$ $I=0+0+27+16=43 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis at a distance 6 cm from its centre of mass is 10 cm . Then, its radius of gyration about a parallel axis through its centre of mass will be
questions.solution:
From the theorem of parallel axis, the moment of inertia $I$ is equal to
$I=I_{\mathrm{CM}}+M a^2$
where $I_{\mathrm{CM}}$ is moment of inertia about centre of mass and $a$ the distance of axis from centre.
$\therefore \quad I=M K^2+M \times(6)^2$
$M K_1^2=M K^2+36 M$
$\Rightarrow \quad K_1^2=K^2+36$
$\Rightarrow \quad(10)^2=K^2+36$
$\Rightarrow \quad K^2=100-36=64$
$\Rightarrow \quad K=8 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ about an axis passing from the edge of the disc and normal to the disc is
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of uniform circular disc about an axis through its centre and normal to its plane
$I_{\mathrm{CG}}=\frac{1}{2} M R^2$
According to theorem of parallel axis, the moment of inertia of the uniform circular disc about an axis passing from the edge of the disc fand normal to the disc,
$I=I_{\mathrm{CG}}+M R^2=\frac{1}{2} M R^2+M R^2=\frac{3}{2} M R^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel has angular acceleration of $3.0 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and an initial angular speed of $2.00 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. In a time of 2 s it has rotated through an angle (in radian) of
questions.solution:
$\theta=\omega_0 t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$
Given, $\alpha=3.0 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^2, \omega_0=2.0 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}, t=2 \mathrm{~s}$.
Hence, $\quad \theta=2 \times 2+\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times(2)^2$ or $\theta=4+6=10 \mathrm{rad}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylinder rolls up an inclined plane, reaches some height and then rolls down (without slipping throughout these motions). The directions of the frictional force acting on the cylinder are
options: up the incline while ascending and down the incline while descending.
up the incline while ascending as well as descending.
down the incline while ascending and up the incline while descending.
down the incline while ascending as well as descending.
questions.solution: See given the figure. It is obvious from figure, that during ascending, a retarding, i.e. anticlockwise moment is required. It should be remembered that torque due to friction has the same sense the angular acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two disc having mass ratio $1 / 2$ and diameter ratio $2 / 1$ then find ratio of moment of inertia.
questions.solution:
Given, mass ratio of two disc
$\mathrm{m}_1: \mathrm{m}_2=1: 2 \text { i.e. } \frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2}=\frac{1}{2}$
and diameter ratio $\frac{\mathrm{d}_1}{\mathrm{~d}_2}=\frac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}=\frac{2}{1}$
$\therefore$ Ratio of their moment of inertia,
$\begin{array}{ll}
\quad \frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_2}= & \frac{\frac{\mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{r}_1^2}{2}}{\frac{\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{r}_2^2}{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_1}{\mathrm{~m}_2} \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_1}{\mathrm{r}_2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^2=\frac{2}{1} \\
\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}_1: \mathrm{I}_2=2: 1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A B C$ is right angled triangular plane of uniform thickness. The sides are such that $A B \gt B C$ as shown in figure. $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are moments of inertia about $A B, B C$ and $A C$, respectively. Then which of the following relations is correct?
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of a body about an axis depends not only on the mass of the body but also on the distribution of mass about the axis. For a given body mass is same, so it will depend only on the distribution of mass about the axis. The mass is farthest from axis $B C$, so $I_2$ is maximum mass is nearest to axis $A C$, so $I_3$ is minimum.
Hence, the correct sequence will be $I_2 \gt I_1 \gt I_3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A disc of moment of inertia $2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$ revolving with $8 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ is placed on another disc of moment of inertia $4 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$ revolving $4 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. What is the angular frequency of composite disc?
questions.solution:
Given that, moment of inertia of discs,
$\mathrm{I}_1=2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2, \mathrm{I}_2=4 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^2$
Angular velocity of discs, $\omega_1=8 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$, $\omega_2=4 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
From angular momentum conservation principle,
$\mathrm{I}_1 \omega_1+\mathrm{I}_2 \omega_2=\left(\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2\right) \omega$
or $\quad 2 \times 8+4 \times 4=(2+4) \omega$
or $\quad \omega=\frac{16+16}{6}=\frac{32}{6}=\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A drum of radius $R$ and mass $M$, rolls down without slipping along an inclined plane of angle $\theta$. The frictional force
options: converts translational energy to rotational energy
decreases the rotational and translational motion
questions.solution: When a body rolls down without slipping along an inclined plane of inclination $\theta$, it rotates about a horizontal axis through its centre of mass and also its centre of mass moves. Therefore, rolling motion may be regarded as a rotational motion about an axis through its centre of mass plus a translational motion of the centre of mass. As it rolls down, it suffers loss in gravitational potential energy provided translational energy due to frictional force is converted into rotational energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc of radius $R$ and mass $M$ about an axis passing from the edge of the disc and normal to the disc is
questions.solution: We should use parallel axis theorem. Moment of inertia of disc passing through its centre of gravity and perpendicular to its plane is $I_{A B}=\frac{1}{2} M R^2$ Using theorem of parallel axes, we have $\begin{aligned} I_{C D} & =I_{A B}+M R^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} M R^2+M R^2 \\ & =\frac{3}{2} M R^2\end{aligned}$ NOTE : The role of moment of inertia in the study of rotational motion is analogous to that of mass in study of linear motion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular momentum of a rotating body changes from $A_0$ to $4 A_0$ in 4 seconds. The torque acting on the body is :
questions.solution: Torque $\begin{aligned} \tau=\frac{d L}{d t} & =\frac{\left(4 A_0-A_0\right)}{4} \\ & =\frac{3 A_0}{4} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a spherical ball rolls on a table without slipping the friction of its total energy associated with rotational energy is :
questions.solution:
Rotational energy of sphere
$E_R=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$
For sphere, moment of inertia
$\begin{aligned} I & =\frac{2}{5} m R^2 \\ \therefore \quad E_R & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2}{5} m R^2\right)\left(\frac{v}{R}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{5} m v^2\end{aligned}$
Translational kinetic energy $E_r=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Total energy } & =\frac{1}{5} m v^2+\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\ & =\frac{7}{10} m v^2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Required fraction $=\frac{\frac{1}{5} m v^2}{\frac{7}{10} m v^2}$
$=\frac{2}{7}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sphere of diameter 0.2 m and mass 2 kg is rolling on an inclined plane with velocity $v=0.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The kinetic energy of the sphere is
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy of sphere while rolling on an inclined plane is given by
$E_K=\text { Translational kinetic energy }+ 	ext{ Rotational kinetic energy}$
$\begin{aligned}= & \frac{1}{2} m v^2+\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 \\ E_K= & \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(0.5)^2+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times 2 \times(0.1)^2 \times\left(\frac{0.5}{0.1}\right)^2 \\ = & 0.25+0.17=0.42 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere is set into motion on a rough horizontal surface with a linear speed $v$ in the forward direction and an angular speed $\frac{V}{R}$ in the anticlockwise direction as shown in figure. Find the linear speed of the sphere when it stops rotating and $\omega=\frac{V}{R}$.
questions.solution:
If we take moment at $A$, then extemal torque will be zero. Therefore, the initial angular momentum = the angular momentum after rotation stop (i.e. only linear velocity exist)
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & M v \times R-\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) I \omega & =M v_0 \times R \\ \Rightarrow & M v R-\frac{2}{5} M R^2 \times \frac{V}{R} & =M v_0 R \\ \Rightarrow & v_0 & =\frac{3 v}{5}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five particles of mass 2 kg are attached to the rim of a circular disc of radius 0.1 m and negligible mass. Moment of inertia of the system about the axis passing through the centre of the disc and perpendicular to its plane is
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia of the given system that contains 5 particles each of mass $=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ on the rim of circular disc of radius 0.1 m and of negligible mass is given by
$=\text { MI of disc }+ \text { MI of particle }$
Since the mass of the disc is negligible therefore, MI of the system $=$ MI of particle
$=5 \times 2 \times(0.1)^2=0.1 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is kept at rest at the top of a sphere of diameter 42 m . When disturbed slightly, it slides down. At what height $h$ from the bottom, the particle will leave the sphere?
questions.solution: The condition of slipping by particle from top is given by $\cos \theta=2 / 3$ From the figure, $h=r \cos \theta=21 \times \frac{2}{3}=14 \mathrm{~m}$ Therefore, the required height at which the particle will leave the sphere is $=21+h=21+14=35 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere and a hollow sphere of the same material and of a same size can be distinguished without weighing
options: by determining their moments of inertia about their coaxial axes
by rolling them simultaneously on an inclined plane
by rotating them about a common axis of rotation
by applying equal torque on them
questions.solution: The acceleration of a body rolling down the plane $=a$. $\therefore \quad a=\frac{g \sin \theta}{1+\frac{K^2}{R^2}}$ where $K$ is radius of gyration and $R$ the radius of sphere. For solid sphere, $\frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{2}{5}$ $\therefore \quad a=\frac{5}{7} g \sin \theta$ $=0.7(g \sin \theta)$ For hollow sphere, $\frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{2}{3}$ $=\frac{3}{5} g \sin \theta$ $=0.6(g \sin \theta)$ Since, acceleration of solid sphere is more than of hollow sphere, it rolls faster, and reaches the bottom of the inclined plane earlier. Hence, solid sphere and hollow sphere can be distinguished by rolling them simultaneously on an inclined plane.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force of $-F \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ acts on $O$, the origin of the coordinate system. The torque about the point $(1,-1)$ is
questions.solution:
Position vector \(\vec{r}=\mathrm{PO}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{O}}-(1 \hat{i}-1 \hat{\mathrm{j}})=-\hat{i}+\hat{j}\)
Torque about point \(P, \vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \vec{\tau}=(-\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \times(-F \hat{k}) \\
& \text{OR } \vec{\tau}=F[(\hat{i} \times \hat{k})-(\hat{j} \times \hat{k})]=F[-\hat{j}-\hat{i}] \\
& \Rightarrow \vec{\tau}=-F[\hat{i}+\hat{j}]
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The reason of current flow in $p-n$ junction in forward bias is
questions.solution: Due to diffusion of charge carriers current flows throw in $p-n$ junction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which logic gate is represented by the following combination of logic gates?
questions.solution: Output of gate $1, Y_1=\bar{A}$ Output of gate $2, Y_2=\bar{B}$ Output of gate 3 , $\begin{aligned} Y & =\overline{Y_1+Y_2}=\overline{\bar{A}+\bar{B}} \\ & =\overline{\bar{A}} \times \overline{\bar{B}} \\ & =A B\end{aligned}$ Which is the output of AND gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The following circuit represents
questions.solution:
Output of upper AND gate $=\bar{A} B$
Output of lower AND gate $=A \bar{B}$
Thus, output of OR gate $=\bar{A} B+A \bar{B}$
This is Boolean expression for XOR gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The transfer ratio $\beta$ of a transistor is 50 . The input resistance of the transistor when used in the common emitter configuration is $2 \mathrm{k} \Omega$. The peak value of the collector AC current for an AC input voltage of 0.02 V peak is
questions.solution:
Given, $\beta=50, R=2 \mathrm{k} \Omega=2 \times 10^3 \Omega$,
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta V_b=0.02 \mathrm{~V} \\ & i_c=?\end{aligned}$
Input current,
$i_b=\frac{\Delta V_b}{R_b}=\frac{0.02}{2000}=10^{-5} \mathrm{~A}$
Also, current gain, $\beta=\frac{i_c}{i_b}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad i_c & =\beta i_b=50 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~A} \\ & =500 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A}=500 \mu \mathrm{A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
For CE configuration $\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{n}$ transistor.
Which of the following statement is correct?
questions.solution: In CE configuration $n-p-n$ transistor, emitter current $\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{E}}\right)$ is equal to sum of collector current $\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)$ and base current $\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}\right)$ i.e, $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{C}}+\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When plate voltage in diode valve is increased from 100 V to 150 V. Then, plate current increases from 7.5 mA to 12 mA, then dynamic plate resistance will be
questions.solution:

$=11.1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which logic gate is represented by the following combination of logic gates?
questions.solution: Output of gate-1, $Y_1=\bar{A}$ Output of gate-2, $Y_2=\bar{B}$ Output of gate-3, $\begin{aligned} Y=\overline{Y_1+Y_2} & =\overline{\bar{A}+\bar{B}} \\ & =\overline{\bar{A}} \overline{\bar{B}} \\ & =A B \end{aligned}$ which is the output of AND gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Carbon, silicon and germanium atoms have four valence electrons each. Their valence and conduction bands are separated by energy band gaps represented by $\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{C}},\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Si}}$ and $\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Ge}}$ respectively. Which one of the following relationships is true in their case?
questions.solution:
Carbon, silicon and germanium are semiconductors.
$\begin{aligned}& \left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{C}}=5.2 \mathrm{eV} \\& \left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Si}}=1.21 \mathrm{eV} \\& \left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Ge}}=0.75 \mathrm{eV}\end{aligned}$
Thus, $\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{C}}\gt\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Si}}$ and $\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{C}}\gt\left(E_g\right)_{\mathrm{Ge}}$
NOTE : Elements carbon, silicon and germanium are found in IVA group of periodic table.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Choose the only false statement from the following
options: Substances with energy gap of the order of 10 eV are insulators
The conductivity of a semiconductor increases with increases in temperature
In conductors the valence and conduction bands may overlap
The resistivity of a semiconductor increases with increase in temperature
questions.solution:
(a) In insulators energy gap is of the order of 5 to 10 eV and it is practically impossible to impart this much amount of energy to the electrons in valence band.
So, as to jump to conduction band.
So, choice (a) is correct.
(b) In semiconductors with the rise in temperature more electrons from valence band jump to conduction band and this results is increase in conductivity. So, choice (b) is correct.
(c) In conductors, the conduction band is either partially filled or the conduction band overlaps on the valence band. So, choice (c) is correct.
(d) In semiconductor, resistivity decreases with increase in temperature.
So, choice (d) is wrong.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Zener diode is used for
questions.solution: Zener diode is a silicon crystal diode having an unusual reverse current characteristic which is particularly suitable for voltage regulating purposes. Due to this characteristic, it is used as voltage stabilizer in many applications in electronics.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In junction diode, the holes are because of
questions.solution: In semiconductor, when an electron leaves its place, a positive charge is left behind and it is known as hole. Hence, holes are created becauase of missing electrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The amplification factor of a triode valve is 15 . If the grid voltage is changed by 0.3 V , the change in plate voltage in order to keep the current constant (in volt) is :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mu=15, \Delta V_g & =0 \cdot 3 \\ \therefore \quad \mu & =\frac{\Delta V_p}{\Delta V_g} \Rightarrow \Delta V_p=\mu \Delta V_g \\ & =15 \times 0.3 \\ & =4.5 \mathrm{~V} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A change of 8.0 mA in the emitter current brings a change of 7.9 mA in the collector current. The value of $\alpha$ will be
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{aligned} \quad I_E & =I_B+I_C \\ \Delta I_E & =\Delta I_B+\Delta I_C\end{aligned}$
According to question,
$\text { Thus, } \quad \begin{aligned}
\Delta I_E & =8.0 \mathrm{~mA} \\
\Delta I_C & =7.9 \mathrm{~mA} \\
\Delta I_B & =8.0 \mathrm{~mA}-7.9 \mathrm{~mA} \\
& =0.1 \mathrm{~mA}
\end{aligned}$
Thus, $\quad \Delta I_B=8.0 \mathrm{~mA}-7.9 \mathrm{~mA}$
Now, $\alpha=\frac{I_C}{I_E}=\frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_E}=\frac{7.9 \mathrm{~mA}}{8.0 \mathrm{~mA}} \cong 0.99$

questions.solution: Field effect transistors are unipolar transistor because they involve single carrier type operation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the $C B$ mode of a transistor, when the collector voltage is changed by 0.5 volt, the collector current changes by 0.05 mA . The output resistance will be
questions.solution:
Here:
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta V_c=0.5 \mathrm{volt}, \\
& \Delta I_c=0.05 \mathrm{~mA}=0.05 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned}$
Output resistance is given by
$\begin{aligned} R_{\text {out }} & =\frac{\Delta V_c}{\Delta I_c} \\ & =\frac{0.5}{0.05 \times 10^{-3}}=10^4 \Omega=10 \mathrm{~K} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A system consist of a cylinder surrounded by a cylindrical shell. A cylinder is a radius $R$ and is made of material of thermal conductivity $K$, whereas a cylindrical shell has inner radius $R$ and outer radius $2 R$ and is made of material of thermal conductivity twice as that of cylinder. Assuming the system in steady state and negligible heat loss across the cylindrical surface, find the effective thermal conductivity of the system, if the two ends of the combined system are maintained at two different temperatures.
questions.solution: $\therefore \quad R_1=\frac{L}{K_1 \cdot \pi R^2}$ and $\begin{aligned} R_2 & =\frac{L}{K_2\left(4 \pi R^2-\pi R^2\right)}=\frac{L}{3 K_2 \pi R^2} \\ R_{\text {eq }} & =\frac{L}{K_{\text {eq }} \cdot 4 \pi R^2}\end{aligned}$ $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in parallel. $\therefore \quad \frac{1}{R_{\text {eq }}}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \frac{4 \pi R^2 K_{\text {eq }}}{L} & =\frac{\pi R^2 K_1}{L}+\frac{3 \pi R^2 K_2}{L} \\ & \Rightarrow & 4 K_{\text {eq }} & =K_1+3 K_2\end{aligned}$ On putting values of $K_1$ and $K_2$, we get $\begin{aligned} 4 K_{\text {eq }} & =K+3(2 K) \quad\left[\because K_1=K, K_2=2 K\right] \\ K_{\text {eq }} & =\frac{K+6 K}{4}=\frac{7 K}{4}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Psychrometer is used to measure
questions.solution: A psychrometer is a device which is used to measure humidity in the atmosphere through the use of two thermometer namely a dry bulb thermometer and a wet bulb thermometer. Through the comparision of both temperatures, individuals determine the relative humidity of the surrounding area by calculating the difference between the temperature. Barometer is used to measure pressure, thermometer is used to measure temperature and hydrometer is used to measure density.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A slab consists of portions of different materials of same thickness and having the conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the slab is
questions.solution:
As two portions of the slab are connected in series, therefore
$\begin{aligned} K & =\frac{\sum x_i}{\sum x_i / K_1}=\frac{d+d}{\frac{d}{K_1}+\frac{d}{K_2}} \\ & =\frac{2 d}{d\left(\frac{1}{K_1}+\frac{1}{K_2}\right)}=\frac{2 K_1 K_2}{K_1+K_2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lead ball at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is dropped from a height of 6.2 km . The ball is heated due to the air resistance and it completely melts just before reaching the ground. The molten substance falls slowly on the ground. If the specific heat of lead $=126 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-10} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ and melting point of lead $=130 \quad{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and suppose that any mechanical energy lost is used to heat the ball, then the latent heat of fusion of lead is
questions.solution:
The gravitational potential energy of the ball $=m g h$
$\begin{aligned} & =m \times 10 \times 6.2 \times 10^3 \\ & =m \times 62 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
Now energy required to take ball from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $330^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
$m \times 126 \times 300=m \times 37800$
Energy required to melt the ball $=m L$ 
where, $L=$ latent heat
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow m \times 6.2 \times 10^4 \times L & =m \times 37800+m L \\ L & =2.4 \times 10^4 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How much should the temperature of a brass rod be increased so as to increase its length by $1 \%$ ? (Given, $\alpha$ for brass is $0.00002^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ ).
questions.solution:
Here, $\Delta \mathrm{T}=$ ?, $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{1}{100}$
$\alpha=0.00002^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$
As $\Delta \mathrm{L}=\alpha \mathrm{L} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
$\therefore \quad \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}$
or $\Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L} \alpha}=\frac{1}{100 \times 0.00002}$
$\Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{10^5}{2 \times 10^2}=500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The earth receives solar radiation at a rate of $8.2 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$. If the sun radiates as the black bodies, the temperature at the surface of the sun will be (the angle subtended by sun on the earth in suppose $0.53^{\circ}$ and Stefan constant is $\sigma=5.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2} \mathrm{~K}^4$ )
questions.solution:
Let diameter of the sun is $d$ while radius of the earth is $r$ then
$\begin{aligned} \frac{D}{2} & \approx 0.53 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \\ & =9.25 \times 10^{-3}\end{aligned}$
The radiation emitted per unit area of the sun is
$4 \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 \sigma T^A=\pi D^2 \sigma T^A$
The radiation received at the earths surface per unit time per unit area is $\frac{\pi \sigma d T^4}{4 \pi r^2}=\frac{\sigma T^4}{4}\left(\frac{d}{R}\right)^2$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & & \frac{\sigma T^4}{4}\left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^2 & =82 \mathrm{Jcm}^{-2} \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\ & \text { or } & \frac{1}{4} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times T^4 \times\left(9.25 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2 \\ & = & \frac{8.2}{10^{-4} \times 60} \mathrm{Wm}^{-2} \\ \Rightarrow & & T & =5794 \mathrm{~K} \cong 5800 \mathrm{~K}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One rod of length 2 m and thermal conductivity 50 unit is attached to another rod of length 1 m and thermal conductivity 100 unit. Temperature of free ends are $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Then temperature of junction point will be
questions.solution: Let $\theta$ be the junction temperature, then given situation is shown in the following figure. The rate of heat current due to conduction in both rod will be same, i.e, $\mathrm{H}_1=\mathrm{H}_2$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{K}_1 \frac{A \Delta \theta_1}{\mathrm{l}_1} & =\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{~A} \cdot \frac{\Delta \theta_2}{\mathrm{l}_2} \\ \mathrm{~K}_1 \frac{\Delta \theta_1}{\mathrm{l}_1} & =\mathrm{K}_2 \frac{\Delta \theta_2}{\mathrm{l}_2} \\ 50 \frac{(70-\theta)}{2} & =100 \frac{(\theta-50)}{1} \\ 5 \theta & =270 \Rightarrow \theta=54^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 50 g ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in insulator vessel, 50 g water of $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is mixed in it, then final temperature of the mixture is (neglect the heat loss)
questions.solution:
According to calorimeter principle,
Heat given $=$ Heat taken
$m_1 s_1(100-t)=m L+m_2 s_2(t-0)$
$\begin{aligned} 50 \times 1(100-t) & =50 \times 80+50 \times 1(t-0) \\ t & =10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A black body has a wavelength of $\lambda$ at temperature 2000 K . Its corresponding wavelength at temperature 3000 K will be
questions.solution:
According to Wien's displacement law,
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda_m T=\text { constant } \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\frac{T_1}{T_2} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda}=\frac{2000}{3000}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \text { or } \quad \lambda_2=\frac{2}{3} \lambda \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ice-cube of density $900 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ is floating in water of density $1000 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$. The percentage of volume of ice-cube outside the water is
questions.solution:
The percentage of volume of ice cube outside the water is
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{p_{\text {water }}-p_{\text {ice }}}{p_{\text {water }}} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{1000-900}{1000} \times 100=10 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $0.1 \mathrm{~m}^3$ of water at $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is mixed with $0.3 \mathrm{~m}^3$ of water at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The final temperature of the mixture is
questions.solution:
Let the final temperature of mixture be $t$.
Heat lost by water at $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$=m s \Delta t$
$=0.1 \times 10^3 \times s_{\text {water }} \times\left(80^{\circ}-t\right)$
$\left(\because m=V \times d=0.1 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg}\right)$
Heat gained by water at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$=0.3 \times 10^3 \times s_{\text {water }} \times\left(t-60^{\circ}\right)$
According to principle of calorimetry
Heat lost $=$ Heat gained
$\therefore \quad 0.1 \times 10^3 \times s_{\text {water }} \times\left(80^{\circ}-t\right)$
$=0.3 \times 10^3 \times s_{\text {water }} \times\left(t-60^{\circ}\right)$
or $\quad\left(80^{\circ}-t\right)=3 \times\left(t-60^{\circ}\right)$
or $\quad 4 t=260^{\circ}$
or $\quad t=65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of the sun can be found out by using
questions.solution:
We know solar constant (heat flux falling on the earth), $\mathrm{S}=1350 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$
Let $E$ be the energy emitted by the sun per second per unit area. Then, the total energy emitted by the sun is one second $4 \pi R^2 E$. This energy is falling on a sphere of radius equal to the radius of the Earth's orbit around the sun i.e., on a sphere of surface area $4 \pi \mathrm{r}^2$.
Energy falling per unit area $=\frac{4 \pi R^2 E}{4 \pi r^2}=\frac{E R^2}{r^2}=S$
Using Stefan Boltzman's law:
$\mathrm{E}=\sigma \mathrm{T}^4$
$\mathrm{T}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{S} \times \mathrm{R}^2}{\sigma \times \mathrm{r}^2}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}=5760 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A black body has a wavelength of $\lambda$ at temperature 2000 K . Its corresponding wavelength at temperature 3000 K will be
questions.solution:
According to Wien's displacement law, or
$\lambda_m T=\text { constant }$
or $\quad \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\frac{T_1}{T_2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda}=\frac{2000}{3000}=\frac{2}{3}$
or $\quad \lambda_2=\frac{2}{3} \lambda$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a compound slab consisting of two different materials having equal lengths, thicknesses and thermal conductivities $K$ and $2 K$ respectively. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the slab is
questions.solution:
Equivalent thermal conductivity of the compound slab,
$\begin{aligned}
K_{\mathrm{eq}} & =\frac{l_1+l_2}{\frac{l_1}{K_1}+\frac{l_2}{K_2}}=\frac{l+l}{\frac{l}{K}+\frac{l}{2 K}} \\
& =\frac{2 l}{\frac{3 l}{2 K}}=\frac{4}{3} K
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following circular rods, (given radius $r$ and length $l$ ) each made of the same material and whose ends are maintained at the same temperature will conduct most heat?
questions.solution:
Heat conduction through a rod is given by
$H=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}=K A\left(\frac{T_1-T_2}{l}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad H \propto \frac{r^2}{l}$   ...(i)
(a) When $r=2 r_0 ; l=2 l_0$
$\begin{aligned} & H \propto \frac{\left(2 r_0\right)^2}{2 l_0} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & H \propto \frac{2 r_0{ }^2}{l_0}\end{aligned}$
(b) When $r=2 r_0 ; l=l_0$
$\begin{aligned} & H \propto \frac{\left(2 r_0\right)^2}{l_0} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & H \propto \frac{4 r_0^2}{l_0}\end{aligned}$
(c) When $r=r_0 ; l=l_0$
$H \propto \frac{r_0^2}{l_0}$
(d) When $r=r_0 ; l=2 l_0$
$H \propto \frac{r_0^2}{2 l_0}$
It is obvious that heat conduction will be more in case (b).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following circular rods, (given radius $r$ and length $l$) each made of the same material and whose ends are maintained at the same temperature will conduct most heat?
questions.solution:
Heat conduction through a rod is rate of change of heat $\left(\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}\right)$.
$\therefore H=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}=K A\left(\frac{T_1-T_2}{l}\right)$
$\Rightarrow H \propto \frac{r^2}{l}...(i)$
(a) When
$\begin{aligned} r & =2 r_0, l=2 l_0 \\ H & \propto \frac{\left(2 r_0\right)^2}{2l_0} \Rightarrow H \propto \frac{2 r_0^2}{l_0}\end{aligned}$
(b) When 
$\begin{aligned} r & =2 r_0, l=l_0 \\ H & \propto \frac{\left(2 r_0\right)^2}{ l_0} \Rightarrow H \propto \frac{4 r_0^2}{l_0}\end{aligned}$
(c) When
$\begin{gathered}r=r_0, l=l_0 \\ H \propto \frac{r_0^2}{l_0}\end{gathered}$
(d) When
$\begin{aligned} r & =r_0, l=2 l_0 \\ H & \propto \frac{r_0^2}{2 l_0}\end{aligned}$
It is obvious that heat conduction will be more in case (b).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rods of same material have same length and area. The heat $\Delta Q$ flows through them for 12 minutes when they are joint side by side. If now both the rods are joined in parallel, then the same amount of heat $\Delta Q$ will flow in :
questions.solution: When rods are joined in series then heat flow $\begin{aligned} & \Delta Q_1=\frac{A\left(T_1-T_2\right) t_1}{\frac{l_1}{K_1}+\frac{l_1}{K_2}} \\ & =\frac{A\left(T_1-T_2\right) t_1}{\frac{l}{K_1}+\frac{l}{K_2}}=\frac{A\left(T_1-T_2\right) t_1}{l} \frac{K}{2}\end{aligned}$ When rods are joined in parallel $\begin{aligned} \Delta Q_2 & =\left(K_1 A+K_2 A\right) \frac{\left(T_1-T_2\right) t_2}{l} \\ & =\frac{2 K A\left(T_1-T_2\right) t_2}{l} \\ \because \Delta Q_1 & =\Delta Q_2\quad(given)\end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad t_2=\frac{t_1}{4}=\frac{12}{4}=3 \mathrm{~min}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of specific heat $0.2 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{kg}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is heated through $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The percentage increase in its mass is
questions.solution:
Heat taken by the given body $=m c \Delta \theta$
{specific heat of body $=0.2 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{kg}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, final temperature of the body $\left.=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right\}$
$\begin{aligned} \Delta E & =m \times 0.2 \times 100=20 \mathrm{mkcal} \\ & =20 \mathrm{~m} \times 4.2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
Now gain in mass is given by
$\Delta m=\frac{\Delta E}{C^2}$ $\left(\because E=m c^2\right)$
$=\frac{20 \mathrm{~m} \times 4.2 \times 10^3}{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}$
Therefore, percentage increase in mass is given by
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 \\ & =\frac{20 m \times 4.2 \times 10^3}{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \times m} \times 100 \\ & =9.3 \times 10^{-11} \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of the black body increases from $T$ to $2 T$. The factor by which the rate of emission will increase, is?
questions.solution:
According to Stefan's law for radiated energy
$E \propto T^4$
Hence,
$\frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{T_1^4}{T_2^4}=\left(\frac{T}{2 T}\right)^4=\frac{1}{16}$
$\therefore \quad E_2=16 E_1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal rod at a temperature of $145^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, radiates energy at a rate of 17 W . If its temperature is increased to $273^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then it will radiate at the rate of
questions.solution:
Given, initial temperature of metal $\operatorname{rod}\left(T_1\right)=145^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $=418 \mathrm{~K}$
Rate of radiated energy $\left(E_1\right)=17 \mathrm{~W}$
