Final temperature $\left(T_2\right)=273^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=273+273=546 \mathrm{~K}$
We know, from the Stefan's law, $E \propto T^4$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { or } \quad \frac{E_1}{E_2}=\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4=\left(\frac{418}{546}\right)^4 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{E_1}{E_2}=0.343 \\ & \end{aligned}$
Therefore, final radiated energy, $E_2=\frac{E_1}{0.343}$
$=\frac{17}{0.343}=49.6 \mathrm{~W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rigid boxes containing different ideal gases are placed on table. Box A contains one mole of nitrogen at temperature $T_0$, while box $B$ contains 1 mole of helium at temperature $7 / 3 T_0$. The boxes are then put into thermal contact with each other and heat flows between them until the gases reach a common final temperature (ignore the heat capacity of boxes) then the final temperature of gases, $T_f$ in terms of $T_0$ is
questions.solution:
As, $T=\frac{n_1 T_1+n_2 T_2}{n_1+n_2}$
$\therefore \quad T_f=\frac{1\left(T_0\right)+1\left(\frac{7}{3} T_0\right)}{1+1}$
$=\frac{10 T_0}{3 \times 2}=\frac{5 T_0}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of the cold junction of thermocouple is $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the temperature of hot junction is $T^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The emf is $E=16 T-0.04 T^2 \quad \mu \mathrm{V}$. The inversion temperature $T_i$ is
questions.solution:
As,
$E=16 T-0.04 T^2$
At inversion temperature, $E=0$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad 16 T-0.04 T^2 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad T_i & =\frac{16}{0.04}=400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lead bullet penetrates into a solid object and melts. Assuming that $50 \%$ of its kinetic energy was used to heat it, the initial speed of the bullet is (the initial temperature of the bullet is $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and its melting point is $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Latent heat of fusion of lead $=2.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$ and specific heat capacity of lead $=125 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$
questions.solution:
Given, $S=125 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$
$\theta=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \theta=300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, m=2.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}$, then
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \times \frac{1}{2}=m S \Delta \theta+m L$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & & \frac{v^2}{4} & =125(300-25)+2.5 \times 10^4 \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{v^2}{4} & =59375 \\ \Rightarrow & & v^2 & =59375 \times 4 \\ \Rightarrow & & v & =\sqrt{237500} \Rightarrow v=487.3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ \Rightarrow & & \approx 490 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerine is $49 \times 10^{-50} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. What is the fractional change in density for a $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature?
questions.solution:
Here, $\gamma=49 \times 19^{-5}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$
$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{T} & =30^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ \mathrm{V}^{\prime} & =\mathrm{V}+\Delta \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{l}+\gamma \Delta \mathrm{T})\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{V}^{\prime}=\mathrm{V}\left(1+49 \times 10^{-5} \times 30\right)=1.0147 \mathrm{~V}$
$\because \quad \rho=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{V}}$ and $\rho^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{0.0147}=0.9855 \rho$
Hence, fractional change in density
$=\frac{\rho-\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}=\frac{\rho-0.9855 \rho}{\rho}=0.0145$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface temperature of the sun which has maximum energy emission at 500 nm is 6000 K . The temperature of a star which has maximum energy emission at 400 nm will be
questions.solution:
By using Wien's displacement law,
$\left(\lambda_m\right)_1 T_1=\left(\lambda_m\right)_2 T_2$
$\Rightarrow \quad 500 \times 6000=400 \times T_2$
$\Rightarrow \quad T_2=\frac{500 \times 6000}{400}$
$T_2=7500 \mathrm{~K}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of inversion of a thermocouple is $620^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the neutral temperature is $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, what is the temperature of cold junction?
questions.solution:
The temperature of inversion is higher than the neutral temperature by the same amount as the neutral temperature is higher than the temperature of cold junction.
As per Key Idea
$t_n-t_0=t_i-t_n$
$\Rightarrow t_0=2 t_n-t_i$
Given, $t_i=620^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, t_n=300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Hence, $t_0=$ temperature of cold junction
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 300-620 \\ & =600-620=-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$
NOTE: Neutral temperature does not change for a metal whether the temperature of cold junction and inversion temperature change by any value.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A steel scale measures the length of a copper wire as 80.0 cm, when both are at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the calibration temperature for the scale. What would the scale read for the length of the rod when both are at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
Given: $\alpha$ for steel $=11 \times 10^{-6}$ per ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $\alpha$ for $\mathrm{Cu}=17 \times 10^{-6}$ per $^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
questions.solution:
Using the relation $l_t=l_0(1+\alpha t)$
$\begin{aligned} & =1 \times\left[1+11 \times 10^{-6} \times\left(40^{\circ}-20^{\circ}\right)\right] \\ & =1.00022 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Now length of copper rod at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\begin{aligned} R_t{ }^{\prime} & =l_0{ }^{\prime}\left(1+\alpha^{\prime} t\right) \\ & =80\left[1+17 \times 10^{-6}\left(40^{\circ}-20^{\circ}\right)\right] \\ & =80.0272 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
Now number of cms observed on the scale
$=\frac{80.0272}{1.00022}=80.0096$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A diatomic gas $(\gamma=1.4)$ does 200 J of work when it is expanded isobarically. Find the heat given to the gas in the process.
questions.solution:
For a diatomic gas
$C_V=\frac{5}{2} R$ and $C_p=\frac{7}{2} R$
The work done in an isobaric process is
$\begin{aligned} W & =p\left(V_2-V_1\right)=n R\left(T_2-T_1\right) \\ \Rightarrow T_2-T_1 & =\frac{W}{n R}\end{aligned}$
The heat given in an isobaric process is
$\begin{aligned} Q & =n C_p\left(T_2-T_1\right) \\ n C_p \frac{W}{n R} & =\frac{7}{2} W \\ & =\frac{7}{2} \times 200 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =700 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the efficiency of an engine is $50 \%$ and its work output is 500 J , then find input.
questions.solution:
$\because$ Efficiency, $\eta=\frac{\text { Workdone }}{\text { Heat input }} \times 100$
or $\frac{\eta}{100}=\frac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Heat input }}=\frac{500}{\text { Heat input }}$
Heat input $=\frac{500 \times 100}{50}=1000 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The efficiency of a heat engine is $1 / 6$. Its efficiency double when the temperature of sink decrease by $62^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its efficiency doubles. Then, what is the temperature of source?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Efficiency of a heat engine,
$\eta=1-\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}$
or $\quad \frac{1}{6}=1-\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}$
$\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=1-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}_1}{6}$  ...(i)
According to question, it's efficiency is doubled when temperature of sink decreased by $62^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
$\therefore \quad 2 \eta=1-\frac{\mathrm{T}_2-62}{\mathrm{~T}_1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2\left(\frac{1}{6}\right)=1-\frac{\left(\frac{5 T_1}{6}-62\right)}{T_1} \quad$ [From Eq. (i)]
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & \frac{5 \mathrm{~T}_1}{6}-62 & =\mathrm{T}_1\left(1-\frac{2}{6}\right)=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}_1}{3} \\ \text { or } & \frac{5 \mathrm{~T}_1}{6}-\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}_1}{3} & =62 \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{6}=62 \\ \text { or } & \mathrm{T}_1=372 \mathrm{~K}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The given $p-V$ diagram shows gases during adiabatic process. Plots 1 and 2 should correspond respectively to
questions.solution:
We know that
$\left(\frac{d p}{d V}\right)=-\gamma\left(\frac{p}{V}\right)$
$\therefore \quad$ Slope $\propto \gamma$
From the Fig. (1), slope of $2\gt$ slope of 1 .
So, plots 1 and 2 should correspond to He and $\mathrm{O}_2$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $p-V$ plots for two gases during adiabatic process as shown in figure plots 1 and 2 should correspond respectively to
questions.solution:
Slope of adiabatic process of $p-V$ diagram
$\left(\frac{d p}{d v}\right)=\gamma \frac{p}{V} \text { i.e. slope } \alpha \gamma$
From graph $(\text { slope })_2\gt$ (slope) $)_1$
$\Rightarrow \gamma_2\gt\gamma_1$
$\gamma$ for monoatomic gas (Ar, He) is greater than $\gamma$ for diatomic gas $\left(\mathrm{O}_2, \mathrm{~N}_2\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A Carnot engine absorbs $6 \times 10^5 \mathrm{cal}$ at $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The work done per cycle by the engine, if its sink is maintained at $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{Q}_1=6 \times 10^5 \mathrm{cal}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T}_1=227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=227+273=500 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \mathrm{~T}_2=127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=127+273=400 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$
Work done/cycle, $\mathrm{W}=$ ?
As $\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{Q}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}$ or
$\mathrm{Q}_2=\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1} \times \mathrm{Q}_1=\frac{400}{500} \times 6 \times 10^5$
$=4.8 \times 10^5 \mathrm{cal}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { As, } & \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2=6 \times 10^5-4.8 \times 10^5 \\ \mathrm{~W} & =1.2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{cal}=1.2 \times 10^5 \times 4.2 \mathrm{~J} \\ \mathrm{~W} & =5.04 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a machine perform 4000 J output work and 1000 J is inside loss due to friction, then the find efficiency?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Efficiency $=\frac{\text { output work }}{\text { input work }} \times 100=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{o}}}{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{i}}} \times 100$
$\begin{aligned} & \because \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{o}}=4000 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{i}}=4000+1000=5000 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \therefore \text { Efficiency }=\frac{4000}{5000} \times 100=80 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The slope of isothermal and adiabatic curves are related as
options: isothermal curve slope $=$ adiabatic curve slope
isothermal curve slope $=\gamma \times$ adiabatic curve slope
adiabatic curve slope $=\gamma \times$ isothermal curve slope
adiabatic curve slope $=\frac{1}{2} \times$ isothermal curve slope
questions.solution:
For isothermal process $p V=$ constant $\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d p}{d V}\right)=-\frac{p}{V}=$ slope of isothermal curve
For adiabatic process, $\mathrm{pV}^\gamma=$ constant
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{d p}{d V}\right)=-\frac{\gamma p}{V}=$ slope of adiabatic curve
Clearly, $\quad\left(\frac{d p}{d V}\right)_{\text {adiabatic }}=\gamma\left(\frac{d p}{d V}\right)_{\text {isothermal }}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of source and sink of a heat engine are $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. An inventor claims its efficiency to be $26 \%$, then
options: it is possible with high probability
it is possible with low probability
questions.solution:
Efficiency of heat engine is
$\eta=1-\frac{T_2}{T_1} \text { or } \eta=\frac{T_1-T_2}{T_1}$
Here, $T_1=273+127=400 \mathrm{~K}$
$\begin{aligned}
& T_2=273+27=300 \mathrm{~K} \\
& \therefore \eta=\frac{400-300}{400}=\frac{100}{400}=0.25=25 \%
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $26 \%$ efficiency is impossible for a given heat engine.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: We consider a thermodynamic system. If $\Delta U$ represents the increase in its internal energy and $W$ the work done by the system, which of the following statements is true?
questions.solution:
An isothermal process is a constant temperature process. In this process,
$\begin{aligned} & T=\text { constant or } \Delta T=0 . \\ \therefore & \Delta U=n C_V \Delta T=0\end{aligned}$
An adiabatic process is defined as one with no heat transfer into or out of a system. Therefore,
$Q=0$
From first law of thermodynamics
$W=-\Delta U$
or $\quad \Delta U=-W$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Carnot engine efficiency is $40 \%$ at hot reservoir temperature $T$. For efficiency $50 \%$, what will be temperature of hot reservoir?
questions.solution:
The efficiency of a heat engine is defined as the ratio of work done to the heat supplied, ie,
$\eta=\frac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Heat input }}=\frac{W}{Q}$
or $\quad \eta=1-\frac{T_2}{T_1}$
where $T_2$ is temperature of sink, and $T_1$ is temperature of hot reservoir
$\therefore \quad \frac{40}{100}=1-\frac{T_2}{T_1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_2}{T_1}=0.6$
$\Rightarrow \quad T_1=0.6 T_1$
Again, $\quad \frac{50}{100}=1-\frac{T_2}{T_1^{\prime \prime}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_2}{T_1{ }^{\prime}}=0.5$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{0.6 T_1}{T_1{ }^{\prime}}=0.5 \\ \therefore & T_1{ }^{\prime}=\frac{0.6}{0.5} T_1=\frac{6}{5} T\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When you make ice cubes, the entropy of water
options: may either increase or decrease depending on the process used
questions.solution: The entropy function gives us a numerical measure of the irreversibility of a given process, $i e$, it is a measure of disorder of a system. During formation of ice cubes orderedness increases, $i e$, disorderness decreases, hence entropy decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following processes is reversible?
options: Electrical heating of a nichrome wire
questions.solution:
(a)It is the process by which heat is transmitted from one place to another without heating the intervening medium.
Hence, it is not a reversible process.
(b) Nichrome wire is made of alloys and has high resistance. When current is passed through it, heat is produced. So, here electrical energy is converted into heat energy. Hence, it is not a reversible process.
(c) It is the process by which heat is transmitted from one point to another through a substance in the direction of fall of temperature without the actual movement of the particles of the substances themselves. Hence, it cannot be reversible.
(d) Isothermal compression is reversible, for example-Carnot cycle, Heat engine.
Thus, choice (d) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas heat engine operates in Carnot cycle between $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. It absorbs $6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{cal}$ of heat at higher temperature. Amount of heat converted to work is
questions.solution:
The heat converted to work is the amount of heat that remains after going through sink.
From the relation $\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}=\frac{T_2}{T_1}$
Given, 
$\begin{aligned} Q_1 & =6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{cal}, \\ T_1 & =227+273=500 \mathrm{~K} \\ T_2 & =127+273=400 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{Q_2}{6 \times 10^4}=\frac{400}{500}$
$\Rightarrow  Q_2=\frac{4}{5} \times 6 \times 10^4=4.8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{cal}$
Now, heat converted to work
$\begin{aligned}& =Q_1-Q_2 \\& =6.0 \times 10^4-4.8 \times 10^4 \\
& =1.2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A perfect gas is found to obey the relation $P V^{3 / 2}=$ constant during an adiabatic process. If such a gas initially at a temperature $T$, is compressed to half of its initial volume, then its final temperature will be :
questions.solution:
Gas obeys the equation
$P V^{3 / 2}=$ constant
Comparing with $P V^\gamma=$ constant
$\gamma=\frac{3}{2}$
For adiabatic process
$\begin{aligned} T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1} & =T_2 V_2^{\gamma-1} \\ T(V)^{3 / 2-1} & =T_2\left(\frac{V}{2}\right)^{3 / 2-1} \\ T_2 & =T(2)^{3 / 2-1} \\ & =(2)^{1 / 2} T\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monoatomic gas supplied the heat $Q$ very slowly keeping the pressure constant. The work done by the gas will be
questions.solution:
For monoatomic gas at constant pressure
$\frac{\Delta U}{Q}=\frac{1}{3}$
or $\quad \Delta U=\frac{Q}{3}$
Now applying first law of thermodynamics
$\begin{aligned} W & =\Delta Q-\Delta U \\ & =Q-\frac{Q}{3}=\frac{2 Q}{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the heat of 110 J is added to a gaseous system, whose internal energy is 40 J , then the amount of external work done is
questions.solution:
Heat added to the system $\Delta Q=110 \mathrm{~J}$
Internal energy $\Delta u=40 \mathrm{~J}$
External work done is given by
$\Delta W=\Delta Q-\Delta u=110-40=70 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Amount of work which can be obtained from $200$ cal heat, will be
questions.solution:
Amount of work done is given by
$W=200 \mathrm{cal}=200 \times 4.2 \mathrm{~J}=840 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The efficiency of an ideal gas with adiabatic exponent $\gamma$ for the shown cyclic process would be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{As}, \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\mathrm{p} \Delta \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{nR} \Delta \mathrm{T}=-\mathrm{nR}\left(2 \mathrm{~T}_0-\mathrm{T}_0\right)=\mathrm{nRT}_0$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { and } \quad \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{CA}}=+2 \mathrm{nRT}_0 \ln 2 \\ & \text { Work done }=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{CA}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{BC}} \\ & =2 \mathrm{nRT}_0 \ln 2-\mathrm{nRT}_0 \\ & =\operatorname{nRT}_0(2 \ln 2-1) \\ & \end{aligned}$
Also, input heat, $\Delta \mathrm{Q}_{\mathrm{BC}}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{nR} \gamma \mathrm{T}_0}{\gamma-1}$
Efficiency $=\frac{\text { Work }}{\text { Input heat }}=\frac{(2 \ln 2-1)}{\gamma /(\gamma-1)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 1 kg of water is converted into steam at the same temperature and at 1 atm ( 100 kPa ). The density of water and steam are $1000 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}$ and $0.6 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}$ respectively. The latent heat of vaporisation of water is $2.25 \times 10^6 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1}$. What will be increase in energy?
questions.solution:
The volume of 1 kg water $=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~m}^3$
and volume of 1 kg steam
$=\frac{1}{0.6} \mathrm{~m}^3$
The increase in volume
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{0.6} \mathrm{~m}^3-\frac{1}{1000} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\ & =(1.7-0.001) \mathrm{m}^3 \\ & \cong 1.7 \mathrm{~m}^3\end{aligned}$
The work done by steam is
$\begin{aligned} W & =(100 \mathrm{kPa})\left(1.7 \mathrm{~m}^3\right) \\ & =1.7 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
The change in intemal energy
$\begin{aligned} \Delta U & =\Delta Q-\Delta W \\ & =2.25 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~J}-1.7 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =208 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The volume of an ideal gas is doubled in an isothermal process. Then, which of the following is true?
options: Work done by the gas is positive
Work done by the gas is negative
Internal energy of the system decreases
Internal energy of the system increases
questions.solution:
For isothermal process,
$d U=0$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Work done }=d W=p\left(V_2-V_1\right) \\ & \therefore \quad d W=+p V \quad\left[\because V_2=2 V_1=2 V\right] \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 1 mole of an ideal gas at an initial temperature of $T \mathrm{~K}$ does $6 R$ joule of work adiabatically. If the ratio of specific heats of this gas at constant pressure and at constant volume is $5 / 3$, then final temperature of the gas will be
questions.solution:
Work done by gas in adiabatic process,
$\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& & W & =\frac{n R\left(T_i-T_f\right)}{\gamma-1} \\
\therefore & 6 R & =\frac{1 \times R\left(T-T_2\right)}{\left(\frac{5}{3}-1\right)} \\
& \text { or } & 6 & =\frac{T-T_2}{2 / 3} \\
& \text { or } & T & -T_2=4 \text { or } T_2=(T-4) \mathrm{K}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The work of 146 kJ is performed in order to compress one kilo mole of a gas adiabatically and in this process the temperature of the gas increases by $7^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The gas is
$\left(R=8.3 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right)$
options: a mixture of monoatomic and diatomic
questions.solution:
For adiabatic process,
$\begin{array}{cc} 
& d Q=0 \\
\text { So, } & d U=-\Delta W \\
\Rightarrow & n C_V d T=+146 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{n f R}{2} \times 7=146 \times 10^3 \\
& {[f \rightarrow \text { Degree of freedom }]} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{10^3 \times f \times 8.3 \times 7}{2}=146 \times 10^3 \\
& f=5.02 \approx 5
\end{array}$
So, it is a diatomic gas.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of absolute temperature. The ratio $\frac{C_p}{C_V}$ for the gas is
questions.solution:
Given, that $p \propto T^3$ or
$p T^{-3}=\text { constant } \quad \ldots(i)$
Also for adiabatic process,
$\mathrm{pV}^\gamma=\text { constant }$
But $p V=R T$ (ideal gas law)
or $V=\frac{R T}{p}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad p\left(\frac{R T}{p}\right)^\gamma & =\text { constant } \\ p^{1-\gamma} T^\gamma & =\text { constant }\end{aligned}$
$\text { or } \quad p T^{\gamma /(1-\gamma)}=\text { constant } \quad \ldots(ii)$
Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}=-3$
or $\gamma=-3+3 \gamma$
or $2 \gamma=3$
$\therefore \gamma=\frac{3}{2}$
$\frac{C_p}{C_V}=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following parameters does not characterise the thermodynamic state of matter?
questions.solution: Work does not characterise the thermodynamic state of matter, it is a path function giving only relationship between two quantities.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In isothermal process, which of the following is not true?
options: No heat enters or leaves the system
questions.solution: In isothermal process, heat enters or leaves the system, to keep the temperature constant, so statement (c) is wrong.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For an adiabatic expansion of a mono atomic perfect gas, the volume increases by $24 \%$. What is the percentage decrease in pressure?
questions.solution:
As we know that
$\begin{aligned} p V^\gamma & =K \\ \therefore \Delta p V^\gamma+p \gamma V^{\gamma-1} \Delta V & =0\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{\Delta p}{p}\right)=-\gamma\left(\frac{\Delta V}{V}\right)$
For monoatomic gas, $\gamma=\frac{5}{3}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta p}{p}=\frac{5}{3} \times 24 \%=40 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F$ is given $F=a t+b t^2$, where, $t$ is time. What are the dimensions of $a$ and $b$ ?
questions.solution:
Force $F=a t+b t^2$
From principle of homogeneity,
Dimension of at $=\frac{[F]}{[t]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{T}]}$
$=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-3}\right]$
Similarly, dimensions of
$b=\frac{[F]}{\left[t^2\right]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]}\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-4}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Match the following.
\begin{array}{ll|l}
\hline A. & Angular momentum & 1. \left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right] \\
B. & Torque & 2. \left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right] \\
C. & Gravitational constant & 3. \left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right] \\
D. & Tension & 4. \left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right] \\
\hline
\end{array}
questions.solution:
Dimension of torque $=\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
Dimension of angular momentum $=\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$
Relation $A-4, B-3$ is right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is dimensions of energy in terms of linear momentum (P), area (A) and Time (T)
questions.solution:
Dimensions of energy in terms of momentum (P). Area (A) and Time (T), is related of
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{P}^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{C}}\ldots(i)$
Writing dimensional formula of both side,
$\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right]^a\left[L^2\right]^b[\mathrm{~T}]^{\mathrm{c}}} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2]}\right]=\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{L}^{\mathrm{a}+\mathfrak{}} \mathrm{T}^{-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}}\right]} \\
&
\end{aligned}$
Comparing the exponents, $\mathrm{a}=1, \mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b}=2$
$\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{~b}=1 \\
\mathrm{~b}=\frac{1}{2} \\
-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{c}=-2-1+\mathrm{c}=-2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{C}=-1
\end{gathered}$
$\therefore$ Form Eq. (i), we have
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{E}=\left[\mathrm{PA}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The physical quantity having the dimensions $\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{-3} \mathrm{~T}^3 \mathrm{~A}^2\right]$ is
questions.solution:
Resistivity, $\rho=\frac{m}{n e^2 \tau}$
$\begin{aligned} \because \quad[\mathrm{p}] & =\frac{[\mathrm{M}]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^{-3}\right][\mathrm{AT}]^2[\mathrm{~T}]} \\ & =\left[\mathrm{ML}^3 \mathrm{~A}^{-2} \mathrm{~T}^{-3}\right]\end{aligned}$
So, electrical conductivity
$\sigma=\frac{1}{\rho}$
$[\sigma]=\frac{1}{[\rho]}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{-3} \mathrm{~A}^2 \mathrm{~T}^3\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular amplitude of a simple pendulum is $\theta_0$. The maximum tension in its string will be
questions.solution: The simple pendulum at angular amplitude $\theta_0$ is shown in the figure. $T_{\max }=m g+\frac{m v^2}{I} \quad \ldots(i)$ When, bob of pendulum comes from $A$ to $B$, it covers a vertical distance $h$. $\therefore \quad \cos \theta_0=\frac{l-h}{l}$ $\Rightarrow \quad h=I(I-\cos \theta)\ldots(ii)$ Also during $A$ to $B$, potential energy of bob converts into kinetic energy $\therefore \quad m g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \therefore \quad v=\sqrt{2 g h} \quad \ldots(iii)$ Thus, using Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii) we obtain $T_{\max }=m g+\frac{2 m g}{I} I\left(1-\cos \theta_0\right)$ $=m g+2 m g\left[1-1+\frac{\theta_0{ }^2}{2}\right]$ (By using binomial theorem) $=m g\left(1+\theta_0^2\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimension of force is
questions.solution:
$\because \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{ma}=\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{t}^2} \quad\left[\because \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{t}^2}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} F & =\frac{[M][L]}{[T]^2} \\ \therefore \quad F & =\left[M^1 L^1 T^{-2}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimensions of resistance in an electrical circuit, in terms of dimension of mass $M$, of length $L$, of time $T$ and of current $I$, would be
questions.solution:
Resistance
$R=\frac{\text { potential difference }}{\text { current }}=\frac{V}{i}=\frac{W}{q i}$
( $\because$ Potential difference is equal to work done per unit charge)
So, Dimensions of $R$
$\frac{[Dimensions\quad of\quad work]}{[Dimensions\quad of\quad charge][Dimensions\quad of\quad current]}$
$=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{IT}][\mathrm{I}]}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{I}^{-2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula for Young's modulus is
questions.solution:
The formula for the Young's modulus
$\mathrm{Y}=\frac{\text { stress }}{\text { strain }}$
Dimensions of $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{Y} & =\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}{\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0} \\ & =\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensions of gravitational constant are
questions.solution:
From the formula $G=\frac{F R^2}{m_1 m_2}$
$\begin{aligned} & G=\frac{\text { Dimensions of dimensions of force } F \times \text { dimension of }(R)^2}{\text { dimensions of mass } M \times \text { dimension of mass } M} \end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{L}^2\right]}{[\mathrm{M}][\mathrm{M}]}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F$ is given by $F=a t+b t^2$, where, $t$ is time. What are the dimensions of $a$ and $b$ ?
questions.solution:
Force $F=a t+b t^2$
From principle of homogeneity, Dimensions of $a t=$ dimensions of $F$
$\therefore$ Dimensions of $a=\frac{[F]}{[t]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{T}]}$
$=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-3}\right]$
Similarly, dimensions of
$b=\frac{[F]}{\left[t^2\right]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]}=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-4}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If mass $(\mathrm{M})$, distance $(\mathrm{L})$ and time $(\mathrm{T})$ are fundamental quantities, then find dimension of torque.
questions.solution:
Dimension of torque
$\begin{aligned}
\tau & =\mathrm{F} \times \mathrm{r}=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right][\mathrm{L}] \\
& =\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the dimensions of Planck's constant and that of the moment of inertia is the dimension of
questions.solution:
$E=h \nu$
$\Rightarrow  h=$ Planck's constant $=\frac{E}{\mu}$
$\therefore [h]=\frac{[E]}{[\mu]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]}$
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$
and $I=$ moment of inertia $=M R^2$
Hence, $\frac{[h]}{[I]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2\right]}=\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{[\mathrm{~T}]}=$ dimensions of frequency
Alternative: $\frac{h}{I}=\frac{E / \mu}{I}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{E \times T}{I}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{m}^2 / \mathrm{s}^2\right) \times \mathrm{s}}{\left(\mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{m}^2\right)} \\ & =\frac{1}{s}=\frac{1}{\text { time }}=\text { frequency }\end{aligned}$
Thus, dimensions of $\frac{h}{I}$ is same as of frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a particle varies with distance $x$ from fixed a origin as $v=\left(\frac{A \sqrt{x}}{x+B}\right)$; where, $A$ and $B$ are constants. The dimensions of $A B$ are
questions.solution:
Given, $v=\frac{A \sqrt{x}}{x+B}$....(i)
Dimensions of $v=$ dimensions of potential energy
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
From Eq. (i)
Dimensions of $B=$ dimensions of $x=\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{LT}^0\right]$
$\therefore$ Dimensions of $A$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\text { dimension of } v \times \text { dimensions of }(x+B)}{\text { dimensions of } \sqrt{x}} \\
& =\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{LT}^0\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^0\right]}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{5 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}$
Hence, dimensions of $A B$
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{5 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{LT}^0\right]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{7 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given that $y=A \sin \left[\left(\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(c t-x)\right)\right]$, where $y$ and $x$ are measured in metre. Which of the following statements is true?
options: The unit of $\lambda$ is same as that of $x$ and $A$
The unit of $\lambda$ is same as that of $x$ but not of $A$
The unit of $c$ is same as that of $\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$
The unit of $(c t-x)$ is same as that of $\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$
questions.solution:
Here $\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(c t-x)$ is dimensionless.
Hence, $\frac{c t}{\lambda}$ is also dimensionless and unit of $c t$ is same as that of $x$. Therefore, unit of $\lambda$ is same as that of $x$. Also unit of $y$ is same as that of $A$, which is also the unit of $x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a system of units if force $(F)$, acceleration $(A)$, and time ( $T$ ) are taken as fundamental units, then the dimensional formula of energy is :
questions.solution:
Let energy $E=F^x A^y T^3$
Writing the dimensions on both sides
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]^x\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-2}\right]^y[\mathrm{~T}]^2} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{M}^x \mathrm{~L}^{x+y} \mathrm{~T}^{-2 x-2 y+z}\right]}\end{aligned}$
On comparing both sides
$\begin{aligned} & x=1 \\ & x+y=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y=2-x=2-1=1 \\ & \text { and } \quad-2 x-2 y+z=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow-2 \times 1-2 \times 1+z=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad z=-2+2+2 \\ & =2 \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Dimensional formula of energy
$=F A T^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Huygen's principle does not use
questions.solution: Huygen's principle use the phenomenon of light as reflection, refraction and diffraction but does not use point of spectra origin.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment with sodium vapour lamp of wavelength 589 nm and the slits 0.589 mm apart, the half angular width of the central maximum is
questions.solution:
In Young's double slit experiment half angular width is given by
$\sin \theta=\frac{\lambda}{d}$
$=\frac{589 \times 10^{-9}}{0.589 \times 10^{-3}}=10^{-3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \theta=\sin ^{-1}(0.001)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If prism angle $\alpha=1^{\circ}, \quad \mu=1.54$, distance between screen and prism $(D)=0.7 \mathrm{~m}$, distance between prism and source $a=0.3 \mathrm{~m}$, $\lambda=180 \pi \mathrm{nm}$, then in Fresnal biprism find the value of $\beta$ (fringe width).
questions.solution:
$a=0.3 \quad \alpha=1^{\circ}, \quad \mu=1.54, \quad$ we know that $d=2 a(\mu-1) \alpha$
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 0.3(1.54-1) \times 1 \\ & =2 \times 0.3 \times 0.54=0.324\end{aligned}$
Fringe width $F=\frac{D \lambda}{d}$
$=\frac{0.7 \times 180 \pi \times 10^{-9}}{0.324}$
$=\frac{7 \times 180 \pi \times 10^{-6}}{324}=10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A source emits electromagnetic waves of wavelength 3 m . One beam reaches the observer directly and other after reflection from a water surface, travelling 1.5 m extra distance and with intensity reduced to $1 / 4$ as compared to intensity due to the direct beam alone. The resultant intensity will be
questions.solution:
We know that a phase change of $\pi$ occurs when the reflection takes place at the boundary of denser medium. This is equivalent to a path difference of $\lambda / 2$.
$\therefore$ Total phase difference $=\pi-\pi=0$
Thus, the two waves superimpose in phase.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Resultant amplitude }=\sqrt{I}+\sqrt{\left(\frac{I}{4}\right)}=\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{I} \\
& \text { Resultant intensity }=\left(\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{I}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4} I=\frac{9}{4} \text { fold. }
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light travels faster in air than that in glass. This is accordance with
questions.solution: Light travels faster in air than that in glass. This is accordance with wave theory of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Angular width of central maximum in the Fraunhoffer diffraction pattern of a slit is measured. The slit is illuminated by light of wavelength $6000 Å$. When the slit is illuminated by light of another wavelength, then the angular width decreases by $30 \%$. The same decrease in angular width of central maximum is obtained when the original apparatus is immersed in a liquid. The refractive index of the liquid will be
questions.solution:
Given, $\lambda_1=6000 Å$,
$\lambda_2=?$
Angular width of central maximum $=\frac{2 \lambda}{a}$
$\theta_1=\frac{2 \lambda_1}{a}$....(i)
and 
$\theta_2=\frac{2 \lambda_2}{a}$
$\because \quad \theta_2=\theta_1 \times 0.7$
$\therefore \quad \theta_1 \times 0.7=\frac{2 \lambda_2}{a}$.....(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{0.7} & =\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_2 & =\lambda_1 \times 0.7=6000 \times 0.7 \\ & =4200 Å\end{aligned}$
when immersed in liquid,
$\begin{aligned} \lambda_2 & =\frac{\lambda_1}{\mu} \\ \because \quad \theta_2 & =\theta_1 \times 0.7\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 \lambda_2}{a}=\frac{2 \lambda_1}{a} \times 0.7 \quad\left[\because \lambda_2=\frac{\lambda_1}{\mu}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & \frac{2 \lambda_1 / \mu}{a} & =\frac{2 \lambda_1}{a} \times 0.7 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{\mu} & =0.7 \Rightarrow \mu=1.42\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: White light is used to illuminate two slits in Young's double slit experiment. The separation between the slits is $b$ and the screen is at a distance $d( \gt \gt b)$ from the slits. At a point on the screen directly in front of one of the slits, which wavelengths are missing?
questions.solution: The situation is shown in the figure The path difference between the waves reaching the point $P$ is $\begin{aligned} \Delta p & =B P-A P \\ & =\left(d^2+b^2\right)^{1 / 2}-d=d\left(1+\frac{b^2}{d^2}\right)^{1 / 2}-d \\ & \approx d\left[1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{b^2}{d^2}\right]-d=\frac{b^2}{2 d} \end{aligned}$ For a dark band $\Delta p=\frac{b^2}{2 d}=(2 n-1) \frac{\lambda}{2}$ where, $n=1,2,3$ or $\lambda=\frac{1}{(2 n-1)} \cdot \frac{b^2}{d}$ Hence, the missing wavelengths are $\frac{b^2}{d}, \frac{b^2}{3 d}, \frac{b^2}{5 d},....$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment with sodium vapour lamp of wavelength 589 nm and the slits 0.589 mm apart, the half angular width of the central maximum is
questions.solution:
In Young's double slit experiment, half angular width is given by
$\sin \theta=\frac{\lambda}{d}$
$=\frac{589 \times 10^{-9}}{0.589 \times 10^{-3}}=10^{-3}$
$\theta=\sin ^{-1}(0.001)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If Young's double slit experiment is performed in water instead of air, then
questions.solution:
Fringe width, $\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}$ or $\beta \propto \lambda$
If the experiment is performed in water, wavelength $\lambda$ decreases, so, fringe width also decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coherent light beams of intensities $I$ and $4 I$ are superposed. The maximum and minimum possible intensities in the resulting beam are
questions.solution: Given, $\begin{aligned} I_1=I, I_2 & =4 I \\ I_{\max } & =\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2 \\ & =(\sqrt{I}+\sqrt{4 I})^2=(3 \sqrt{I})^2=9 I \\ I_{\min } & =\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2=(\sqrt{I}-\sqrt{4 I})^2 \\ & =(-\sqrt{I})^2=I\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Interference was observed in an interference chamber when air was present. Now, the chamber is evacuated and if the same light is used, a careful observation will show
options: interference in which breadth of the fringe will be slightly increased
questions.solution:
When chamber is evacuated, the refractive index ( $\mu$ ) decreases. Therefore, wavelength $\left(\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mu}\right)$ increases.
Now, fringe width is given by
$\begin{aligned} & \beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \beta \propto \lambda\end{aligned}$
Therefore, as wavelength increases, the fringe width will increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum number of possible interference maxima for slit-separation equal to twice the wavelength in Young's double-slit experiment, is
questions.solution:
For possible interference maxima on the screen, the condition is
$d \sin \theta=n \lambda\ldots(i)$
Given : $d=$ slit - width $=2 \lambda$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore & 2 \lambda \sin \theta & =n \lambda \\
\Rightarrow & & 2 \sin \theta & =n
\end{aligned}$
The maximum value of $\sin \theta$ is 1 , hence,
$n=2 \times 1=2$
Thus, Eq. (i) must be satisfied by 5 integer values $i e,-2,-1,0,1,2$. Hence, the maximum number of possible interference maxima is 5 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the sun rays are incidenting at $60^{\circ}$ angle and intensity is $I$. If the sun rays are made incident at $30^{\circ}$ angle, then what will be the intensity?
questions.solution:
Ratio of intensities $\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{\cos \theta_1}{\cos \theta_2}=\frac{\cos 60^{\circ}}{\cos 30^{\circ}}$
$I_2=\sqrt{3} I_1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum numbers of possible interference maxima for slit separation equal to twice the wavelength in Young's double slit experiment is
questions.solution:
The condition of interference maxima is
$\begin{aligned} d \sin \theta & =n \lambda \\ \sin \theta & =\frac{n \lambda}{d}\end{aligned}$
Given, 
$\begin{gathered}d=2 \lambda \\ \sin \theta=\frac{n \lambda}{2 \lambda}=n / 2\end{gathered}$
The magnitude of $\sin \theta$ lies between 0 and 1
When $n=0, \sin \theta=0 \Rightarrow \theta=0$
When $n=1, \sin \theta=1 / 2 \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ}$
When $n=2, \sin \theta=1 \Rightarrow \theta=90^{\circ}$
Thus, there is central maximum $\left(\theta=0^{\circ}\right)$, on other side of it maxima lie at $\theta=30^{\circ}$ and $\theta=90^{\circ}$, so maximum number of possible interference maxima is 5.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The intensity of each of the two slits in Young's double slit experiment is $I_0$. Calculate the minimum separation between the points on the screen, where intensities are $2 I_0$ and $I_0$. If fringe width is $b$
questions.solution:
We know that, intensity, $I=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Case I } 2 I_0=4 I_0 \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x \\ & \frac{\pi}{2}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{Y_1 d}{D}\left[\because \Delta x=\frac{Y_1 d}{D} \text { and } \phi=\frac{\pi}{2}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad Y_1=\frac{\lambda D}{4 d}......(i) \\ & \end{aligned}$
Case II $I_0=4 I_0 \cdot \cos ^2 \frac{\phi}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x$
$\frac{2 \pi}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{Y_2 d}{D} \quad\left[\because \phi=\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right.$ and $\left.\Delta x \frac{y_2 d}{D}\right]$
$\therefore \quad Y_2=\frac{\lambda D}{3 d}$.....(ii)
$\therefore \quad \Delta Y=Y_2-Y_1=\frac{\lambda D}{3 d}-\frac{\lambda D}{4 d}=\frac{\lambda D}{12 d}$
$\because \quad \frac{\lambda D}{d}=b$
[given]
$\Delta Y=\frac{b}{12}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In YDSE angular fringe width is $0.1^{\prime}$ wavelength of light used is $6000 Å$. Find the distance between two slits.
questions.solution:
In YDSE,
angular fringe width $\theta=0.1$
and wavelength of light, $\lambda=6000 Å=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$
If ' $d$ ' be the distance between slits then, $\theta=\frac{\lambda}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{d}=\frac{\lambda}{\theta}=\frac{6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.1}=6 \times 10^{-6}=6 \mu \mathrm{m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two slits are separated by a distance of 0.5 mm and illuminated with light of $\lambda=6000 Å$. If the screen is placed 2.5 m , from the slits. The distance of the third bright image from the centre will be
questions.solution:
Distance of nth bright fringe from the centre,
$\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{\mathrm{nD} \lambda}{\mathrm{d}}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{y}_3 & =\frac{3 \times 6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 2.5}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =9 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the distance between two consecutive nodes, if for a string $\mathrm{T}=0.25 \mathrm{~N}, \mu=1 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{m} \mathrm{f}$ $=250 \mathrm{~Hz}$.
questions.solution:
Given, Tension in the string, $\mathrm{T}=0.25 \mathrm{~N}$ a mass
per unit length, $\mu=1 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{m}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$
Velocity of wave, $\quad v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{H}}\ldots(i)$
The distance between two consecutive nodes is equal to half the wave length ' $\lambda$ '.
$\therefore \quad v=f \lambda\ldots(ii)$
Form Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{f} \lambda=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{m}}} \Rightarrow \lambda & =\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mu}}=\frac{1}{250} \sqrt{\frac{0.25}{10^{-6}}} \\
\lambda & =2
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Difference between two consecutive nodes
$=\frac{\lambda}{2}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Linear density of a string of is $1.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and wave equation is $y=0.021 \sin (x+30 t)$. Find, the tension in the string where, $x$ in metre and $t$ in second.
questions.solution:
Mass per unit length $m=1.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$
$y=0.021 \sin (x+30 t)$
Comparing with $y=a \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}\right)$
$a=0.021, \omega=30$
$2 \pi n=30$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad n=\frac{30}{2 \pi} \\ & \text { We know that } \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=1 \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \lambda & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \\ \text { Frequency, } \quad n & =\frac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}} \\ \frac{30}{2 \pi} & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{T}{1.3 \times 10^{-4}}} \\ T & =1.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 900 \\ & =0.117=0.12 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Progressive waves are represented by the equation
$\begin{aligned} & y_1=a \sin (\omega t-x) \\ \text { and } \quad & y_2=b \cos (\omega t-x)\end{aligned}$
The phase difference between waves is
questions.solution: As it is clear that the phase difference between $\cos \theta$ and $\sin \theta$ representing two simple harmonic motion is $90^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coherent light beams of intensities $I$ and $4 I$ are superposed. The maximum and minimum possible intensities in the resulting beam are
questions.solution:
The net intensity due to interference of two beams of intensities \(\mathrm{I}_1, \mathrm{I}_2\) with a phase difference of \(\phi\) is
\(\mathrm{I}_{\text {net }}=\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2+2 \sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1} \sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2} \cos \phi\)
Thus, \(\mathrm{I}_{\text {net }_{\text {max }}}\) occurs for \(\cos \phi=1\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\text {net }_{\max }}=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1}+\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2=9 \mathrm{I}\)
Similarly, \(\mathrm{I}_{\text {net }_{\min }}\) occurs for \(\cos \phi=-1\)
\(\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\text {net }_{\min }}=\left(\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_1}-\sqrt{\mathrm{I}_2}\right)^2=\mathrm{I}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An engine moving towards a wall with a velocity $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ emits a note of 1.2 kHz . Speed of sound in air is $350 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The frequency of the note after reflection from the wall as heard by the driver of the engine is
questions.solution:
The reflected sound appears to propagate in a direction opposite to that of moving engine. Thus, the source and the observer can be presumed to approach each other with same velocity.
$v^{\prime}=\frac{v\left(v+v_o\right)}{\left(v-v_s\right)}$
$=v\left(\frac{v+v_s}{v-v_s}\right) \quad\left(\because v_o=v_s\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad v^{\prime}=1.2\left(\frac{350+50}{350-50}\right)$
$=\frac{1.2 \times 400}{300}$
$=1.6 \mathrm{kHz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $y=3 \sin \pi\left(\frac{t}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right)$ represents an equation of a progressive wave, where $t$ is in second and $x$ is in metre. The distance travelled by the wave in 5 s is
questions.solution:
The given equation of a progressive wave is
$y=3 \sin \pi\left(\frac{t}{2}-\frac{x}{4}\right)=3 \sin 2 \pi\left(\frac{t}{4}-\frac{x}{8}\right)$
The standard equation of a progressive wave is
$y=y_0 \sin 2 \pi\left(\frac{t}{T}-\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)$
Comparing these two equations, we get
$T=4 \mathrm{~s}, \quad \lambda=8 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore$ Velocity of wave,
$v=\frac{\lambda}{T}=\frac{8}{4}=2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Distance travelled by wave in time $t$ is
$s=v t$
or $\quad s=2 \times 5=10 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If frequency $(v)$ of light is $5 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and speed of light in air is $3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Find the ratio of wavelength of light in a medium of refractive index ' 2 ' to air
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{v}=v \lambda$, Here, $v$ is same as source of light.
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{v} \propto \lambda, \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \\
& \text { But } \mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{n}_1}, \mathrm{v}_2 \text { (in air) }=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{\mathrm{n}_2}=\mathrm{c} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{\mathrm{c} / \mathrm{n}_1}{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}_1=2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If speed of sound in air is $340 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and in water is $1480 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If frequency of sound is 1000 kHz, then find wavelength in water
questions.solution:
Given that, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{w}}=1480 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{f}=1000 \mathrm{kHz}$
Speed, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{w}}=\mathrm{f} \lambda$
$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1480}{1000 \times 10^3}=1.480 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A organ pipe open on both ends in the $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ harmonic is in resonance with a source of 1000 Hz . The length of pipe is 16.6 cm and speed of sound in air is $332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Find the value of $n$.
questions.solution:
Given that, $\mathrm{f}=1000 \mathrm{~Hz}, \mathrm{v}=332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{l}=16.6 \mathrm{~cm}$$=16.6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
Frequency in a organ pipe open on both ends,
$\mathrm{f}=\frac{\mathrm{nv}}{2 \mathrm{l}}$
or $\quad \mathrm{n}=\frac{2 \mathrm{fl}}{\mathrm{v}}$
or $\quad \mathrm{n}=\frac{2 \times 1000 \times 16.6 \times 10^{-2}}{332}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A tuning fork $A$ produces 4 beats/s with another tuning fork $B$ of frequency 320 Hz . On filing one of the prongs of $A, 4$ beats $/ \mathrm{s}$ are again heard when sounded with the same fork $B$. Then, the frequency of the fork $A$ before filing is
questions.solution:
There are 4 beats between $A$ and $B$, therefore the possible frequencies of $A$ are 316 or 324 that is $(320 \pm 4) \mathrm{Hz}$.
When the prong of $A$ is filed, its frequency becomes greater than the original frequency. If we assume that original frequency of $A$ is 324 then on filing its frequency will be greater than 324 . The beats between $A$ and $B$ will be more than 4 . But it is given that the beats are again 4 , therefore, 324 is not possible.
Therefore, required frequency must be 316 Hz . (This is true, because on filing the frequency may increase so as to give 4 beats with $B$ of frequency 320 Hz).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Vibrations of rope tied by two rigid ends shown by equation $y=\cos 2 \pi t \sin 2 \pi x$, then minimum length of the rope will be
questions.solution:
Equation of vibration
$\begin{aligned} y & =1 \cos 2 \pi t \sin 2 \pi x \\
& =1 \cos 2 \pi \sin \frac{\pi x}{1 / 2} \end{aligned}$
Comparing this equation with
$y=a \cos 2 \pi \sin \frac{\pi x}{\lambda} \Rightarrow a=1$
Minimum length of string
$=\frac{1}{2} a=\frac{1}{2} \times 1=\frac{1}{2} m$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which one of the following statements is true?
options: Both light and sound waves in air are transverse
The sound waves in air are longitudinal while the light waves are transverse
Both light and sound waves in air are longitudinal
Both light and sound waves can travel in vacנum
questions.solution:
Sound wave is longitudinal because the medium particles move parallel to the direction of wave propagation, whereas a light wave is transverse because the medium particles move perpendicular to direction of wave propagation.
Hence, option 2 is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When an open organ is dipped in water upto half of its height, then its frequency will become
questions.solution:
Frequency of open organ pipe is double than that of closed pipe.
If frequency of open pipe $=n$
$\therefore$ Frequency of closed pipe $=\frac{n}{2}$
On inserting an open pipe into water upto half of its height, it will behave like a closed pipe of half length and frequency is inversely proportional to the length of air column.
Hence, frequency of closed pipe will become double $=2 \times \frac{n}{2}=n$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed organ pipe of length 20 cm is sounded with tuning fork in resonance. What is frequency of tuning fork? $(v=332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})$
questions.solution: If we adjust the length of air-column in closed organ pipe as such its any natural frequency equals to the frequency of tuning fork, then the amplitude of forced vibrations of air-column increases very much. This is the state of resonance. At first resonance $l=\frac{\lambda}{4}$ So, frequency of tuning fork $f=\frac{v}{\lambda}=\frac{v}{4 l}$ Given, $l=20 \mathrm{~cm}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}, v=332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Hence, $\quad f=\frac{0.332}{4 \times 0.2}=415 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A transverse wave is expressed as,
$y=y_0 \sin 2 \pi f t$
For what value of $\lambda$, maximum particle velocity equals to 4 times the wave velocity?
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$y=y_0 \sin 2 \pi f t$
where $A=y_0, k=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$
According to question,
$\begin{array}{rlrl}v & =4 v_p \\ \therefore & A \omega & =4 \frac{\omega}{k} \\ \therefore & y_0 \omega & =\frac{4 \omega}{k} \\ \Rightarrow & & y_0=\frac{4}{k} & =\frac{4}{2 \pi} \times \lambda\end{array}$
Therefore, wavelength $\lambda=\frac{\pi y_0}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation of a wave is $y=5 \sin \left(\frac{t}{0.04}-\frac{x}{4}\right)$; where, $x$ is in cm and $t$ is in second. The maximum velocity of the wave will be
questions.solution:
Equation of wave
$y=5 \sin \left(\frac{t}{0.04}-\frac{x}{4}\right)$
The standard equation of a wave in the given form is
$y=a \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}\right)$
Comparing the given equation with the standard equation, we get
$a=5 \text { and } \omega=\frac{1}{0.04}=25$
Therefore, maximum velocity of particles of the medium,
$v_{\max }=a \omega=5 \times 25=125 \mathrm{cms}^{-1}=1.25 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two vibrating strings of the same material but lengths $L$ and $2 L$ have radii $2 r$ and $r$ respectively. They are stretched under the same tension. Both the strings vibrate in their fundamental modes, the one of length $L$ with frequency $v_1$ and the other with frequency $v_2$. The ratio $v_1 / v_2$ is
questions.solution:
Fundamental frequency,
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& v=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\pi r^2 \rho}} \\
\text { or } & v \propto \frac{1}{l r} \\
\therefore & \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{l_2 r_2}{l_1 r_1}=\frac{2 L \times r}{L \times 2 r}=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one-fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency ?
questions.solution:
Given : $v_o=\frac{v}{5} \Rightarrow v_o=\frac{320}{5}=64 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
When observer moves towards the stationary source, then
$\begin{aligned}
n^{\prime} & =\left(\frac{v+v_0}{v}\right) n \\
n^{\prime} & =\left(\frac{320+64}{320}\right) n \\
n^{\prime} & =\left(\frac{384}{320}\right) n \\
\frac{n^{\prime}}{n} & =\frac{384}{320}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, percentage increase
$\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{n^{\prime}-n}{n}\right) & =\left(\frac{384-320}{320} \times 100\right) \% \\
& =\left(\frac{64}{320} \times 100\right) \%=20 \%
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sound absorber attenuates the sound level by 20 dB . The intensity decreases by a factor of
questions.solution:
Let intensity of sound be $I$ and $I^{\prime}$ Loudness of sound initially
$\beta_1=10 \log \left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$
Later,
$\beta_2=10 \log \left(\frac{I^{\prime}}{I_0}\right)$
Given,
$\beta_2-\beta_1=20$
$\therefore \quad 20=10 \log \left(\frac{I^{\prime}}{I}\right)$
$\therefore \quad I^{\prime}=100 I$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time of reverberation of a room $A$ is one second. What will be the time (in seconds) of reverberation of a room, having all the dimensions double of those of room $A$ ?
questions.solution:
Sabine formula for reverberation time is
$T=\frac{0.16 \mathrm{~V}}{\Sigma a S}$
where $V$ is volume of hall in $\mathrm{m}^3$.
$\Sigma a S=a_1 S_1+a_2 S_2+\ldots$
$=$ total absorption of the hall (room)
Here, $S_1, S_2, S_3 \ldots \ldots$ are surface areas of the absorbers and $a_1, a_2, a_3 \ldots$. . are their respective absorption coefficients.
$\therefore \quad \frac{T^{\prime}}{T}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{S^{\prime}} \times \frac{S}{V}=\frac{(2)^3}{(2)^2}=\frac{8}{4}=2$
Hence, $\quad T^{\prime}=2 T=2 \times 1=2 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which one of the following statements is true?
options: Both light and sound waves in air are transverse
The sound waves in air are longitudinal while the light waves are transverse
Both light and sound waves in air are longitudinal
Both light and sound waves can travel in vacuum
questions.solution: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium oscillate about their mean or equilibrium position along the direction of propagation of the wave itself. Sound waves are longitudinal in nature. In transverse wave, the particles of the medium oscillate about their mean or equilibrium position at right angles to the direction of propagation of wave itself. Light waves being electromagnetic are transverse waves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point source emits sound equally in all directions in a non-absorbing medium. Two points $P$ and $Q$ are at distance of 2 m and 3 m respectively from the source. The ratio of the intensities of the waves at $P$ and $Q$ is
questions.solution:
The average power per unit area that is incident perpendicular to the direction of propagation is called the intensity.
Intensity of sound
$I=\frac{P}{4 \pi r^2}$
or, $I \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$
or, $\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)^2$
here, $r_1=2 \mathrm{~m}, r_2=3 \mathrm{~m}$
Substituting the values, we have
$\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two vibrating tuning forks produce progressive waves given by $y_1=4 \sin 500 \pi t$ and $y_2=2 \sin 506 \pi t$. Number of beats produced per minute is
questions.solution:
To reach the solution the given wave equations must be compared with standard equation of progressive wave.
So,
$\begin{aligned} & y_1=4 \sin 500 \pi t...(i) \\ & y_2=2 \sin 506 \pi t...(ii)\end{aligned}$
Comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii) with $y=a \sin \omega t...(iii)$
We have, $\omega_1=500 \pi$
$\Rightarrow f_1=\frac{500 \pi}{2 \pi}=250$ beats $/ \mathrm{s}$
and $\omega_2=506 \pi$
$\Rightarrow f_2=\frac{506 \pi}{2 \pi}=253$ beats $/ \mathrm{s}$
Thus, number of beats produced
$\begin{aligned} & =f_2-f_1=253-250 \\ & =3 \text { beats } / \mathrm{s} \\ & =3 \times 60 \text { beats } / \mathrm{min} \\ & =180 \text { beats } / \mathrm{min}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: There are 26 tuning forks arranged in the decreasing order of their frequencies. Each tuning fork gives 3 beats with the next. The first one is octave of the last. What is the frequency of 18 th tuning fork?
questions.solution:
Each tuning forck gives 3 beats with the next, so the difference in the frequencies of two consecutive forks is 3 .
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad f_{26} & =f_1+(n-1) \times-3 \\ f & =2 f+(26-1) \times-3 \\ f & =75 \mathrm{~Hz} .\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Frequency of 18 th tuning fork
$\begin{aligned} f_{18} & =f_1+(18-1) \times-3 \\ & =2 \times 75+17 \times-3 \\ & =150-51=99 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person is observing two trains one coming towards him and other leaving with the same velocity $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If their whistling frequencies are 240 Hz each, then the number of beats per second heard by the person will be : (if velocity of sound is $320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
questions.solution:
Observed frequency of first train
$\begin{aligned} n_1 & =\frac{v}{v-v_s} \times n \\ & =\frac{320}{320-4} \times 240 \\ & =\frac{320}{316} \times 240 \\ & =243 \mathrm{~Hz} .\end{aligned}$
Observed frequency of second train
$\begin{aligned} n_2 & =\frac{v}{v+v_s} n \\ & =\frac{320}{320+4} \times 240 \\ & =237 \mathrm{~Hz} .\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Number of beats $=243-237=6$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation of stationary wave along a stretched string is given by $y=5 \sin \frac{\pi x}{3} \cos 40 \pi t$, where, $x$ and $y$ are in cm and $t$ in second. The separation between two adjacent nodes is :
questions.solution:
Given, equation
$y=5 \sin \frac{\pi x}{3} \cos 40 \pi t$
Comparing with standard equation $y=A \sin k x \cos \omega t$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\therefore & k & =\frac{\pi}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} & =\frac{\pi}{3} \\ \lambda & =6 \mathrm{~cm}\end{array}$
Separation between two consecutive nodes $=\frac{\lambda}{2}=\frac{6}{2}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Equations of motion in the same direction are given by
$\begin{aligned}
& y_1=2 a \sin (\omega t-k x) \\
& y_2=2 a \sin (\omega t-k x-\theta)
\end{aligned}$
The amplitude of the medium particle will be
questions.solution:
The given equations of motion are
$\begin{aligned} & y_1=2 a \sin (\omega t-k x) \\ & y_2=2 a \sin (\omega t-k x-\theta)\end{aligned}$
Now the resultant equation of wave is given by
$\begin{aligned} & y=y_1+y_2 \\ & =2 a \sin (\omega t-k x)+2 a \sin (\omega t-k x-\theta) \\ & y=2 a\left[2 \sin \frac{(\omega t-k x+\omega t-k x-\theta)}{2}\times \cos \frac{\omega t-k x-(\omega t-k x-\theta)}{2}\right]\end{aligned}$
$y=4 a \cos \frac{\theta}{2} \sin \left(\omega t-k x-\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$ ...(1)
Now comparing equation (1) with $y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)$, we have
Resultant amplitude $A=4 a \cos \frac{\theta}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed orgon pipe and an open orgon pipe are tuned to the same fundamental frequency. What is the ratio of their lengths?
questions.solution:
Avoiding end correction, the length of the closed organ pipe is
$l_1=\frac{\lambda_1}{4}$ or $\lambda_1=4 l_1$
The length of open organ pipe is
$l_2=\frac{\lambda_2}{2}$ or $\lambda_2=2 l_2$
Here, $n_1=n_2$
So, $\frac{v}{\lambda_1}=\frac{v}{\lambda_2}$ or $\frac{v}{4 l_1}=\frac{v}{2 l_2}$
Therefore, $l_1: l_2=1: 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A resonance air column of length 40 cm resonates with a tuning fork of frequency 450 Hz . Ignoring end correction, the velocity of sound in air will be
questions.solution:
Here : Length of column $=40 \mathrm{~cm}=0.4 \mathrm{~m}$
Frequency of tuning fork $=450 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Using the formula $n=\frac{v}{4 l}$ or
$\begin{aligned}v & =450 \times 4 \times 0.4 \\& =720 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If vibration of string is to be two times, then tension in the string must be made
questions.solution:
Here: Initial vibration $n_1=n$, final vibration $n_2=2 n$
Initial tension $T_1=T$
Vibration of frequency of string
$n=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad n \propto \sqrt{T}$
Hence,
$\frac{n_1}{n_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}}$
or $\frac{T_1}{T_2}=\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{n}{2 n}\right)^2=\frac{1}{4}$
or $T_2=4 T_1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A siren emitting sound of frequency 500 Hz is going away from a stationary listener with a speed of $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, the frequency of sound heard directly from the siren is
questions.solution:
Here: Frequency of siren $=500 \mathrm{~Hz}$
The speed of siren $v_s=50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Using Doppler's principle
$n^{\prime}=\frac{v}{v+v_s} n$
or $\quad n^{\prime}=\left(\frac{330}{330+50}\right) \times 500$
or $\quad n^{\prime}=\frac{330 \times 500}{380} = 434.2 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When sound waves travel from air to water which one of the following remains constant?
questions.solution: Frequency of a sound wave remains independent of the medium through which it travels.Wavelength, velocity and phase change with the change in media.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed organ pipe of length 20 cm is sounded with tuning fork in resonance. What is the frequency of tuning fork? $(v=332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s})$
questions.solution: Key Idea : When length of air column is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$, then first resonance occurs. If we adjust the length of air-column in closed organ pipe as such its any natural frequency equals to the frequency of tuning fork, then the amplitude of forced vibrations of air-column increases very much. This is the state of resonance. At first resonance $l=\frac{\lambda}{4}$ So, frequency of tuning fork $f=\frac{v}{\lambda}=\frac{v}{4 l}$ Given, $l=20 \mathrm{~cm}=0.2 \mathrm{~m}, v=332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Hence, $f=\frac{332}{4 \times 0.2}=415 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How much intense is 80 dB sound in comparision to 40 dB ?
questions.solution:
$\because \mathrm{B}_1=10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_0}\right)$
and $\quad \mathrm{B}_2=10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{\mathrm{I}_0}\right)$
$\because$ Given, $\mathrm{B}_1=80 \mathrm{~dB}, \mathrm{~B}_2=40 \mathrm{~dB}$
$\therefore \quad 80=10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_0}\right)$
or $\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_0}=10^8$ and $40=10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{\mathrm{I}_0}\right)$
or $\quad \frac{\mathrm{I}_2}{\mathrm{I}_0}=10^4$
Hence, $\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{I}_2}=10^4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If frequencies are $(v-1),(v+1)$, then find beats.
questions.solution:
For frequencies $v_1=v-1$ and $v_2=v+1$.
$\begin{aligned}
\text { Beats } & =\text { difference of frequencies } \\
& =v_2-v_1=(v+1)-(v-1)=v+1-v+l=2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A stationary wave equation is given as $y=20 \sin (20 x) \cos (1000 t)$.
What will be the speed of stationary wave?
questions.solution: In stationary waves, the positions of nodes and antinodes do not change with time, hence speed of stationary wave is not defined.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 4 kg block is suspended from the ceiling of an elevator through a spring having a linear mass density of $19.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. Find the speed with respect to spring with which a wave pulse can proceed on the spring if the elevator accelerates up at the rate of $2.0 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. Take $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
questions.solution: Given, $m=19.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ Now see the free body diagram. The equation for the motion $\begin{aligned} T-4 g-4 a & =0 \\ T & =4(a+g)=4(2+10) \\ & =48 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$ Now wave speed, $\begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{\left(\frac{T}{m}\right)} \\ & =\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}=50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sound source producing waves of frequency 300 Hz and wavelength 1 m observer is stationary, while source is going away with the velocity $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then apparent frequency heared by the observer is
questions.solution:
Apparent frequency $n^{\prime}=n\left(\frac{v}{v+v_s}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} v & =\text { velocity of sound, } \\ v_s & =\text { velocity of source }\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad n^{\prime}=300\left(\frac{332}{332+30}\right)=273 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bat emitting an ultrasonic wave of frequency $4.5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~Hz}$ at speed of $6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ between two parallel walls. The two frequencies heared by the bat will be
questions.solution: Frequency received by bat after reflection from wall (1). $\begin{aligned} t_2 & =f\left[\frac{v-u}{v+u}\right] \\ & =4.5 \times 10^4\left[\frac{330-6}{300+6}\right] \\ & =4.34 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass 7 kg is executing circular motion with time period of 11 sec . Find out centripetal force, if radius of circle is 10 m .
questions.solution:
Given, Time period $(\mathrm{T})=11 \mathrm{sec}$, mass of particle $(\mathrm{m})=7 \mathrm{~kg}$
and $\quad$ radius $(\mathrm{r})=10 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad$ Speed of particle, $v=\frac{2 \pi r}{T}=\frac{2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10}{11}$ $=\frac{40}{7} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ Centripetal force, $\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{7 \times \frac{40}{7} \times \frac{40}{7}}{10}=\frac{160}{7} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is moving with $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in a circle of radius 5 m , find out magnitude of average velocity if particle moved by $60^{\circ}$ in 1 sec.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, velocity of particle } v=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \text { radius }\left(r^{\prime}\right)=5 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { Angular displacement }(\theta)=60^{\circ} \\ & \therefore \quad \theta=60 \times \frac{\pi}{180} \mathrm{rad} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\frac{\pi}{3} \mathrm{rad} \\ & \end{aligned}$ When particle moves at an angle of $60^{\circ}$, then according to figure, displacement AB is equal to radius $r$ because it forms an equilateral triangle. $\therefore$ Average velocity $=\frac{\text { displacement } \mathrm{AB}}{\text { time }}=\frac{\mathrm{r}^{\prime}}{1}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man applying force of 20 N on an object at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ from the horizontal. If object moved by 20 m in horizontal direction, then work done by the force on the object is
questions.solution:
Given, force, $\mathrm{f}=20 \mathrm{~N}$,
angle
$\theta=60^{\circ}$
and displacement $(\mathrm{s})=20 \mathrm{~m}$,
$\therefore$ Work done by the force
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{W} & =\mathrm{fs} \cos \theta=20 \times 20 \times \cos 60^{\circ}=20 \times 20 \times \frac{1}{2} \\
& =200 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is executing oscillation about the origin on $X$-axis. Its potential energy is $V(x)=K|x|^3$. Where $K$ is a positive constant. If the amplitude of oscillation is $a$, then its time period $T$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
Given, $V=k x^3$
Total energy $(E)=$ Maximum potential energy
$=k a^3$
From conservation of energy
$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^2+k x^2 & =k a^3 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^2 & =k\left(a^3-x^3\right) \\ \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}\left(a^3-x^3\right)} \\ \frac{d x}{\sqrt{a^3-x^3}} & =\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}} d t\end{aligned}$
or Integrating $\int_0^a \frac{d x}{\sqrt{a^3-x^3}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}} \int_0^{T / 4} d t$ 
or $\int_0^a \frac{d x}{\sqrt{a^3-x^3}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}} \frac{T}{4}$
Let $x=a \sin \theta \Rightarrow d x=a \cos \theta d \theta$
$\therefore \int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{a \cos \theta d \theta}{\sqrt{a^3-(a \sin \theta)^3}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{m}} \frac{T}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rod of length $l$ is free to rotate in a vertical plane about a fixed horizontal axis through $B$. The rod begins rotating from rest from its unstable equilibrium position. When, it has turned through an angle $\theta$, its angular velocity $\omega$ is given by
questions.solution: When the rod rotates through an angle $\theta$, the centre of gravity falls through a distance h. From $\triangle B C G^{\prime}$ $\begin{aligned} & \cos \theta=\frac{(l / 2)-h}{\mu_2} \\ & or, h=\frac{1}{2}(1-\cos \theta)\end{aligned}$ Decrease in PE $=m g \frac{1}{2}(1-\cos \theta)...(i)$ The decrease in P.E. is equal to the kinetic energy of rotation $\left(\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2\right)$ $(\mathrm{KE})_{\text {rotational }}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{m l^2}{3}\right) \omega^2...(ii)$ From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{m l^2}{3}\right) \omega^2 & =m g \frac{1}{2}(1-\cos \theta) \\ \omega & =\sqrt{\frac{6 g}{l}} \sin \frac{\theta}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires are stretched through same distance. The force constant of second wire is half as that of the first wire. The ratio of work done to stretch first wire and second wire will be
questions.solution:
AsW $=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$
If both wires are stretched through same distance, then $W \propto k$.
It is given that $k_2=\frac{k_1}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{W_1}{W_2} & =\frac{k_1}{k_2} \\ & =\frac{k_1}{\frac{k_1}{2}}=\frac{2}{1}=2: 1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Work is being performed, when a weight lifter lifts a base ball off a weight rack. This is due to
questions.solution: Due to gravity, barbell is lifted and hence work is done.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle slides on surface of a fixed smooth sphere starting from topmost point. The angle rotated by the radius through the particle, when it leaves contact with the sphere, is
questions.solution: See the diagram Let the velocity be $v$ when the body leaves the surface. From free body diagram, $\frac{m v^2}{R}=m g \cos \theta$. $\Rightarrow \quad v^2=R g \cos \theta$ ...(i) Again from work energy principle, change in $\mathrm{KE}=$ work done $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} m v^2-0=m g[R-R \cos \theta]$ $v^2=2 g R(1-\cos \theta)$ ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii) $\begin{aligned} R g \cos \theta & =2 g R(1-\cos \theta) \\ 3 g R \cos \theta & =2 g R \\ \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta & =\frac{2}{3} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is released from certain height which losses $50 \%$ of its kinetic energy on striking the ground, it will attain a height again
questions.solution:
Ratio of heights
$\frac{h_1}{h_2}=\frac{100}{100-50}=2$
$h_2=\frac{h_1}{2}=\frac{1}{2}$ of the initial height.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If we increase kinetic energy of a body $300 \%$, then per cent increase in its momentum is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & p^2=2 M E \\ & \therefore \quad p \propto \sqrt{E} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{p_1}{p_2}=\sqrt{\frac{E_1}{E_2}}=\sqrt{\frac{E}{E+3 E}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad p_2 & =2 p_1 \\ \% \text { Increase } & =\frac{p_2-p_1}{p_1} \times 100\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{2 p_1-p_1}{p_1} \times 100=100 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A engine pumps up 100 kg of water through a height of 10 m in $5$s . Given that, the efficiency of engine is $60 \%$. If $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the power of this engine is
questions.solution:
Efficiency of engine $\eta=60 \%$.
$\begin{aligned} \text { Thus, power } & =\frac{\text { work } / \text { time }}{\eta} \\ & =\frac{100}{60} \times \frac{m g h}{t}
\end{aligned}$
Given, $m=100 \mathrm{~kg}, \quad h=10 \mathrm{~m}, \quad t=5 \mathrm{~s}$ and $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
$\begin{aligned}\text { Hence, power } & =\frac{100}{60} \times \frac{100 \times 10 \times 10}{5} \\& =3.3 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~W}=3.3 \mathrm{~kW}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass 100 g is thrown vertically upward with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The work done by the force of gravity during the time the particle goes up is
questions.solution:
The height $(h)$ transversed by particle while going up is
$n=\frac{u^2}{2 g}=\frac{25}{2 \times 9.8}$
Work done by gravity force $=m \mathrm{g} \cdot \mathrm{h}$
$=0.1 \times g \times \frac{25}{2 \times 9.8} \cos 180^{\circ}$
(angle between force and displacement is $180^{\circ})$
$\therefore \quad W=-0.1 \times \frac{25}{2}=-1.25$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas expands $0.25 \mathrm{~m}^3$ at constant pressure $10^3 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$, the work done is
questions.solution:
From the formula of work done
$W=p \Delta V=10^3 \times 0.25=250 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done by a force acting on a body is as shown in the graph. The total work done in covering an initial distance of 20 m is
questions.solution: Work done $W=$ Area $A B C D E F G A$ $=$ Area $A B C D G+$ Area $D E F G$ $=\frac{1}{2} \times(15+10) \times 10+\frac{1}{2} \times(10+20) \times 5$ $=125+75=200 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball of mass 2 kg moving with velocity $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, collides with spring of natural length 2 m and force constant $144 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. What will be length of compressed spring?
questions.solution: Let spring is compressed by a length $x$. Kinetic energy of ball = Potential energy of spring $i e, \quad \frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} k x^2$ Given, $\quad m=2 \mathrm{~kg}, v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, k=144 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(3)^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 144 \times x^2$ $\therefore \quad x=\sqrt{\frac{9}{72}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~m}$ Hence, length of compressed spring $=2-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ $=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}-1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ $=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential energy of a body is given by $U=A-B x^2$ (where $x$ is the displacement). The magnitude of force acting on the particle is
questions.solution:
$U=A-B x^2$
$F=-\frac{d U}{d v}=2 B x$
$F \propto x$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass 100 g is thrown vertically upwards with a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The work done by the force of gravity during the time the particle goes up is
questions.solution: The height ( $h$ )traversed by particle while going up is $h=\frac{u^2}{2 g}=\frac{25}{2 \times 9.8}$ Work done by gravity force $=m \overrightarrow{\mathbf{g}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{h}}$ $=0.1 \times g \times \frac{25}{2 \times 9.8} \cos 180^{\circ}$ [Angle between force and displacement is $180^{\circ}$ ] $\therefore W=-0.1 \times \frac{25}{2}=-1.25 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A machine gun fires 360 bullets per minute, with a velocity of $600 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. If the power of the gun is 5.4 kW , then mass of each bullet is
questions.solution:
Here, $\mathrm{n}=\frac{360}{60}=6$ bullets s ${ }^{-1}$
Velocity, $\mathrm{v}=600 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}, \mathrm{~m}=$ ?
Power of gun = Power of bullets
$5.4 \times 10^3=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~nm}) \mathrm{v}^2$
$2 \times 5400=6 \times \mathrm{m}(600)^2$
or $\quad \begin{aligned} \mathrm{m} & =\frac{2 \times 5400}{6 \times 600 \times 600} \mathrm{~kg} \\ & =\frac{1}{200} \mathrm{~kg}=\frac{1000}{200} \mathrm{~g}=5 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two 20 g flatworms climb over a very thin wall, 10 cm high. One of the worm is 20 cm long, the other is wider and only 10 cm long. Which of the following statement is correct regarding them?
options: 20 cm worm has done more work against gravity
10 cm worm has done more work against gravity
Both worms have done equal work against gravity
Ratio of work done by both the worms is $4: 5$
questions.solution: The work done against gravity can be calculated from the increases in height of the centre of mass. The centre of mass of a worm folded in two is located at the middle of either half, i.e. at a point one-quarter of the worm's total length from one end, as given in the figure below: Thus, the centre of mass of the narrow flat worm travels 5 cm up the wall; while that of the broad one moves 7.5 cm . Hence, work done by 10 cm worm is more against gravity. Also,the ratio of the work done is $2: 3$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Kinetic energy of a particle is increased by 4 times. What will be the relation between initial and final momentum?
questions.solution:
$\because$ Relation between kinetic energy and momentum is $\mathrm{p}_1=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mk}_1}$
$\because$ Kinetic energy is increased by 4 times
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{k}_2=4 \mathrm{k}_1$
Hence, $\mathrm{p}_2=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mk}_2}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}\left(4 \mathrm{k}_1\right)}=2 \sqrt{2 \mathrm{mk}_1}$
